Persistence</a> (29)</strong><ul><li><a href="#data-persistence-nosql" class="internal-link">NoSQL</a> (6)</li><li><a href="#data-persistence-orms" class="internal-link">ORMs</a> (2)</li><li><a (12)</li></ul></li><li><strong><a href="#devops" class="internal-link">DevOps</a> (17)</strong><ul><li><a href="#devops-deployment-strategies" class="internal-link">Deployment Strategies</a> class="internal-link">Security</a> (32)</strong><ul><li><a href="#security-authentication" class="internal-link">Authentication</a> (12)</li></ul></li></ul><hr /><h1 id="ai-ml">AI &amp; ML</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you reach for classic ML instead of an LLM API?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Classic ML wins when the task is a well-defined prediction with labeled data: classification, regression, ranking — millisecond latency and near-zero per-request cost at scale</li>
<li dir="auto">LLMs win when the task involves open-ended language understanding or generation, training data is scarce, or iteration speed matters more than unit cost</li>
<li dir="auto">A common production pattern: prototype with an LLM to validate the product, then distill the stable, high-volume part into a small fine-tuned model</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: classic ML trades upfront data and training effort for cheap, fast, predictable inference; LLMs trade per-call cost and latency for flexibility and zero training<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/AI &amp; ML.md" class="internal-link">AI &amp; ML</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does evaluation discipline matter more than model choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Without held-out evaluation, every model swap, prompt change, or retraining run is a guess — improvements cannot be distinguished from noise or regressions</li>
<li dir="auto">Production failures are dominated by data and distribution problems (drift, leakage, segment regressions), which only evaluation and monitoring catch — not by raw model capability</li>
<li dir="auto">A weaker model with solid evaluation and a feedback loop improves over time; a stronger model without them silently degrades</li>
<li dir="auto">This is why every branch of this section has its own evaluation pages: <a class="internal-link" href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Evaluation" aria-label="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Evaluation" data-tooltip-position="top">ML Evaluation</a> and the general <a class="internal-link" href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Evaluation" aria-label="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Evaluation" data-tooltip-position="top">LLM Evaluation</a>, which RAG and agents specialize in <a class="internal-link" href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Evaluation/Evaluation" aria-label="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Evaluation/Evaluation" data-tooltip-position="top">RAG Evaluation</a> and <a class="internal-link" href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Evaluation" aria-label="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Evaluation" data-tooltip-position="top">Agent Evaluation</a><br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/AI &amp; ML.md" class="internal-link">AI &amp; ML</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do the six principles translate into concrete engineering work?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Fairness → slice-based evaluation, dataset audits, per-segment production monitoring</li>
<li dir="auto">Reliability and safety → adversarial testing, fallback paths, staged rollout via shadow mode</li>
<li dir="auto">Privacy and security → data minimization, anonymization, encryption, RBAC on models and data</li>
<li dir="auto">Inclusiveness → multilingual and accessibility evaluation, low-resource device testing</li>
<li dir="auto">Transparency → model cards, decision logging, explanatory UI for consequential outputs</li>
<li dir="auto">Accountability → human review gates, escalation ownership, rollback procedures</li>
<li dir="auto">The common thread: every principle is testable and monitorable — if it only lives in a policy document, it is not implemented<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Responsible AI.md" class="internal-link">Responsible AI</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is aggregate accuracy insufficient evidence that a system is fair?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Aggregate metrics average over the whole population, so strong majority-group performance can mask poor performance on minority groups</li>
<li dir="auto">A system can be 95% accurate overall while being 70% accurate for a specific demographic — the aggregate number hides exactly the disparity fairness is about</li>
<li dir="auto">The fix is slice-based evaluation: measure the same metrics per demographic segment and treat a large gap as a defect, even when the aggregate looks healthy</li>
<li dir="auto">This mirrors the segmentation principle used elsewhere in monitoring: averages lie<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Responsible AI.md" class="internal-link">Responsible AI</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="ai-ml-llm">LLM</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does adding more context often make answers worse, not better?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Model attention is uneven: it concentrates on the start and end of the window and underweights the middle ("lost in the middle"), so critical evidence placed there is effectively ignored</li>
<li dir="auto">Overall quality degrades as input grows even when the added tokens are relevant — attention dilutes across more material (context rot)</li>
<li dir="auto">Irrelevant tokens actively compete with relevant ones for finite attention, and larger contexts cost more and add latency</li>
<li dir="auto">The fix is signal density: fewer, higher-quality, well-ordered chunks beat a larger but noisier window — measure quality as you vary context size rather than assuming more helps<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering.md" class="internal-link">Context Engineering</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the main techniques for keeping a long-running agent's context under control?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Compaction: summarize or prune older turns before they crowd out the task; cap tool-result size to only the fields needed</li>
<li dir="auto">Offloading: move large intermediate state to a scratchpad or filesystem and pass lightweight references back into the window</li>
<li dir="auto">Isolation: split work across sub-agents along context boundaries so each window stays focused</li>
<li dir="auto">Selection and ordering: retrieve few high-signal chunks and place the most important evidence at the start and end</li>
<li dir="auto">Accounting: track cumulative tokens per iteration and compact or terminate before the window fills, rather than letting the runtime truncate the oldest (often most important) messages<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Context Engineering.md" class="internal-link">Context Engineering</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How can Matryoshka dimensionality reduction lower embedding storage costs without significant recall loss?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use the <code>dimensions</code> parameter to truncate vectors to 256 or 512 dimensions. Models trained with Matryoshka objectives produce vectors where any prefix is independently meaningful. Truncation to 256 dims reduces storage by ~6x and speeds up ANN search proportionally. Validate by comparing recall@k on your evaluation set at 256, 512, and full dimensions — MRL models can retain strong retrieval quality at reduced dimensions, but the actual degradation is model-specific and corpus-dependent. If recall drops unacceptably, use a two-stage approach: retrieve at reduced dimensions, then re-rank candidates using full-dimensional vectors.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Embeddings.md" class="internal-link">Embeddings</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can switching to a higher-scoring embedding model cause recall to drop on existing queries?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Different embedding models produce vectors in different geometric spaces — cosine similarity between vectors from two different models is meaningless. If the corpus is not re-embedded with the new model, queries encoded with the new model are compared against vectors from the old model, producing degraded or random rankings. The fix is to re-embed the entire corpus with the new model before switching query-time inference. This is also why embedding caches must key by model name and version.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Embeddings.md" class="internal-link">Embeddings</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is domain-finetuning the embedding model justified over improving chunking or retrieval?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Finetuning is justified when retrieval failures are specifically caused by semantic mismatches on domain terminology — queries and relevant documents contain the same domain concepts but land far apart in vector space. If failures trace to split logical units (chunking issue), missing metadata filters (retrieval pipeline issue), or query ambiguity (query translation issue), finetuning the embedding model will not help. Diagnose by examining the top-k retrieved chunks for failed queries: if semantically relevant chunks exist but rank low, the embedding model is the bottleneck.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Embeddings.md" class="internal-link">Embeddings</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you fine-tune instead of using RAG or prompting?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Fine-tune when the gap is <strong>behavioral</strong> — format, tone, refusal policy, domain style, or structured-output reliability that prompting cannot stabilize</li>
<li dir="auto">Use <strong>RAG</strong> when the gap is <strong>knowledge</strong> — facts that change, need citation, or are too large to fit in context; fine-tuning bakes a stale, untraceable snapshot</li>
<li dir="auto">Use <strong>prompting</strong> first for everything: it is the cheapest to iterate and solves most tasks without a training pipeline</li>
<li dir="auto">The best systems combine them: fine-tune behavior into the weights, retrieve current facts at query time</li>
<li dir="auto">Decision test: if the model retrieves the right information but presents it wrong, fine-tune; if it presents things well but lacks the facts, use RAG<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Fine-tuning.md" class="internal-link">Fine-tuning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is LoRA the default fine-tuning method rather than full fine-tuning?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">LoRA freezes the base model and trains tiny low-rank adapters (~0.1–1% of parameters), capturing most of full fine-tuning's quality at a fraction of the compute and memory</li>
<li dir="auto">Adapters are megabytes, not gigabytes — cheap to store, swap, stack, or merge into the base weights for zero inference overhead</li>
<li dir="auto">Because the base weights are frozen, LoRA largely avoids the catastrophic forgetting that narrow full fine-tuning causes</li>
<li dir="auto">QLoRA extends this by quantizing the base to 4-bit, making large-model fine-tuning feasible on a single modest GPU</li>
<li dir="auto">Full fine-tuning is reserved for when the absolute quality ceiling matters and compute is ample<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Fine-tuning.md" class="internal-link">Fine-tuning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the main ways a fine-tuning project fails even when training loss looks good?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Trying to inject knowledge: the model hallucinates at the edges and goes stale — that is a RAG problem</li>
<li dir="auto">Catastrophic forgetting: narrow data erodes general capability, invisible unless you evaluate broadly</li>
<li dir="auto">Overfitting a small set: memorization instead of generalization, from too many epochs or too high a learning rate</li>
<li dir="auto">Format mismatch: training examples that do not match the production chat template silently degrade results</li>
<li dir="auto">The common thread: training loss measures fit to training data, not task performance — always evaluate the fine-tune against the base model on a held-out and golden set<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Fine-tuning.md" class="internal-link">Fine-tuning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is adjusting temperature and top_p simultaneously discouraged?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Both reshape the same token probability distribution but through different mechanisms. Temperature scales the logit distribution (sharpening or flattening), while top_p truncates it to a cumulative mass threshold. Changing both creates unpredictable interactions — a low temperature already concentrates probability mass, so a low top_p on top of it may have no additional effect, while a high temperature with a low top_p creates conflicting signals. Tuning one while keeping the other at default keeps behavior predictable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Generation.md" class="internal-link">Generation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a grounded response still contain unsupported claims despite citation tags?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Models can attach citation markers to claims without verifying entailment. The citation looks correct but the cited passage may not actually support the claim — it may be topically related but not evidentially sufficient. This is why citation generation alone is not grounding: a separate claim-to-source verification step (NLI or similar) is needed to confirm that each cited passage actually entails the claim it is attached to.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Generation.md" class="internal-link">Generation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should constrained decoding be preferred over JSON mode for structured output?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Constrained decoding enforces a specific JSON schema at the token level during generation — the output is structurally compliant by construction. JSON mode only guarantees valid JSON without schema enforcement, so the model can return any valid JSON structure. Use constrained decoding when downstream systems depend on a specific schema (API contracts, database inserts, tool arguments). Use JSON mode when you want parsable output but the structure is flexible or exploratory.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Generation.md" class="internal-link">Generation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the minimum useful guardrail set for a production LLM application?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">(1) Allowlisted tools/actions only, (2) strict output schema validation, (3) PII scanning on outputs, (4) prompt injection detection on inputs, (5) an abstention/escalation path for uncertainty. These five controls catch the most common failure modes at low cost. Add content safety classifiers and human-in-the-loop for high-risk domains.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Guardrails.md" class="internal-link">Guardrails</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you test guardrails?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Build a red-team test suite: injection attempts ("ignore all previous instructions"), jailbreaks ("you are DAN"), data exfiltration attempts, and out-of-scope requests. Run the suite on every model or prompt change. Track pass rates over time — a regression in the red-team suite is a deployment blocker.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Guardrails.md" class="internal-link">Guardrails</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can RAG-grounded systems still hallucinate significantly?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Hallucinations.md" class="internal-link">Hallucinations</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does RLHF increase hallucination risk while perceived quality improves?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Hallucinations.md" class="internal-link">Hallucinations</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you separate retrieval failure from generation hallucination in a RAG pipeline?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Hallucinations.md" class="internal-link">Hallucinations</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does "the model just predicts the next token" matter for system design?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Everything an LLM outputs is a high-probability continuation, not a fact lookup — fluency and truth are uncorrelated by default</li>
<li dir="auto">This is why grounding (RAG, citations), output validation, and evaluation pipelines exist: they add the correctness guarantees that next-token prediction does not provide</li>
<li dir="auto">It also explains prompt sensitivity: the prompt is not a query against a database, it is the conditioning context that reshapes the entire output distribution</li>
<li dir="auto">Design rule: treat model output as untrusted, probabilistic input to the rest of the system — validate structure with schemas and content with checks<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/LLM.md" class="internal-link">LLM</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the practical difference between a base model and an instruction-tuned model?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A base model is an autocompleter: given "What are some famous social networks?" it may continue with more questions in the same style rather than answering</li>
<li dir="auto">An instruction-tuned model has been trained on instruction-response pairs (SFT) and aligned with human preferences (RLHF/DPO), so it interprets input as a task to perform</li>
<li dir="auto">Base models are used for further fine-tuning and research; instruction-tuned models are what APIs serve and products build on</li>
<li dir="auto">The distinction matters when reading benchmarks and papers: results on base models do not transfer directly to chat-tuned variants<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/LLM.md" class="internal-link">LLM</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you decide between prompting, RAG, and fine-tuning to adapt an LLM?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Climb the ladder cheapest-first. Start with prompting — zero-shot or few-shot — because it needs no training and you iterate in seconds; most tasks never need more. Reach for RAG when the model lacks the <em>knowledge</em>: facts that change faster than you can retrain, or answers that must cite sources. Reach for fine-tuning when the problem is <em>behavior</em>, not knowledge — the model has the right information but won't hold a format, tone, or policy after you've exhausted prompting. The classic trap is fine-tuning to inject facts: it bakes in a snapshot that starts aging immediately and gives no traceability. The mature setup often combines them — fine-tune for behavior, RAG for current facts.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/LLM.md" class="internal-link">LLM</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why route requests across models instead of picking one good model?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">There is no single best model: frontier models maximize quality but cost the most and are slowest; small models are cheap and fast but weaker — most traffic does not need the frontier model</li>
<li dir="auto">Routing sends the easy majority to a cheap model and reserves the expensive one for the hard fraction, cutting cost and latency without sacrificing quality where it matters</li>
<li dir="auto">A cascade escalates only after a cheap-model failure; a classifier routes up front by predicted difficulty or task type</li>
<li dir="auto">The tradeoff is added routing complexity and the risk of mis-routing a hard query to a weak model — calibrate the router on real traffic and validate escalation triggers<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Model Selection and Routing.md" class="internal-link">Model Selection and Routing</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why pick a model on your own evaluation rather than public benchmarks?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Public leaderboards aggregate over generic tasks and are heavily optimized by vendors, so they overfit and rarely predict performance on a specific production distribution</li>
<li dir="auto">The same model can top a benchmark yet underperform on your task, or vice versa — the only reliable signal is measurement on your labeled task set</li>
<li dir="auto">Benchmarks are useful to shortlist candidates, not to make the final choice</li>
<li dir="auto">This mirrors the MTEB caveat for embedding models: a high leaderboard score does not guarantee domain fit<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Model Selection and Routing.md" class="internal-link">Model Selection and Routing</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is prompt injection fundamentally harder to prevent than SQL injection?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM.md" class="internal-link">OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does Excessive Agency (LLM06) compound with Prompt Injection (LLM01)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM.md" class="internal-link">OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should system prompts be treated as public rather than secret?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM.md" class="internal-link">OWASP vulnerabilities on AI LLM</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Agents</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the ReAct pattern outperform chain-of-thought reasoning alone for tasks requiring external knowledge?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Chain-of-thought generates reasoning traces but has no mechanism to verify claims against external reality. When the model encounters a factual question it must rely on parametric memory, which produces confident but fabricated answers. ReAct interleaves reasoning with tool calls — the model can search, look up, or compute before continuing its chain. This grounds each step in real data, cutting hallucination. The original paper showed this on HotpotQA: CoT alone frequently hallucinated intermediate facts, while ReAct retrieved them. The tradeoff is latency and cost — each tool call adds a round trip and tokens.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Agent Loop.md" class="internal-link">Agent Loop</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the three most critical production safeguards for an agent loop?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ol>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Iteration cap</strong> — prevents infinite loops and cost runaway. Without a hard limit a confused agent loops indefinitely. Set per-request, not globally. 2. <strong>Token budget tracking</strong> — each iteration grows the context. Track cumulative tokens and terminate or summarize before hitting the window limit. Without this the model silently loses earlier context and quality degrades. 3. <strong>Tool call validation</strong> — validate function names and arguments against the schema before execution. A hallucinated tool call that passes through can trigger unintended side effects — database writes, payments, external API calls. Return errors to the model so it can self-correct.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Agent Loop.md" class="internal-link">Agent Loop</a></em></li>
</ol>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is a simple prompt chain preferable to an agent loop?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the task decomposes into a fixed, predictable sequence of steps. A prompt chain — step A then validate then step B then validate then step C — is cheaper, faster, more debuggable, and produces more consistent results. The agent loop adds value only when you cannot predict the steps in advance: the model must decide dynamically which tools to call and in what order based on intermediate results. Most production systems that call themselves agents are actually prompt chains, and that is the right choice for the majority of use cases.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Agent Loop.md" class="internal-link">Agent Loop</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use a workflow instead of an autonomous agent?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use a workflow when the task decomposes into predictable steps with clear inputs and outputs at each stage</li>
<li dir="auto">Workflows are cheaper, faster, and more debuggable than autonomous agents</li>
<li dir="auto">Use an autonomous agent only when steps are unpredictable, the task is open-ended, and you have feedback mechanisms (tests, eval criteria) to catch errors</li>
<li dir="auto">Most production "agents" are actually workflows — and that is the right choice for the majority of use cases</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: workflows trade flexibility for reliability; agents trade reliability for adaptability<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Agents.md" class="internal-link">Agents</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do autonomous agents accumulate error, and how do you bound it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each step an agent takes is conditioned on the output of the last one, so a small early mistake — a misread tool result, a wrong assumption — gets carried forward and compounds, pushing the agent further off track with every loop. That compounding is the main reason to prefer a workflow when you can: fixed code paths don't drift. When you do need autonomy, you bound the damage with hard iteration caps, explicit gates that validate progress before continuing, transparency into the planning steps so you can see where it went wrong, and a human-in-the-loop escape hatch when the agent is blocked. The throughline is feedback: the agent needs a way to catch its own drift before it cascades.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Agents.md" class="internal-link">Agents</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes a task a good fit for an autonomous agent?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Two things together: the task is genuinely open-ended — you can't predict the number of steps or write a fixed workflow for it — and it has a clear, checkable success signal the agent can use to judge its own progress. That's why coding works (tests pass or fail), customer support works (the issue is resolved or not), and research works (claims trace back to sources). Tasks with vague or delayed success criteria are where agents flail, because there's no feedback to correct the compounding error. If you can't define what "done" and "correct" look like in a way the system can check, the task isn't ready for an agent yet.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Agents.md" class="internal-link">Agents</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does MCP use a host-client-server architecture instead of letting the LLM talk to servers directly?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The host mediates all communication between the LLM and MCP servers. This is a deliberate security boundary — the host can enforce policies (which tools the model can call, which resources it can read), require user approval for sensitive actions, and aggregate capabilities from multiple servers without any server being aware of the others. If the LLM talked to servers directly, there would be no central point to enforce access control, log actions, or prevent a compromised server from manipulating the model. The client-per-server model also isolates failures — one crashed server does not affect others.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Model Context Protocol.md" class="internal-link">Model Context Protocol</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose function calling over MCP even for a shared tool?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the tool involves sensitive business logic that should not be externalized (e.g., pricing calculations, compliance checks), when you need tight coupling between the tool and the application's internal state (e.g., in-memory session data), or when the overhead of running a separate server process is not justified. Function calling is also simpler to debug — the tool definition and implementation live in the same codebase. MCP's value is reusability across clients, so if only one client will ever use the tool, function calling is the better choice.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Model Context Protocol.md" class="internal-link">Model Context Protocol</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes MCP servers harder to secure than traditional REST APIs?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Three factors compound. First, tool descriptions are untrusted input that directly influences LLM behavior — a vector that traditional APIs do not have. Second, MCP servers often get broad access (database connections, file system access, API keys) because they need to fulfill diverse model requests, violating least-privilege by default. Third, the protocol lacks built-in permission scoping — OAuth authenticates and authorizes the client's access to the server, but does not restrict which specific tools or operations the client can invoke. Traditional REST APIs typically have endpoint-level authorization, rate limiting, and input validation as standard practice. MCP servers need all of these but the ecosystem is young enough that many servers ship without them.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Model Context Protocol.md" class="internal-link">Model Context Protocol</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is multi-agent coordination justified over a single agent with more tools?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Justified under three conditions: context pollution (subtask degrades main agent reasoning), parallelization (independent paths need concurrent execution), specialization (20+ tools degrade selection accuracy, or conflicting behavioral modes needed)</li>
<li dir="auto">If none apply, single agent wins: 3–10× fewer tokens, lower latency, single linear trace for debugging</li>
<li dir="auto">Many teams investing months in multi-agent discover equivalent results from better prompting on one agent</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: multi-agent buys context isolation and parallelism at the cost of coordination overhead and debugging complexity<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Multi-Agentic Systems.md" class="internal-link">Multi-Agentic Systems</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does context-centric decomposition outperform problem-centric decomposition?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Problem-centric (code agent + test agent + review agent) forces constant coordination — each agent needs context from the others, creating lossy handoffs</li>
<li dir="auto">Context-centric splits along natural context boundaries — agent handling a feature also handles its tests because it already has the context</li>
<li dir="auto">Introduce a new agent only when context genuinely cannot fit in one window</li>
<li dir="auto">Reduces handoff count, cuts token overhead, prevents compounding information loss at each transfer</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: context-centric may produce broader agents (more tools per agent), but avoids the "telephone game" of multi-hop handoffs<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Multi-Agentic Systems.md" class="internal-link">Multi-Agentic Systems</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes multi-agent failures harder to diagnose than single-agent failures?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Semantic opacity: natural language errors pass as "valid" data between agents — no schema violations, no exceptions raised. A hallucinated fact from Agent A becomes trusted input for Agent B</li>
<li dir="auto">Non-linear traces: multiple interleaving reasoning chains with handoffs instead of one sequential trace, making root cause analysis harder</li>
<li dir="auto">Emergent behavior: agent interactions produce outcomes no single agent's instructions predict</li>
<li dir="auto">Known anti-pattern: two agents entering a politeness loop, each thanking the other, consuming budget without task progress — correct behavior per agent, catastrophic in combination</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: multi-agent gains specialization but loses the single-trace debuggability that makes single-agent failures straightforward to fix<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Multi-Agentic Systems.md" class="internal-link">Multi-Agentic Systems</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is tool design often more impactful than prompt engineering in agentic systems?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">In a single LLM call, the prompt is the entire interface — prompt quality is everything</li>
<li dir="auto">In an agentic system, the model interacts with tools across multiple loop iterations — each tool call is a decision point where errors compound</li>
<li dir="auto">Wrong tool selection, malformed arguments, or unhelpful error messages cascade across steps</li>
<li dir="auto">Anthropic's SWE-bench team spent more time improving tool interfaces than prompts, because tool quality directly determined task success rate</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: tool design effort is amortized across all agent runs; prompt tweaks are fragile and session-specific<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Tools.md" class="internal-link">Tools</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How would you decide between one broad tool and many narrow tools?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Narrow tools reduce parameter count and ambiguity — fewer hallucinated arguments per call</li>
<li dir="auto">Too many tools degrades selection accuracy — MCPGauge measured 9.5% accuracy drop from irrelevant tool presence alone</li>
<li dir="auto">Split when a tool serves genuinely different use cases needing different descriptions and parameters</li>
<li dir="auto">Keep together when operations share context and the model would naturally chain them</li>
<li dir="auto">As tool count grows and selection confusion appears, apply routing or tool filtering to keep the active set focused</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: per-call reliability (narrow) versus selection accuracy (fewer options)<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Tools.md" class="internal-link">Tools</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes a tool safe for caching in an agent loop versus unsafe?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Cache-safe: read-only (no side effects), deterministic output for same inputs within TTL, acceptable staleness</li>
<li dir="auto">Examples: database lookups, weather API calls, search queries — all safe with short TTLs</li>
<li dir="auto">Cache-unsafe: mutates state (create/update/delete), result depends on rapidly changing context, caching would mask failures</li>
<li dir="auto">Critical failure mode: caching a write operation returns cached "success" without executing — silent data inconsistency</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: cache hit rate and latency savings versus freshness and correctness guarantees<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Tools.md" class="internal-link">Tools</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h4>Evaluation</h4><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can the same model post very different scores on the same agent benchmark?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Agent benchmarks score a model <em>plus its scaffold</em> — planning loop, retries, tool design, prompt — and the scaffold contributes a large share of the result</li>
<li dir="auto">Two teams running the same base model with different harnesses get different SWE-bench numbers, so cross-paper comparisons are unreliable unless the scaffold is held constant</li>
<li dir="auto">Contamination and harness-specific prompt tuning add further variance over time</li>
<li dir="auto">The takeaway: use leaderboards to shortlist models, then re-evaluate candidates under <em>your</em> scaffold on <em>your</em> tasks</li>
<li dir="auto">Holding the scaffold constant for a fair comparison costs engineering setup, but without it the numbers don't mean what they appear to<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Agent Benchmarks.md" class="internal-link">Agent Benchmarks</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is pass^k a more honest agent metric than pass@1, and when does it matter most?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">pass@1 averages success over independent attempts and hides variance; an agent that solves a task 6/10 times looks similar to one that solves it 10/10</li>
<li dir="auto">pass^k credits a task only if solved on all k tries, directly measuring reliability — the property production users actually experience</li>
<li dir="auto">It matters most for high-stakes or unattended tasks (payments, code merges) where one failure in k is unacceptable</li>
<li dir="auto">pass^k is typically well below pass@1, so reporting only pass@1 overstates production readiness</li>
<li dir="auto">Measuring pass^k costs k× the eval runs, so spend it on the tasks where variance is intolerable and use cheaper mean success for low-stakes breadth<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Agent Benchmarks.md" class="internal-link">Agent Benchmarks</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is outcome-only scoring insufficient for evaluating an agent, and what do you add?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A correct final state can be reached by a wasteful or wrong path — three failed tool calls before a lucky success scores identically to a clean solve</li>
<li dir="auto">Outcome-only hides cost, latency, and compounding error risk, so a more expensive or fragile agent looks equal to a cheaper reliable one</li>
<li dir="auto">Add process metrics: tool-call validity (deterministic), tool-selection and trajectory quality (judge or reference), and efficiency counters (steps, cost, latency)</li>
<li dir="auto">Add reliability: run each task k times and report pass^k, since stochastic trajectories make a single run an unreliable estimate</li>
<li dir="auto">Process and reliability scoring multiply eval cost (k runs, a judge call per trace), so spend it where path quality and variance actually affect users and keep cheap outcome+efficiency gates everywhere else<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you detect and measure agent non-termination and looping?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Non-termination shows up as runs that hit the step cap without reaching a terminal state; track cap-hit rate as a first-class metric</li>
<li dir="auto">Oscillation shows up as repeated identical or alternating tool calls; detect by hashing (tool, args) per step and flagging repeats within a trajectory</li>
<li dir="auto">Both inflate cost and latency long before they change task success, so latency/step-count distributions catch them earlier than outcome metrics</li>
<li dir="auto">Mitigation: enforce step and cost caps, add progress checks, and make the agent's plan explicit so a judge can see where it stalled</li>
<li dir="auto">Tight caps cut runaway cost but can truncate genuinely hard tasks — set caps from the step-count distribution of known-good runs, not a round number<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why report tool-selection and argument accuracy separately instead of one tool-call score?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The two failures have different root causes and fixes: selection errors point at tool descriptions and prompt routing, argument errors point at grounding and schema design</li>
<li dir="auto">A single averaged number can look healthy while one axis is broken — 95% selection with 70% arguments averages to a misleading 82%</li>
<li dir="auto">They need different scorers: selection is reference/judge, arguments are exact for structured fields and semantic for free text</li>
<li dir="auto">Acting on the blended number wastes effort optimizing the half that is already fine</li>
<li dir="auto">Per-axis scoring costs more labels and more judge calls, but without it you can't tell which fix to ship — spend the granularity on the tools where a wrong call is expensive<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Tool-Call Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Tool-Call Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are deterministic schema checks necessary but not sufficient for tool-call evaluation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">They catch malformed JSON, unknown tools, and wrong arity for free and pre-execution, so they can block a bad call before it runs</li>
<li dir="auto">They are blind to semantics: a call with perfect schema and a wrong order id or mis-scoped query passes every check and then executes against real state</li>
<li dir="auto">They cannot judge selection or necessity — whether a tool should have been called at all</li>
<li dir="auto">Pair them with reference argument matching (for structured fields) and a judge (for selection/necessity), and add confirmation steps for irreversible actions</li>
<li dir="auto">The semantic layers cost labels and judge calls, so gate them on the high-risk tools rather than running them on every read-only call<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Tool-Call Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Tool-Call Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When do you use reference-trajectory matching versus an LLM judge over the trace?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Reference matching fits tasks with a small, knowable set of correct paths; it is cheap, objective, and can assert hard constraints (subset = stayed in bounds, superset = required work done)</li>
<li dir="auto">It breaks when many paths are valid — strict matching then penalizes correct alternate solutions and measures conformance, not quality</li>
<li dir="auto">A judge handles open-ended tasks and rates plan quality, redundancy, and recovery that matching cannot see, at the cost of a model call per run and long-context bias</li>
<li dir="auto">Common setup: subset match as a cheap safety gate (no out-of-scope tools) plus a judge for path quality, backed by outcome and efficiency metrics</li>
<li dir="auto">Matching is cheap but brittle and quality-blind; judging is flexible but expensive and biased — choose by how enumerable the correct paths are<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Trajectory Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Trajectory Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a high trajectory-judge score be misleading, and how do you guard against it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">If the judge sees the task succeeded, outcome leakage makes it rationalize a messy path as good</li>
<li dir="auto">On long traces the judge skims the middle and rewards verbosity, so a bloated trajectory can outscore a clean one</li>
<li dir="auto">Guard by withholding the outcome from the path judge (or scoring path and outcome separately) and by validating against human labels on long runs</li>
<li dir="auto">Pair the judge with objective efficiency counters (steps, cost, redundant calls) that a biased judge cannot launder</li>
<li dir="auto">Separate, calibrated, step-localized judging costs more calls, so spend it on the long, high-stakes trajectories where path quality actually matters<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Agents/Evaluation/Trajectory Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Trajectory Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Evaluation</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are relative regression thresholds preferable to absolute quality targets for release gates?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Absolute thresholds are brittle across data changes, model updates, and workload shifts</li>
<li dir="auto">A threshold set during initial launch becomes meaningless after the data doubles or input distribution shifts</li>
<li dir="auto">Relative thresholds (no more than N% regression from baseline) adapt automatically because the baseline tracks the current system state</li>
<li dir="auto">They prevent the failure mode where a team sets an ambitious absolute target, cannot reach it, and disables the gate entirely</li>
<li dir="auto">Relative thresholds do require a consistent baseline measurement maintained across releases, which adds CI/CD complexity — but that cost is far lower than the risk of shipping regressions absolute thresholds can't catch after the first data evolution<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Building an Evaluation Set.md" class="internal-link">Building an Evaluation Set</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should a team invest in a human-annotated golden set versus relying on synthetic generation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Synthetic generation bootstraps evaluation quickly and covers breadth at low cost</li>
<li dir="auto">LLM-generated inputs cluster around patterns the model finds easy to generate, missing adversarial cases, ambiguous inputs, and domain edge cases</li>
<li dir="auto">A golden set is worth the investment when the system serves high-stakes decisions (medical, legal, financial) where evaluation failures have direct business or safety impact</li>
<li dir="auto">Golden sets also serve as regression gates — known past failures are captured permanently, preventing recurrence (see <a class="internal-link" href="Golden Test Set and Regression Runs">Golden Test Set and Regression Runs</a>)</li>
<li dir="auto">In practice, combine both: synthetic for broad coverage, golden for regression gating on known failure modes</li>
<li dir="auto">Golden sets do cost annotator time (typically 2-4 hours per 100 examples) and ongoing maintenance as the data evolves — invest proportionally to the cost of an undetected evaluation failure in your domain<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Building an Evaluation Set.md" class="internal-link">Building an Evaluation Set</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the minimum useful set of deterministic checks for a tool-using agent?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">(1) Allowlisted tools/actions only, (2) strict output schema validation, (3) PII scanning on outputs, (4) injection-resistant formatting, (5) length constraints. These catch the most common failure modes cheaply before any expensive evaluation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Deterministic Checks.md" class="internal-link">Deterministic Checks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When are classic metrics (BLEU/ROUGE) useful?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Mainly for narrow summarization/translation style tasks and as weak signals. For open-ended assistants, rubric-based scoring and pairwise ranking usually track real quality better.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why run deterministic checks before an LLM judge rather than relying on the judge alone?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Deterministic checks are microseconds and free; LLM-judge calls cost API tokens and seconds — running the cheap gate first avoids paying to judge output that is already invalid</li>
<li dir="auto">Hard constraints (schema validity, disallowed actions, PII, length) have a zero false-positive rate when expressed as rules, whereas a judge can mis-rule on them</li>
<li dir="auto">A judge can be distracted into scoring an output "good" that a deterministic rule would reject outright (a fluent answer that violates the output contract)</li>
<li dir="auto">The two are complementary, not redundant: deterministic checks enforce hard contracts, judges evaluate soft quality — see <a class="internal-link" href="Deterministic Checks">Deterministic Checks</a><br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why isn't a strong offline score enough to ship an LLM change?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Offline sets are frozen samples; production traffic shifts in phrasing, intent, and edge-case mix the set never captured</li>
<li dir="auto">Iterating against a fixed set invites evaluation overfitting — the prompt gets tuned to the benchmark's distribution, not to real quality</li>
<li dir="auto">Outcome metrics that matter (task resolution, escalation, retention) depend on multi-turn user behavior that no static set simulates</li>
<li dir="auto">Treat offline evaluation as a release gate, then confirm with <a class="internal-link" href="Online Evaluation and AB Tests" aria-label="Online Evaluation and AB Tests" data-tooltip-position="top">online evaluation</a> before trusting the change<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why keep a frozen holdout slice separate from the golden set you iterate on?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Iterating prompts against the golden set until scores improve turns it into a training set — improvements stop measuring generalization</li>
<li dir="auto">The holdout slice is never used for tuning, so it stays an unbiased estimate of real quality</li>
<li dir="auto">Use the main golden set for day-to-day iteration; check the holdout only at release gates</li>
<li dir="auto">If holdout and golden-set scores diverge (golden improves, holdout does not), you have overfit to the golden set — refresh it from production traffic</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: the holdout "wastes" labeled cases you cannot tune on, but without it you cannot trust any of your numbers<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Golden Test Set and Regression Runs.md" class="internal-link">Golden Test Set and Regression Runs</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does a new failure belong in the golden set versus a targeted eval suite?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Add it to a targeted suite when it represents a specific failure mode you want fast, focused signal on (injection, PII leakage, groundedness) — small suites run quickly and localize regressions</li>
<li dir="auto">Add it to the golden set when it represents the normal operating range: a realistic user request that the system must keep handling correctly</li>
<li dir="auto">Production incidents usually warrant both: a targeted case reproducing the exact failure, and a generalized case covering the query pattern</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: targeted suites give precision but narrow coverage; the golden set gives breadth but slower, noisier signal — incidents are cheap opportunities to grow both<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Golden Test Set and Regression Runs.md" class="internal-link">Golden Test Set and Regression Runs</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should I prefer LLM-as-a-judge over classic metrics, and how do I know the judge is trustworthy?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use judges for open-ended generation where semantics matter and deterministic metrics (exact match, BLEU) can't capture quality.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use classic metrics for deterministic outputs or when you need hard guarantees.</li>
<li dir="auto">The key signal: if a human would need to read the answer to evaluate it, a judge model probably should too.</li>
<li dir="auto">Measure judge trustworthiness by checking agreement with a small human-labeled set.</li>
<li dir="auto">Track drift over time by re-running a fixed gold dataset after model updates.</li>
<li dir="auto">Why: judge reliability is not assumed — it must be validated and maintained like any other test harness.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/LLM-as-a-Judge.md" class="internal-link">LLM-as-a-Judge</a></em></li>
</ul>
