</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should I prefer pairwise comparisons over rubric scorecards?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Pairwise works best when iterating rapidly and the goal is "better than baseline" rather than a specific threshold.</li>
<li dir="auto">Scorecards work better when you need hard pass/fail criteria, want to track specific dimensions over time, or need to gate a release on a minimum score.</li>
<li dir="auto">Pairwise results aggregate into win-rates or Elo ratings, which are useful for comparing prompt versions or model checkpoints.</li>
<li dir="auto">Why: relative preference is cognitively easier for both humans and models than assigning an absolute score, so pairwise tends to produce more consistent verdicts on subjective quality.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/LLM-as-a-Judge.md" class="internal-link">LLM-as-a-Judge</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the most dangerous pitfalls when using LLM-as-a-judge in production?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Verbosity bias: judges prefer longer answers even when shorter ones are correct. Mitigate with a conciseness dimension and length caps.</li>
<li dir="auto">Position bias: in pairwise, judges favor whichever answer appears first. Always randomize A/B order.</li>
<li dir="auto">Hidden coupling: using the same model as judge and candidate inflates scores. Use a different judge model.</li>
<li dir="auto">Calibration drift: judge behavior shifts as the underlying model is updated. Maintain a gold dataset and re-run calibration periodically.</li>
<li dir="auto">Why: these biases are systematic, not random — they silently corrupt your eval signal and can cause you to ship regressions.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/LLM-as-a-Judge.md" class="internal-link">LLM-as-a-Judge</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you determine the minimum sample size for an A/B test?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use a power analysis: specify the minimum detectable effect (e.g., 2% improvement in resolution rate), desired statistical power (80%), and significance level (p &lt; 0.05). Tools like statsmodels or online calculators give the required sample size. Underpowered tests produce false positives that waste engineering effort.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Online Evaluation and AB Tests.md" class="internal-link">Online Evaluation and AB Tests</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between data drift and model degradation in online evaluation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Data drift is a change in the input distribution (users asking different types of questions). Model degradation is a drop in output quality for the same input distribution. Both show up as metric regressions in online monitoring, but the remediation differs: drift may require retraining or prompt updates; degradation may indicate a model update or infrastructure issue.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Evaluation/Online Evaluation and AB Tests.md" class="internal-link">Online Evaluation and AB Tests</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Prompting</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is automated prompt optimization worth the setup cost?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It is worth it when prompt quality directly impacts key metrics and manual iteration is too slow.</li>
<li dir="auto">You need repeatable optimization across many tasks or datasets, not one-off prompt crafting.</li>
<li dir="auto">You have an evaluation loop (datasets, acceptance metrics, or human labels) to score changes objectively.</li>
<li dir="auto">Without measurement infrastructure, automation usually adds complexity without reliable gains.<br />
Why this matters: the optimization loop only pays off when evaluation discipline exists.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompting/Automated Prompt Optimization.md" class="internal-link">Automated Prompt Optimization</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is PAL's key insight compared to chain-of-thought prompting?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">PAL moves intermediate reasoning from free-form text to executable code.</li>
<li dir="auto">The interpreter, not the language model, performs deterministic computation.</li>
<li dir="auto">This reduces arithmetic/symbolic errors common in text-only reasoning traces.</li>
<li dir="auto">It naturally connects to tool-use agents that route subtasks to calculators, Python, or external systems.<br />
Why this matters: it separates language understanding from computation for better reliability.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompting/Automated Prompt Optimization.md" class="internal-link">Automated Prompt Optimization</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you start with zero-shot versus few-shot?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Start zero-shot for simple, well-specified tasks (basic classification, extraction, rewrite).</li>
<li dir="auto">Move to one-shot/few-shot when output format is inconsistent or class boundaries are ambiguous.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use one-shot first for lightweight steering, then increase shots only if failure patterns persist.<br />
Why this matters: it minimizes prompt complexity and token cost while improving reliability only where needed.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompting/In-Context Learning.md" class="internal-link">In-Context Learning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes a good few-shot example set?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Consistent input and output structure.</li>
<li dir="auto">Coverage of real label space and edge cases.</li>
<li dir="auto">Examples representative of production distribution, not synthetic easy cases.</li>
<li dir="auto">Minimal but sufficient count (often 1-5) to show mapping clearly.<br />
Why this matters: demonstration quality usually matters more than raw quantity.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompting/In-Context Learning.md" class="internal-link">In-Context Learning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the main failure modes of few-shot prompting?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Breakdown on tasks requiring multi-step reasoning.</li>
<li dir="auto">Sensitivity to example order and formatting drift.</li>
<li dir="auto">Context-window pressure from too many demonstrations.</li>
<li dir="auto">Poor transfer when task needs external facts not present in model knowledge.<br />
Why this matters: these limits tell you when to pivot to other techniques instead of adding more shots.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompting/In-Context Learning.md" class="internal-link">In-Context Learning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you choose prompt chaining instead of a single large prompt?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Choose chaining when the task has clear stages with different goals.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use chaining when intermediate validation reduces business or safety risk.</li>
<li dir="auto">Prefer chaining when you need step-level observability for debugging.</li>
<li dir="auto">Accept the latency and token overhead when reliability matters more than speed.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use a single prompt for simple, low-risk tasks with stable behavior.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompting/Prompt Composition.md" class="internal-link">Prompt Composition</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the main risk of generated knowledge prompting, and how do you mitigate it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The generated background facts can be wrong or fabricated.</li>
<li dir="auto">Treat generated facts as untrusted intermediate context.</li>
<li dir="auto">Constrain the final prompt to use only explicit generated facts.</li>
<li dir="auto">Validate critical claims against trusted context before final output.</li>
<li dir="auto">Add abstain or reject behavior when facts are inconsistent or low confidence.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompting/Prompt Composition.md" class="internal-link">Prompt Composition</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a practical meta prompting workflow for improving a weak prompt?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Collect real failures and cluster them by error type.</li>
<li dir="auto">Ask the model to critique the current prompt against those failures.</li>
<li dir="auto">Request a revised prompt with explicit constraints and output schema.</li>
<li dir="auto">Evaluate on a held-out test set, not only the examples used for revision.</li>
<li dir="auto">Version prompts and keep rollback criteria if quality regresses.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompting/Prompt Composition.md" class="internal-link">Prompt Composition</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do prompt anatomy and model settings have to be designed together?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Prompt text defines intent and constraints, settings define sampling behavior.</li>
<li dir="auto">A precise prompt can still fail with overly random settings.</li>
<li dir="auto">Conservative settings can still produce poor output if instructions are ambiguous.</li>
<li dir="auto">Reliable systems tune both and evaluate with task-specific metrics.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompting/Prompting.md" class="internal-link">Prompting</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you prefer few-shot prompting over pure instruction prompting?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When output format is strict or hard to describe in words.</li>
<li dir="auto">When label boundaries are subtle and examples clarify decision edges.</li>
<li dir="auto">When consistency matters more than novelty.</li>
<li dir="auto">Start with minimal examples, then add edge cases.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompting/Prompting.md" class="internal-link">Prompting</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How would you debug a prompt that is accurate but too verbose and expensive?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Tighten output indicator with length limits and schema.</li>
<li dir="auto">Lower <code>max tokens</code> and add stop sequences.</li>
<li dir="auto">Keep <code>temperature</code> low for deterministic concise tasks.</li>
<li dir="auto">Evaluate token usage and failure rate after each change.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompting/Prompting.md" class="internal-link">Prompting</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does Chain-of-Thought usually help, and when can it hurt?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Helps on multi-step arithmetic, logic, and planning where intermediate state tracking matters.</li>
<li dir="auto">Helps when tasks are decomposable into small correct sub-steps.</li>
<li dir="auto">Can hurt on simple factual queries by adding unnecessary tokens and extra room for drift.</li>
<li dir="auto">Can also hurt if the first bad assumption propagates through a single chain.<br />
<strong>Why:</strong> this tests whether the candidate understands mechanism-level fit, not just a slogan like "CoT is always better".<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompting/Reasoning Techniques.md" class="internal-link">Reasoning Techniques</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Explain the cost vs. accuracy tradeoff in self-consistency.</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Accuracy often improves because multiple sampled chains reduce single-path bias.</li>
<li dir="auto">Cost and latency increase roughly with sample count N.</li>
<li dir="auto">Diminishing returns appear after a small N; tune N against SLA and budget.</li>
<li dir="auto">Works best when there is a clear way to compare or vote on final answers.<br />
<strong>Why:</strong> this checks practical engineering judgment under production constraints.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompting/Reasoning Techniques.md" class="internal-link">Reasoning Techniques</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is Tree of Thoughts justified over CoT or self-consistency?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use ToT when success requires explicit exploration of alternatives and backtracking.</li>
<li dir="auto">Typical cases: planning, combinatorial puzzles, multi-constraint decision paths.</li>
<li dir="auto">Not justified for easy tasks where CoT already reaches target quality.</li>
<li dir="auto">Choose search strategy (BFS/DFS/beam) based on branching factor, depth, and compute budget.<br />
<strong>Why:</strong> this evaluates whether the candidate can map algorithmic search cost to task difficulty.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/Prompting/Reasoning Techniques.md" class="internal-link">Reasoning Techniques</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>RAG</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should retrieval cache keys be based on processed query text instead of raw embeddings?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Processed query text and transformation version are deterministic, auditable, and stable across embedding model upgrades. Raw embedding bytes are opaque, change with every model swap, and make cache invalidation on model upgrade impossible without full cache flush. Keying on processed text also aligns cache correctness with the query translation pipeline — if the translation changes, the key changes automatically.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Caching.md" class="internal-link">Caching</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is response caching riskier than embedding caching?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Embedding is a pure function: same text plus same model always produces the same vector. Response generation depends on the system prompt template, the retrieved evidence (which changes with index updates), the user's permissions, and the model version — all mutable. A cached response can become wrong when any of these inputs change without cache invalidation. Embedding cache entries only go stale when the source text or model version changes, which are infrequent and structurally detectable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Caching.md" class="internal-link">Caching</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is semantic caching safe to deploy, and when should it be avoided?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Semantic caching is safe when the domain is narrow, queries are repetitive, false positives have low cost, and you can calibrate a reliable similarity threshold on a held-out set. It should be avoided in high-stakes domains (medical, legal, financial) where a wrong cached answer causes harm, in multi-turn conversations where context changes the correct answer, and when the query distribution is too diverse to find a threshold that balances hit rate against false-positive rate.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Caching.md" class="internal-link">Caching</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does parent-child chunking often improve answer completeness over child-only retrieval?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Child chunks maximize retrieval precision — small, focused units match specific queries well. But child-only context can miss adjacent constraints that the answer depends on (e.g., a policy rule in one child and its exception in a sibling). Parent expansion restores the surrounding context during generation, reducing partial or decontextualized answers. The tradeoff is increased context size and potential noise from the broader parent span.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Chunking.md" class="internal-link">Chunking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should a team move from recursive to structure-aware chunking?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Move when retrieval errors consistently trace back to broken structural units — split tables, bisected code blocks, or separated clause/exception pairs. Recursive splitting respects paragraph boundaries but is blind to document-specific structures. Structure-aware chunking preserves those units at the cost of format-specific parser investment and ongoing parser maintenance.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Chunking.md" class="internal-link">Chunking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is semantic chunking not always superior to simpler rule-based approaches?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Semantic chunking places boundaries at actual topic shifts, which sounds ideal. But it requires embedding every span during ingestion (high cost), the similarity threshold is sensitive to embedding model and domain (tuning burden), and threshold instability can cause over-fragmentation or oversized chunks. For documents with reliable structural markers (headings, sections), recursive or structure-aware chunking achieves similar boundary quality at a fraction of the ingestion cost.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Chunking.md" class="internal-link">Chunking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is sampled LLM-as-judge scoring preferred over scoring every response in production?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Scoring every response doubles per-request cost and adds latency if synchronous.</li>
<li dir="auto">At production scale (thousands of queries/hour), full scoring is prohibitively expensive.</li>
<li dir="auto">Sampled scoring (5–20%) provides statistical coverage of the quality distribution at a fraction of the cost.</li>
<li dir="auto">Stratified sampling ensures rare but important query types (multi-hop, negation, edge-case domains) are represented.</li>
<li dir="auto">Async execution decouples scoring from user-facing latency — users never wait for the judge.</li>
<li dir="auto">Full scoring is reserved for offline evaluation runs against labeled eval sets.</li>
<li dir="auto">Lower sample rates cut cost but raise the odds of missing a localized regression in a small query cluster; start at 10–20% and reduce only once the distribution looks stable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Monitoring.md" class="internal-link">Monitoring</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should RAG alerting use relative regression thresholds instead of absolute quality targets?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Absolute thresholds ("faithfulness &gt; 0.9") are brittle across corpus changes, model updates, and query distribution shifts.</li>
<li dir="auto">A threshold calibrated at launch becomes meaningless after the corpus doubles or query mix evolves.</li>
<li dir="auto">Relative thresholds ("no more than 5% drop from 7-day rolling baseline") adapt automatically because the baseline tracks current system state.</li>
<li dir="auto">Relative thresholds prevent the failure mode where a team sets an ambitious absolute target, cannot reach it consistently, and silently disables the alert.</li>
<li dir="auto">Baselines must be recomputed after intentional pipeline changes (model swap, prompt update, index rebuild) to avoid false alarms on expected shifts.</li>
<li dir="auto">Relative thresholds can miss slow drift that stays inside the rolling window, so pair them with a periodic absolute floor check — a monthly look at whether the baseline itself is still acceptable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Monitoring.md" class="internal-link">Monitoring</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does monitoring differ from evaluation in a RAG system, and why do you need both?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Evaluation validates a pipeline configuration against a labeled dataset before deployment — it gates releases.</li>
<li dir="auto">Monitoring validates the pipeline continuously against live traffic after deployment — it catches production regressions.</li>
<li dir="auto">Eval sets are static snapshots; production traffic shifts continuously with new query patterns, corpus updates, and model provider changes.</li>
<li dir="auto">Monitoring catches failure modes that no static eval set anticipates: seasonal query shifts, silent model downgrades, load-dependent degradation.</li>
<li dir="auto">The feedback loop connects them: failing production traces identified by monitoring get added to the eval set, preventing recurrence in future releases.</li>
<li dir="auto">Monitoring alone catches problems only after users feel them; evaluation alone misses production-specific failures. You need both.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Monitoring.md" class="internal-link">Monitoring</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does query translation often improve recall but sometimes hurt precision, and how do you detect the tradeoff?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Additional query variants cover vocabulary the original misses, hitting more document neighborhoods and improving recall</li>
<li dir="auto">Weak variants introduce concepts not in the original intent, pulling related-but-irrelevant documents into the candidate set</li>
<li dir="auto">Precision drop is invisible in aggregate recall metrics — recall goes up while noisy candidates are buried in the ranked list</li>
<li dir="auto">Detection: measure precision@k (not just recall@k) before and after translation, segmented by query type</li>
<li dir="auto">If recall@20 improves but precision@5 drops, translation helps coverage but hurts candidates that reach the generator</li>
<li dir="auto">Key mitigation: always include the original query as a variant and discard translations that drift too far from the original intent<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Query Translation.md" class="internal-link">Query Translation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is decomposition a better choice than multi-query, and when does it hurt?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Decomposition fits when the original query has distinct sub-problems requiring separate evidence — comparisons, multi-entity questions, timeline questions</li>
<li dir="auto">Multi-query fits when the question has a single intent but user phrasing may not match document terminology</li>
<li dir="auto">Decomposition hurts on single-intent questions: sub-questions fragment a coherent topic and lose the constraints that make the original specific</li>
<li dir="auto">The test: if sub-questions can be answered independently and concatenated answers address the original, decomposition fits</li>
<li dir="auto">If sub-questions only make sense in context of the original question, use multi-query instead</li>
<li dir="auto">Key tradeoff: decomposition adds a synthesis LLM call and risks context fragmentation; multi-query only adds retrieval calls<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Query Translation.md" class="internal-link">Query Translation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can HyDE outperform direct query embedding for vague questions but fail on specific factual queries?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Vague queries produce sparse, underspecified embeddings equidistant from many document clusters — poor retrieval signal</li>
<li dir="auto">HyDE generates a hypothetical answer paragraph, creating a denser point in embedding space closer to real answer documents</li>
<li dir="auto">The encoder's bottleneck filters hallucinated details while preserving the correct semantic neighborhood</li>
<li dir="auto">For specific factual queries (error codes, versions), the LLM may hallucinate wrong details — different error code, different version</li>
<li dir="auto">The hallucinated embedding steers retrieval toward documents matching the wrong specifics</li>
<li dir="auto">Direct query embedding, while sparse, preserves exact tokens that <a class="internal-link" href="Retrieval#Sparse Retrieval — Keyword Search (BM25)" aria-label="Retrieval &gt; Sparse Retrieval — Keyword Search (BM25)" data-tooltip-position="top">keyword search</a> in a hybrid setup can catch</li>
<li dir="auto">HyDE failure is stealth: retrieved documents look topically relevant but answer the wrong specific question<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Query Translation.md" class="internal-link">Query Translation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is GraphRAG a better fit than plain vector retrieval?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When answers require explicit entity relations, dependency paths, or multi-hop joins that are hard to recover from independent text chunks. Examples: compliance tracing across policy documents, architecture dependency analysis, supply chain impact assessment. Skip GraphRAG for simple fact lookups where vector similarity suffices.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/RAG Patterns.md" class="internal-link">RAG Patterns</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is hybrid search plus reranking usually added before GraphRAG or agentic RAG?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Hybrid search and reranking fix the most common production failure first: the right evidence is missing or buried under noisy chunks. They reuse the same corpus and retrieval pipeline, so the integration cost is lower than building agents or knowledge graphs. GraphRAG and agentic RAG are justified only when evals show relationship reasoning or multi-tool orchestration is the actual bottleneck. The tradeoff is that hybrid search improves retrieval quality cheaply, while graph and agentic systems buy extra capability at a large indexing, latency, and observability cost.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/RAG Patterns.md" class="internal-link">RAG Patterns</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should advanced RAG patterns be introduced incrementally instead of all at once?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each pattern adds independent failure modes and observability needs. Incremental rollout isolates impact, allows A/B measurement against baseline, and prevents compounding complexity from masking root causes. Start with the pattern that addresses your highest-frequency failure mode.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/RAG.md" class="internal-link">RAG</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does fine-tuning beat adding more retrieval sophistication?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the failure is behavioral, not factual: the model retrieves the right evidence but keeps producing the wrong format, tone, or policy behavior despite prompt iteration. Retrieval upgrades cannot fix behavior encoded in weights. Conversely, fine-tuning cannot fix missing or stale knowledge — it bakes in a snapshot that starts aging immediately and provides no source traceability. Diagnose first: if faithfulness is high but style or policy compliance is low, fine-tune; if evidence is missing or wrong, improve retrieval.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/RAG.md" class="internal-link">RAG</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When a RAG answer is wrong, how do you tell whether retrieval or generation is at fault?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Split the pipeline and score the two halves separately, because the fixes are opposite. First check whether the right evidence was retrieved at all: if the relevant chunk never made it into the context, it's a retrieval failure — improve chunking, hybrid search, or reranking, and no amount of prompt tuning will help. If the evidence <em>was</em> present but the answer ignored or contradicted it, that's a generation/faithfulness failure — tighten the prompt, add groundedness checks, or use a stronger model. This is exactly why RAG evaluation reports retrieval and generation as separate metrics; a single end-to-end accuracy number hides which half to fix.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/RAG.md" class="internal-link">RAG</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can reranking improve offline <a class="internal-link" href="Monitoring#Retrieval Quality Metrics" aria-label="Monitoring &gt; Retrieval Quality Metrics" data-tooltip-position="top">nDCG</a> without visible quality improvement for end users?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The improvement may be in ranking positions that the generator does not use. If the generator only reads the top-3 chunks, improvements at positions 4–5 are invisible to users. Evaluate whether the reranker changes the top-k composition that actually enters the prompt, not just overall <a class="internal-link" href="Monitoring#Retrieval Quality Metrics" aria-label="Monitoring &gt; Retrieval Quality Metrics" data-tooltip-position="top">nDCG</a>. Also verify that the eval set reflects production query distribution — gains on eval-set query types may not represent the queries users actually send.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Re-ranking.md" class="internal-link">Re-ranking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does reranking hurt retrieval quality instead of helping?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the reranker is out-of-distribution for your domain. A reranker trained on short web passages may misjudge relevance on long technical documents, internal terminology, or multilingual content — demoting actually relevant documents. Also when the candidate count is too small: if first-stage recall@N is already low, the reranker only reorders noise. Always compare <a class="internal-link" href="Monitoring#Retrieval Quality Metrics" aria-label="Monitoring &gt; Retrieval Quality Metrics" data-tooltip-position="top">recall and precision</a> before and after reranking on domain-specific queries.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Re-ranking.md" class="internal-link">Re-ranking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is ColBERT faster than a cross-encoder at query time despite also using token-level scoring?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">ColBERT pre-computes per-token document embeddings at index time and stores them. At query time, only the query tokens need encoding (one forward pass regardless of candidate count). Scoring is a MaxSim matrix operation over pre-stored vectors, not a full transformer forward pass per document. Cross-encoders must run a complete forward pass for each query-document pair because they jointly encode the concatenated input. The tradeoff is that ColBERT needs multi-vector storage, which uses more space and requires specialized indexes.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Re-ranking.md" class="internal-link">Re-ranking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can vector-only retrieval underperform on technical support workloads?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Technical support queries often include exact identifiers: error codes, version strings, API paths, and SKU IDs. Embedding models capture meaning, not specific tokens — a query for "error E4392 in v2.3" may retrieve content about error handling in general rather than the specific code. The failure is subtle because returned chunks are topically related, so the LLM synthesizes a plausible but wrong answer. Keyword search catches these exact tokens because they are rare in the corpus (high BM25 weight), which is why hybrid retrieval is essential for these workloads.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Retrieval.md" class="internal-link">Retrieval</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does hybrid retrieval perform worse than single-mode retrieval?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the weaker search mode contributes more noise than signal to the fused list. On homogeneous corpora where one mode dominates (e.g., scientific documents with consistent terminology and natural-language queries), the non-dominant mode pulls in marginally relevant candidates that dilute fused results. In production benchmarks, vector-only has beaten hybrid on scientific corpora because keyword search on specialized vocabulary introduced noise. Always evaluate hybrid against single-mode baselines on your actual corpus before committing to the added complexity.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Retrieval.md" class="internal-link">Retrieval</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does HNSW recall degrade silently as the vector database grows?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">HNSW navigates a graph to find approximate nearest neighbors, not an exhaustive scan. The <code>ef_search</code> parameter controls how many candidate nodes the search visits. At small corpus sizes, a moderate <code>ef_search</code> finds most true neighbors. As the corpus grows, the graph becomes denser and the same <code>ef_search</code> misses more true neighbors — the search path does not explore enough of the graph to find them. Latency stays stable because the search still visits the same number of candidates, and no errors are raised. The only signal is less relevant retrieved chunks. Detection requires explicit <a class="internal-link" href="Monitoring#Retrieval Quality Metrics" aria-label="Monitoring &gt; Retrieval Quality Metrics" data-tooltip-position="top">Recall@k</a> monitoring against a ground-truth test set.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Retrieval.md" class="internal-link">Retrieval</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do vector databases use approximate nearest-neighbor search instead of exact search?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Exact (brute-force) search compares the query against every stored vector — correct, but O(N) per query and far too slow once there are millions of vectors</li>
<li dir="auto">ANN indexes (HNSW, IVF, IVF-PQ) trade a small, measurable amount of recall for orders-of-magnitude lower latency, returning most of the true nearest neighbors in sub-millisecond time</li>
<li dir="auto">The recall given up is silent: there is no error, and latency stays stable, so it only shows up as slightly worse retrieved context unless you measure ANN recall against brute-force ground truth</li>
<li dir="auto">The right operating point is chosen by tuning index parameters (<code>ef_search</code>, <code>nprobe</code>) on the recall–latency curve for your corpus and SLA<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Vector Databases.md" class="internal-link">Vector Databases</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use pgvector or an existing search engine instead of a dedicated vector database?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When you already run the database (Postgres, OpenSearch) and the corpus is modest: keeping vectors beside your relational or lexical data avoids operating a second system and simplifies hybrid search</li>
<li dir="auto">pgvector gives you transactional consistency and joins with existing data, which a standalone vector DB cannot</li>
<li dir="auto">Reach for a dedicated vector database when scale, filtered-search recall under narrow filters, or specialized indexes (IVF-PQ, DiskANN) exceed what the add-on supports</li>
<li dir="auto">The tradeoff is operational simplicity (one system) versus peak scale and index flexibility (dedicated engine) — start with the add-on and migrate only when a real limit is hit<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Vector Databases.md" class="internal-link">Vector Databases</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does HNSW recall degrade as the corpus grows, and how do you catch it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">HNSW finds neighbors by walking a proximity graph, exploring a number of candidates set by <code>ef_search</code>; as the graph grows denser, a fixed <code>ef_search</code> covers proportionally less of it and misses more true neighbors</li>
<li dir="auto">Latency stays constant (the search visits the same number of candidates) and no error is raised, so infrastructure dashboards show everything healthy while retrieval quality silently drops</li>
<li dir="auto">Detection requires an explicit Recall@k check against brute-force ground truth on a scheduled query set</li>
<li dir="auto">The fix is to re-tune <code>ef_search</code> upward as the corpus grows, accepting a little more latency to hold recall<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Vector Databases.md" class="internal-link">Vector Databases</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h4>Evaluation</h4><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does ANN recall degrade silently as the corpus grows while latency stays flat?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">At fixed <code>ef_search</code>/<code>nprobe</code>, the search explores a constant amount of work regardless of corpus size</li>
<li dir="auto">As more vectors crowd the graph, that fixed search path covers a proportionally smaller fraction of it, so true neighbors are missed more often</li>
<li dir="auto">Latency is driven by the search budget, not corpus size, so it stays constant and gives no signal that recall has dropped</li>
<li dir="auto">Detection requires explicit ANN recall checks against brute-force ground truth on a scheduled query set — dashboards watching only latency will not catch it</li>
<li dir="auto">Fix: re-tune <code>ef_search</code>/<code>nprobe</code> as the corpus grows, or move to a parameterization that scales the search budget with index size</li>
<li dir="auto">Raising the search budget restores recall but increases p99 latency — pick the knee of the recall-vs-latency curve rather than maxing either<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Evaluation/Component-Level Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Component-Level Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is token-level IoU more informative than Recall@k when comparing chunking strategies?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Recall@k only asks whether a relevant chunk appeared; it ignores how much irrelevant text rode along inside that chunk</li>
<li dir="auto">A strategy can hit high Recall@k by returning large, noisy chunks that waste context-window tokens on irrelevant content</li>
<li dir="auto">Token IoU scores the overlap between retrieved tokens and the gold evidence span, penalizing both missed evidence (recall) and noise (precision) in one number</li>
<li dir="auto">Concrete gap: the default 800/400 OpenAI config reached 87.9% token recall but 1.4% token precision — Recall@k would call it a success while IoU exposes the waste</li>
<li dir="auto">Use IoU when context budget or generator attention dilution matters; fall back to ablation via Recall@k and Faithfulness when building token-span ground truth is too expensive</li>
<li dir="auto">IoU needs <code>(query, gold_span)</code> labels, which cost more to produce than binary relevance — invest only where chunk efficiency materially affects cost or answer quality<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Evaluation/Component-Level Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Component-Level Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can aggregate retrieval metrics improve while individual user segments degrade?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Aggregate metrics average across query types and tenants, masking localized regressions</li>
<li dir="auto">A pipeline change improving average Recall@5 can simultaneously degrade recall by double digits on a specific tenant's query cluster</li>
<li dir="auto">RAG workloads are heterogeneous — different query types, document formats, and languages have different retrieval characteristics</li>
<li dir="auto">The fix is segment-level evaluation: slice by tenant, language, query cluster, and document source type</li>
<li dir="auto">Flag any segment that degrades beyond the threshold, even when the aggregate improves</li>
<li dir="auto">Per-segment evaluation does cost more to maintain (more ground-truth labels, more compute per release), but it catches the most common RAG evaluation failure in production — choose granularity based on how heterogeneous your query population is<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Evaluation/Evaluation Metrics.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation Metrics</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Given high Faithfulness (0.91) and low Context Recall (0.54), which pipeline layer do you fix first and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">High faithfulness means the model correctly uses the context it receives — generation is not the problem</li>
<li dir="auto">Low context recall means retrieval misses roughly half the necessary evidence — the retrieval layer is the bottleneck</li>
<li dir="auto">Retrieval quality is a hard ceiling on answer quality: the generator cannot use evidence it never sees</li>
<li dir="auto">Fix retrieval first: add hybrid search (BM25 + dense), expand k, review metadata filters, check embedding domain fit</li>
<li dir="auto">Do not touch prompts or generation settings until recall improves — optimizing generation against incomplete evidence is wasted effort</li>
<li dir="auto">After retrieval fix, re-measure both: if faithfulness drops as recall improves, the additional context is confusing the model — add re-ranking or improve prompt grounding</li>
<li dir="auto">Improving recall often decreases precision (more chunks = more noise), so pair recall improvements with re-ranking to maintain context quality<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Evaluation/Evaluation Metrics.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation Metrics</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why decompose RAG evaluation into separate retrieval, generation, and end-to-end layers?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because a single "quality" score tells you something broke but not where, and the fix is completely different per layer. Retrieval metrics ask whether the right evidence even reached the generator; generation metrics ask whether the answer is faithful to that evidence and actually addresses the question; end-to-end asks whether the user's task got solved. The decomposition is what separates the two failures that look identical from outside: perfect retrieval with a model that ignores the context, and flawless generation over irrelevant documents the retriever never should have returned. Without it you chase retrieval tuning that can't fix a generation bug — and burn a sprint doing it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What belongs in RAG evaluation specifically versus general LLM evaluation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">RAG reuses the whole general eval stack — LLM-as-a-judge, deterministic checks, golden sets, synthetic generation, the online/A-B loop — and adds only what's genuinely RAG-shaped on top. That specific part is three things: retrieval-quality metrics (did the right chunks arrive, ranked well), faithfulness/groundedness (is the answer supported by the retrieved evidence rather than the model's parametric memory), and the labeling problem of which chunks count as relevant when a query maps to several. Everything else is inherited. Keeping that line clean is what stops the eval system from being rebuilt per domain — the general machinery lives once, and RAG, agents, and plain prompts each specialize it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When several chunks are relevant to one query, how do you decide which retrieval metric to score with?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">First classify <em>why</em> they are relevant — substitutable (any one fully answers) versus complementary (the answer needs all of them combined)</li>
<li dir="auto">Substitutable: score HitRate@k and MRR — success is one good chunk arriving early; strict recall or MAP wrongly penalizes not retrieving redundant copies</li>
<li dir="auto">Complementary: score Recall@k and Context Recall — every required chunk must arrive; HitRate is misleading because one-of-three looks like a pass but yields an incomplete answer</li>
<li dir="auto">When chunks differ in usefulness rather than count, use graded labels (0/1/2) and nDCG@k, the only common metric that consumes grades and rewards ranking the best chunk first</li>
<li dir="auto">Set k from how many chunks the generator actually consumes; for complementary cases k must be at least the number of required chunks or recall is capped below 1 by construction</li>
<li dir="auto">Graded multi-chunk labels cost far more annotation effort than single-chunk binary labels — invest only where the generator genuinely fuses multiple sources (multi-hop, comparison), and keep single-label binary sets for simple lookup queries<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Evaluation/Retrieval Evaluation Sets.md" class="internal-link">Retrieval Evaluation Sets</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do synthetically generated retrieval eval sets often report worse recall than the system delivers in production?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Chunk-anchored generation labels only the source chunk as relevant, but the synthesized query is frequently answerable by other unlabeled chunks (duplicated text, overview paragraphs, near-identical FAQ entries)</li>
<li dir="auto">The retriever returns a genuinely relevant chunk, but because it is not in the ground truth, Precision@k and MRR score it as a miss — a false negative in the labels, not a retrieval failure</li>
<li dir="auto">This deflates absolute numbers and, worse, can rank a better retriever below a worse one, corrupting model-selection decisions</li>
<li dir="auto">Fix: after generation, run a second pass (the retriever plus an LLM judge over top-k) to label additional chunks that also answer the query, or discard ambiguous queries</li>
<li dir="auto">Secondary cause: lexical leakage — the LLM copies rare phrasing from the source chunk, inflating exact-match and BM25 scores on queries no real user would type</li>
<li dir="auto">The dedup/judge pass does add LLM cost per query and some noise of its own, but without it synthetic retrieval metrics are systematically pessimistic and unreliable for ranking pipelines<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/LLM/RAG/Evaluation/Retrieval Evaluation Sets.md" class="internal-link">Retrieval Evaluation Sets</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="ai-ml-machine-learning">Machine Learning</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between data drift and concept drift?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Data drift: the input distribution P(X) changes (users ask different questions, new product categories appear). The model's learned relationship P(Y|X) may still be valid.<br />
Concept drift: the relationship P(Y|X) changes (what constitutes spam evolves, fraud patterns shift). The model's learned relationship is no longer valid — retraining is required.<br />
Cost of confusing them: retraining on new data for concept drift is necessary; retraining for benign data drift wastes resources and may reduce performance on the original distribution.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Data Drift.md" class="internal-link">Data Drift</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you detect drift when labels are delayed?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use proxy metrics: escalation rate, user complaints, re-contact rate, or model confidence distributions. Monitor input feature distributions (PSI, KS test) as an early warning signal. When labels arrive, compute actual performance metrics and compare to baseline.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Data Drift.md" class="internal-link">Data Drift</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How should an ML pipeline be designed to ship weekly batch churn scoring now while preserving a path to real-time scoring later?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Start with batch to ship value, but define a stable feature contract and a single preprocessing implementation shared by batch and online</li>
<li dir="auto">Store features and predictions with versioned schema so you can backtest and replay later</li>
<li dir="auto">Use a model registry with stage promotion and rollback, and keep training code runnable in CI</li>
<li dir="auto">Plan the online boundary now: which features are available at request time and which require async enrichment</li>
<li dir="auto">Add monitoring from day one so you have drift and label delay visibility before moving to real time<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Machine Learning.md" class="internal-link">Machine Learning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What should be checked first when a binary classifier shows 98 percent accuracy but support tickets rise, and which metric should be optimized next?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Check class balance and the confusion matrix; high accuracy can hide poor recall on the minority class</li>
<li dir="auto">Inspect label quality and leakage sources, especially time based joins and post event signals</li>
<li dir="auto">Pick metrics based on cost: optimize precision if false positives are expensive, recall if misses are expensive, or use PR AUC for heavy imbalance</li>
<li dir="auto">Tune the decision threshold using a cost curve or expected value, not the default 0.5</li>
<li dir="auto">Run slice based evaluation to find the segments where the model fails and decide whether to collect more data or add features<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Machine Learning.md" class="internal-link">Machine Learning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What tradeoffs and rollout plan are appropriate when the best model exceeds a strict API latency budget?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Measure end to end latency budget including preprocessing, network, and tail latencies, then decide if you can meet SLO with scaling or caching</li>
<li dir="auto">Consider a smaller model, distillation, quantization, or a two stage setup where a cheap model gates the expensive one</li>
<li dir="auto">Use canary or A B rollout with guardrails on latency and key business metrics, plus automated rollback</li>
<li dir="auto">Keep a safe fallback decision path, such as a baseline model or rules, for overload and error conditions</li>
<li dir="auto">Align evaluation with production: test on representative traffic and monitor training serving skew after launch<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Machine Learning.md" class="internal-link">Machine Learning</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you fine-tune a small model instead of prompting an LLM for an NLP task?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Fine-tune when you have labeled training data and the task is well-defined (classification, NER, sentiment).</li>
<li dir="auto">Fine-tuned small models (BERT, DistilBERT) run at millisecond latency on-device vs seconds for LLM API calls.</li>
<li dir="auto">Per-request cost is near zero for on-device inference vs $0.01–$0.10+ per LLM call at scale.</li>
<li dir="auto">LLMs win when the task is complex (multi-step reasoning, summarization), training data is scarce, or rapid iteration matters more than unit cost.</li>
<li dir="auto">Fine-tuning costs you labeled data and training effort upfront, then pays it back as millisecond latency and near-zero per-request cost at scale; prototype with an LLM, then check whether production volume justifies the fine-tune.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Natural Language Processing.md" class="internal-link">Natural Language Processing</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do multilingual NLP models underperform monolingual ones, and when is that acceptable?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Multilingual models split their capacity across 100+ languages, so per-language representation quality is lower.</li>
<li dir="auto">Monolingual models concentrate all parameters on one language, achieving higher accuracy on that language's benchmarks.</li>
<li dir="auto">Multilingual models are acceptable when you need coverage across many languages and per-language accuracy can be slightly lower.</li>
<li dir="auto">For high-stakes tasks (medical NER, legal classification), the accuracy gap may be unacceptable — use language-specific models.</li>
<li dir="auto">Multilingual models trade per-language accuracy for breadth of coverage; accept that when you serve many languages on limited ML infrastructure, but validate explicitly on the languages you actually care about.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Natural Language Processing.md" class="internal-link">Natural Language Processing</a></em></li>
</ul>
