</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why start every new AI feature in Shadow Mode?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Shadow Mode lets you validate accuracy on real production data without any risk of automated errors. You measure false positive rate, edge cases, and distribution shift before giving the model any authority. Skipping Shadow Mode means discovering failures in production where they have real consequences.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Spectrum Of Automations.md" class="internal-link">Spectrum Of Automations</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What signals indicate it is safe to move from Partial to Full Automation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Precision on automated cases is consistently above your acceptable error threshold across multiple weeks. Monitoring dashboards show stable metrics with no degradation. The cost of errors is low enough that automated mistakes are recoverable. You have alerting in place to detect metric drift quickly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Spectrum Of Automations.md" class="internal-link">Spectrum Of Automations</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Evaluation</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a model with high ROC-AUC still produce unusable probabilities?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">ROC-AUC measures ranking — whether positives score above negatives — and is invariant to any monotonic transform of the scores</li>
<li dir="auto">That invariance means the absolute probability values can be arbitrarily distorted (all squashed near 0.9, say) without changing AUC at all</li>
<li dir="auto">Downstream logic that compares <code>p</code> to a threshold or computes <code>p × value</code> then makes wrong decisions despite the perfect ranking</li>
<li dir="auto">The fix is to measure calibration separately (reliability diagram, Brier, ECE) and apply post-hoc calibration; AUC will never surface the problem<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Calibration.md" class="internal-link">Calibration</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does calibration not matter, and when is it essential?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It does not matter when only the ranking or the top-k is used: search results, recommendation shortlists, lead prioritization — there a strong ROC-AUC or PR-AUC is sufficient</li>
<li dir="auto">It is essential when the probability feeds a decision: expected-value thresholds, cost-sensitive actions, abstention/escalation gates, risk scores shown to humans, or probability averaging across an ensemble</li>
<li dir="auto">It is also essential when probabilities from different models or time periods are compared, since miscalibration makes them non-comparable</li>
<li dir="auto">Rule of thumb: if a human or a formula reads the number, calibrate it; if only the order is consumed, you may not need to<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Calibration.md" class="internal-link">Calibration</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is temperature scaling the default calibration method for neural networks?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Modern deep nets are systematically overconfident, and the miscalibration is largely a global scaling of the logits rather than a per-class distortion</li>
<li dir="auto">Temperature scaling fits a single scalar T that divides the logits before softmax, which is exactly the right shape for that global overconfidence</li>
<li dir="auto">Because it preserves the argmax, accuracy is unchanged — you fix probabilities without touching the model's decisions</li>
<li dir="auto">It needs only a small held-out set and one parameter, so it rarely overfits; the limitation is that it cannot repair calibration that varies by region or class<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Calibration.md" class="internal-link">Calibration</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you distrust a single evaluation metric?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When the metric does not encode the cost asymmetry of the decision, for example accuracy on imbalanced classes</li>
<li dir="auto">When the test set does not represent production traffic, for example a random split on time-dependent data</li>
<li dir="auto">When the metric looks good overall but fails on critical slices or cohorts</li>
<li dir="auto">When the metric is a ranking metric but you need calibrated probabilities for downstream logic<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the offline–online gap the central risk in ML evaluation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Offline metrics are computed on a frozen sample; production traffic shifts in distribution, user behavior, and edge-case mix that the sample never captured</li>
<li dir="auto">A change can improve the offline number while leaving real outcomes flat — or reverse them — because the offline set rewards patterns that do not generalize</li>
<li dir="auto">Leakage, non-representative splits, and metric-proxy mismatch all widen the gap silently</li>
<li dir="auto">The mitigation is layered: offline metrics as a release gate, production monitoring to catch drift, and controlled experiments to confirm a real effect before trusting it<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you optimize precision vs recall, and how do you operationalize the choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Optimize precision when false positives are expensive: blocking legitimate users (and churning them), flooding a manual review queue with false alarms, or triggering expensive downstream actions (automated account lockouts). Optimize recall when misses are dangerous: fraud slipping through, safety violations in content moderation (regulatory exposure), or medical screening (missed diagnosis). Operationalize by setting a hard constraint on the priority metric (e.g., "recall ≥ 0.95"), then maximizing the other. Freeze the threshold and monitor both metrics daily with alerting on ≥5% drift.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Classification Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Classification Evaluation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you pick a classification threshold in practice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ol>
<li dir="auto">Define the business constraint: "recall must be ≥ 0.95" or "FP rate must be ≤ 0.02." 2. Plot the precision-recall curve on a held-out validation set. 3. Find the threshold that satisfies your constraint while maximizing the complementary metric. 4. Validate on a separate golden test set (not the validation set used for selection). 5. Freeze the threshold in production config (not hardcoded). 6. Set up monitoring: if the metric drops ≥5% on weekly golden-set runs, trigger re-evaluation. A static threshold degrades as the data distribution shifts, so schedule quarterly threshold reviews or move to dynamic thresholding with confidence calibration.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/Classification Evaluation.md" class="internal-link">Classification Evaluation</a></em></li>
</ol>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does ROC-AUC measure?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It measures ranking quality across thresholds.</li>
<li dir="auto">A higher ROC-AUC means positives usually get higher scores than negatives.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/ROC-AUC and PR-AUC.md" class="internal-link">ROC-AUC and PR-AUC</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should I use PR-AUC instead of ROC-AUC?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use PR-AUC when positives are rare, like fraud or anomaly detection.</li>
<li dir="auto">PR-AUC shows how precision changes as recall increases, which is key when false positives are costly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/ROC-AUC and PR-AUC.md" class="internal-link">ROC-AUC and PR-AUC</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can high accuracy be misleading on imbalanced data?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">If negatives dominate, a model can predict mostly negatives and still get high accuracy.</li>
<li dir="auto">Check PR-AUC, precision, and recall to understand real positive-class performance.<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Machine Learning/Evaluation/ROC-AUC and PR-AUC.md" class="internal-link">ROC-AUC and PR-AUC</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="ai-ml-tooling">Tooling</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do hierarchical instruction files often outperform a single giant root file?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Rules stay close to the code they govern, reducing ambiguity in mixed-language or mixed-architecture repos</li>
<li dir="auto">Subdirectory instructions can refine local constraints without polluting global context</li>
<li dir="auto">Smaller scoped files usually improve relevance-to-token ratio in the agent prompt</li>
<li dir="auto">The tradeoff is governance complexity: teams need clear precedence and periodic cleanup<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Agent Instructions.md" class="internal-link">Agent Instructions</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the fastest way to detect that an instruction file is harming agent quality?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Repeated command failures from outdated scripts or wrong paths</li>
<li dir="auto">Inconsistent code style across tasks despite explicit rules</li>
<li dir="auto">Frequent edits that violate architecture constraints the file claims to enforce</li>
<li dir="auto">Rapid fix is to trim to high-signal rules, remove contradictions, then re-add specifics incrementally<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Agent Instructions.md" class="internal-link">Agent Instructions</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should a team keep instructions minimal and rely more on repository inspection?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Early prototype phases where architecture and conventions are changing weekly</li>
<li dir="auto">Small codebases where implicit conventions are obvious from a few files</li>
<li dir="auto">Teams that cannot maintain rule quality yet; stale detail is worse than concise accurate guidance</li>
<li dir="auto">As complexity grows, move from minimal to layered instructions before inconsistency becomes expensive<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Agent Instructions.md" class="internal-link">Agent Instructions</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is an agentic coding tool not just "better autocomplete"?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Autocomplete predicts local tokens, while coding agents execute multi-step plans across files and tools</li>
<li dir="auto">Agents can run verification commands and revise output based on failures, which autocomplete does not do autonomously</li>
<li dir="auto">The mechanism changes risk: agent output must be governed with approvals, tests, and repository rules</li>
<li dir="auto">Net benefit comes from closed-loop execution, not from raw text quality alone<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Coding Agents.md" class="internal-link">Coding Agents</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should a team prefer terminal agents over IDE agents?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Prefer terminal agents when workflows are command-heavy, scriptable, and CI-first</li>
<li dir="auto">Prefer IDE agents when developers rely on interactive navigation, visual diffing, and editor-native refactors</li>
<li dir="auto">The deciding factor is operational fit with current engineering process, not vendor marketing claims</li>
<li dir="auto">Teams can mix both if shared instruction files and validation gates keep behavior consistent<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Coding Agents.md" class="internal-link">Coding Agents</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What controls reduce production risk when adopting coding agents?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Repository instruction files to encode architecture and testing constraints</li>
<li dir="auto">Hooks and policy checks to block unsafe operations automatically</li>
<li dir="auto">Bounded execution: cost limits, step limits, and explicit approval points</li>
<li dir="auto">Mandatory verification (build, tests, lint) before merge, regardless of which model produced the patch<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Coding Agents.md" class="internal-link">Coding Agents</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should destructive-operation controls live in PreToolUse rather than PostToolUse?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Hooks.md" class="internal-link">Hooks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you design hook pipelines that improve quality without making the agent unusably slow?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Hooks.md" class="internal-link">Hooks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use an LLM-based hook instead of a deterministic script?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Hooks.md" class="internal-link">Hooks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does MCP reduce integration complexity for agent tooling ecosystems?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Without a shared protocol, each client-service pair needs custom integration code, creating N x M scaling</li>
<li dir="auto">MCP changes this to N + M: each client implements MCP once, each service exposes one MCP server</li>
<li dir="auto">The shared primitives (tools, resources, prompts) standardize capability discovery and invocation</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: protocol standardization simplifies interoperability but adds runtime/server management overhead<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Plugins.md" class="internal-link">Plugins</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should a team intentionally limit plugin count even if more integrations are available?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When accuracy and latency degrade from too many tool schemas in context</li>
<li dir="auto">When the security team cannot properly review or monitor additional server dependencies</li>
<li dir="auto">When task scope is narrow and extra plugins create more selection noise than value</li>
<li dir="auto">Good practice is progressive enablement: start minimal, add only tools that measurably improve outcomes<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Plugins.md" class="internal-link">Plugins</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do skills usually improve agent reliability more than repeating instructions in every prompt?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Skills are persistent and reusable, so important constraints are present every time instead of being forgotten in ad-hoc prompts</li>
<li dir="auto">They standardize behavior across contributors and sessions, reducing output variance</li>
<li dir="auto">Structured frontmatter plus explicit usage guidance improves skill selection and timing</li>
<li dir="auto">Skills are version-controlled artifacts, so teams can review and evolve them like code<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Skills.md" class="internal-link">Skills</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you choose project-scoped skills over user-global skills?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Choose project scope when conventions are repository-specific and must be shared by all contributors</li>
<li dir="auto">Choose project scope when CI, linters, or architecture rules are tightly coupled to that codebase</li>
<li dir="auto">Use user-global skills for personal productivity preferences that should not override team policy</li>
<li dir="auto">If a rule would surprise teammates in a PR review, it should be project-scoped, not global<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Skills.md" class="internal-link">Skills</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you choose between a terminal-first coding agent and an IDE-first coding agent?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Start with team workflow: terminal-first fits script-heavy and CI-centric teams, IDE-first fits interactive editing-heavy teams</li>
<li dir="auto">Compare observability: terminal-first often exposes every command clearly, while IDE-first may optimize UX but hide low-level steps</li>
<li dir="auto">Validate integration needs: if your process depends on PR workflows, issue trackers, and editor navigation, IDE integration can reduce friction</li>
<li dir="auto">Check policy controls: hooks, rules files, and approval gates matter more than UI style in regulated or production-sensitive environments<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Tooling.md" class="internal-link">Tooling</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are agent instructions and hooks more important than model quality in long-running repos?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Instructions encode project constraints (architecture, naming, testing expectations) that the base model does not know</li>
<li dir="auto">Hooks create automatic guardrails, catching policy violations before merge</li>
<li dir="auto">Better models can still make wrong local decisions without repo-specific constraints</li>
<li dir="auto">Reliable behavior over months depends on repeatable controls, not one-off prompt quality<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Tooling.md" class="internal-link">Tooling</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the practical difference between a coding agent and a code review agent?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Coding agents optimize change creation: plan, edit, run checks, iterate</li>
<li dir="auto">Review agents optimize change evaluation: identify risk, gaps, regressions, and missing tests</li>
<li dir="auto">They complement each other: one increases delivery speed, the other protects quality and maintainability</li>
<li dir="auto">Mature teams use both to keep throughput high without lowering merge standards<br />
<em><a href="Home/AI &amp; ML/Tooling/Tooling.md" class="internal-link">Tooling</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="architecture">Architecture</h1><h2 id="architecture-application-architecture">Application Architecture</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does Clean Architecture differ from traditional N Layer, and when does the extra indirection pay off</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">N Layer often keeps downward dependencies from UI to business to data, while Clean Architecture enforces inward dependencies toward policy.</li>
<li dir="auto">In Clean Architecture, inner layers define contracts and outer layers implement them, so domain logic survives framework changes.</li>
<li dir="auto">The indirection pays off when business rules are complex, testing speed matters, and infrastructure churn is expected over multiple years.</li>
<li dir="auto">For short lived services with shallow rules, the same indirection can slow delivery with little resilience gain.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff statement: Clean Architecture buys adaptability and testability by paying upfront complexity and wiring overhead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Application Architecture/Clean Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Clean Architecture</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What happens when you apply Clean Architecture to a simple CRUD service</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">You often create command handlers ports and adapters that do little more than pass data through, so cycle time drops without stronger correctness.</li>
<li dir="auto">Teams spend effort maintaining abstractions instead of shipping user value because domain complexity never materializes.</li>
<li dir="auto">Operationally it can still work, but the architecture tax shows up in onboarding and debugging cost.</li>
<li dir="auto">A pragmatic approach is to keep boundaries light and evolve toward stricter clean boundaries only where invariants and integration volatility appear.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff statement: using full Clean Architecture too early protects against hypothetical future change while charging real present complexity.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Application Architecture/Clean Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Clean Architecture</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the Dependency Rule and why does it matter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">All source code dependencies must point inward — toward higher-level policies (domain). Outer layers (infrastructure, UI) depend on inner layers; inner layers never depend on outer layers. This means you can change databases, frameworks, or delivery mechanisms without touching business rules.<br />
Cost: requires defining interfaces in inner layers and wiring implementations in outer layers — more upfront structure.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Application Architecture/Layered Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Layered Architecture</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between traditional layered and Onion/Clean Architecture?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Traditional layered: UI → Business Logic → Data Access → Database. Changing the DB affects everything above. Onion/Clean: Infrastructure → Application → Domain. The Domain has zero dependencies; Infrastructure implements Domain interfaces. The key difference is dependency inversion at the data access boundary.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Application Architecture/Layered Architecture.md" class="internal-link">Layered Architecture</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the key difference between MVC and MVVM?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">In MVC, the Controller handles requests and explicitly selects which View to render — the View is passive. In MVVM, the View binds to the ViewModel's observable state — the ViewModel does not know about the View. MVVM enables automatic UI updates via data binding; MVC requires the Controller to pass data to the View on each request.<br />
Cost: MVVM requires binding infrastructure (<code>INotifyPropertyChanged</code>, <code>ICommand</code>) which adds boilerplate; MVC is simpler for stateless request/response flows.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Application Architecture/MVC MVVM.md" class="internal-link">MVC MVVM</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the ViewModel more testable than the Controller?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The ViewModel has no dependency on HTTP context, routing, or view rendering — it is a plain C# class. You can unit-test it by calling commands and asserting property values. The Controller depends on <code>HttpContext</code>, <code>IActionResult</code>, and the MVC pipeline, requiring more setup or integration tests.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Application Architecture/MVC MVVM.md" class="internal-link">MVC MVVM</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you prevent version conflicts between plug-ins that depend on different versions of the same library?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use <code>AssemblyLoadContext</code> to isolate each plug-in's dependencies. Each context has its own assembly resolution scope, so plug-in A's dependency on <code>Newtonsoft.Json</code> 12.x does not conflict with plug-in B's dependency on 13.x. Cost: each context adds memory overhead and prevents type sharing across contexts — objects from one context cannot be cast to types from another.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Application Architecture/Plug-in Architecture (MicroKernel).md" class="internal-link">Plug-in Architecture (MicroKernel)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you version extension point interfaces without breaking existing plug-ins?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Treat the extension point interface as a public API with semantic versioning. For breaking changes, introduce a new interface version (<code>IPlugin</code>, <code>IPlugin2</code>) and support both simultaneously via an adapter. Existing plug-ins implement the old interface; new plug-ins implement the new one. The core adapts old plug-ins to the new contract. Cost: adapter proliferation over time — set a deprecation timeline and remove old interfaces after a migration window.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Application Architecture/Plug-in Architecture (MicroKernel).md" class="internal-link">Plug-in Architecture (MicroKernel)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is plug-in architecture the wrong choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When all features are known upfront and no third-party extensibility is needed. The loading complexity, versioning challenges, and security surface (untrusted code running in-process) are not justified for internal applications. A monolith with feature flags is simpler and safer. Plug-in architecture earns its complexity when customers or third parties need to extend the product without modifying the core.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Application Architecture/Plug-in Architecture (MicroKernel).md" class="internal-link">Plug-in Architecture (MicroKernel)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="architecture-distributed-systems">Distributed Systems</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you design gateway aggregation endpoints for client efficiency, and what do you keep out of the gateway?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use gateway routing for normal traffic and add a few targeted aggregation endpoints where a client — usually mobile — would otherwise make five round trips for one screen. The gateway composes those reads and tunes the payload, but it stays thin: auth, throttling, routing, transformation, observability, and nothing else. Business rules, transactions, and domain invariants live in the backend services, with correlation IDs flowing across the fan-out so you can trace a slow screen. The line to hold: aggregation is response-shaping, not orchestration — the moment decision logic creeps in, you have a distributed monolith.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/API Gateway.md" class="internal-link">API Gateway</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose a single API gateway versus a BFF approach?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A single gateway is simpler to run and the right default when clients have similar needs and ship on a shared cadence. Reach for BFF — a gateway or route set per client type — when web, mobile, and partner clients want materially different payloads, auth, or latency profiles, or when separate teams own them and want deployment autonomy. The catch is operational: every extra gateway is one more thing to deploy, monitor, and secure. Start with one and split to BFF only when client divergence or team boundaries actually justify the cost.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/API Gateway.md" class="internal-link">API Gateway</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Where do API Gateway and service mesh responsibilities belong in one architecture?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">They handle different traffic planes, so they sit side by side rather than compete. The gateway owns north-south traffic — clients entering the system — so edge auth, TLS termination, external rate limits, and API surface control belong there. The mesh owns east-west traffic between internal services: mTLS, retries, traffic shifting, and per-service telemetry. The gateway guards the front door; the mesh governs the hallways.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/API Gateway.md" class="internal-link">API Gateway</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">If CAP is only about partitions, why do we still tune consistency levels on healthy clusters?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because CAP only describes the partition case, and partitions are rare — PACELC covers the other 99% of the time. Its "ELC" half says that even with healthy links (Else), a replicated store still trades Latency against Consistency: wait for a quorum to confirm and you get fresher, better-ordered reads at higher latency; answer from the nearest replica and you get speed with a chance of staleness. That is exactly what read-consistency levels, session guarantees, quorum sizes, and timeout tuning are dialing in. CAP tells you how to fail; PACELC tells you what you pay every day.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/CAP theorem.md" class="internal-link">CAP theorem</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Is a system "CP" or "AP" as a whole?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Don't think of it system-wide — decide per operation, because different endpoints have different correctness budgets. A <code>PlaceOrder</code> or ledger write wants CP: refuse it under partition rather than risk split-brain or a double charge. A <code>GetRecommendations</code> read wants AP: keep serving slightly stale data because availability beats freshness there. A profile read might only need session consistency. So the same system is CP on some paths and AP on others; labeling the whole platform and applying one rule everywhere is the mistake. Map each operation to its business invariant and its allowed staleness window.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/CAP theorem.md" class="internal-link">CAP theorem</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is "pick two of three" a misleading way to state CAP?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because in any system that replicates data across machines, partition tolerance isn't something you opt out of — networks drop packets and isolate nodes whether you like it or not. So "CA" isn't really on the menu: the moment a partition hits, a system that didn't plan for it just stops being correct or stops responding. The honest framing is that P is a given, and the real decision is what you do <em>during</em> a partition — sacrifice consistency to stay available (AP), or sacrifice availability to stay consistent (CP). With no partition you can have both; CAP only forces the choice inside the failure window.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/CAP theorem.md" class="internal-link">CAP theorem</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you guarantee read-your-writes when writes go to a strong store but reads come from an eventually consistent cache?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The problem is the gap between a committed write and a cache that hasn't caught up. The cleanest fix is to route that user's immediate post-write reads past the cache to a strong or session-consistent path, just while freshness matters. Pair it with write-through or explicit invalidation on update so the cache converges quickly, and carry a version or timestamp so you can reject a stale follow-up write. Keep the writes idempotent so a retry is harmless. You don't need global strong consistency — only read-your-writes for the one user who just acted.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Consistency Models.md" class="internal-link">Consistency Models</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Which chat features should use linearizable, causal, and eventual consistency?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Map each feature to the weakest model that still feels correct. Message ordering and reply chains need at least causal consistency — a reply must never show up before the message it answers. Typing indicators can be eventual; a dropped or reordered one costs nothing, and the latency win is worth it. Read receipts in a compliance-sensitive context might justify something stronger. The skill on display is refusing a one-size-fits-all level: spend strong-consistency latency only where a user would actually notice the inconsistency.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Consistency Models.md" class="internal-link">Consistency Models</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can linearizability reduce availability during a network partition, and how do you contain the blast radius?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Linearizable operations need a quorum to agree on one order, and a partition can cut a node off from that quorum. To stop two sides committing conflicting truths, the isolated side must reject or delay those operations — that refusal is the availability you lose (the CP corner of CAP). Contain the blast radius by scoping strong consistency to the writes that truly need it — payments, inventory decrements — and serving everything else from weaker models with explicit degraded-mode behavior. Partition tolerance isn't optional; the real choice is which operations fail closed and which keep serving.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Consistency Models.md" class="internal-link">Consistency Models</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is 2PC problematic in microservices?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">2PC requires all participants to hold locks during the prepare-to-commit window. In microservices, this window spans network calls, making lock duration unpredictable.</li>
<li dir="auto">The coordinator is a single point of failure. A crash after PREPARE but before COMMIT leaves participants locked indefinitely.</li>
<li dir="auto">Most microservice infrastructure (HTTP APIs, NoSQL, cloud queues) doesn't support XA transactions.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: 2PC gives strong consistency but at the cost of availability and throughput. CAP theorem: under a partition, you must choose consistency or availability — 2PC chooses consistency.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Distributed Transactions.md" class="internal-link">Distributed Transactions</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the Outbox pattern guarantee at-least-once event delivery?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The event is written to an <code>OutboxMessages</code> table in the same DB transaction as the domain change. If the transaction commits, the event is durable.</li>
<li dir="auto">A background worker reads unprocessed outbox entries and publishes them to the broker, retrying until the broker acknowledges.</li>
<li dir="auto">This guarantees at-least-once delivery (the event may be published more than once if the worker crashes after publishing but before marking the entry as processed).</li>
<li dir="auto">Consumers must be idempotent to handle duplicate events.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: adds a background worker and an extra DB table. The cost is worth it for any event that must not be lost.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Distributed Transactions.md" class="internal-link">Distributed Transactions</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is idempotency essential for reliable retry strategies, and what happens without it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Retries are a certainty in distributed systems because clients cannot always distinguish failure from delayed success.</li>
<li dir="auto">Idempotency converts ambiguous outcomes into deterministic behavior, so retries do not amplify side effects.</li>
<li dir="auto">Without it, transient faults become correctness bugs such as duplicate charges or duplicate shipment commands.</li>
<li dir="auto">Idempotency also improves operational recovery because replay jobs and dead letter reprocessing become safe.</li>
<li dir="auto">Stronger idempotency guarantees cost extra storage, key lifecycle management, and stricter request validation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Idempotency.md" class="internal-link">Idempotency</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you implement idempotency for a payment endpoint that processes charges through a third party provider?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Require <code>Idempotency-Key</code> for <code>POST /payments</code> and hash a canonical request payload.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use an atomic insert into an idempotency table keyed by that header to gate concurrent duplicates.</li>
<li dir="auto">If key exists with matching hash and completed state, return cached response with same status code.</li>
<li dir="auto">Pass the same key to the provider when supported so upstream retries are also deduplicated.</li>
<li dir="auto">If key exists but hash differs, reject with conflict to prevent accidental key reuse.</li>
<li dir="auto">Caching and replaying responses makes retries predictable, but it adds storage overhead and couples you to the stored response schema.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Idempotency.md" class="internal-link">Idempotency</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you pick a load-balancing algorithm for requests with highly variable duration, like AI inference?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Round robin is the right default when instances are similar and requests cost about the same, but it falls apart when duration varies — exactly the inference case, where one long completion ties up an instance while round robin keeps handing it new work. Least-connections handles that better by routing to the instance with the fewest in-flight requests, which tracks real load when durations are uneven. The catch is that connection count doesn't capture per-request CPU cost, so a few short-but-expensive prompts can still skew it. For inference you validate the choice by measuring p95/p99 latency and backend saturation under representative load rather than trusting any default.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Load Balancing.md" class="internal-link">Load Balancing</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When do you choose an L4 load balancer over L7?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">L4 balances on connection metadata — IP, port, protocol — without reading the payload, so it's fast and works for any TCP/UDP traffic, but it can't decide based on HTTP path, host, or headers. L7 parses HTTP, so it can route by path or header and do canary, A/B, and edge auth, at the cost of more processing per request. Choose L4 when you need raw throughput and generic transport routing; choose L7 the moment routing depends on request content. Plenty of systems use both — L4 at the edge for raw distribution, L7 deeper for content-aware routing.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Load Balancing.md" class="internal-link">Load Balancing</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must readiness checks differ from liveness checks behind a load balancer?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">They answer different questions, and conflating them keeps broken instances in rotation. Liveness is "is the process alive" — if it fails, the orchestrator restarts the pod. Readiness is "can this instance serve traffic right now," so it must actually probe critical dependencies (database, cache, downstream) and let the balancer pull a started-but-not-ready instance out of rotation without restarting it. The classic bug is a <code>/health</code> that always returns 200: the process is up, its database is unreachable, and the balancer keeps routing real traffic into failures. Keep liveness cheap and dependency-free; put the dependency checks in readiness.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Load Balancing.md" class="internal-link">Load Balancing</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you design webhook consumers to prevent event loss and duplicate processing?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Verify the HMAC signature on every request first, to reject forged payloads. Then defend against duplicates with the provider's event ID as an idempotency key — stored durably and checked before you act, because at-least-once delivery means the same webhook arrives twice eventually. Acknowledge fast: return <code>200</code> and process asynchronously from a durable queue so slow work never trips the sender's timeout. And since your consumer can be down when an event fires, add reconciliation — periodically poll the provider's event-list API to catch what you missed. Signature for trust, idempotency for duplicates, reconciliation for gaps.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Webhooks.md" class="internal-link">Webhooks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose webhooks over a shared message broker for inter-service event delivery?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Webhooks win when you cross an organizational boundary — a SaaS or third-party provider you don't control can't publish into your broker, but it can POST to a URL. Inside your own platform a message broker is usually better: durable fan-out, back-pressure, replay, and per-key ordering that raw HTTP callbacks don't give you. They aren't exclusive, though — the common pattern is to receive external webhooks at the edge and immediately republish them onto an internal broker, so you get HTTP reach at the boundary and broker guarantees everywhere inside.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Webhooks.md" class="internal-link">Webhooks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you protect a webhook endpoint against replay attacks?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Sign a timestamp alongside the payload — in the body or as a signed header — and reject anything outside a tight window, say older than five minutes, so a captured request can't be replayed later. The HMAC has to cover both the payload and the timestamp together, or an attacker just swaps one without invalidating the signature. Inside the window, your idempotency key (the delivery ID) still catches a fast replay. Signature proves authenticity, the timestamp bounds the replay window, idempotency handles what slips through.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Webhooks.md" class="internal-link">Webhooks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Message Queues</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you design a Kafka topic to keep per-customer ordering while handling high throughput?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use <code>customer_id</code> as the partition key so every event for a customer lands in the same partition and stays ordered — Kafka only guarantees order within a partition. Then provision enough partitions for parallelism, sized from measured per-partition consumer throughput against your latency target, and scale the consumer group up to (but not beyond) the partition count, since extra consumers just sit idle. Watch for hot partitions: if one customer dominates traffic, a plain <code>customer_id</code> key overloads one partition, so move to a composite key like <code>customer_id:region</code>. Keep consumers <a class="internal-link" href="Idempotency" aria-label="Idempotency" data-tooltip-position="top">idempotent</a>, because rebalances and retries will reprocess records. Ordering and scale pull against each other, and the partition key is where you resolve the tension.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Message Queues/Kafka.md" class="internal-link">Kafka</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Compare at-most-once, at-least-once, and exactly-once in Kafka — which fits payment events?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">At-most-once commits the offset before processing, so a crash loses events — almost never acceptable for payments. At-least-once processes first and commits after, so a crash before commit reprocesses; it's the common production default and safe as long as handlers are idempotent. Exactly-once is real but narrow: Kafka transactions plus an idempotent producer give atomic consume-process-produce, but only for Kafka-to-Kafka flows — a database write or HTTP call inside the handler still needs its own idempotency or an outbox. For payments, the usual answer is at-least-once with strict idempotency (dedupe on a payment ID), reaching for exactly-once only when a duplicate side effect is too costly to risk and the whole flow stays inside Kafka.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Message Queues/Kafka.md" class="internal-link">Kafka</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is MSMQ a maintenance choice rather than a new-system choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It's <strong>Windows-only</strong> — it cannot run on Linux or in containers, which blocks containerization and cloud-native deployment. Its distributed-transaction story depends on <strong>MSDTC</strong>, which is fragile and unavailable in containers, and <code>System.Messaging</code> doesn't exist in .NET 5+. Modern cross-platform brokers (RabbitMQ, Azure Service Bus, Kafka) provide the same durable async messaging without the platform lock-in, so MSMQ is justified only where it's already deployed and migration cost isn't worth it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Message Queues/MSMQ.md" class="internal-link">MSMQ</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does a transactional receive prevent message loss in MSMQ?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The message is removed from the queue <strong>only if the transaction commits</strong>. You <code>Begin</code> a <code>MessageQueueTransaction</code>, <code>Receive</code> under it, process the message, then <code>Commit</code>. If processing throws, you <code>Abort</code> and the message is <strong>returned to the queue</strong> for retry. This is the local-transaction equivalent of an ack: a crash mid-processing leaves the message available rather than consumed-and-lost (the same guarantee modern brokers give via manual ack / visibility timeout).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Message Queues/MSMQ.md" class="internal-link">MSMQ</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What replaces MSMQ's MSDTC distributed transactions today?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The <strong>Outbox pattern</strong>: write the outgoing message to a table in the <em>same</em> local DB transaction as the domain change, then a background relay publishes it to the broker with retries (at-least-once + idempotent consumers). For multi-step cross-service workflows, a <strong>Saga</strong> with compensating transactions. Both avoid MSDTC's coordinator fragility — see <a class="internal-link" href="Distributed Transactions">Distributed Transactions</a>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Message Queues/MSMQ.md" class="internal-link">MSMQ</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you use RabbitMQ to absorb bursty ingress when producers outpace consumers?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Put a durable queue between the ingress and the workers so bursts land in the queue instead of overwhelming the worker tier. Keep the ingress path thin — validate, enqueue, return <code>200</code> fast — so a webhook sender never waits on your processing. Then scale competing consumers horizontally off the same queue, and use prefetch (<code>BasicQos</code>) so one slow worker can't hoard unacked messages while others sit idle. Route poison messages to a dead-letter exchange and watch queue depth, redelivery rate, and message age. The queue is the shock absorber: it turns a traffic spike into a temporary backlog instead of dropped requests or a melted worker tier.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Message Queues/RabbitMQ.md" class="internal-link">RabbitMQ</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you implement at-least-once delivery, and what new risk appears?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">At-least-once is four settings working together: a durable queue, persistent messages (<code>DeliveryMode = 2</code>), publisher confirms so the producer knows the broker accepted the message, and manual consumer ack so a message isn't removed until processing succeeds. If a consumer crashes before acking, the broker redelivers. The risk that buys you is duplicates — a redelivery can race an ack — so consumers must be <a class="internal-link" href="Idempotency" aria-label="Idempotency" data-tooltip-position="top">idempotent</a>, keyed on a stable message ID with a dedupe store. You trade the possibility of loss for the certainty of occasional duplicates, which is the far easier problem to make safe.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Message Queues/RabbitMQ.md" class="internal-link">RabbitMQ</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose RabbitMQ over Kafka?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Choose RabbitMQ when you want a smart broker doing the routing — direct, topic, fanout, and header exchanges, per-message TTL, priorities, dead-lettering — for task queues, request-reply, and command dispatch where low latency and flexible routing matter more than retention. Choose <a class="internal-link" href="Kafka">Kafka</a> when you need a durable, replayable log: high-throughput event streams, ordering per partition, and multiple independent consumers re-reading history by offset. The rough line is that RabbitMQ moves a message and forgets it, while Kafka stores an event history. If you keep wishing you could re-consume past messages, you actually wanted Kafka.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Message Queues/RabbitMQ.md" class="internal-link">RabbitMQ</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Scalability Patterns</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What architectural prerequisites must be met before horizontal scaling works?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Service must be stateless — no in-memory session or local file state.</li>
<li dir="auto">All shared state externalized (Redis for cache/session, blob storage for files, database for persistent data).</li>
<li dir="auto">A <a class="internal-link" href="Load Balancing" aria-label="Load Balancing" data-tooltip-position="top">load balancer</a> must distribute traffic across instances.</li>
<li dir="auto">Without statelessness, adding instances creates inconsistency rather than capacity.</li>
<li dir="auto">Without a load balancer, all traffic still hits one node.<br />
<strong>Why this is strong:</strong> It shows horizontal scaling is an architectural property, not just an ops action. Many engineers think "add more servers" is always safe.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Scalability Patterns/Horizontal Scaling.md" class="internal-link">Horizontal Scaling</a></em></li>
</ul>
