</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can horizontal scaling fail even with many instances?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Database becomes the bottleneck — each instance opens its own connection pool (20 instances × 100 pool size = 2,000 connections).</li>
<li dir="auto">Stateful services silently break (in-memory session on pod 1 invisible to pod 2).</li>
<li dir="auto">Uneven load distribution from sticky sessions or hot partitions.</li>
<li dir="auto">Thundering herd during scale-out when new instances aren't ready fast enough.</li>
<li dir="auto">Connection pool or thread pool saturation before CPU becomes the bottleneck.<br />
<strong>Why this is strong:</strong> It demonstrates awareness that scaling one tier shifts the bottleneck downstream — a classic distributed-systems trap.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Scalability Patterns/Horizontal Scaling.md" class="internal-link">Horizontal Scaling</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose read replicas instead of CQRS for a scaling problem?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Choose read replicas when main pressure is read throughput on an existing relational model.</li>
<li dir="auto">Choose CQRS when read/write models diverge and read projections need different shape or storage.</li>
<li dir="auto">Mention consistency behavior: replicas have lag; CQRS read models are eventually consistent by design.</li>
<li dir="auto">Mention complexity: replicas are simpler operationally than full CQRS/event projection pipelines.<br />
<strong>Why this is strong:</strong> It balances architecture fit, consistency, and operational cost.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Scalability Patterns/Scalability Patterns.md" class="internal-link">Scalability Patterns</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is vertical scaling the right first move over horizontal scaling?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When the workload is stateful and hard to shard (e.g., a relational database).</li>
<li dir="auto">When the bottleneck is clearly resource-bound on a single node.</li>
<li dir="auto">When the operational cost of distributed coordination (consensus, partitioning, eventual consistency) exceeds the cost of a larger machine.</li>
<li dir="auto">Vertical scaling is faster to implement and avoids distributed-systems complexity.</li>
<li dir="auto">It's the right first move for most monoliths and managed databases in early growth phases.<br />
<strong>Why this is strong:</strong> It shows the candidate reasons about tradeoffs (complexity vs cost) rather than defaulting to "just add more servers."<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Scalability Patterns/Vertical Scaling.md" class="internal-link">Vertical Scaling</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does vertical scaling have diminishing returns at high scale?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Hardware cost scales super-linearly: premium SKUs charge a multiplier for the same resource increment.</li>
<li dir="auto">Not all workloads parallelize across additional cores — Amdahl's Law bounds speedup by the serial fraction.</li>
<li dir="auto">A process with 20% serial code can never exceed 5x speedup regardless of core count.</li>
<li dir="auto">Memory bandwidth and cache coherency become bottlenecks as core counts grow.</li>
<li dir="auto">The cost-per-unit-of-capacity curve inflects sharply at high tiers.<br />
<strong>Why this is strong:</strong> It demonstrates understanding of hardware economics and parallelism limits, not just "bigger is better."<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Scalability Patterns/Vertical Scaling.md" class="internal-link">Vertical Scaling</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the failure mode of relying solely on vertical scaling for availability?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A single large node is a single point of failure — any hardware fault, OS crash, or maintenance window causes full outage.</li>
<li dir="auto">Vertical scaling increases capacity but does nothing for availability.</li>
<li dir="auto">Mitigation: pair with redundancy (active-passive failover, read replicas, multi-AZ deployment).</li>
<li dir="auto">Azure SQL Business Critical tier includes a built-in secondary replica for reads and failover.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is distinct from <a class="internal-link" href="Horizontal Scaling">Horizontal Scaling</a>, which inherently distributes failure across nodes.<br />
<strong>Why this is strong:</strong> It shows the candidate distinguishes capacity from availability — a common interview blind spot.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Distributed Systems/Scalability Patterns/Vertical Scaling.md" class="internal-link">Vertical Scaling</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="architecture-patterns">Patterns</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does CQS make code easier to reason about?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When a method returns a value, you know it has no side effects — you can call it freely, cache its result, or call it multiple times without changing state. When a method returns void, you know it changes state but produces no data you depend on. This separation eliminates the need to read the implementation to understand whether a call is safe to repeat or reorder. It also makes testing easier: queries can be tested without checking state changes; commands can be tested without checking return values.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/CQS.md" class="internal-link">CQS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is it pragmatic to violate CQS?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Three common justified exceptions: (1) Stack.Pop() — splitting into Peek() + Remove() introduces a race condition in concurrent code. (2) Repository.Add() returning the generated ID — the ID is produced by the database; returning it avoids an extra round-trip. (3) Async I/O methods — <code>Task&lt;T&gt;</code> methods that perform I/O and return a result are idiomatic in .NET even when they have side effects. The principle is a guideline: apply it where it improves clarity, relax it where strict adherence creates awkward APIs.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/CQS.md" class="internal-link">CQS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Explain <code>Transient</code>, <code>Scoped</code>, and <code>Singleton</code> lifetimes with a safe production example each.</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The three differ by how long the container keeps one instance. Transient is a fresh instance per resolution — fine for lightweight stateless services like mappers or formatters. Scoped is one instance per scope, which in ASP.NET Core means per HTTP request; the canonical case is <code>DbContext</code>, where one unit of work per request keeps tracking and transactions coherent. Singleton is one instance for the app's lifetime — good for thread-safe shared state like a cache, an <code>IClock</code>, or <code>IHttpClientFactory</code> — but it must be thread-safe and must never capture a scoped dependency. The thing to get right: lifetime is about shared state and thread-safety, not performance.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Dependency Injection.md" class="internal-link">Dependency Injection</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Service Locator an anti-pattern in business logic, and when is it acceptable?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Service Locator means reaching into <code>IServiceProvider</code> (<code>GetRequiredService&lt;T&gt;()</code>) from inside a class instead of declaring the dependency in its constructor. In business logic that hides what the class actually needs — the constructor stops telling the truth — so a missing registration fails at runtime instead of compile time, and tests have to mimic the container instead of just passing fakes. It's acceptable in the places that genuinely pick implementations at runtime: factories, middleware activation, and explicit scope management in background jobs. The rule: constructor injection for anything with business logic; the locator only in infrastructure that has no other way to resolve.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Dependency Injection.md" class="internal-link">Dependency Injection</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a captive dependency, and how do you fix it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It's when a longer-lived service captures a shorter-lived one — classically a singleton or <code>IHostedService</code> holding a scoped <code>DbContext</code>. The scoped object then outlives its intended request boundary, giving you stale state, broken EF Core change tracking, and disposal at the wrong time. In Development the scope validator catches it and throws <code>InvalidOperationException</code>; in Production it just misbehaves quietly. The fix is to not inject the scoped service at all: inject <code>IServiceScopeFactory</code>, open a short scope where you need the work, resolve inside it, and let it dispose. The tell to watch for is a singleton constructor asking for anything request-scoped.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Dependency Injection.md" class="internal-link">Dependency Injection</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does an event bus differ from a message broker?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Event bus is a <strong>dispatch mechanism</strong> — resolves handlers and invokes them, typically in-process</li>
<li dir="auto">Message broker is <strong>infrastructure</strong> — durable middleware (RabbitMQ, Kafka, Service Bus) that persists and delivers messages across processes</li>
<li dir="auto">In-process bus loses unprocessed events on crash; broker persists them</li>
<li dir="auto">Bus abstraction can sit on top of a broker (MassTransit does this) — bus adds handler resolution and retry semantics, broker provides transport and durability</li>
<li dir="auto">Cost of broker: operational complexity, network latency, serialization overhead</li>
<li dir="auto">Cost of in-process bus: no durability, no cross-service delivery</li>
<li dir="auto">A broker guarantees delivery at the cost of operational complexity; the in-process bus is simple but ephemeral<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Event Bus.md" class="internal-link">Event Bus</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should event handlers be independent rather than ordered?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Event bus contract is fan-out — "these N things happen when X occurs" — not a sequential pipeline</li>
<li dir="auto">If handler B depends on handler A's side effect, you have a hidden workflow disguised as fan-out</li>
<li dir="auto">Execution order depends on DI registration order, which is fragile and undocumented</li>
<li dir="auto">Breaks when: order changes (new handler inserted), parallelism enabled, or handler moved to another service</li>
<li dir="auto">Fix: model the dependency as a saga or command chain where A publishes a new event that triggers B</li>
<li dir="auto">This makes the dependency visible, testable, and deployable independently</li>
<li dir="auto">You end up with more events and handlers, but each is self-contained and the workflow is explicit in the codebase<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Event Bus.md" class="internal-link">Event Bus</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is MediatR INotification insufficient as an event bus?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Exception isolation</strong>: default MediatR stops at the first handler failure — unacceptable when independent side effects (email, analytics, inventory) must not block each other</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Scope isolation</strong>: all handlers share the same DI scope, so DbContext mutations in handler A leak into handler B</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Execution strategy</strong>: default is sequential; parallel execution requires custom <code>INotificationPublisher</code></li>
<li dir="auto">Each fix requires either replacing MediatR's publisher or building a custom bus</li>
<li dir="auto">A custom bus gives you control over all three concerns, but you lose MediatR's pipeline-behavior ecosystem (validation, logging, caching) and auto-discovery<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Event Bus.md" class="internal-link">Event Bus</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does EF Core's DbContext already implement the Unit of Work pattern?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><code>DbContext</code> tracks all changes to loaded entities in its change tracker.</li>
<li dir="auto"><code>SaveChangesAsync()</code> wraps all pending inserts, updates, and deletes in a single database transaction.</li>
<li dir="auto">This means multiple repository operations within one request share the same <code>DbContext</code> instance and commit atomically — exactly what Unit of Work provides.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: this only works if all repositories share the same <code>DbContext</code> instance (Scoped lifetime in ASP.NET Core DI). If you accidentally register <code>DbContext</code> as Singleton or Transient, the Unit of Work semantics break.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Repository &amp; UoW.md" class="internal-link">Repository &amp; UoW</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is a generic <code>IRepository&lt;T&gt;</code> an anti-pattern?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A generic repository exposes the same interface for every entity, including operations that don't make sense for some aggregates (e.g., <code>GetAll()</code> on an <code>Order</code> aggregate with millions of rows).</li>
<li dir="auto">It encourages callers to treat all entities the same, bypassing aggregate-specific access patterns and invariants.</li>
<li dir="auto">It often leaks <code>IQueryable&lt;T&gt;</code>, coupling callers to EF Core.</li>
<li dir="auto">Better: aggregate-specific interfaces with domain-meaningful methods. Use a generic base class for the implementation, but expose a specific interface.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Repository &amp; UoW.md" class="internal-link">Repository &amp; UoW</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Architectural Patterns</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is CQRS worth the operational complexity, and when is it an anti-pattern?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">CQRS earns its complexity when the read and write sides genuinely want different shapes — a high read:write ratio, broad or expensive queries, real invariants on the write side, and a need to scale the two paths independently. Then a denormalized read model serves screens cheaply while the write model stays focused on enforcing rules. It's an anti-pattern for ordinary CRUD at low scale: the projection pipelines, eventual-consistency handling, and two models to evolve and test are pure tax when one model would have served both. Apply it per bounded context where the constraints justify it, never as a global default.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Architectural Patterns/CQRS.md" class="internal-link">CQRS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Does CQRS require Event Sourcing or two databases?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">No — and conflating them is the most common CQRS misconception. CQRS is only about separating the command model from the query model, and that can be two classes against the same database. Separate stores, message brokers, and <a class="internal-link" href="Event Sourcing">Event Sourcing</a> are options you add when scale or auditability demands them, not requirements. You can run CQRS on a single relational database, projecting into a denormalized view table inside the same transaction. Start with the cheapest separation that solves the problem and add infrastructure only when a real constraint forces it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Architectural Patterns/CQRS.md" class="internal-link">CQRS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you handle eventual consistency between the write and read models?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">With asynchronous projection a command can succeed before the read model catches up, so the user who just acted sees stale data. The pragmatic fixes: serve that user's immediate follow-up read from the write model (read-your-own-writes for the session), show a soft "updating…" state, and monitor projection lag so you notice drift. Underneath, make projections idempotent — brokers are at-least-once, so a projector can see the same event twice — using upserts and a record of handled event IDs. Eventual consistency is a UX-and-idempotency problem, not a reason to abandon the split.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Architectural Patterns/CQRS.md" class="internal-link">CQRS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between an Entity and a Value Object?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Entity: has a unique identity that persists over time. Two entities with the same data are different if their IDs differ. Example: <code>Order</code> with <code>OrderId</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Value Object: defined entirely by its attributes, no identity. Two Value Objects with the same data are equal. Immutable. Example: <code>Money(10, "USD")</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Rule of thumb: if you care about <em>which</em> instance it is, it's an Entity. If you only care about <em>what</em> it contains, it's a Value Object.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: Value Objects are simpler to reason about (immutable, no identity tracking) but require copying on mutation. Use them for concepts like Money, Address, DateRange, Coordinates.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Architectural Patterns/Domain-Driven Design.md" class="internal-link">Domain-Driven Design</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should external code only interact with an Aggregate through its root?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The Aggregate Root enforces all invariants for the cluster. If external code modifies a <code>LineItem</code> directly, it bypasses the <code>Order</code>'s consistency checks.</li>
<li dir="auto">Example: adding a line item after an order is confirmed should be rejected. If <code>LineItem</code> is modified directly, the <code>Order</code> never gets a chance to enforce this rule.</li>
<li dir="auto">Practical implication: repositories load and save entire Aggregates, not individual child entities. EF Core's change tracking makes this natural.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: loading the full Aggregate for every operation can be expensive if the Aggregate is large. This is a signal that the Aggregate boundary is too wide.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Architectural Patterns/Domain-Driven Design.md" class="internal-link">Domain-Driven Design</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does Event Sourcing justify its complexity over CRUD plus an audit-log table?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">CRUD + audit table can satisfy compliance for many systems with lower operational overhead.</li>
<li dir="auto">Event Sourcing is justified when domain behavior depends on historical intent and replay, not only final values.</li>
<li dir="auto">If you need deterministic rebuild of multiple read models, Event Sourcing is stronger.</li>
<li dir="auto">If temporal queries are frequent and core to product value, Event Sourcing can pay off.</li>
<li dir="auto">If team maturity for schema evolution and projection operations is low, choose CRUD first.</li>
<li dir="auto">The honest default is CRUD plus an audit table; Event Sourcing earns its cost only when replay and historical intent are core to the product, not merely compliance.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Architectural Patterns/Event Sourcing.md" class="internal-link">Event Sourcing</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you evolve event schemas safely without breaking old streams?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use explicit event versioning strategy.</li>
<li dir="auto">Prefer backward-compatible additive changes.</li>
<li dir="auto">Introduce upcasters/adapters for old payloads.</li>
<li dir="auto">Keep integration tests that replay production-like historical streams.</li>
<li dir="auto">Treat event contracts as long-lived public interfaces.</li>
<li dir="auto">Schema evolution is the most common production failure mode in event-sourced systems — especially when teams skip compatibility testing across historical streams.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Architectural Patterns/Event Sourcing.md" class="internal-link">Event Sourcing</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Design Patterns</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you decide which GoF category a pattern belongs to?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Creational if the problem is about object construction — hiding how, when, or which type to instantiate. Structural if the problem is about composing classes or adapting interfaces into larger, more convenient structures. Behavioral if the problem is about assigning responsibility or defining the communication flow between objects.</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The category signals intent, not class structure: Proxy (Structural) and Decorator (Structural) look identical structurally but serve different behavioral intents.</li>
<li dir="auto">When in doubt: ask "Is this about <em>making</em> objects, <em>assembling</em> objects, or <em>communicating between</em> objects?"</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: categories help communicate design intent quickly, but the same structure can serve different intents — always explain the <em>why</em>, not just the pattern name.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Design Patterns.md" class="internal-link">Design Patterns</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does using a design pattern become an anti-pattern?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the variation it enables doesn't exist yet and isn't clearly anticipated. Patterns add abstractions — more classes, more indirection, harder debugging — and abstraction has a cost.</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Introduce a pattern at the second variation point (rule of three / YAGNI), not speculatively.</li>
<li dir="auto">Most common offenders: Singleton hiding shared mutable state, Factory Method for a class that never has variants, Builder for objects with 2 properties.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: patterns pay off over time through extension without modification; they cost upfront in complexity. The break-even is when the second concrete variant appears or when the roadmap makes variation certain.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Design Patterns.md" class="internal-link">Design Patterns</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the Information Expert principle and why does it reduce coupling?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Information Expert assigns responsibility to the class that has the information needed to fulfill it. This keeps related data and behavior together, reducing the need for one class to reach into another to get data. The result is lower coupling and higher cohesion — each class does what it knows.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/GRASP.md" class="internal-link">GRASP</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does GRASP differ from SOLID?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">GRASP focuses on responsibility assignment — which class should own a behavior or data. SOLID focuses on design principles for maintainability (single responsibility, open/closed, etc.). They are complementary: GRASP guides initial design decisions; SOLID guides refactoring and long-term maintainability.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/GRASP.md" class="internal-link">GRASP</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h4>Behavioral</h4><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is undo not feasible, and how do you handle it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Undo is not feasible when the operation has external side effects that can't be reversed: sending an email (can't unsend), charging a card (refund is a new operation, not a reversal), or publishing an event to a message bus. In these cases, implement compensating transactions instead of true undo: <code>CancelOrderCommand</code> is the compensation for <code>PlaceOrderCommand</code>. Document which commands support true undo and which only support compensation. The tradeoff: true undo is simpler for the caller; compensating transactions are more realistic for distributed systems.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Command.md" class="internal-link">Command</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does MediatR implement the Command pattern, and what does it add?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">MediatR separates the command (data + intent) from the handler (execution logic). <code>PlaceOrderCommand</code> carries the order data; <code>PlaceOrderCommandHandler</code> knows how to process it. The mediator routes commands to handlers without the sender knowing the handler. MediatR adds: pipeline behaviors (Chain of Responsibility around the handler), notification publishing (one command → multiple handlers), and request/response typing. The tradeoff: MediatR adds a dependency and indirection; direct service calls are simpler. MediatR earns its complexity when you need pipeline behaviors (validation, logging, caching) applied consistently across all commands.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Command.md" class="internal-link">Command</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does ASP.NET Core Middleware differ from a classical Chain of Responsibility?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Classical CoR uses a linked list of handlers where each holds a reference to the next. ASP.NET Core Middleware uses a delegate pipeline: each middleware is a <code>Func&lt;RequestDelegate, RequestDelegate&gt;</code> that wraps the next <code>RequestDelegate</code>. The pipeline is compiled at startup into a single delegate chain. The difference: ASP.NET Core's pipeline is immutable after build (no dynamic handler insertion); classical CoR can be modified at runtime. ASP.NET Core's approach is more performant (no virtual dispatch per handler) but less flexible. The tradeoff: startup-time composition vs runtime flexibility.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Chain of Responsibility.md" class="internal-link">Chain of Responsibility</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should a handler stop the chain vs pass the request along?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Stop the chain when the handler has definitively handled the request — either successfully (order approved) or with a terminal failure (fraud detected, stop processing). Pass along when the handler's check passes and subsequent handlers may still reject. The key question: is this handler's decision final? Fraud detection is final (don't process fraudulent orders regardless of other checks). Stock check is final for that item but not for the order (other items may still be available). Design each handler to be authoritative about its own domain and ignorant of others.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Chain of Responsibility.md" class="internal-link">Chain of Responsibility</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you handle a request that should be processed by multiple handlers (not just the first one)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Change the chain to not short-circuit — every handler processes the request and the results are aggregated. This is the "collect all errors" variant of validation: instead of stopping at the first failure, all handlers run and all errors are collected. The tradeoff: short-circuiting is faster (stops at first failure) but gives less feedback; full traversal is slower but gives complete error information. For user-facing validation, full traversal is usually better UX. For security checks (fraud, sanctions), short-circuit immediately — don't reveal which check failed.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Chain of Responsibility.md" class="internal-link">Chain of Responsibility</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does EF Core use LINQ Expression Trees as an Interpreter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When you write <code>dbContext.Orders.Where(o =&gt; o.Total &gt; 100)</code>, the C# compiler doesn't compile the lambda to IL — it compiles it to an <code>Expression&lt;Func&lt;Order, bool&gt;&gt;</code> (an expression tree, an AST). EF Core's query translator walks this AST using <code>ExpressionVisitor</code>: <code>VisitBinary</code> for <code>o.Total &gt; 100</code> emits <code>Total &gt; @p0</code>; <code>VisitMember</code> for <code>o.Total</code> emits the column name. The final SQL is assembled from the visited nodes. This is the Interpreter pattern: the expression tree is the grammar; EF Core is the interpreter; SQL is the output. The cost: EF Core can only interpret expressions it knows — calling a custom method in a LINQ query throws <code>InvalidOperationException</code> because the interpreter doesn't have a rule for that method.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Interpreter.md" class="internal-link">Interpreter</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you compile an expression tree to a delegate instead of interpreting it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the same expression is evaluated many times (e.g., a discount rule evaluated on every order in a batch). <code>Expression.Lambda&lt;Func&lt;DiscountContext, bool&gt;&gt;(tree).Compile()</code> converts the expression tree to IL and returns a delegate that runs at near-native speed. The compilation itself is expensive (milliseconds); cache the compiled delegate. The tradeoff: compilation adds startup cost and memory; interpretation adds per-evaluation cost. Break-even is roughly 100+ evaluations of the same expression. EF Core compiles and caches query expressions for this reason.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Interpreter.md" class="internal-link">Interpreter</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the difference between Interpreter and Strategy for runtime rule selection?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Strategy selects a pre-written algorithm at runtime. Interpreter evaluates a rule defined as data at runtime. With Strategy, the algorithms are written in code and selected by name or type. With Interpreter, the rule is a data structure (expression tree, string) that the interpreter evaluates. Interpreter is more flexible (rules can be composed from primitives without code changes) but more complex (requires a grammar and interpreter). Strategy is simpler (just inject the right implementation) but requires a code change for each new algorithm. Use Strategy when the set of algorithms is known at compile time; use Interpreter when rules are defined by non-developers or change without deployment.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Interpreter.md" class="internal-link">Interpreter</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you return <code>IEnumerable&lt;T&gt;</code> vs <code>IReadOnlyList&lt;T&gt;</code> vs <code>IAsyncEnumerable&lt;T&gt;</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Return <code>IReadOnlyList&lt;T&gt;</code> when the collection is fully materialized and callers need random access or <code>Count</code>. Return <code>IEnumerable&lt;T&gt;</code> when the collection is lazy or the caller only needs sequential access. Return <code>IAsyncEnumerable&lt;T&gt;</code> when the source is async (database, network) and you want to stream results without buffering all of them. The tradeoff: <code>IReadOnlyList&lt;T&gt;</code> is simpler but requires full materialization; <code>IAsyncEnumerable&lt;T&gt;</code> is memory-efficient but requires <code>await foreach</code> at the call site. Default to <code>IReadOnlyList&lt;T&gt;</code> for small collections; use <code>IAsyncEnumerable&lt;T&gt;</code> when the collection could be large or unbounded.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Iterator.md" class="internal-link">Iterator</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does the compiler generate for a <code>yield return</code> method?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The compiler generates a private class implementing <code>IEnumerator&lt;T&gt;</code> and <code>IEnumerable&lt;T&gt;</code>. The method body is split into states at each <code>yield return</code> point. <code>MoveNext()</code> advances the state machine to the next <code>yield return</code>, executes the code between yields, and returns <code>true</code>. <code>Current</code> returns the last yielded value. The generated class captures all local variables as fields. This is why <code>yield return</code> methods can't use <code>ref</code> locals or <code>unsafe</code> code — the state machine can't capture those.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Iterator.md" class="internal-link">Iterator</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does Mediator become a bottleneck or anti-pattern?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the mediator becomes a god class that knows too much — if <code>CheckoutCommandHandler</code> grows to 300 lines with complex branching, the complexity moved from the controller to the handler without being reduced. The mediator pattern reduces coupling but doesn't reduce complexity. Also avoid Mediator when the interaction is simple and direct: if <code>OrderService</code> only ever calls <code>InventoryService</code>, a direct dependency is clearer than routing through a mediator. The signal: if you can't explain what the mediator does without listing all its handlers, it's too complex.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Mediator.md" class="internal-link">Mediator</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do MediatR pipeline behaviors implement the Chain of Responsibility pattern?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each <code>IPipelineBehavior&lt;TRequest, TResponse&gt;</code> wraps the next behavior in the pipeline. <code>Handle(request, next, ct)</code> calls <code>next()</code> to continue or returns early to short-circuit. Behaviors are registered in order; the outermost runs first. This is exactly Chain of Responsibility: each behavior decides whether to pass the request along. The difference from a manual chain: MediatR's pipeline is configured via DI registration order, not explicit <code>SetNext()</code> calls. The tradeoff: DI-based ordering is less explicit but easier to configure.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Mediator.md" class="internal-link">Mediator</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you prevent the memento from growing unbounded in memory?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Limit the history depth: keep only the last N mementos (a bounded stack). For long-running sessions, serialize mementos to Redis or a database instead of keeping them in memory. For abandoned cart recovery, store only the latest snapshot (not the full history). The tradeoff: deeper history = more undo steps but more memory. For most UX scenarios, 10-20 undo steps is sufficient. For audit/compliance scenarios, store all snapshots in a database with a TTL.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Memento.md" class="internal-link">Memento</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is Memento overkill compared to simpler approaches?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the state is small and the undo operation is simple. If "undo remove item" just means re-adding the item, store the removed item directly — no need for a full cart snapshot. Memento earns its complexity when: (1) the state is complex and interrelated (discount + items + shipping options all affect each other), (2) you need multiple undo levels, or (3) you need to restore state across sessions (abandoned cart). For single-step undo of simple operations, store the delta (what changed) rather than the full snapshot.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Memento.md" class="internal-link">Memento</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does subscribing to an event with <code>async void</code> cause problems?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>async void</code> event handlers can't be awaited. If the handler throws, the exception propagates to the synchronization context (often crashing the app) rather than being catchable by the publisher. If the handler does async work, the publisher doesn't know when it completes — the event returns before the async work finishes. Use <code>async void</code> only for top-level event handlers in UI frameworks where the framework expects it. For server-side observers, use an explicit <code>Task</code>-returning interface and <code>await Task.WhenAll(observers.Select(o =&gt; o.OnStatusChangedAsync(...)))</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Observer.md" class="internal-link">Observer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you prevent memory leaks from event subscriptions?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Three approaches: (1) <strong>Unsubscribe in <code>Dispose()</code></strong> — implement <code>IDisposable</code> and unsubscribe in <code>Dispose()</code>. (2) <strong>Weak event pattern</strong> — use <code>WeakEventManager</code> (WPF) or <code>WeakReference&lt;T&gt;</code> to hold subscriber references; the publisher doesn't prevent GC. (3) <strong>Scoped lifetime</strong> — if both publisher and subscriber have the same DI lifetime (both scoped), they're collected together. The tradeoff: explicit unsubscription is reliable but requires discipline; weak events are automatic but add complexity and slight performance overhead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Observer.md" class="internal-link">Observer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use <code>IObservable&lt;T&gt;</code> (Rx) instead of plain events?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When you need stream operators: filtering (<code>Where</code>), transformation (<code>Select</code>), throttling (<code>Throttle</code>), combining multiple streams (<code>Merge</code>, <code>CombineLatest</code>), or backpressure. Plain events are push-only with no composition. Rx adds a rich operator library over the observable stream. Use Rx when: you're processing a stream of events with complex filtering or timing requirements (e.g., "notify only if status changes twice within 5 seconds"). The cost: Rx has a steep learning curve and adds a dependency. For simple one-to-many notification, plain events or explicit interfaces are sufficient.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Observer.md" class="internal-link">Observer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the <code>async</code>/<code>await</code> compiler implement the State pattern?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The compiler transforms an <code>async</code> method into a struct implementing <code>IAsyncStateMachine</code>. The struct has a <code>state</code> field (an integer) representing the current position in the method. <code>MoveNext()</code> is a switch on <code>state</code>: each case resumes execution from the last <code>await</code> point. When an <code>await</code> suspends, the state is saved and <code>MoveNext()</code> returns. When the awaited task completes, <code>MoveNext()</code> is called again with the next state. Local variables become fields on the struct (captured state). This is exactly the State pattern: the method's execution state drives behavior, and transitions happen automatically at each <code>await</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/State.md" class="internal-link">State</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use the <code>Stateless</code> library instead of hand-written state classes?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the state machine has many states and transitions that are better expressed declaratively. <code>Stateless</code> lets you define <code>machine.Configure(State.Paid).Permit(Trigger.Ship, State.Shipped).OnEntry(() =&gt; SendShipmentEmail())</code> — the transition table is explicit and readable. Hand-written state classes are better when each state has complex behavior beyond transitions (not just "what's the next state" but "what does Ship() do in this state"). The tradeoff: <code>Stateless</code> is concise for transition-heavy machines; hand-written classes are better for behavior-heavy states.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/State.md" class="internal-link">State</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you handle state transitions that require async operations (e.g., sending a refund on cancel)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Make <code>TransitionTo()</code> async and await the side effects before completing the transition. Or use the Observer pattern: raise a <code>StatusChanged</code> event after transitioning, and let async observers handle side effects. The second approach keeps state transitions synchronous and side effects decoupled. The tradeoff: synchronous transitions are simpler but can't await side effects; event-based side effects are decoupled but harder to reason about ordering and failure handling.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/State.md" class="internal-link">State</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use a Strategy interface vs a <code>Func&lt;T, TResult&gt;</code> delegate?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use a <code>Func&lt;T, TResult&gt;</code> delegate when the strategy is simple (one method, no state, no dependencies). <code>Func&lt;Order, decimal&gt;</code> is a perfectly valid shipping cost strategy for simple cases. Use an interface when: the strategy has multiple methods, needs DI-injected dependencies, needs to be registered in a DI container, or needs to carry state. The tradeoff: delegates are simpler and more flexible; interfaces are more explicit and support DI. Start with delegates; introduce an interface when the strategy grows beyond a single expression.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Strategy.md" class="internal-link">Strategy</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you handle strategy selection when multiple strategies apply?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Define a priority or exclusivity rule. Options: (1) <strong>First-match wins</strong> — strategies are ordered by priority; the first applicable one is used. (2) <strong>Explicit selection</strong> — the caller specifies the strategy by name or type. (3) <strong>Composite strategy</strong> — all applicable strategies run and results are combined (e.g., sum all applicable discounts). The tradeoff: first-match is simple but order-dependent; explicit selection is clear but requires the caller to know strategy names; composite is flexible but may produce unexpected combinations.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Strategy.md" class="internal-link">Strategy</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use Template Method vs Strategy for algorithm variation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Template Method uses inheritance — the variation is in a subclass. Strategy uses composition — the variation is in an injected object. Use Template Method when: the algorithm skeleton is stable, the variations are tightly coupled to the base class, and you don't need to swap algorithms at runtime. Use Strategy when: you need to swap algorithms at runtime, the algorithm is independent of the class using it, or you want to avoid inheritance. The tradeoff: Template Method is simpler (no extra interface) but creates tight inheritance coupling; Strategy is more flexible but requires an extra interface and injection.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Template Method.md" class="internal-link">Template Method</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the "Hollywood Principle" and how does Template Method implement it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">"Don't call us, we'll call you." The base class calls the subclass's methods (abstract steps), not the other way around. The subclass doesn't control when its methods are called — the template method does. This inverts the typical inheritance relationship: instead of the subclass calling <code>super.method()</code>, the base class calls <code>this.abstractStep()</code>. The benefit: the algorithm's structure is controlled by the base class; subclasses can't accidentally skip steps or change the order. The cost: subclasses are tightly coupled to the base class's algorithm structure.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Template Method.md" class="internal-link">Template Method</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is double dispatch and why does Visitor need it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Single dispatch: the method called depends on the runtime type of ONE object (the receiver). Double dispatch: the method called depends on the runtime types of TWO objects (the element AND the visitor). Without double dispatch, <code>visitor.Visit(item)</code> would call the <code>Visit(ICartItem)</code> overload — the compiler resolves overloads at compile time based on the declared type. <code>item.Accept(visitor)</code> → <code>visitor.Visit(this)</code> forces the compiler to resolve the overload based on <code>this</code>'s concrete type at runtime. The cost: two virtual calls instead of one; the pattern is non-obvious to developers unfamiliar with it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Visitor.md" class="internal-link">Visitor</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does EF Core use ExpressionVisitor, and what does it do?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">EF Core's query translator is an <code>ExpressionVisitor</code> that walks the LINQ expression tree and converts it to SQL. <code>dbContext.Orders.Where(o =&gt; o.Total &gt; 100).Select(o =&gt; o.Id)</code> builds an expression tree; EF Core visits each node: <code>VisitMethodCall</code> for <code>Where</code> and <code>Select</code>, <code>VisitBinary</code> for <code>o.Total &gt; 100</code>, <code>VisitMember</code> for <code>o.Total</code> and <code>o.Id</code>. Each visit emits SQL fragments. The final SQL is assembled from the visited nodes. This is why EF Core can translate LINQ to SQL but throws <code>InvalidOperationException</code> for expressions it can't translate — the visitor doesn't know how to visit that node type.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Visitor.md" class="internal-link">Visitor</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use pattern matching instead of Visitor?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the element hierarchy is small (2-4 types), changes frequently (new types added regularly), or the operations are simple (one-liners per type). Pattern matching is more readable, requires no <code>Accept()</code> method on elements, and handles new element types with a compiler warning (exhaustiveness checking with <code>_</code> catch-all). Visitor earns its complexity when: you have 5+ element types, 5+ operations, and the hierarchy is stable. The signal: if adding a new element type requires editing more than 3 visitor classes, the hierarchy is too unstable for Visitor.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Behavioral/Visitor.md" class="internal-link">Visitor</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h4>Creational</h4><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you add a new product type (e.g., IFraudDetector) to an existing Abstract Factory without breaking all existing factories?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">You can't without modifying the interface — that's the fundamental tension. Options: (1) Add the method with a default implementation in the interface (<code>default IFraudDetector CreateFraudDetector() =&gt; new NoOpFraudDetector()</code>), which avoids breaking existing factories but hides missing implementations. (2) Use a separate <code>IFraudDetectorFactory</code> interface and compose it with <code>IPaymentProviderFactory</code> at the call site. (3) Accept the breaking change and update all factories — justified when the product is truly part of the family. The tradeoff: default implementations hide gaps; separate interfaces reduce cohesion; breaking changes are honest but costly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Abstract Factory.md" class="internal-link">Abstract Factory</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is Abstract Factory overkill compared to a simpler approach?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When you have only one product family (no need to swap providers), or when products don't need to stay compatible with each other. A single <code>IPaymentProcessor</code> interface with DI registration is sufficient if you never need <code>IReceiptGenerator</code> to match the processor's provider. Abstract Factory's value is the <strong>family constraint</strong> — if that constraint doesn't exist in your domain, you're adding indirection for no benefit. Cost: every new product type requires updating the factory interface and all implementations.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Abstract Factory.md" class="internal-link">Abstract Factory</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does Abstract Factory relate to the DI container in modern .NET?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The DI container IS an Abstract Factory at runtime. <code>services.AddSingleton&lt;IPaymentProcessor, StripePaymentProcessor&gt;()</code> registers a factory for <code>IPaymentProcessor</code>. The container creates compatible families when you register all related services together. The difference: DI containers don't enforce family consistency at compile time — you can register <code>StripePaymentProcessor</code> with <code>PayPalReceiptGenerator</code> and the compiler won't complain. A typed Abstract Factory interface enforces this at compile time. Use DI for flexibility; use a typed factory when family consistency is a hard requirement.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Abstract Factory.md" class="internal-link">Abstract Factory</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does Builder's <code>Build()</code> method justify its existence over a constructor?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When <code>Build()</code> does work a constructor shouldn't: cross-field validation (gift wrap requires a message), computed fields (total = subtotal - discount), default derivation (billing = shipping if not set), or async initialization. Constructors should be fast and never throw business logic exceptions. <code>Build()</code> is the right place for invariant enforcement that spans multiple fields. The tradeoff: <code>Build()</code> can throw at runtime; <code>required</code> properties fail at compile time. Use <code>required</code> when all fields are independent; use Builder when fields interact.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Builder.md" class="internal-link">Builder</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the Director role work, and when do you need it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A Director encapsulates a specific construction sequence, calling builder methods in a fixed order. Example: <code>StandardOrderDirector.BuildGiftOrder(builder, customer, items)</code> always calls <code>ShipTo</code>, <code>WithGiftWrap</code>, <code>WithShipping(Express)</code> in the right order. Directors are useful when the same construction sequence is reused across multiple call sites, or when the sequence itself is a business rule (gift orders always use express shipping). Without a director, each call site must know the correct sequence — a form of knowledge duplication. The tradeoff: directors add a class; without them, call sites are more flexible but less consistent.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Architecture/Patterns/Design Patterns/Creational/Builder.md" class="internal-link">Builder</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does <code>WebApplicationBuilder</code> use a builder instead of a constructor with parameters?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
