</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why exactly <code>V−1</code> relaxation passes, and what does a change on the <code>V</code>-th pass mean?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">In a graph with no negative cycle, every shortest path is simple, so it uses at most <code>V−1</code> edges.</li>
<li dir="auto">After pass <code>k</code>, all shortest paths of <code>k</code> edges or fewer are finalized, so <code>V−1</code> passes finalize every distance.</li>
<li dir="auto">If a <code>V</code>-th pass still relaxes an edge, some path is <em>still</em> getting shorter past the <code>V−1</code>-edge limit — only a negative cycle allows that.</li>
<li dir="auto">So the <code>V</code>-th pass is not wasted work: it is precisely the negative-cycle detector, and it is why Bellman-Ford can do something <a class="internal-link" href="Dijkstra">Dijkstra</a> structurally cannot — a decisive reason to accept its <code>O(VE)</code> cost when negative edges are in play.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Bellman-Ford.md" class="internal-link">Bellman-Ford</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you extract the actual negative cycle, not just detect one?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Find a vertex <code>x</code> that relaxed on the <code>V</code>-th pass; it is either on a negative cycle or reachable from one.</li>
<li dir="auto">Walk <code>pred</code> back <code>V</code> times starting from <code>x</code>; because a <code>pred</code>-walk cannot leave a cycle it has entered, after <code>V</code> steps you are guaranteed to be <em>inside</em> the cycle.</li>
<li dir="auto">From that in-cycle vertex, follow <code>pred</code> until you return to it, collecting the vertices — that sequence is the cycle.</li>
<li dir="auto">This turns detection into a concrete answer: in currency arbitrage the extracted loop is the exact sequence of trades to execute, so "there is a cycle" becomes "here is the money."<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Bellman-Ford.md" class="internal-link">Bellman-Ford</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is currency arbitrage a Bellman-Ford problem, and what is the weight trick?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Model each currency as a vertex and each exchange rate <code>r</code> as a directed edge; a profitable loop multiplies rates to more than 1.</li>
<li dir="auto">Take <code>-log(rate)</code> as the edge weight; then a product of rates greater than 1 becomes a <em>sum</em> of weights less than 0.</li>
<li dir="auto">A profitable arbitrage cycle is therefore exactly a negative-weight cycle, which Bellman-Ford detects and extracts.</li>
<li dir="auto">The insight is that a multiplicative optimization was converted into an additive shortest-path one by taking logs — the same reframing lets shortest-path machinery answer a finance question it was never designed for.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Bellman-Ford.md" class="internal-link">Bellman-Ford</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does bidirectional search reduce complexity from <code>O(b^d)</code> to <code>O(b^(d/2))</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A single search reaching depth <code>d</code> expands <code>O(b^d)</code> nodes because the frontier grows exponentially with depth.</li>
<li dir="auto">Two searches meeting in the middle each reach only depth <code>d/2</code>, expanding <code>O(b^(d/2))</code> nodes apiece.</li>
<li dir="auto">Since <code>2·b^(d/2)</code> is exponentially smaller than <code>b^d</code>, total work collapses — for <code>b=10, d=6</code> that's roughly <code>2,000</code> nodes versus <code>1,000,000</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">The insight is geometric: two small search "spheres" growing toward each other sweep far less volume than one large sphere of the same reach — which is why it's a go-to optimization for high-branching point-to-point queries, and why the win is often in memory as much as time.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Bidirectional Search.md" class="internal-link">Bidirectional Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is it wrong to stop bidirectional search the moment the two frontiers meet?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The first node found in both visited sets gives <em>a</em> connecting path, but not necessarily the shortest one.</li>
<li dir="auto">A cheaper path can pass through a different node whose forward and backward halves haven't both been expanded yet, so first contact can over-report the cost.</li>
<li dir="auto">The correct rule tracks the best meeting cost <code>μ</code> and keeps expanding until the sum of the two frontiers' minimum keys (<code>top_F + top_B</code>) is at least <code>μ</code>, proving no unexpanded path can do better.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is the single subtlest part of the algorithm: a naive implementation that returns on first collision is a common, silent correctness bug — the termination condition, not the meeting, is what makes the answer optimal.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Bidirectional Search.md" class="internal-link">Bidirectional Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What must a graph support for bidirectional search to be usable, and what is front-to-front vs front-to-back?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The graph must be searchable <em>backward</em> from the target: predecessors must be enumerable (a reverse adjacency list for directed graphs, or an invertible move function for implicit state spaces).</li>
<li dir="auto">The target must be a concrete node, not merely a goal-test, since the backward search needs a starting point.</li>
<li dir="auto">For bidirectional heuristic search, front-to-back estimates distance to the opposite search's fixed origin (cheap, weaker), while front-to-front estimates distance to the opposite search's current frontier (accurate, expensive).</li>
<li dir="auto">These requirements decide applicability: on a reversible, point-to-point routing graph bidirectional A<em> is a big win, but on a forward-only implicit search defined by a goal predicate it simply doesn't apply — pick unidirectional [[A-Start Search|A</em> Search]] there.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Bidirectional Search.md" class="internal-link">Bidirectional Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does Dijkstra require non-negative edge weights?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Dijkstra.md" class="internal-link">Dijkstra</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What data structures make Dijkstra practical at scale?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Dijkstra.md" class="internal-link">Dijkstra</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you prefer A* over Dijkstra?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Dijkstra.md" class="internal-link">Dijkstra</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must the <code>k</code>-loop be the outermost of the three?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><code>dist[i][j]</code> after stage <code>k</code> is defined as the shortest <code>i</code>-to-<code>j</code> path using only intermediates in <code>{0, …, k}</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">That definition requires the whole matrix to finish updating for one <code>k</code> before starting the next, so <code>k</code> names a <em>stage</em> that must complete atomically.</li>
<li dir="auto">If <code>k</code> is not outermost, the relaxation reads <code>dist[i][k]</code> or <code>dist[k][j]</code> cells that belong to an unfinished stage, so the recurrence consumes half-updated data.</li>
<li dir="auto">The failure is silent — the code still runs and returns numbers — which is exactly why this is the single most important thing to get right; an interviewer asks it because loop order is where real implementations quietly break.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Floyd-Warshall.md" class="internal-link">Floyd-Warshall</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the in-place update on one matrix correct, giving <code>O(V²)</code> space?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The naive DP keeps a separate matrix per stage <code>k</code>, costing <code>O(V³)</code> space.</li>
<li dir="auto">During stage <code>k</code>, the two cells read are <code>dist[i][k]</code> and <code>dist[k][j]</code>; neither improves <em>within</em> stage <code>k</code>, because a shortest path through <code>k</code> never uses <code>k</code> as an intermediate of its own sub-legs.</li>
<li dir="auto">So reading and writing the same array during stage <code>k</code> yields identical results to keeping a previous copy.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is why the textbook algorithm is safely two-dimensional despite three loops — recognizing that the read cells are stable is what lets you drop a whole dimension of memory.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Floyd-Warshall.md" class="internal-link">Floyd-Warshall</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does Floyd-Warshall detect a negative cycle, and how is that different from Bellman-Ford?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">After the full sweep, any <code>dist[i][i] &lt; 0</code> means there is a negative-weight path from <code>i</code> back to itself — a negative cycle through <code>i</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">It reports negative cycles for <em>all</em> vertices at once as a by-product of the all-pairs computation, with no extra pass.</li>
<li dir="auto"><a class="internal-link" href="Bellman-Ford">Bellman-Ford</a> instead runs a dedicated <code>V</code>-th relaxation pass from one source and can walk predecessors to extract the actual cycle.</li>
<li dir="auto">The practical split: use Floyd-Warshall when you already want the whole distance matrix and just need a yes/no on cycles; use Bellman-Ford when you need the concrete cycle from a specific source, as in arbitrage extraction.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Floyd-Warshall.md" class="internal-link">Floyd-Warshall</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When do you pick BFS over DFS?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">BFS is preferred for shortest path by edge count in unweighted graphs.</li>
<li dir="auto">DFS is preferred for deep traversal tasks like cycle detection and topological ordering.</li>
<li dir="auto">BFS uses more memory on wide graphs because of the frontier queue.</li>
<li dir="auto">Both are O(V+E), so pick by the property you need, not by speed: BFS guarantees shortest paths but its frontier can hold a whole layer; DFS uses depth-bounded memory but gives no distance guarantee.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Graph Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Graph Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Dijkstra not valid with negative edges?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Dijkstra assumes once a node is finalized, its best distance is known.</li>
<li dir="auto">Negative edges can later produce a shorter route to a finalized node.</li>
<li dir="auto">Bellman Ford handles negative edges by repeated relaxation.</li>
<li dir="auto">Dijkstra is faster (O((V+E) log V)) but only valid with non-negative weights; Bellman–Ford accepts negative edges at O(V·E) — pay the slower cost only when weights can go negative.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Graph Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Graph Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Adjacency list or adjacency matrix?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Adjacency list is the default for sparse graphs (most real-world graphs): O(V+E) space and efficient neighbor iteration.</li>
<li dir="auto">Adjacency matrix uses O(V²) space but answers "is there an edge A→B?" in O(1).</li>
<li dir="auto">In .NET, <code>Dictionary&lt;T, List&lt;T&gt;&gt;</code> is a common adjacency-list implementation.</li>
<li dir="auto">The list saves memory and speeds traversal on sparse graphs; the matrix trades O(V²) memory for constant-time edge checks, so reach for it only on dense graphs or edge-query-heavy workloads.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Graph Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Graph Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Greedy Best-First Search neither optimal nor complete?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It orders the frontier by <code>h(n)</code> alone, the estimated cost to the goal, and never accounts for <code>g(n)</code>, the cost already spent.</li>
<li dir="auto">Because it ignores accumulated cost, a locally attractive step can lock it into a globally expensive path — so the returned path can be far from shortest (not optimal).</li>
<li dir="auto">On an infinite graph, or without a visited set, it can chase an ever-improving heuristic down a fruitless branch forever (not complete).</li>
<li dir="auto">The takeaway: GBFS trades every correctness guarantee for speed, so it only belongs where "any path, fast" is genuinely acceptable — otherwise <a class="internal-link" href="A-Start Search" aria-label="A-Start Search" data-tooltip-position="top">A* Search</a> gives you the same goal-seeking behavior with optimality restored.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Greedy Best-First Search.md" class="internal-link">Greedy Best-First Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Where does GBFS sit on the spectrum between Dijkstra and A*?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><a class="internal-link" href="Dijkstra">Dijkstra</a> ranks purely by <code>g</code> (cost-so-far) and ignores the goal — optimal but explores in all directions.</li>
<li dir="auto">GBFS ranks purely by <code>h</code> (cost-to-go) and ignores accumulated cost — fast toward the goal but suboptimal and incomplete.</li>
<li dir="auto"><a class="internal-link" href="A-Start Search" aria-label="A-Start Search" data-tooltip-position="top">A* Search</a> ranks by <code>f = g + h</code>, combining both — the principled middle that keeps GBFS's goal pull while regaining optimality.</li>
<li dir="auto">Seeing the three as one family clarifies design choices: you're really tuning how much to trust the heuristic, and weighted A<em> lets you dial continuously between the A</em> middle and the greedy extreme.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Greedy Best-First Search.md" class="internal-link">Greedy Best-First Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the concave-obstacle failure mode and how do you avoid it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A wall shaped like a pocket around the goal gives every cell inside it a low <code>h</code>, so GBFS is lured in.</li>
<li dir="auto">Once inside, the direct heading to the goal is blocked, and with no cost awareness GBFS thrashes along the barrier re-committing to the blocked direction before escaping the long way.</li>
<li dir="auto">Avoid it by using a cost-aware search (A* or <a class="internal-link" href="Dijkstra">Dijkstra</a>) on maps with concave geometry, or by precomputing an obstacle-aware heuristic like a navigation-mesh distance.</li>
<li dir="auto">It illustrates that a heuristic is only a hint: when map geometry contradicts the straight-line estimate, pure greedy search pays for its lack of memory — which is exactly the case A<em> was designed to handle.<br />
</em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Greedy Best-First Search.md" class="internal-link">Greedy Best-First Search</a>*</li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do residual back-edges make the greedy augmenting-path approach correct?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A greedy first augmenting path can commit flow to an edge that a better solution would not use, and capacity constraints then block the optimum.</li>
<li dir="auto">Each unit of forward flow <code>u → v</code> creates a residual back-arc <code>v → u</code> of equal residual capacity.</li>
<li dir="auto">A later augmenting path can traverse that back-arc to retract and reroute the earlier flow — an undo operation.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is what lets the loop keep going until no <code>s → t</code> path remains; without back-edges the algorithm can wedge strictly below the maximum, so the back-edge is the whole reason greedy augmentation is provably optimal.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Maximum Flow.md" class="internal-link">Maximum Flow</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">State the max-flow min-cut theorem and explain how to recover the minimum cut.</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">An <code>s</code>-<code>t</code> cut splits vertices into <code>S</code> (with <code>s</code>) and <code>T</code> (with <code>t</code>); its capacity is the total capacity of edges crossing <code>S → T</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Any flow value is at most any cut capacity (weak duality); the theorem says the maximum flow equals the minimum cut capacity.</li>
<li dir="auto">After the algorithm halts, let <code>S</code> be the vertices reachable from <code>s</code> in the final residual graph; the original edges from <code>S</code> to <code>T</code> are the min cut and are all saturated.</li>
<li dir="auto">This gives a checkable optimality certificate and turns max-flow into a min-cut solver, which is exactly how segmentation and project-selection problems are attacked.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Maximum Flow.md" class="internal-link">Maximum Flow</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Edmonds–Karp <code>O(V·E²)</code> while Ford–Fulkerson is only <code>O(E · f*)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Ford–Fulkerson bounds iterations by the flow value <code>f*</code> because each augmentation adds at least one unit — a bound that blows up with large capacities and fails entirely on irrational ones.</li>
<li dir="auto">Edmonds–Karp always augments along the shortest (fewest-edge) path via BFS.</li>
<li dir="auto">Under shortest-path augmentation the BFS distance from <code>s</code> to any vertex never decreases, and each edge can be the bottleneck only <code>O(V)</code> times, capping augmentations at <code>O(V·E)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">With <code>O(E)</code> per BFS that is <code>O(V·E²)</code>, crucially <strong>independent of capacity magnitudes</strong> — the reason it is preferred whenever capacities are large or non-integer.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Maximum Flow.md" class="internal-link">Maximum Flow</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are Kruskal's and Prim's both guaranteed to find an optimal MST?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Both rely on the <strong>cut property</strong>: for any partition (cut) of the vertices, the minimum-weight edge crossing the cut belongs to some MST. Kruskal's adds the globally lightest edge that joins two components (a valid cut); Prim's adds the lightest edge leaving the current tree (the cut between tree and non-tree). Each step is provably "safe," so the greedy result is optimal.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Minimum Spanning Tree.md" class="internal-link">Minimum Spanning Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose Kruskal's over Prim's?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Kruskal's shines on <strong>sparse</strong> graphs and when the input is already an edge list — its cost is the edge sort plus near-constant Union-Find operations. Prim's, using a priority queue over adjacency lists, is better on <strong>dense</strong> graphs where E approaches V². Both are O(E log V)-ish, so the deciding factor is graph density and representation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Minimum Spanning Tree.md" class="internal-link">Minimum Spanning Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does Kruskal's produce on a disconnected graph?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A minimum spanning <strong>forest</strong> — one MST per connected component — because no edges bridge the components. It will add fewer than V−1 edges total; detecting that shortfall is how you discover the graph isn't connected.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Minimum Spanning Tree.md" class="internal-link">Minimum Spanning Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does Kosaraju's second DFS run on the transposed graph in decreasing finish order?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The first DFS's finish times give a reverse topological order of the <em>condensation</em> DAG; the last-finishing vertex sits in a source SCC.</li>
<li dir="auto">Transposing every edge turns that source into a sink — a component you can enter but never leave.</li>
<li dir="auto">Starting the second DFS from the highest-finish vertex therefore stays trapped inside one SCC instead of leaking into others.</li>
<li dir="auto">Peeling sinks off the reversed graph one DFS tree at a time is what makes each tree exactly one component — drop either the transpose or the finish order and the isolation guarantee collapses.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Strongly Connected Components.md" class="internal-link">Strongly Connected Components</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">In Tarjan's algorithm, what does <code>low[v] == disc[v]</code> mean, and why does it identify an SCC root?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><code>disc[v]</code> is <code>v</code>'s discovery time; <code>low[v]</code> is the smallest discovery time reachable from <code>v</code>'s subtree via tree edges and one back/cross edge to an on-stack vertex.</li>
<li dir="auto">Equality means nothing in <code>v</code>'s subtree found a route back to a vertex discovered before <code>v</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">So <code>v</code> is the entry point of its component; popping the stack down to <code>v</code> yields exactly that SCC.</li>
<li dir="auto">This single comparison replaces Kosaraju's whole second pass — the reason Tarjan needs only one traversal.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Strongly Connected Components.md" class="internal-link">Strongly Connected Components</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the condensation of a digraph always a DAG, and what does that buy you?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Each SCC is a maximal mutually reachable set, so a cycle between two distinct components would make them one component — contradiction.</li>
<li dir="auto">With no cycles, the condensation admits a topological order.</li>
<li dir="auto">That lets you run DAG-only techniques (topological sort, DAG dynamic programming, 2-SAT implication solving) on graphs that originally had cycles.</li>
<li dir="auto">SCC decomposition is therefore the standard "cycle-removal" preprocessing that unlocks the whole toolbox of DAG algorithms on arbitrary digraphs.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Strongly Connected Components.md" class="internal-link">Strongly Connected Components</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a topological sort only exist for a DAG?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A valid order requires every edge <code>u → v</code> to place <code>u</code> before <code>v</code>. A cycle <code>a → b → … → a</code> would require <code>a</code> before <code>b</code> and <code>b</code> before <code>a</code> simultaneously — impossible. So the order exists <strong>iff</strong> the graph is acyclic, which is why both algorithms double as cycle detectors.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Topological Sort.md" class="internal-link">Topological Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does Kahn's algorithm detect a cycle?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It only ever enqueues nodes whose in-degree has reached 0 (all dependencies resolved). Nodes inside a cycle never reach in-degree 0, so they're never emitted. If the produced order contains fewer than V nodes, the leftover nodes form (or depend on) a cycle.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Topological Sort.md" class="internal-link">Topological Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Is the topological order unique?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Generally no. Whenever multiple nodes have no outstanding dependencies, any of them may come next, so most DAGs admit many valid orders. To get a deterministic or "smallest" order, replace Kahn's queue with a priority queue keyed by your tie-break rule.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Graph Algorithms/Topological Sort.md" class="internal-link">Topological Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Paradigms</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What distinguishes backtracking from plain brute force?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Brute force generates every complete candidate and then checks it. Backtracking builds candidates incrementally and <strong>prunes</strong> — the instant a partial candidate violates a constraint, it abandons that entire subtree without expanding it. Same worst case, but pruning often removes the overwhelming majority of the search space in practice.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Backtracking.md" class="internal-link">Backtracking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must you "un-choose" after recursing?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Backtracking shares one mutable state (the current partial solution / used-set) across all branches to avoid copying. After exploring a choice you must revert it so the next sibling branch starts from the correct state. Skipping the undo leaks state between branches and produces wrong results.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Backtracking.md" class="internal-link">Backtracking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is backtracking the wrong tool?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the problem is an <em>optimisation</em> with overlapping subproblems (use DP — exponentially faster) or when a provable greedy choice exists (faster still). Backtracking shines for <em>enumeration</em> and <em>constraint satisfaction</em> where you genuinely must search configurations, and where pruning makes the exponential worst case rare in practice.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Backtracking.md" class="internal-link">Backtracking</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does branch-and-bound differ from backtracking?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Both explore a tree of partial solutions and prune subtrees, but on different criteria.</li>
<li dir="auto"><a class="internal-link" href="Backtracking">Backtracking</a> prunes a branch when it becomes <strong>infeasible</strong> — the partial solution already violates a constraint.</li>
<li dir="auto">Branch-and-bound prunes a branch that is feasible but whose <strong>optimistic bound cannot beat the incumbent</strong> — the best complete solution found so far.</li>
<li dir="auto">So backtracking answers "does a valid configuration exist / enumerate them," while B&amp;B answers "which valid configuration is <em>optimal</em>."</li>
<li dir="auto">The practical upshot: the moment your problem has an objective function to optimise, you want a bound, and plain backtracking leaves that pruning power on the table.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Branch and Bound.md" class="internal-link">Branch and Bound</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must the bounding function be optimistic, and how does that mirror A* search?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The bound is an estimate of the best achievable in a subtree; pruning happens when that estimate is no better than the incumbent.</li>
<li dir="auto">If the bound ever under-estimates (for maximisation), a subtree containing the true optimum could be pruned — giving a wrong answer.</li>
<li dir="auto">So the bound must be <strong>admissible</strong>: never worse than the real subtree optimum — the exact same condition as an <a class="internal-link" href="A-Start Search" aria-label="A-Start Search" data-tooltip-position="top">A* Search</a> heuristic never overestimating the remaining cost.</li>
<li dir="auto">Both algorithms are guided tree searches whose correctness depends on an estimate that only ever errs in the optimistic direction; B&amp;B is essentially A* over an optimisation decision tree.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is why you can safely borrow LP relaxations as bounds: an optimal fractional solution provably dominates the integer one, so it can never lie pessimistically.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Branch and Bound.md" class="internal-link">Branch and Bound</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Does branch-and-bound change the complexity class of NP-hard problems?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">No — the worst case remains exponential; on adversarial inputs the bound prunes nothing and it degenerates to brute-force enumeration.</li>
<li dir="auto">It improves the constant factor and the typical case, often by orders of magnitude, which is why real MIP solvers (CPLEX, Gurobi) rely on it.</li>
<li dir="auto">A tight bound and a strong early incumbent (often a <a class="internal-link" href="Greedy Algorithms" aria-label="Greedy Algorithms" data-tooltip-position="top">greedy</a> seed) prune more subtrees and shrink the practical runtime.</li>
<li dir="auto">The takeaway for a real decision: B&amp;B buys you <em>practicality and a certificate of optimality</em>, not polynomial time — so on very large instances, budget for a heuristic fallback rather than a proven optimum.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Branch and Bound.md" class="internal-link">Branch and Bound</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">State the Master Theorem and apply it to merge sort and Karatsuba.</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">For <code>T(n) = a·T(n/b) + f(n)</code>, compare <code>f(n)</code> with <code>n^(log_b a)</code> (the leaf work).</li>
<li dir="auto">Case 1 (<code>f</code> smaller): <code>Θ(n^(log_b a))</code>; Case 2 (<code>f</code> equal): <code>Θ(n^(log_b a) · log n)</code>; Case 3 (<code>f</code> larger, regular): <code>Θ(f(n))</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Merge sort is <code>2T(n/2)+O(n)</code>: <code>log_2 2 = 1</code>, <code>f(n)=n</code> matches, so Case 2 gives <code>O(n log n)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Karatsuba is <code>3T(n/2)+O(n)</code>: <code>log_2 3 ≈ 1.585</code>, <code>f(n)=n</code> is smaller, so Case 1 gives <code>O(n^1.585)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">The count of subproblems <code>a</code> sits in the <em>exponent</em>, not a constant factor — which is why dropping one recursive multiply (4→3) is a genuine asymptotic win, not a micro-optimisation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Divide and Conquer.md" class="internal-link">Divide and Conquer</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does memoising merge sort buy nothing while memoising Fibonacci buys everything?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Merge sort's subproblems are <strong>disjoint</strong>: each recursive call sorts a distinct slice of the array exactly once, so there is no repeated work to cache.</li>
<li dir="auto">Naive Fibonacci's subproblems <strong>overlap</strong>: <code>fib(n-1)</code> and <code>fib(n-2)</code> both recompute <code>fib(n-3)</code> and below, exponentially many times.</li>
<li dir="auto">Caching only helps when the same subproblem recurs — that is the definition of <a class="internal-link" href="Dynamic Programming">Dynamic Programming</a> versus plain divide-and-conquer.</li>
<li dir="auto">So the decision to memoise is not about the recursive <em>shape</em> (both look identical) but about whether subproblems are shared — get this wrong and you either waste memory or miss an exponential speedup.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Divide and Conquer.md" class="internal-link">Divide and Conquer</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does divide-and-conquer parallelise so naturally, and where does the base-case cutoff fit?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The subproblems are disjoint, so they share no mutable state and can run on separate cores with no locking — the model behind fork/join and map-style GPU kernels.</li>
<li dir="auto">The recursion depth is only <code>O(log n)</code>, so the tree is wide and shallow — lots of independent work to spread out.</li>
<li dir="auto">The base-case cutoff exists because per-call overhead dominates on tiny inputs, so real implementations defer small slices to an iterative algorithm like <a class="internal-link" href="Insertion Sort">Insertion Sort</a>.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is why production sorts (<a class="internal-link" href="Introsort">Introsort</a>, <a class="internal-link" href="Tim Sort">Tim Sort</a>) are hybrids: divide-and-conquer for the asymptotics, insertion sort for the constant factor — the paradigm is a scaffold, not the whole algorithm.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Divide and Conquer.md" class="internal-link">Divide and Conquer</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What two properties must a problem have for dynamic programming to apply?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Optimal substructure</strong> (an optimal solution is built from optimal solutions of subproblems) and <strong>overlapping subproblems</strong> (the same subproblem is solved repeatedly). Without overlap, plain divide-and-conquer (like merge sort) is enough — there's nothing to cache. Without optimal substructure, combining sub-answers doesn't yield the global optimum.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Dynamic Programming.md" class="internal-link">Dynamic Programming</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the difference between memoisation and tabulation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Both store subproblem results to avoid recomputation. <strong>Memoisation</strong> is top-down: recurse naturally and cache on the way, computing only the states you actually need. <strong>Tabulation</strong> is bottom-up: iteratively fill a table from base cases in dependency order, with no recursion and easier space optimisation. They produce the same answers; the choice is about overhead, stack depth, and memory.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Dynamic Programming.md" class="internal-link">Dynamic Programming</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is DP related to greedy and divide-and-conquer?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">All three break a problem into subproblems. <strong>Divide-and-conquer</strong> subproblems are <em>independent</em> (no overlap) so no caching helps. <strong>DP</strong> subproblems <em>overlap</em>, so you cache. <strong>Greedy</strong> is a special case where a single local choice is provably optimal, so you don't even explore alternatives — when greedy works it's faster than DP, but it works far less often.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Dynamic Programming.md" class="internal-link">Dynamic Programming</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does greedy fail for general coin change but succeed for standard currency?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Standard coin systems are <em>canonical</em> — each coin is large enough that taking as many of the biggest as possible is always optimal, provable by exchange argument. Arbitrary systems break that: for {1,3,4} making 6, greedy takes 4 then 1+1 (3 coins), but 3+3 (2 coins) is better. The greedy-choice property simply doesn't hold, so you need DP.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Greedy Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Greedy Algorithms</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you prove a greedy algorithm is correct?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Usually an <strong>exchange argument</strong>: assume an optimal solution differs from the greedy one, then show you can swap one of its choices for the greedy choice without making it worse — repeating until it becomes the greedy solution. This proves greedy is at least as good as any optimal solution. The "greedy stays ahead" technique (induct that greedy's partial solution is never behind) is a common variant.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Greedy Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Greedy Algorithms</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">For activity selection, why is "earliest finish time" the right greedy key?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Choosing the activity that finishes earliest leaves the maximum remaining time for everything else, and an exchange argument shows any optimal schedule's first activity can be replaced by the earliest-finishing one without reducing the count. Other keys (shortest duration, earliest start) lack this property and can be beaten by counter-examples.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Paradigms/Greedy Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Greedy Algorithms</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Patterns</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What precondition makes binary search on the answer valid, and how do you check it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The feasibility predicate must be <strong>monotonic</strong>: <code>feasible(x)</code> transitions exactly once from false to true (or true to false) as <code>x</code> increases.</li>
<li dir="auto">That single boundary is what binary search locates; if feasibility oscillates there is no boundary to find.</li>
<li dir="auto">Verify it by argument, not code: show a larger candidate can only make the condition easier to satisfy (more capacity never needs more days).</li>
<li dir="auto">Skipping this proof is the classic failure — the search still runs and returns a confident but wrong answer, so monotonicity is the first thing to establish, before touching the template.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Binary Search on Answer.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search on Answer</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the complexity <code>O(log(range) · C)</code> and not tied to input size the usual way?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Each step halves the <em>answer</em> range <code>[lo, hi]</code>, so it takes <code>log(hi − lo)</code> iterations.</li>
<li dir="auto">Every iteration runs the <code>feasible</code> check, costing <code>C</code> (often <code>O(n)</code> — a single greedy pass over the input).</li>
<li dir="auto">So total cost is <code>O(log(range) · C)</code>; for Koko that's <code>O(log(maxPile) · n)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">The log is over the numeric range, so even a <code>10^18</code>-wide answer space is only ~60 checks — which is why this beats DP whenever the checker is cheap relative to computing the optimum directly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Binary Search on Answer.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search on Answer</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you handle a real-valued (floating-point) answer without an epsilon comparison?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Don't loop until <code>hi - lo &lt; eps</code>: the correct eps is problem-specific and a too-tight value can stall on floating-point rounding.</li>
<li dir="auto">Instead run a <strong>fixed iteration count</strong> — around 100 iterations shrinks the range by <code>2^100</code>, far past double precision.</li>
<li dir="auto">Return <code>lo</code> (or <code>(lo + hi) / 2</code>) after the fixed loop.</li>
<li dir="auto">This trades a negligible amount of extra work for guaranteed termination and no precision-tuning, which matters because eps bugs are silent and hard to reproduce.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Binary Search on Answer.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search on Answer</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does <code>x &amp; (x - 1)</code> clear the lowest set bit, and what is it used for?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Subtracting 1 flips the lowest set bit to 0 and turns all the zeros below it into ones; AND-ing with the original keeps everything above unchanged and wipes that low region. Repeatedly applying it visits exactly the set bits — Brian Kernighan's popcount runs in O(number of set bits). It also gives the one-liner power-of-two test (<code>(x &amp; (x-1)) == 0</code>).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Bit Manipulation.md" class="internal-link">Bit Manipulation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is XOR ideal for finding a single unpaired element?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">XOR is commutative and self-inverse: <code>a ^ a = 0</code> and <code>a ^ 0 = a</code>. XOR-ing every element together cancels each pair to 0, leaving only the element that has no partner — in O(n) time and O(1) space, with no hash set.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Bit Manipulation.md" class="internal-link">Bit Manipulation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the difference between an arithmetic and a logical right shift?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">An arithmetic shift (<code>&gt;&gt;</code> on signed types) preserves the sign by copying the sign bit into the high positions, so negative numbers stay negative. A logical shift (<code>&gt;&gt;</code> on unsigned types, or <code>&gt;&gt;&gt;</code> in C# 11) fills with zeros. Picking the wrong one corrupts results when the high bit is set.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Bit Manipulation.md" class="internal-link">Bit Manipulation</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is cyclic sort <code>O(n)</code> despite the nested <code>while</code> that can re-process an index?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Each swap moves at least one value into its permanent home, and a placed value never leaves.</li>
<li dir="auto">There are only <code>n</code> values, so there are at most <code>n</code> swaps across the whole run.</li>
<li dir="auto">The outer walk contributes another <code>n</code> steps, so total work is <code>O(n)</code>, not <code>O(n²)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">The nested-loop shape is misleading — amortising over the "each swap finalises a value" invariant is what proves linearity, which is the standard interview follow-up.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Cyclic Sort.md" class="internal-link">Cyclic Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What precondition does cyclic sort require, and why can't you use it on arbitrary arrays?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The values must be a permutation of <code>1..n</code> (or <code>0..n−1</code>) so that every value maps to exactly one home index.</li>
<li dir="auto">That value-equals-index mapping is what lets you place elements without any comparisons.</li>
<li dir="auto">On arbitrary integers there is no home index, so the swap target is undefined and the method breaks.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is why the pattern is unlocked specifically by "n numbers in the range 1..n" wording — the range constraint is not incidental, it is the whole mechanism.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Cyclic Sort.md" class="internal-link">Cyclic Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">For finding a duplicate, when do you use cyclic sort versus fast-and-slow pointers?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Both are <code>O(n)</code> time and <code>O(1)</code> extra space.</li>
<li dir="auto">Cyclic sort rearranges the array in place, so it needs write access to the input.</li>
<li dir="auto">If the array is <strong>read-only</strong>, cyclic sort is disallowed; <a class="internal-link" href="Fast and Slow Pointers">Fast and Slow Pointers</a> treats indices as a linked list and detects the cycle without writing.</li>
<li dir="auto">The deciding factor is whether you may mutate the input — a constraint interviewers add specifically to force the pointer solution.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Cyclic Sort.md" class="internal-link">Cyclic Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must the fast and slow pointers meet if and only if there is a cycle?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The fast pointer gains exactly one step on the slow pointer every iteration.</li>
<li dir="auto">With no cycle the path ends, so the fast pointer reaches <code>null</code> and the loop stops without a meeting.</li>
<li dir="auto">With a cycle both pointers eventually enter it; thereafter the gap between them shrinks by one each step, modulo the cycle length, so it inevitably reaches zero.</li>
<li dir="auto">This biconditional is what makes the test sound: a meeting proves a cycle and running off the end proves acyclicity, with no false positives either way.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Fast and Slow Pointers.md" class="internal-link">Fast and Slow Pointers</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">After the pointers meet, why does resetting one to the head and stepping both find the cycle entry?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">At the meeting, slow has gone <code>d</code> steps and fast <code>2d</code>, and their difference <code>d</code> is a whole number of cycle lengths <code>k * lambda</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">The meeting node is therefore <code>d - mu</code> steps into the cycle, where <code>mu</code> is the head-to-entry distance.</li>
<li dir="auto">A pointer restarted at the head reaches the entry after <code>mu</code> steps; the other, stepped <code>mu</code> times from <code>d - mu</code> into the cycle, lands at <code>d = k * lambda</code>, which is the entry again modulo <code>lambda</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Understanding this is what lets you re-derive the second phase under interview pressure instead of hoping you remembered "reset to head" correctly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Fast and Slow Pointers.md" class="internal-link">Fast and Slow Pointers</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you prefer a hash-set visited approach over fast and slow pointers?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Both detect cycles in <code>O(n)</code> time; the set costs <code>O(n)</code> space, Floyd costs <code>O(1)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">The set trivially gives you the <em>set</em> of visited nodes and the entry (the first repeat) without a second phase.</li>
<li dir="auto">Fast/slow needs the extra entry-finding phase but touches no extra memory and never mutates the structure.</li>
<li dir="auto">So the deciding factor is space and mutability: pick Floyd when memory is tight or the data is read-only, and the hash set only when you already need the visited nodes for another purpose.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Fast and Slow Pointers.md" class="internal-link">Fast and Slow Pointers</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does sorting by start make a single linear sweep sufficient to merge overlapping intervals?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">After sorting by start, any interval that can overlap <code>current</code> must begin at or before <code>current.end</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">So a single comparison <code>next.start &lt;= current.end</code> decides overlap — no need to look further ahead or backward.</li>
<li dir="auto">Non-overlapping intervals appear as a gap, at which point <code>current</code> is final and can be emitted.</li>
<li dir="auto">This turns an <code>O(n²)</code> all-pairs overlap check into one <code>O(n)</code> pass after an <code>O(n log n)</code> sort, which is why the sort — not the sweep — is the cost that matters.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Merge Intervals.md" class="internal-link">Merge Intervals</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the sweep-line / delta-counting method find the minimum number of meeting rooms?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Emit <code>+1</code> at every start and <code>−1</code> at every end, then sort all events by coordinate.</li>
<li dir="auto">Walk the events keeping a running sum; the sum at any point is the number of simultaneously active meetings.</li>
<li dir="auto">The maximum the running sum reaches is the minimum number of rooms required.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tie handling is load-bearing: process ends before starts at equal coordinates for half-open intervals, or a meeting ending exactly as another begins will wrongly demand a second room.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Merge Intervals.md" class="internal-link">Merge Intervals</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the interval convention (closed vs half-open) change the answer, and how do you handle it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It decides whether touching endpoints count as overlap: <code>[1,2]</code> and <code>[2,3]</code> merge if closed, stay separate if half-open.</li>
<li dir="auto">The convention maps directly to the comparison operator — <code>&lt;=</code> for closed, <code>&lt;</code> for half-open.</li>
<li dir="auto">Meeting-room problems usually want half-open so back-to-back bookings share resources.</li>
<li dir="auto">Getting this wrong produces off-by-one errors that pass small tests and fail on boundary-touching cases, so state the convention explicitly before coding.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Merge Intervals.md" class="internal-link">Merge Intervals</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a monotonic stack O(n) when it looks like a nested loop?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The outer loop visits each index once and the inner loop only pops.</li>
<li dir="auto">Every index is pushed exactly once and can be popped at most once, so total pops across the whole run are bounded by <code>n</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">The inner "pop while" therefore does at most <code>n</code> work summed over all outer iterations, not <code>n</code> work per iteration.</li>
<li dir="auto">This amortised bound is the whole reason to reach for the pattern: it converts an obvious <code>O(n^2)</code> nearest-greater scan into <code>O(n)</code>, which is the difference between usable and not on large inputs.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Monotonic Stack and Queue.md" class="internal-link">Monotonic Stack and Queue</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why store indices rather than values on a monotonic stack?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The comparison only needs the value, which you can fetch as <code>a[index]</code> at any time.</li>
<li dir="auto">Many target problems (daily temperatures, histogram width, trapping rain water) ask for a <em>distance</em> or <em>width</em> between positions.</li>
<li dir="auto">Distance is <code>i - j</code>; with only values on the stack the positions are gone and the answer is unrecoverable.</li>
<li dir="auto">Defaulting to indices costs nothing and keeps every distance-based variant reachable, so it is the safer habit even when a given problem needs only the value.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Monotonic Stack and Queue.md" class="internal-link">Monotonic Stack and Queue</a></em></li>
</ul>
