</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can't a plain sliding window give the maximum of every window in O(n)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A sliding window maintains an aggregate updated as elements enter and leave.</li>
<li dir="auto">A sum supports removal — subtract the departing element — so it updates in <code>O(1)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">A maximum has no inverse operation: when the current max leaves, you do not know the next-largest without rescanning.</li>
<li dir="auto">A monotonic deque solves this by keeping only "still possibly maximal" indices in decreasing order, so the front is the max and each index enters and leaves once, preserving <code>O(n)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Monotonic Stack and Queue.md" class="internal-link">Monotonic Stack and Queue</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can prefix sums plus a hash map count sub-arrays summing to k when a sliding window cannot?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A range sum is <code>run_j - run_{i-1}</code>, so a range ending at <code>j</code> hits <code>k</code> whenever an earlier prefix equalled <code>run_j - k</code> — a hash-map lookup, no ordering assumed.</li>
<li dir="auto">A sliding window instead grows and shrinks a contiguous range, which only works if the sum moves monotonically as the window expands.</li>
<li dir="auto">Negative numbers break that monotonicity: a longer window can become valid again, so there is no correct shrink rule.</li>
<li dir="auto">When the array can contain negatives, or you must <em>count</em> all qualifying ranges rather than find one, prefix + hash map is the correct tool and the window is simply wrong, not just slower.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Prefix Sum.md" class="internal-link">Prefix Sum</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does a 2D prefix sum answer a rectangle query in O(1), and why the four terms?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Precompute <code>P[i][j]</code> as the sum of everything from the origin to <code>(i-1, j-1)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">A rectangle sum is <code>P[r2+1][c2+1] - P[r1][c2+1] - P[r2+1][c1] + P[r1][c1]</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">The two subtractions strip off the region above and the region to the left of the target rectangle; those overlap in the top-left corner, so it gets removed twice and the final term adds it back.</li>
<li dir="auto">This inclusion–exclusion is what lets integral images (Viola–Jones face detection) evaluate box features in constant time regardless of box size.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Prefix Sum.md" class="internal-link">Prefix Sum</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you abandon prefix sums for a Fenwick or segment tree?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Prefix sums assume the array is static; any element update invalidates every prefix from that index onward.</li>
<li dir="auto">Rebuilding is <code>O(n)</code>, so interleaving <code>q</code> updates with queries degrades to <code>O(nq)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">A Fenwick tree supports point update and prefix query both in <code>O(log n)</code>, and a segment tree extends that to range updates and arbitrary associative range queries.</li>
<li dir="auto">The deciding question is mutability: read-only data keeps prefix sums (unbeatable <code>O(1)</code> queries); any writes between queries mean the log-factor structure is the correct trade.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Prefix Sum.md" class="internal-link">Prefix Sum</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a sliding window O(n) and not O(n·k)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because each step only adds the entering element and removes the leaving one — O(1) — instead of re-summing all k elements. Across the whole pass <code>right</code> advances n times and <code>left</code> advances at most n times, so total work is O(n) regardless of window size.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Sliding Window.md" class="internal-link">Sliding Window</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does the sliding-window approach break, and what replaces it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the validity constraint isn't monotonic with window growth — most commonly <strong>arrays with negative numbers</strong> for sum-based problems, where a longer window can flip from invalid back to valid. There you use <strong>prefix sums + a hash map</strong> (to find ranges with a given sum) rather than a growing/shrinking window.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Sliding Window.md" class="internal-link">Sliding Window</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you get the maximum of every fixed window in O(n)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use a <strong>monotonic deque</strong> of indices kept in decreasing value order: push the new index (popping smaller values off the back) and pop the front when it slides out of the window. The front is always the current window's max, giving O(1) amortised per step.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Sliding Window.md" class="internal-link">Sliding Window</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">To find the <code>k</code> largest elements, why do you keep a min-heap rather than a max-heap?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The size-<code>k</code> min-heap's root is the <em>smallest</em> of the <code>k</code> best elements seen so far — the weakest survivor.</li>
<li dir="auto">A new element only matters if it beats that weakest survivor, which is exactly the <code>O(1)</code> root comparison a min-heap gives you.</li>
<li dir="auto">When it does, you pop the root (evict the weakest) and push the newcomer, keeping the heap at size <code>k</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">A max-heap would expose the <em>strongest</em> element at the root, which you never want to evict — so the polarity is inverted from most people's first instinct, and getting it right is the whole trick.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Top-K Elements.md" class="internal-link">Top-K Elements</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the streaming heap <code>O(n log k)</code> and when does that beat sorting?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The heap never exceeds <code>k</code> elements, so each push/pop is <code>O(log k)</code>, not <code>O(log n)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Over <code>n</code> elements the total is <code>O(n log k)</code> time and <code>O(k)</code> space.</li>
<li dir="auto">This beats a full sort's <code>O(n log n)</code> whenever <code>k ≪ n</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">More importantly it needs only <code>O(k)</code> memory, so it is the only option when the input is a stream too large to hold — the real reason to reach for it beyond the constant-factor speedup.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Top-K Elements.md" class="internal-link">Top-K Elements</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is Quickselect's complexity, and how do you eliminate its worst case?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Quickselect partitions like quicksort but recurses into only the side holding the K-th position, giving <code>O(n)</code> average time because the problem shrinks geometrically.</li>
<li dir="auto">Its worst case is <code>O(n²)</code>, triggered by consistently bad pivots (e.g. sorted input with a naive pivot).</li>
<li dir="auto">A randomised pivot makes the bad case improbable; median-of-medians guarantees <code>O(n)</code> worst case at a higher constant factor.</li>
<li dir="auto">Choose randomisation for typical workloads and median-of-medians only when adversarial input or hard real-time bounds demand a guarantee — the same trade <a class="internal-link" href="Quick Sort" aria-label="Quick Sort" data-tooltip-position="top">introsort</a> makes.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Top-K Elements.md" class="internal-link">Top-K Elements</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the converging two-pointer two-sum require a sorted array?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The decision "move left vs move right" relies on knowing that increasing <code>left</code> only increases the sum and decreasing <code>right</code> only decreases it — i.e. monotonicity, which sortedness guarantees. On unsorted data that invariant is gone, so the pointer moves can skip the actual answer.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Two Pointers.md" class="internal-link">Two Pointers</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do fast and slow pointers detect a cycle in a linked list?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Advance <code>slow</code> by one node and <code>fast</code> by two each step (Floyd's tortoise-and-hare). If there's a cycle, <code>fast</code> eventually laps and meets <code>slow</code>; if <code>fast</code> reaches the end (<code>null</code>), the list is acyclic. It's O(n) time and O(1) space — no visited set needed.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Two Pointers.md" class="internal-link">Two Pointers</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is two pointers different from a sliding window?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Both move indices forward in O(n), but a sliding window maintains a <em>contiguous sub-range and an aggregate over it</em> (sum, distinct count), expanding/shrinking to satisfy a constraint. Two pointers typically compares or rearranges individual elements (pair sum, partition, merge). Tracking a running window metric ⇒ sliding window; comparing/partitioning ends ⇒ two pointers.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Patterns/Two Pointers.md" class="internal-link">Two Pointers</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Search Algorithms</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Aho-Corasick's search cost independent of the number of patterns?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">All patterns share one trie, so common prefixes collapse into shared paths and the text drives a single walk through the automaton.</li>
<li dir="auto">Each text character causes one goto transition (plus failure-link hops that are amortized <code>O(1)</code> overall, same argument as KMP), regardless of how many patterns exist.</li>
<li dir="auto">Output links let a single state report every pattern that ends there, so multiple simultaneous matches cost only the <code>z</code> matches emitted, not a re-scan per pattern.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is exactly why intrusion-detection engines like Snort and <code>fgrep -f</code> use it: they match thousands of signatures against a stream in one <code>O(n+z)</code> pass instead of thousands of separate scans.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Aho-Corasick.md" class="internal-link">Aho-Corasick</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are failure links and output links, and what breaks if you omit each?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A failure link points from a state (string <code>s</code>) to the state for the longest proper suffix of <code>s</code> that is still a prefix of some pattern — the trie-wide version of KMP's <code>lps</code> — and drives correct transitions on a mismatch.</li>
<li dir="auto">Omitting failure links breaks the automaton entirely: on a miss it has no way to resume without rescanning the text.</li>
<li dir="auto">Output links chain a state to shorter patterns that also end there (like <code>he</code> ending inside <code>she</code>), and walking them enumerates all simultaneous matches.</li>
<li dir="auto">Omitting output links keeps the scan correct in position but silently misses every nested pattern — the classic bug of dropping <code>he</code> when matching <code>{he, she, hers}</code> in <code>"ushers"</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Aho-Corasick.md" class="internal-link">Aho-Corasick</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is the automaton built, and why does the order matter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Insert all patterns into a trie, then fill failure and output links with a breadth-first traversal from the root.</li>
<li dir="auto">BFS processes nodes in increasing depth, so a node's failure target — always at a strictly smaller depth — is already finalized when computed.</li>
<li dir="auto">A node's failure link is found by following its parent's failure link and taking the child on the same edge character, recursing toward the root until one exists; its output link is the nearest failure-reachable pattern-ending node.</li>
<li dir="auto">Getting the order wrong (e.g. DFS) would reference unfinished failure links and produce a broken automaton, which is why the level-by-level guarantee is load-bearing, not incidental.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Aho-Corasick.md" class="internal-link">Aho-Corasick</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does binary search require sorted data?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The half-split decision depends on monotonic ordering.</li>
<li dir="auto">Without sorting, <code>a[mid] &lt; target</code> gives no guarantee about where the target can be.</li>
<li dir="auto">Binary search can skip over the target on unsorted input and return false negatives.</li>
<li dir="auto">The O(log n) speed is conditional on the sorted precondition — if data arrives unsorted you must pay an O(n log n) sort first or fall back to a linear scan, so assert or document the requirement.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Binary Search.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you find the first occurrence of a duplicated value?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">On equality, store <code>mid</code> as a candidate answer instead of returning immediately.</li>
<li dir="auto">Continue searching the left half by setting <code>right = mid - 1</code> to look for an earlier match.</li>
<li dir="auto">Keep the same loop condition and overflow-safe midpoint calculation.</li>
<li dir="auto">Return the stored candidate after the loop ends.</li>
<li dir="auto">This variant never early-exits, so it does slightly more work than a plain search in exchange for well-defined behavior on duplicates — which you need whenever first/last-match semantics matter.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Binary Search.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why use <code>mid = left + (right - left) / 2</code> instead of <code>(left + right) / 2</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><code>(left + right)</code> can exceed the integer maximum on large arrays, wrapping to a negative index.</li>
<li dir="auto"><code>left + (right - left) / 2</code> computes the same midpoint without ever forming the oversized sum.</li>
<li dir="auto">This exact bug shipped in the JDK's binary search for years before being caught.</li>
<li dir="auto">The safe form reads slightly less obviously but is correct across the full index range, so prefer it over the textbook average in any production code.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Binary Search.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does Boyer-Moore scan the pattern right-to-left, and how does that make it sublinear?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Comparing from the pattern's last character means a mismatch reveals a text character that, if absent from the pattern, lets the whole pattern jump past it in one move.</li>
<li dir="auto">The bad-character rule can therefore skip up to <code>m</code> characters per mismatch, so the algorithm inspects only about <code>n/m</code> characters on typical text — <code>O(n/m)</code> best case.</li>
<li dir="auto">Left-to-right scanning could never justify a jump larger than one on the same information, because it learns nothing about the characters ahead.</li>
<li dir="auto">The bigger the alphabet, the more often the mismatching character is absent from the pattern, so Boyer-Moore's advantage scales up precisely on the large-alphabet text (English, source code) that real find tools face.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Boyer-Moore.md" class="internal-link">Boyer-Moore</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What do the bad-character and good-suffix rules each contribute, and why do most implementations drop the good-suffix rule?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The bad-character rule shifts to align the pattern's rightmost copy of the mismatching text character, giving big skips on large alphabets.</li>
<li dir="auto">The good-suffix rule reuses the suffix that already matched, aligning it with another occurrence inside the pattern, which helps on repetitive patterns the bad-character rule handles poorly.</li>
<li dir="auto">The algorithm takes the larger of the two shifts, so it is never worse than either alone.</li>
<li dir="auto">The good-suffix table's index arithmetic is error-prone for a small real-world gain, so production code (<code>grep</code>, <code>memmem</code>) usually ships Boyer-Moore-Horspool with only the bad-character rule.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Boyer-Moore.md" class="internal-link">Boyer-Moore</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you not use Boyer-Moore, and what would you use instead?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">On small alphabets (DNA, binary) its skips shrink to near one, erasing the advantage — prefer <a class="internal-link" href="KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm" aria-label="KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm" data-tooltip-position="top">KMP</a> or a bit-parallel Shift-Or.</li>
<li dir="auto">When input can be adversarial and you need a guaranteed linear bound, plain Boyer-Moore's <code>O(nm)</code> worst case is unacceptable — use KMP, or add Galil's rule to recover <code>O(n)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">When matching many patterns at once, running Boyer-Moore per pattern is <code>O(k·n)</code> — <a class="internal-link" href="Aho-Corasick">Aho-Corasick</a> scans once regardless of pattern count.</li>
<li dir="auto">The rule of thumb: Boyer-Moore is the default for one literal pattern over a large alphabet in benign text; step away from it the moment the alphabet is tiny, the input is hostile, or the patterns are plural.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Boyer-Moore.md" class="internal-link">Boyer-Moore</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you choose BFS over DFS?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/DFS BFS.md" class="internal-link">DFS BFS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can recursive DFS cause stack overflow and how do you fix it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/DFS BFS.md" class="internal-link">DFS BFS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you detect cycles using DFS in a directed graph?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/DFS BFS.md" class="internal-link">DFS BFS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is exponential search <code>O(log i)</code> rather than <code>O(log n)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The doubling phase stops as soon as <code>bound</code> reaches or passes the target's position <code>i</code>, after about <code>log i</code> steps.</li>
<li dir="auto">The binary search then covers a range of size at most <code>i/2</code>, another <code>O(log i)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Neither phase ever looks at the whole array, so the cost tracks the target's position, not the array length.</li>
<li dir="auto">This makes it strictly better than binary search when the target is near the front (<code>i ≪ n</code>) and no worse when it is near the end.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Exponential Search.md" class="internal-link">Exponential Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What lets exponential search work on an unbounded or unknown-length array?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Binary search needs <code>n</code> to compute a midpoint, so it cannot even start without knowing the length.</li>
<li dir="auto">Exponential search only ever asks "is <code>a[bound]</code> still below the target?", probing indices it generates itself.</li>
<li dir="auto">The doubling stops the moment a probe reaches or exceeds the target, bounding a finite range without ever needing the total size.</li>
<li dir="auto">That is why it fits streams, lazily materialized lists, and indexable-but-unmeasurable sources where binary search is inapplicable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Exponential Search.md" class="internal-link">Exponential Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Where does exponential search appear in a real production system?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Timsort's merge step switches into "galloping mode" when one run keeps winning the comparison.</li>
<li dir="auto">Galloping is exponential search: it doubles ahead to find how many elements to copy from the winning run in one go.</li>
<li dir="auto">This turns a long stretch of one-at-a-time comparisons into <code>O(log k)</code> work for a skip of <code>k</code> elements.</li>
<li dir="auto">It is why Timsort is fast on partially ordered real-world data — the same front-loaded advantage exponential search gives on lookups.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Exponential Search.md" class="internal-link">Exponential Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does interpolation search beat binary search only on uniform data?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Its probe assumes values grow linearly with index, so the interpolated position is accurate only when gaps between values are even.</li>
<li dir="auto">On uniform data each probe shrinks the candidate count to its square root, giving <code>O(log log n)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">On skewed data the estimate is consistently wrong, the range barely shrinks, and it degrades to <code>O(n)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">So the choice is a bet on the distribution: right, it is dramatically faster; wrong, it is worse than the binary search you gave up — measure before committing.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Interpolation Search.md" class="internal-link">Interpolation Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What input makes interpolation search degrade to O(n), and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Exponentially growing keys such as <code>1, 2, 4, 8, …, 2^k</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">The interpolation formula weights by value magnitude, so for a target in the large tail it estimates a position near the far end and near <code>lo</code> for small targets, moving the boundary by roughly one element per probe.</li>
<li dir="auto">With <code>O(1)</code> progress per step over <code>n</code> elements the total work is <code>O(n)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is why adversarial or naturally heavy-tailed data (timestamps, frequencies) is a hard no for interpolation search.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Interpolation Search.md" class="internal-link">Interpolation Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Where does the division by zero come from and how do you handle it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The probe divides by <code>a[hi] − a[lo]</code>, the value span of the current range.</li>
<li dir="auto">When every value in the range is equal — a run of duplicates or a collapsed one-element range — that span is zero.</li>
<li dir="auto">Dividing by it throws or yields a nonsensical index.</li>
<li dir="auto">Guard it: detect <code>a[lo] == a[hi]</code>, resolve the block with a direct equality check, and return — the same defensive habit as the overflow-safe midpoint in binary search.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Interpolation Search.md" class="internal-link">Interpolation Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is <code>m = √n</code> the optimal block size for jump search?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The cost has two parts: <code>n/m</code> jumps to reach the target's block and up to <code>m</code> steps to scan it.</li>
<li dir="auto">Total cost is <code>f(m) = n/m + m</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Minimizing gives <code>f′(m) = −n/m² + 1 = 0</code>, so <code>m = √n</code> and the total is <code>2√n = O(√n)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Larger blocks over-lengthen the scan; smaller blocks over-multiply the jumps — <code>√n</code> balances the two phases exactly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Jump Search.md" class="internal-link">Jump Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would jump search ever beat binary search, given it is asymptotically slower?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Binary search probes scattered indices across the whole array — cheap on RAM, expensive where random access is not <code>O(1)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Jump search only ever steps forward, and its single backward movement is a bounded scan of at most <code>√n</code> adjacent elements.</li>
<li dir="auto">On tape, singly linked structures, or streamed/paged sources where forward reads are cheap but seeking back is costly, that bounded locality beats binary search's jumping around.</li>
<li dir="auto">On any ordinary array it loses — the win exists only under the expensive-backward-seek cost model.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Jump Search.md" class="internal-link">Jump Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does jump search require sorted data, and what breaks without it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The jump phase relies on <code>a[block] &lt; target</code> implying the target cannot be before that block — that inference needs monotonic ordering.</li>
<li dir="auto">On unsorted data an early block might straddle the target's value by accident, so a passing jump gives no guarantee about what lies behind it.</li>
<li dir="auto">The algorithm would skip past the target and the final block scan would look in the wrong place.</li>
<li dir="auto">Like binary search, its speed is conditional on the sorted precondition; on unsorted input fall back to <a class="internal-link" href="Linear Search">Linear Search</a>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Jump Search.md" class="internal-link">Jump Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does the prefix function (LPS array) encode and why does it matter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm.md" class="internal-link">KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is KMP worth the implementation complexity over naive search?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm.md" class="internal-link">KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does KMP compare to Rabin-Karp for single-pattern matching?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm.md" class="internal-link">KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is linear search the <em>right</em> choice over binary search?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The data is unsorted and will be searched only once — sorting first costs <code>O(n log n)</code>, more than the single <code>O(n)</code> scan it would enable.</li>
<li dir="auto"><code>n</code> is tiny (a handful of elements), where constant factors and cache behavior dominate asymptotic order.</li>
<li dir="auto">The structure has no cheap random access (a singly linked list), so binary search cannot compute a midpoint in <code>O(1)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">You need to find <em>all</em> matches or apply a predicate, not just one exact key.</li>
<li dir="auto">In each case the precondition binary search needs (sorted, random-access) is absent or not worth establishing, so the simpler scan wins in practice.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Linear Search.md" class="internal-link">Linear Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a linear scan of an array beat an asymptotically faster tree search?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A contiguous array is walked in strict address order, which the CPU prefetcher predicts perfectly, so most accesses hit cache.</li>
<li dir="auto">A balanced tree stores nodes across the heap; each step follows a pointer to an unpredictable address, often causing a cache miss.</li>
<li dir="auto">For small <code>n</code> the <code>O(log n)</code> node count is small, so the miss penalty per node outweighs the reduced number of comparisons.</li>
<li dir="auto">Big-O ignores the constant factor of a memory access, which can span two orders of magnitude between L1 and main memory — so measure on realistic sizes before assuming the lower-complexity structure is faster.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Linear Search.md" class="internal-link">Linear Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the average number of comparisons for a successful vs unsuccessful linear search?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Successful search over <code>n</code> elements averages <code>(n + 1) / 2</code> comparisons, assuming each position is equally likely to hold the target.</li>
<li dir="auto">Unsuccessful search always performs all <code>n</code> comparisons before concluding the target is absent.</li>
<li dir="auto">This means "not found" is the true worst case and it happens on every miss, not rarely.</li>
<li dir="auto">Budget capacity and latency for the full <code>O(n)</code>; the halved average only applies to guaranteed hits with uniform positions.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Linear Search.md" class="internal-link">Linear Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the rolling hash achieve O(1) window updates?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Rabin Karp Search.md" class="internal-link">Rabin Karp Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is character-by-character verification necessary on hash match?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Rabin Karp Search.md" class="internal-link">Rabin Karp Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you choose Rabin-Karp over KMP?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Rabin Karp Search.md" class="internal-link">Rabin Karp Search</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the first decision before picking a search algorithm?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Check whether data is sorted, because that immediately enables Binary Search.</li>
<li dir="auto">Identify data shape: array, graph, or text stream, because each has specialized methods.</li>
<li dir="auto">Decide whether worst-case guarantees or average speed matters more.</li>
<li dir="auto">Checking these preconditions first avoids picking an algorithm whose assumptions your data violates — the most common source of wrong or slow searches.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Search Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Search Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is one search algorithm never best for all cases?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Different algorithms optimize for different constraints such as ordering, memory, and preprocessing.</li>
<li dir="auto">Workload shape changes the winner: single lookup, repeated queries, or many patterns.</li>
<li dir="auto">Correctness constraints can force specific methods, for example sorted input for Binary Search.</li>
<li dir="auto">Every choice trades preprocessing and memory against query speed; the senior move is to weigh those for the actual workload instead of reaching for a default.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Search Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Search Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does preprocessing (sorting or indexing) pay off versus a plain linear scan?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A one-off search over unsorted data is just O(n) — sorting first (O(n log n)) would cost more than it saves.</li>
<li dir="auto">Once many queries hit the same data, a single sort or index build is amortized across all of them and each query drops to O(log n) or O(1).</li>
<li dir="auto">Indexes (hash maps, B-trees) trade memory and write cost for fast reads.</li>
<li dir="auto">Preprocessing front-loads cost and memory to make repeated queries cheap, so justify it by query volume, not by instinct.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Search Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Search Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is ternary search the wrong choice for looking up a value in a sorted array?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Each level of ternary search spends two comparisons to shrink the range to one third, costing <code>2·log₃ n ≈ 1.82·ln n</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Binary search spends one comparison to shrink to one half, costing <code>log₂ n ≈ 1.44·ln n</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Both are <code>O(log n)</code>, but ternary's constant factor is strictly larger — more work for the same result.</li>
<li dir="auto">The three-way split buys nothing on monotone data; reserve ternary search for unimodal extremum finding, where binary search does not apply at all.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Ternary Search.md" class="internal-link">Ternary Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can ternary search find a unimodal maximum when binary search cannot?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Binary search needs a single comparison at one point to decide which half to keep, which requires a monotone ordering.</li>
<li dir="auto">A unimodal function is not monotone, so one probe cannot tell you which side of the peak you are on.</li>
<li dir="auto">Two probes <code>f(m1)</code> and <code>f(m2)</code> do: whichever is smaller lies on the far side of the peak, so that third is safe to discard.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is why the algorithm is the tool for one-dimensional convex optimization and geometric extremum problems, not for ordered lookups.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Ternary Search.md" class="internal-link">Ternary Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is golden-section search and when does it beat plain ternary search?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Golden-section search places the two probes at the golden ratio so one probe lands exactly where a probe is needed next iteration.</li>
<li dir="auto">That reused evaluation cuts the cost from two function calls per step to one.</li>
<li dir="auto">The convergence rate per step is slightly slower, but with half the evaluations it wins on total function calls.</li>
<li dir="auto">It matters when <code>f</code> is expensive — a simulation or physical measurement — and is irrelevant when <code>f</code> is a cheap array read.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Ternary Search.md" class="internal-link">Ternary Search</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does <code>z[i]</code> mean, and how does the Z-box keep the whole computation linear?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><code>z[i]</code> is the length of the longest substring starting at index <code>i</code> that is also a prefix of the string.</li>
<li dir="auto">The Z-box <code>[l, r]</code> is the match interval with the largest <code>r</code> known to equal a prefix; inside it, <code>z[i]</code> can often be copied from the mirror position <code>z[i-l]</code> with zero comparisons.</li>
<li dir="auto">Naive character comparisons only occur when extending past <code>r</code>, and each such comparison either fails or pushes <code>r</code> one step right; since <code>r</code> never moves left and caps at <code>n</code>, total work is <code>O(n)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is the same amortized argument as KMP's failure function, which is why the two carry equivalent power — the Z-array just expresses it as a forward-looking prefix match rather than a recursive fallback.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Z-Algorithm.md" class="internal-link">Z-Algorithm</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you use the Z-algorithm to find a pattern in text, and why the separator?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Concatenate <code>S = P + sep + T</code> where <code>sep</code> is a sentinel absent from both strings, then compute the Z-array of <code>S</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Every index <code>i</code> inside the <code>T</code> region with <code>z[i] == m</code> (the pattern length) marks an exact occurrence of <code>P</code> at that spot.</li>
<li dir="auto">The separator caps every Z-value at <code>m</code> so no match can straddle the boundary between pattern and text, keeping occurrences exact.</li>
<li dir="auto">This gives deterministic <code>O(n+m)</code> matching with no collision risk — the trade versus KMP is extra space for the concatenated string against, for many people, a more intuitive mental model.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Z-Algorithm.md" class="internal-link">Z-Algorithm</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose the Z-algorithm over KMP or Rabin-Karp?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Over <a class="internal-link" href="Rabin Karp Search" aria-label="Rabin Karp Search" data-tooltip-position="top">Rabin-Karp</a>: when you need an unconditional linear bound and want to avoid hash tuning and collision verification.</li>
<li dir="auto">Over <a class="internal-link" href="KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm" aria-label="KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) Algorithm" data-tooltip-position="top">KMP</a>: when the problem is naturally about prefixes of the whole string (periodicity, longest common prefix per suffix, occurrence counting), where the Z-array reads off the answer directly.</li>
<li dir="auto">Against both for pure in-place text search on huge text: KMP wins on memory because it stores only the pattern's <code>O(m)</code> table instead of an <code>O(n+m)</code> array.</li>
<li dir="auto">The practical driver is rarely speed (all three are linear here) but clarity and memory — reach for Z when its prefix framing matches the problem, KMP when space is tight.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Search Algorithms/Z-Algorithm.md" class="internal-link">Z-Algorithm</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Sorting Algorithms</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does Counting Sort beat the <code>O(n log n)</code> comparison lower bound?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The <code>Ω(n log n)</code> bound applies only to sorts whose only operation is comparing two elements — there are <code>n!</code> possible orderings and each comparison yields one bit.</li>
<li dir="auto">Counting Sort never compares elements; it uses each key's value directly as an array index, reading the key's structure instead of ranking pairs.</li>
<li dir="auto">That is why it achieves <code>O(n + k)</code> linear time.</li>
<li dir="auto">The escape is not free: it only works when keys are integers over a bounded range, so the speedup is a trade of generality for a domain assumption — always check <code>k = O(n)</code> before assuming the win holds.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Counting Sort.md" class="internal-link">Counting Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must the placement pass iterate the input backwards?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">After the prefix sum, <code>count[v]</code> holds the index one past the last slot reserved for key <code>v</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Decrementing before each write and consuming the input from the tail places the last equal element in the highest slot and earlier ones below it, preserving input order.</li>
<li dir="auto">A forward pass with the same scheme reverses equal keys, breaking stability.</li>
<li dir="auto">Stability is optional standalone but mandatory when Counting Sort is the digit pass inside LSD <a class="internal-link" href="Radix Sort">Radix Sort</a>, which is wrong without it — so the loop direction is a correctness requirement, not a style choice.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Counting Sort.md" class="internal-link">Counting Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does Counting Sort's memory cost make it the wrong choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Space and time are both <code>O(n + k)</code>, so the counter array scales with the key range, not the number of elements.</li>
<li dir="auto">Sorting a handful of values with keys up to <code>10^9</code> allocates a billion counters — gigabytes to sort ten numbers.</li>
<li dir="auto">The rule of thumb is to require <code>k = O(n)</code>; beyond that, <a class="internal-link" href="Radix Sort">Radix Sort</a> keeps each pass's range small, or a comparison sort avoids the range dependence entirely.</li>
<li dir="auto">Recognizing the <code>k</code>-dominates-<code>n</code> failure is what separates a linear-time win from an accidental out-of-memory crash in production.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Counting Sort.md" class="internal-link">Counting Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is bubble sort never used in production?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Bubble sort has O(n²) average and worst-case time with poor cache behavior — each swap touches two adjacent elements, causing many cache misses on large arrays. Insertion sort is strictly better for small arrays (same O(n²) but fewer comparisons and better cache access). For large arrays, introsort (Array.Sort in .NET) is O(n log n). Bubble sort has no scenario where it is the best choice.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Bubble Sort.md" class="internal-link">Bubble Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is bubble sort's one practical advantage?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The early-exit optimization gives O(n) best case on already-sorted input — the same as insertion sort. However, insertion sort achieves this with fewer comparisons and better cache behavior, so even in this case insertion sort is preferred. Bubble sort's main value is pedagogical: it is easy to explain and visualize.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Bubble Sort.md" class="internal-link">Bubble Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Bucket Sort only <code>O(n)</code> on average, and what breaks that?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The scatter step is <code>O(1)</code> per element with no comparisons, so cost is dominated by the per-bucket inner sorts.</li>
<li dir="auto">With uniformly distributed keys and <code>k ≈ n</code> buckets, each bucket holds <code>O(1)</code> elements and every inner sort is <code>O(1)</code> expected, giving <code>O(n + k)</code> total.</li>
<li dir="auto">Skewed data piles elements into few buckets; a single dominant bucket makes its <a class="internal-link" href="Insertion Sort">Insertion Sort</a> <code>O(n^2)</code>, collapsing the average-case win.</li>
<li dir="auto">The uniformity precondition is the whole ballgame — assert it or bound the worst case with a comparison sort per bucket, because on real skewed data Bucket Sort silently degrades to quadratic.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Bucket Sort.md" class="internal-link">Bucket Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do Bucket Sort, Counting Sort, and Radix Sort relate?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">All three are non-comparison sorts that read key structure to beat the <code>Ω(n log n)</code> bound, differing in how they bucket.</li>
<li dir="auto"><a class="internal-link" href="Counting Sort">Counting Sort</a> uses one bucket per distinct integer key and needs no inner sort — it is Bucket Sort's degenerate case for small discrete ranges.</li>
<li dir="auto"><a class="internal-link" href="Radix Sort">Radix Sort</a> applies stable bucketing repeatedly, once per digit, which removes Bucket Sort's dependence on a uniform distribution.</li>
<li dir="auto">Choosing among them is really choosing which domain assumption you can afford: exact small integers (Counting), fixed-width keys of any distribution (Radix), or known-uniform continuous keys (Bucket).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Bucket Sort.md" class="internal-link">Bucket Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does Bucket Sort connect to sorting data that does not fit in memory?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Partitioning keys into ranged buckets is the same idea used by external merge sorts and the MapReduce / Spark shuffle: a partitioner routes each key to the bucket for its range.</li>
<li dir="auto">Each bucket is sorted independently — on disk, or on a separate reducer — and the sorted buckets concatenate in range order.</li>
<li dir="auto">The uniformity assumption resurfaces as data skew: a hot partition that holds most keys starves the others, exactly the one-dominant-bucket worst case at cluster scale.</li>
<li dir="auto">Understanding Bucket Sort's failure mode is what lets you diagnose and fix a skewed distributed shuffle, so the toy algorithm maps directly onto a real production bottleneck.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Bucket Sort.md" class="internal-link">Bucket Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a "turtle" and why does it make bubble sort slow?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A turtle is a small value near the <em>end</em> of the array; bubble sort's forward passes move small values leftward only one position per pass.</li>
<li dir="auto">So a small element stranded at the tail needs <code>O(n)</code> passes to reach the front, which is the dominant cost that keeps bubble sort at <code>O(n²)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Large values near the front ("rabbits") are not the problem — they move right quickly — the asymmetry is what hurts.</li>
<li dir="auto">Comb sort fixes exactly this by starting with a wide gap so a turtle jumps most of the way home in one comparison — naming the failure mode is more useful than the algorithm itself.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Comb Sort.md" class="internal-link">Comb Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does comb sort's shrinking gap relate to Shell sort's?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Both start with a large gap and shrink it to <code>1</code>, using the wide gaps to move elements a long way cheaply and the final <code>gap = 1</code> pass to finish.</li>
<li dir="auto">Comb sort applies the gap to <a class="internal-link" href="Bubble Sort">Bubble Sort</a>-style adjacent-comparison-and-swap; <a class="internal-link" href="Shell Sort">Shell Sort</a> applies it to <a class="internal-link" href="Insertion Sort">Insertion Sort</a>, which shifts a run of elements to open a hole.</li>
<li dir="auto">Insertion sort leaves each subsequence fully sorted per pass, so Shell sort carries less residual disorder forward and performs better.</li>
<li dir="auto">They are the same idea on two different bases, and the stronger base wins — which is why Shell sort, not comb sort, is the one that survives into real constrained-environment code.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Comb Sort.md" class="internal-link">Comb Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Is comb sort ever the right production choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Its worst case is still <code>O(n²)</code>, it is not stable, and typical performance only approximates <code>O(n log n)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto"><a class="internal-link" href="Shell Sort">Shell Sort</a> beats it with the same gap idea on a better base, and <a class="internal-link" href="Introsort">Introsort</a> / <a class="internal-link" href="Tim Sort">Tim Sort</a> beat both with guaranteed <code>O(n log n)</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Its one advantage is that it is trivially short and explains, in a few lines, <em>why</em> bubble sort is quadratic.</li>
<li dir="auto">Treat it as a teaching device, not a tool — if you find yourself typing comb sort into shipping code, you wanted a library sort instead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Comb Sort.md" class="internal-link">Comb Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does introsort use heap sort as a fallback instead of just using quicksort?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Quicksort averages O(n log n) with great cache behaviour but degrades to O(n²) on adversarial pivots. Introsort tracks recursion depth and, when it exceeds ~2·log(n) (a sign quicksort is going quadratic), switches the offending partition to heap sort — which guarantees O(n log n) in O(1) space. You get quicksort's speed normally and heap sort's safety in the worst case.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Heap Sort.md" class="internal-link">Heap Sort</a></em></p>
