</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is building the heap O(n) rather than O(n log n)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Bottom-up heapify sifts each node down only as far as its subtree height. Most nodes are near the leaves and barely move; the few near the root are rare. Summing height·count across levels converges to O(n), unlike inserting n elements one at a time (each up to O(log n)) which is O(n log n).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Heap Sort.md" class="internal-link">Heap Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is heap sort usually slower than quicksort despite the same average complexity?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Cache locality. Quicksort scans memory sequentially during partitioning, which the CPU cache loves. Heap sort's sift-down hops between a parent and its far-apart children (<code>2i+1</code>, <code>2i+2</code>), causing frequent cache misses on large arrays. Same Big-O, but a worse constant factor in practice.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Heap Sort.md" class="internal-link">Heap Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is insertion sort faster than O(n log n) algorithms?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">For small arrays (n ≤ 20–50), insertion sort outperforms merge sort and quick sort because it has no recursion overhead, no auxiliary memory allocation, and excellent cache locality. This is why Timsort and introsort switch to insertion sort for small partitions — the constant factor advantage outweighs the asymptotic disadvantage.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Insertion Sort.md" class="internal-link">Insertion Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is insertion sort used as the base case in Timsort?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Timsort (Python's and Java's default sort) uses insertion sort for runs shorter than ~64 elements. At that size, insertion sort's O(n²) worst case is bounded (64² = 4096 operations), its cache-friendly sequential access beats merge sort's random access pattern, and it requires no extra memory. The combination of O(n) best case on nearly-sorted data and low constant factors makes it ideal for the small-partition base case.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Insertion Sort.md" class="internal-link">Insertion Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does introsort remove quicksort's <code>O(n²)</code> worst case without losing its speed?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It tracks recursion depth and, once depth exceeds <code>2·⌊log₂ n⌋</code> — the fingerprint of pathologically unbalanced partitions — switches that partition to <a class="internal-link" href="Heap Sort">Heap Sort</a>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Heap sort guarantees <code>O(n log n)</code> in <code>O(1)</code> space, so the worst case is capped while well-behaved partitions keep running as quicksort.</li>
<li dir="auto">Small partitions (~16) are deferred to a single final <a class="internal-link" href="Insertion Sort">Insertion Sort</a> pass to avoid recursion overhead on tiny ranges.</li>
<li dir="auto">The heap-sort branch is insurance that almost never fires, so you pay quicksort's fast, cache-friendly average case in practice while contractually eliminating the quadratic tail — which mattered because that tail was a real denial-of-service vector.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Introsort.md" class="internal-link">Introsort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does Java use Tim sort for objects but a quicksort variant for primitives?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Introsort and dual-pivot quicksort are not stable: their partition and fallback phases reorder equal keys.</li>
<li dir="auto">For primitives (<code>int</code>, <code>double</code>), two equal values are indistinguishable, so instability is unobservable and the faster, in-place, cache-friendly quicksort wins with no downside.</li>
<li dir="auto">For objects, equal keys can carry different identity or secondary fields, so callers rely on stable order — Java uses <a class="internal-link" href="Tim Sort">Tim Sort</a>, which is stable and adaptive, accepting its <code>O(n)</code> merge buffer.</li>
<li dir="auto">The split is a deliberate engineering trade: pay for stability only where it is observable, and buy raw speed everywhere it is not.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Introsort.md" class="internal-link">Introsort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a "quicksort killer" and why did it justify introsort becoming the default?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It is an input crafted so that a deterministic pivot rule (first, last, or even median-of-three) produces maximally unbalanced partitions at every level, forcing <code>O(n²)</code> time and <code>O(n)</code> stack depth.</li>
<li dir="auto">Because runtimes sort attacker-supplied data (HTTP params, JSON arrays), that quadratic path is a denial-of-service vector, not just a benchmark curiosity.</li>
<li dir="auto">Introsort's depth limit converts the attack into a harmless switch to heap sort, guaranteeing <code>O(n log n)</code> regardless of input.</li>
<li dir="auto">That is why <code>std::sort</code> and .NET <code>Array.Sort</code> standardized on introsort: the guarantee is a security property, not merely a performance nicety.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Introsort.md" class="internal-link">Introsort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is merge sort preferred over quick sort for linked lists?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Merge sort requires no random access — it only needs to traverse lists sequentially and re-link nodes. The merge step is O(1) extra space on linked lists (just pointer manipulation, no copy buffer). Quick sort's partitioning requires random access to swap elements by index, which is O(n) on a linked list. For linked list sorting, merge sort is O(n log n) time and O(1) extra space; quick sort degrades to O(n²) or requires O(n) extra space.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Merge Sort.md" class="internal-link">Merge Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does merge sort's O(n) space cost become a real problem?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When sorting large datasets in memory-constrained environments: sorting 1 GB of data requires another 1 GB merge buffer. For external sorting (data larger than RAM), merge sort's sequential access pattern is actually an advantage — it maps well to disk I/O. For in-memory sorting where stability is not required and memory is constrained, quick sort (in-place, O(log n) stack) is the better choice.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Merge Sort.md" class="internal-link">Merge Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What causes quick sort's O(n²) worst case and how does introsort prevent it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">O(n²) occurs when every pivot is the minimum or maximum of its partition — each partition step reduces the array by only one element. This happens on already-sorted or reverse-sorted input with a fixed pivot (first or last element). Introsort (.NET's Array.Sort) prevents it by switching to heapsort when recursion depth exceeds 2×log(n), guaranteeing O(n log n) worst case while keeping quick sort's cache-friendly average case.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Quick Sort.md" class="internal-link">Quick Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is quick sort faster than merge sort in practice despite the same O(n log n) average?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Quick sort is in-place — it requires only O(log n) stack space versus merge sort's O(n) auxiliary array. The in-place partitioning accesses memory sequentially, giving better CPU cache utilization. Merge sort's merge step requires copying between arrays, causing more cache misses. For in-memory sorting where stability is not required, quick sort's constant factor advantage typically makes it 20–40% faster than merge sort.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Quick Sort.md" class="internal-link">Quick Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must Radix Sort's per-digit sort be stable?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Each LSD pass sorts by one digit and trusts that ties on that digit are already ordered by the less-significant digits sorted in earlier passes.</li>
<li dir="auto">A stable inner sort preserves that established order among equal current-digit keys; an unstable one scrambles it.</li>
<li dir="auto">The result of an unstable inner sort is not "slightly off" — it is simply wrong.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is precisely why <a class="internal-link" href="Counting Sort">Counting Sort</a> (stable when it places elements in a backward pass) is the canonical inner loop, and why stability is a correctness requirement rather than a nice-to-have.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Radix Sort.md" class="internal-link">Radix Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you radix-sort signed integers and IEEE-754 floats?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Radix Sort treats keys as unsigned magnitudes, but signed and float encodings are not monotonic when read as unsigned bits.</li>
<li dir="auto">For signed integers, flip the sign bit (bias by <code>2^(bits-1)</code>) so the range becomes non-negative and ordered, then undo it after sorting.</li>
<li dir="auto">For floats, flip all bits of negative values and only the sign bit of positives, which makes the raw bit patterns compare as unsigned integers in numeric order; reverse the transform afterward.</li>
<li dir="auto">This bit trick is what lets GPU and database radix sorts handle real numeric columns in a single unsigned pass — without it the "linear sort" quietly returns garbage.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Radix Sort.md" class="internal-link">Radix Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the base <code>b</code> affect Radix Sort's cost, and how do you choose it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Cost is <code>O(d · (n + b))</code> where <code>d = floor(log_b(maxKey)) + 1</code>, so a larger <code>b</code> shrinks <code>d</code> (fewer passes) but grows the <code>O(b)</code> counter array per pass.</li>
<li dir="auto">Too-large <code>b</code> blows the counter table out of cache and wastes memory; too-small <code>b</code> means many passes over the data.</li>
<li dir="auto">Byte-wise radix (<code>b = 256</code>, four passes for 32-bit keys) keeps the 256-entry table in cache — a common sweet spot.</li>
<li dir="auto">Choosing <code>b</code> is the practical lever that decides whether Radix Sort actually beats an <code>O(n log n)</code> sort or just loses to cache misses.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Radix Sort.md" class="internal-link">Radix Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does selection sort make exactly O(n) swaps, and when does that matter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Selection sort scans the entire unsorted suffix to find the minimum, then performs exactly one swap per iteration — n−1 swaps total. This matters when writes are significantly more expensive than reads, such as flash memory with limited write cycles or EEPROM. In those cases, minimizing swaps is worth the O(n²) comparison cost. For RAM-based sorting, the swap count advantage is irrelevant — use insertion sort or Array.Sort instead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Selection Sort.md" class="internal-link">Selection Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is selection sort not stable, and how can it be made stable?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Selection sort swaps the minimum element into position, which can move an equal element past another equal element. For example, sorting [3a, 3b, 1] by value: the 1 swaps with 3a, giving [1, 3b, 3a] — the relative order of the two 3s is reversed. To make it stable, replace the swap with a shift (like insertion sort does), but that eliminates the O(n) swap advantage. In practice, if stability is needed, use merge sort.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Selection Sort.md" class="internal-link">Selection Sort</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Shell sort faster than plain insertion sort even though the final pass is a full insertion sort?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Plain insertion sort only swaps adjacent elements, so an element <code>k</code> slots from its home takes <code>k</code> moves — <code>O(n²)</code> on reverse-sorted data.</li>
<li dir="auto">Large-gap passes let an element jump <code>h</code> positions per move, clearing most long-distance disorder cheaply and early.</li>
<li dir="auto">Insertion sort is <code>O(n)</code> on nearly-sorted input, so after the coarse passes the final <code>h = 1</code> pass has almost nothing left to move.</li>
<li dir="auto">The work is front-loaded into cheap coarse passes so the one expensive pass never actually gets expensive — that reshaping of <em>where</em> the work happens, not a better comparison, is the whole speedup.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Shell Sort.md" class="internal-link">Shell Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the choice of gap sequence change Shell sort's complexity class?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Each gap pass only guarantees the array is "<code>h</code>-sorted"; how much residual disorder survives into the next pass depends on how the gaps interleave positions.</li>
<li dir="auto">Shell's original <code>n/2^k</code> keeps all gaps even until the last, so odd and even indices never mix until <code>h = 1</code>, leaving <code>O(n²)</code> work.</li>
<li dir="auto">Hibbard's <code>2^k − 1</code> mixes positions and provably reaches <code>O(n^1.5)</code>; Ciura's tuned sequence is the best measured but has no proven bound.</li>
<li dir="auto">The gap sequence is a tunable parameter that moves the whole algorithm between <code>O(n²)</code> and <code>O(n^1.5)</code>, which is why Shell sort is really a <em>family</em> of algorithms rather than one — pick the sequence deliberately.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Shell Sort.md" class="internal-link">Shell Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you deploy Shell sort in real code today?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">In constrained environments — bootloaders, firmware, minimal C libraries like uClibc's <code>qsort</code> — where recursion depth, an <code>O(n)</code> merge buffer, and code size all matter.</li>
<li dir="auto">It is short, purely iterative, in place with <code>O(1)</code> space, and needs no auxiliary allocation, so it degrades gracefully under memory pressure.</li>
<li dir="auto">On a general-purpose machine it is beaten by <a class="internal-link" href="Introsort">Introsort</a> and <a class="internal-link" href="Tim Sort">Tim Sort</a>, which are asymptotically better and bounded.</li>
<li dir="auto">Choose it when the operating constraints, not the input size, are the binding limit — otherwise a library <code>O(n log n)</code> sort is the right default.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Shell Sort.md" class="internal-link">Shell Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you choose between Merge Sort and Quick Sort in production?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Merge sort gives reliable <code>O(n log n)</code> worst-case behavior and stable ordering.</li>
<li dir="auto">Quick sort is often faster in practice on in-memory arrays due to cache behavior.</li>
<li dir="auto">Quick sort has worst-case <code>O(n^2)</code> if pivot strategy is poor, so randomized or introspective variants are safer.</li>
<li dir="auto">Why it matters: this choice affects latency tail risk, memory usage, and correctness when stable ordering is required.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Sorting Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is Insertion Sort still a good choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It is strong on very small arrays because constant overhead is tiny.</li>
<li dir="auto">It performs well on nearly sorted data where shifts are minimal.</li>
<li dir="auto">It is commonly used as a base case inside hybrid production sort implementations.</li>
<li dir="auto">Why it matters: knowing this avoids overengineering and explains hybrid sort internals in interviews.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Sorting Algorithms</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does .NET's built-in Array.Sort use, and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">.NET uses an introspective sort (IntroSort): it starts with Quick Sort for fast average performance, switches to Heap Sort when recursion depth exceeds a threshold (to guarantee O(n log n) worst case), and uses Insertion Sort for small partitions (to exploit its low overhead on nearly-sorted data).<br />
This hybrid approach demonstrates why production sort implementations combine multiple algorithms rather than using a single one.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms.md" class="internal-link">Sorting Algorithms</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes Tim sort adaptive, and why does that matter for real data?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It scans for existing ascending or strictly-descending runs and merges them instead of halving blindly, so any order already present is reused for free.</li>
<li dir="auto">Descending runs are reversed in place; short runs are padded to <code>minrun</code> (32–64) with binary <a class="internal-link" href="Insertion Sort">Insertion Sort</a>; galloping mode block-copies when one run keeps winning.</li>
<li dir="auto">On already-sorted input it finds one run and runs in <code>O(n)</code>; worst case is still <code>O(n log n)</code>, stable throughout.</li>
<li dir="auto">Real inputs — append-mostly lists, time-ordered logs, pre-grouped records — are rarely random, so an adaptive sort beats a fixed <code>O(n log n)</code> sort on exactly the data production systems actually feed it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Tim Sort.md" class="internal-link">Tim Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the merge-stack invariants and why do they exist?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Runs are pushed on a stack; for the top three lengths <code>X, Y, Z</code> (Z deepest) Tim sort enforces <code>Z &gt; Y + X</code> and <code>Y &gt; X</code>, merging when either breaks.</li>
<li dir="auto">This keeps adjacent runs within a small size ratio, so each merge is between roughly equal-length runs.</li>
<li dir="auto">Balanced merges are what bound the total merge cost to <code>O(n log n)</code> — unbalanced runs would let merging degrade toward quadratic.</li>
<li dir="auto">The invariants are the correctness-and-performance contract of the algorithm, which is exactly why a gap in checking them (the 2015 bug) was both a crash and a performance risk, not a cosmetic flaw.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Tim Sort.md" class="internal-link">Tim Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What did formal verification reveal about Tim sort in 2015?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">de Gouw et al. attempted to prove the Java/Python merge-collapse routine correct and instead found the invariant was only checked on the top runs, leaving deeper violations possible.</li>
<li dir="auto">A crafted run-length pattern could break the invariant, and since the merge stack was sized assuming the invariant held, it could overflow and throw an out-of-bounds exception.</li>
<li dir="auto">The stopgap fix enlarged the stack; the invariant check was subsequently corrected to actually restore the property.</li>
<li dir="auto">It is a concrete case where code deployed to hundreds of millions of users for years still harbored an adversarially reachable crash — evidence that testing and ubiquity are not substitutes for a proof of a loop invariant.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Algorithms/Sorting Algorithms/Tim Sort.md" class="internal-link">Tim Sort</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="computer-science-data-structures">Data Structures</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a data structure? Which ones do you know? Which of them exist in .NET?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A data structure is a way to organize related data into a collection-like object. Examples include arrays, lists, queues, stacks, linked lists, dictionaries/hash tables, hash sets, graphs, and trees. .NET provides built-in implementations for many of these (for example <code>Array</code>, <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code>, <code>Queue&lt;T&gt;</code>, <code>Stack&lt;T&gt;</code>, <code>LinkedList&lt;T&gt;</code>, <code>Dictionary&lt;TKey, TValue&gt;</code>, <code>HashSet&lt;T&gt;</code>).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Data Structures.md" class="internal-link">Data Structures</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you choose between <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code>, <code>Dictionary&lt;TKey, TValue&gt;</code>, and <code>HashSet&lt;T&gt;</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> when you need ordered, index-based access and the primary operations are iteration or positional lookup. Use <code>Dictionary&lt;TKey, TValue&gt;</code> when you need fast lookup, insertion, and deletion by a unique key. Use <code>HashSet&lt;T&gt;</code> when you only need membership testing and set operations (union, intersection, difference) without associated values.<br />
The wrong choice shows up as O(n) scans that should be O(1) lookups.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Data Structures.md" class="internal-link">Data Structures</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does collection choice matter more than micro-optimization?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Switching from O(n) linear search to O(1) hash lookup reduces work by orders of magnitude at scale. No amount of loop unrolling or SIMD on the O(n) path matches that.<br />
Focus on algorithmic complexity first, then optimize constant factors within the chosen structure if profiling shows it matters.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Data Structures.md" class="internal-link">Data Structures</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you use <code>LinkedList&lt;T&gt;</code> over <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> in .NET?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Almost never in practice. <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> (backed by a contiguous array) has better cache locality, lower memory overhead per element, and faster iteration. <code>LinkedList&lt;T&gt;</code> only wins when you need frequent insertions/deletions in the middle of a very large collection and already hold a reference to the node.<br />
In most .NET code, <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> is the correct default.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Data Structures.md" class="internal-link">Data Structures</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Composite Structures</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does an LRU cache need a doubly linked list specifically?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Both operations must be O(1): <code>Get</code> moves a node to the head and <code>Put</code> evicts the tail. Moving or removing an arbitrary node in O(1) requires constant-time access to its neighbours, which only a doubly linked list provides. A singly linked list would need an O(n) scan to find the predecessor.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Composite Structures/LRU Cache.md" class="internal-link">LRU Cache</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do the hash map and linked list divide responsibility?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The hash map answers "where is key k?" in O(1) (random access by key). The linked list answers "what is the recency order?" and supports O(1) move-to-front and remove-from-tail (ordering). Neither structure alone gives both fast lookup <em>and</em> fast ordered eviction.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Composite Structures/LRU Cache.md" class="internal-link">LRU Cache</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is cache pollution and which policy resists it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A large one-off scan touches many items once, pushing the genuinely hot items out of an LRU cache even though they'll be needed again. Frequency-aware policies (LFU, TinyLFU/W-TinyLFU) resist it by favouring items accessed <em>often</em>, not merely <em>recently</em>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Composite Structures/LRU Cache.md" class="internal-link">LRU Cache</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Graph Structures</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is a disjoint set represented in memory?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">As a forest of trees stored in a flat <code>_parent</code> array: <code>_parent[i]</code> is the parent of element <code>i</code>, and a root points to itself. A second <code>_rank</code> (or <code>_size</code>) array holds the metadata used to keep trees shallow during merges. No linked nodes or per-element allocation — just two integer arrays.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Disjoint Set.md" class="internal-link">Disjoint Set</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does the "representative" of a set mean, and how do you get it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The representative is the root of an element's tree — the canonical element that identifies the whole set. You obtain it by calling <code>find(x)</code>, which walks up the parent chain to the root. Two elements are in the same set exactly when they share a representative.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Disjoint Set.md" class="internal-link">Disjoint Set</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why store the structure as arrays instead of linked tree nodes?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Arrays give O(1) index access, no per-node allocation, and excellent cache locality. Mapping elements to integers 0…n-1 lets <code>find</code>/<code>union</code> be simple array reads and writes, which is what makes the structure fast in practice.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Disjoint Set.md" class="internal-link">Disjoint Set</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does a disjoint set beat BFS for connectivity, and what do you give up?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When edges arrive over time and connectivity queries interleave with insertions: each union/find is O(α(n)) ≈ O(1), versus O(V + E) to re-traverse per query. You give up everything except component identity — no paths, no distances, no directed reachability, and merges are irreversible (no edge deletion).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Graph Structures.md" class="internal-link">Graph Structures</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Which collections are typically used for BFS?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>Queue&lt;T&gt;</code> for frontier and <code>HashSet&lt;T&gt;</code> for visited tracking.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Graph.md" class="internal-link">Graph</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When must you use Bellman–Ford instead of Dijkstra?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When edges can have negative weights. Dijkstra settles each node once on the assumption that a shorter path can never appear later — negative edges violate that. Bellman–Ford relaxes all edges V−1 times and can also report a negative cycle.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Graph.md" class="internal-link">Graph</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you detect a cycle in a directed graph during DFS?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Three-color marking: white (unvisited), grey (on the current recursion stack), black (fully explored). Encountering a grey node via an edge means a back-edge → a cycle. (Topological sort via Kahn's algorithm detects the same condition: leftover nodes with non-zero in-degree.)<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Graph Structures/Graph.md" class="internal-link">Graph</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Hash-based Structures</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a Bloom filter have false positives but never false negatives?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Adding an item sets specific bits to 1 and never clears any. So if a queried item's bits include a 0, it was definitely never added (false negatives impossible). But its k bits could all have been set to 1 by <em>other</em> items, producing a false "maybe present" — a false positive.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Bloom Filter.md" class="internal-link">Bloom Filter</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can't you delete an element from a standard Bloom filter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Bits are shared across elements. Clearing the bits for one item might clear a bit another present item relies on, which would then produce a false negative — violating the core guarantee. Counting Bloom filters fix this by storing counters you can decrement.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Bloom Filter.md" class="internal-link">Bloom Filter</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Where does a Bloom filter save the most work in practice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">As a cheap pre-check in front of an expensive lookup. A database checks the Bloom filter before reading an SSTable from disk: "definitely not present" lets it skip the I/O entirely, and only "maybe" requires the real read. Most absent-key queries are answered from RAM.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Bloom Filter.md" class="internal-link">Bloom Filter</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between <code>HashSet&lt;T&gt;</code> and <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> for membership checks?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>HashSet&lt;T&gt;.Contains</code> is O(1) average; <code>List&lt;T&gt;.Contains</code> is O(n).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Hash Set.md" class="internal-link">Hash Set</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can <code>HashSet&lt;T&gt;.Contains</code> fail for logically equal objects?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because hash/equality contracts are broken (for example, mismatched <code>GetHashCode</code>).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Hash Set.md" class="internal-link">Hash Set</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does putting a Bloom filter in front of a HashMap (or disk lookup) pay off?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When most queries are for <strong>absent</strong> keys and the authoritative lookup is expensive (disk read, network call, or a map too big for RAM). The filter answers "definitely not" from a few bits per element, skipping the expensive path; only "maybe" (true hits plus the ~1% false positives) pays full price. If most queries hit, the filter is pure overhead — every hit still does the real lookup.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Hash-based Structures.md" class="internal-link">Hash-based Structures</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What single failure mode degrades every hash-based structure at once?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Skewed hash distribution. A weak or non-uniform <code>GetHashCode</code> degrades all three: the map and set walk O(n) bucket chains, and the Bloom filter's false-positive rate inflates as correlated hashes concentrate bits. The hash-flooding <em>attack</em> specifically targets the bucketed structures — an attacker packs one chain with chosen keys.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/Hash-based Structures.md" class="internal-link">Hash-based Structures</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What data structure is a hash map, and why does it give O(1) average lookups?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Keys are mapped to buckets by hash code, with equality checks resolving collisions inside a bucket. Computing a hash and jumping straight to one bucket avoids scanning every element, so lookup cost is roughly constant regardless of size — negligible at 5 elements, enormous at 5 million. The cost is unordered iteration and a correctness dependency on the hash contract.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/HashMap.md" class="internal-link">HashMap</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can hash map performance degrade from O(1) to O(n)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Excessive collisions put many keys in the same bucket, so operations must compare more entries one by one. Poor <code>GetHashCode</code> distribution — or an attacker deliberately choosing colliding keys — concentrates entries and degrades the whole table toward a linear scan.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/HashMap.md" class="internal-link">HashMap</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a bad <code>GetHashCode</code> implementation create both correctness and performance risk?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Hash maps rely on the hash and equality contracts. If equal keys do not produce equal hashes, lookups can fail or silently duplicate entries (correctness). If hashes are poorly distributed, bucket chains grow and every operation slows (performance). A stronger hash spreads keys better but costs more per call — for adversarial input prefer collision-resistant hashing even at that cost.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Hash-based Structures/HashMap.md" class="internal-link">HashMap</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Linear Structures</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is array indexing O(1)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The runtime computes the element address directly as <code>base + i * elementSize</code> — a multiply and an add, independent of the index or the array length. This requires fixed-size elements and contiguous storage, which is exactly what an array guarantees.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Arrays.md" class="internal-link">Arrays</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does an array scan beat a linked-list traversal by 10× or more when both are O(n)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Contiguity. A cache line brings in many elements at once and the hardware prefetcher streams the next lines ahead of the scan, so most accesses hit L1 (~1 ns). Linked-list nodes are scattered allocations, so each <code>Next</code> can be a full main-memory miss (~100 ns) the prefetcher can't predict. Big-O counts operations; the constant factor here is memory latency.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Arrays.md" class="internal-link">Arrays</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does jagged <code>T[][]</code> usually outperform multidimensional <code>T[,]</code> in .NET?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each row of a jagged array is an SZ-array — the single-dimensional zero-based shape the runtime accesses via dedicated IL instructions and the JIT optimizes aggressively (including bounds-check elimination). <code>T[,]</code> uses slower generalized accessors with much weaker JIT support. The jagged layout pays an extra indirection and per-row allocations, which is why flattening to a 1D array is the next step for truly hot 2D code.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Arrays.md" class="internal-link">Arrays</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the array covariance trap?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>string[]</code> implicitly converts to <code>object[]</code> (a pre-generics design decision), so the type system lets you assign an <code>int</code> through the <code>object[]</code> view — and the runtime throws <code>ArrayTypeMismatchException</code> at the write. Keeping that check sound costs a runtime type check on every store into a reference-type array. Generic variance (<code>IEnumerable&lt;out T&gt;</code>) later fixed this properly by allowing covariance only on read-only surfaces.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Arrays.md" class="internal-link">Arrays</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you distinguish a full buffer from an empty one when head equals tail?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Three standard fixes: (1) keep an explicit <code>count</code>; (2) sacrifice one slot so "full" is <code>(tail + 1) % n == head</code> and "empty" is <code>head == tail</code>; or (3) use unbounded monotonic head/tail counters and compare/mask them. Each trades a little space or arithmetic for an unambiguous state.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Circular Buffer.md" class="internal-link">Circular Buffer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a circular buffer allocation-free and why does that matter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It writes into a pre-allocated fixed array and reuses slots via index wrap-around, so steady-state operation allocates nothing and creates no GC pressure. That matters on hot paths (audio, networking, high-frequency telemetry) where per-item allocation would cause latency spikes from garbage collection.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Circular Buffer.md" class="internal-link">Circular Buffer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you overwrite the oldest element instead of rejecting new writes?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Overwrite when you only care about the <em>most recent</em> N items and old data is disposable — debug ring logs, latest sensor readings, a frame buffer. Reject (or block/backpressure) when every item must be processed — a task queue — so you don't silently drop work.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Circular Buffer.md" class="internal-link">Circular Buffer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does a deque add over a stack and a queue, and when is that worth it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">O(1) insert and remove at both ends — a stack mutates only one end, a queue inserts at one and removes at the other. It's worth it when one algorithm needs both disciplines on the same sequence, like sliding-window maximum (push back, evict from back, expire from front) or a work-stealing queue (owner LIFO on one end, thieves FIFO on the other).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Deque.md" class="internal-link">Deque</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Ring buffer vs doubly-linked list as the deque backbone — how do you choose?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The ring buffer stores elements contiguously: zero per-item allocation, cache-friendly, O(1) ends with an occasional O(n) grow. The linked list gives O(1) ends with no resizes but pays a node allocation (~40 bytes of overhead on x64) per element and a potential cache miss per traversal step. Default to the ring buffer; take <code>LinkedList&lt;T&gt;</code> only for small/cold deques or when you also need O(1) removal of arbitrary nodes you hold references to.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Deque.md" class="internal-link">Deque</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the ThreadPool's work-stealing queue need to be a deque?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The owning thread pushes and pops LIFO at one end — the newest task's data is still hot in its cache. Idle threads steal FIFO from the <em>other</em> end — the oldest task, farthest from what the owner is touching. Separating owner and thief onto opposite ends is what keeps contention rare; a single-ended structure would put them on the same slot.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Deque.md" class="internal-link">Deque</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the sliding-window-maximum deque solution O(n) when each window looks like its own scan?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Amortization over the whole pass: each index is pushed onto the deque exactly once and popped at most once (from the back when dominated by a newer larger value, or from the front when it expires). Total deque operations are bounded by 2n regardless of window size, versus O(n·k) for rescanning every k-wide window.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Deque.md" class="internal-link">Deque</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> implemented under the hood?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It wraps an internal <code>T[]</code> buffer and tracks <code>Count</code> (logical size) separately from <code>Capacity</code> (array length).</li>
<li dir="auto">When <code>Count</code> would exceed <code>Capacity</code>, it allocates a new array of double the size, copies the elements, and drops the old one.</li>
<li dir="auto">Contiguous storage is what makes index access O(1) and iteration cache-friendly.</li>
<li dir="auto">The contiguous array gives speed and locality but makes mid-sequence inserts O(n) — that shaping decision is the whole reason to sometimes pick another structure.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Dynamic Array.md" class="internal-link">Dynamic Array</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between <code>Count</code> and <code>Capacity</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><code>Count</code> is the number of elements you have actually added.</li>
<li dir="auto"><code>Capacity</code> is how many the current backing array can hold before a resize is needed.</li>
<li dir="auto">You can raise <code>Capacity</code> ahead of time to avoid resizes; <code>Count</code> only changes via add/remove.</li>
<li dir="auto">A large pre-set <code>Capacity</code> removes resize copies but holds memory you may not use, so pre-size only when the count is roughly known.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Dynamic Array.md" class="internal-link">Dynamic Array</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do <code>Clear()</code> and <code>Remove()</code> affect <code>Capacity</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Both change only <code>Count</code>; the backing array (and thus <code>Capacity</code>) is left intact.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is deliberate — reusing the array avoids re-allocating if the list fills up again.</li>
<li dir="auto">To actually reclaim memory, call <code>TrimExcess()</code> or set <code>Capacity</code> explicitly.</li>
<li dir="auto">Keeping capacity speeds up refill but pins memory in long-lived lists that briefly spiked large — trim those explicitly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Dynamic Array.md" class="internal-link">Dynamic Array</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is <code>LinkedList&lt;T&gt;</code> often slower than <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> despite O(1) inserts/removes?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">CPU cache locality dominates real workloads: <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> keeps data contiguous and prefetch-friendly.</li>
<li dir="auto">Linked-list nodes are scattered on the heap, so every traversal step is a cache-missing pointer chase.</li>
<li dir="auto">The O(1) edit only helps if you <em>already hold the node</em> — finding it first is O(n) and erases the advantage.</li>
<li dir="auto">Big-O favors the linked list, but constant factors favor the array — trust measurements over asymptotics for in-memory n.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/LinkedList.md" class="internal-link">LinkedList</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is <code>LinkedList&lt;T&gt;</code> the right choice in .NET?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When your algorithm already stores <code>LinkedListNode&lt;T&gt;</code> handles and performs many inserts/removes around them (e.g. an LRU cache moving a node to the front).</li>
<li dir="auto">When splicing whole sublists by pointer is a core operation.</li>
<li dir="auto">When you must avoid the O(n) element shift that <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> pays for mid-sequence edits at very large sizes.</li>
<li dir="auto">You accept worse iteration speed and per-node memory overhead to get guaranteed O(1) local restructuring.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/LinkedList.md" class="internal-link">LinkedList</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a common signal to migrate from <code>LinkedList&lt;T&gt;</code> to <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">If code frequently searches by index or value <em>before</em> each edit, you are paying O(n) traversal repeatedly.</li>
<li dir="auto">That pattern means you are not actually exploiting held node handles — the linked list's only advantage.</li>
<li dir="auto">Iteration-heavy or random-access code is another clear signal.</li>
<li dir="auto">Switching to <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> improves locality and iteration but reintroduces O(n) mid-sequence inserts, so confirm those are rare before migrating.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/LinkedList.md" class="internal-link">LinkedList</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is <code>Span&lt;T&gt;</code> a <code>ref struct</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A <code>ref struct</code> is confined to the stack: it cannot be boxed, captured in a lambda, stored in a class field, or used as a generic type argument.</li>
<li dir="auto">This guarantees the span never outlives the buffer it points to (stack, <code>stackalloc</code>, or array), preserving memory safety.</li>
<li dir="auto">The same restriction is why it cannot cross <code>await</code> or <code>yield</code> — those would move it to the heap.</li>
<li dir="auto">You give up flexibility (no async, no fields) for zero-copy, allocation-free access, so it earns its place only on synchronous hot paths.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Span.md" class="internal-link">Span</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you choose <code>Memory&lt;T&gt;</code> instead of <code>Span&lt;T&gt;</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use <code>Memory&lt;T&gt;</code> when the buffer must survive an async boundary, be stored in a field, or live beyond one synchronous scope.</li>
<li dir="auto"><code>Memory&lt;T&gt;</code> is a heap-storable handle; call <code>.Span</code> to get a <code>Span&lt;T&gt;</code> view at the point of synchronous use.</li>
<li dir="auto"><code>Span&lt;T&gt;</code> remains the right choice for the tight synchronous parsing/slicing loop itself.</li>
<li dir="auto"><code>Memory&lt;T&gt;</code> buys async/storage flexibility at the cost of one extra indirection — use <code>Span&lt;T&gt;</code> where every nanosecond counts, <code>Memory&lt;T&gt;</code> where lifetime demands it.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Span.md" class="internal-link">Span</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between <code>Span&lt;T&gt;</code> and <code>ReadOnlySpan&lt;T&gt;</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><code>ReadOnlySpan&lt;T&gt;</code> is the same windowed view but forbids writes through it, so it can wrap immutable data such as <code>string</code> (as <code>ReadOnlySpan&lt;char&gt;</code>).</li>
<li dir="auto">Methods should accept <code>ReadOnlySpan&lt;T&gt;</code> when they only read, which makes them callable from the widest set of sources.</li>
