<li dir="auto"><code>Span&lt;T&gt;</code> converts implicitly to <code>ReadOnlySpan&lt;T&gt;</code>, but not the reverse.</li>
<li dir="auto">So accept <code>ReadOnlySpan&lt;T&gt;</code> in method signatures for reach and intent; use the mutable <code>Span&lt;T&gt;</code> only when you actually write back.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Span.md" class="internal-link">Span</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is an explicit <code>Stack&lt;T&gt;</code> better than recursion?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use it when graph/tree depth may be large or unbounded — an explicit stack lives on the heap and avoids a <code>StackOverflowException</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">It also gives you control over traversal state you can inspect, pause, or resume, which recursion hides in call frames.</li>
<li dir="auto">The traversal logic is identical: push instead of recurse, pop instead of return.</li>
<li dir="auto">It is more verbose than recursion, but it is the safe default whenever input depth is attacker- or data-controlled.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Stack.md" class="internal-link">Stack</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can <code>Stack&lt;T&gt;</code> be a poor fit for work queues?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Work queues usually need FIFO fairness so the oldest item is handled first.</li>
<li dir="auto">LIFO processing serves the newest item first, which can starve older items indefinitely under sustained load.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is a correctness/SLA bug, not a performance one — it passes tests but misbehaves in production bursts.</li>
<li dir="auto">A stack is simpler and cache-friendly, but choose <code>Queue&lt;T&gt;</code> (or a priority queue) the moment ordering guarantees matter.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Stack.md" class="internal-link">Stack</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the complexity of <code>Push</code> and why is it not always constant in practice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><code>Push</code> is <strong>amortized</strong> O(1): most pushes just write a slot and bump the counter.</li>
<li dir="auto">When the backing array is full, <code>Push</code> allocates a doubled array and copies all elements — that individual call is O(n).</li>
<li dir="auto">Doubling makes any sequence of n pushes cost O(n) total, so the average stays O(1).</li>
<li dir="auto">Pre-size with <code>new Stack&lt;T&gt;(capacity)</code> when the count is known to flatten those resize spikes on latency-sensitive paths.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Stack.md" class="internal-link">Stack</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is <code>Queue&lt;T&gt;</code> suitable for BFS?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">BFS must visit nodes in order of increasing distance — all of layer <em>k</em> before any of layer <em>k+1</em>.</li>
<li dir="auto">A FIFO queue naturally enforces that: neighbors enqueued earlier (closer) are dequeued earlier.</li>
<li dir="auto">Swapping in a stack would turn the traversal into DFS, changing the result.</li>
<li dir="auto">The queue guarantees correct level order, but its frontier can hold a whole layer of the graph — a memory cost you accept when shortest-path-by-hops correctness matters.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Queue.md" class="internal-link">Queue</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you replace <code>Queue&lt;T&gt;</code> with <code>PriorityQueue&lt;TElement, TPriority&gt;</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When correctness depends on priority rather than arrival time — Dijkstra, schedulers, SLA-driven dispatch.</li>
<li dir="auto">A FIFO queue would serve a low-priority older item ahead of an urgent newer one.</li>
<li dir="auto"><code>PriorityQueue</code> keeps a heap so the smallest key is always dequeued first.</li>
<li dir="auto">It moves you from O(1) enqueue/dequeue to O(log n) heap operations, so pay it only when ordering by priority is actually required.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Queue.md" class="internal-link">Queue</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a queue be a production reliability problem even if operations are O(1)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Per-operation complexity says nothing about system-level throughput.</li>
<li dir="auto">If producers persistently outpace consumers, the queue grows without bound — memory climbs and latency spikes as items wait longer.</li>
<li dir="auto">This is a flow-control problem, not an algorithmic one.</li>
<li dir="auto">An unbounded in-memory queue maximizes ingest but risks OOM; a bounded queue/channel adds backpressure that protects the system at the cost of rejecting or blocking producers.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Linear Structures/Queue.md" class="internal-link">Queue</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Trees</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can AVL reads be faster than red-black reads?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">AVL trees keep a stricter height bound, so searches often traverse fewer levels, at the cost of more rotations during writes.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/AVL Tree.md" class="internal-link">AVL Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the AVL balance factor invariant, and where is it checked?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">For every node, height(left) − height(right) must stay in {−1, 0, +1}. After each insert or delete you walk back up the modified path recomputing heights; the first node at ±2 gets rotated.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/AVL Tree.md" class="internal-link">AVL Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do double rotations (Left-Right / Right-Left) exist?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A single rotation on a zig-zag imbalance just mirrors the zig-zag instead of fixing it. The inner (middle-valued) node must first be rotated outward to form a straight chain, then a single rotation lifts it to the top.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/AVL Tree.md" class="internal-link">AVL Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why did .NET choose red-black over AVL for <code>SortedSet&lt;T&gt;</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A general-purpose collection can't assume read-heavy usage. Red-black bounds repair work per mutation (≤2 rotations on insert, ≤3 on delete) where AVL deletes can cascade O(log n) rotations; the price — up to ~40% taller worst-case height — only hurts pure-read workloads.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/AVL Tree.md" class="internal-link">AVL Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a B+ tree good for range scans?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Once the first leaf is found, adjacent leaves can be scanned in key order without walking back up the tree.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/B+ Tree.md" class="internal-link">B+ Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the two structural differences between a B+ tree and a B-tree?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Data (or record pointers) lives only in the leaves — internal nodes are pure routing keys — and the leaves are chained into a sorted linked list. Splits, merges, and the equal-leaf-depth invariant are shared with the B-tree.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/B+ Tree.md" class="internal-link">B+ Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does keeping data out of internal nodes make lookups faster, not slower?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Routing entries are tiny compared to records, so internal pages hold 5–10× more keys, fan-out grows, the tree gets shallower, and the whole routing level typically fits in cache. Every search pays a full descent to a leaf, but that descent is shorter and mostly cache-hits.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/B+ Tree.md" class="internal-link">B+ Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">In InnoDB, why is a lookup through a secondary index roughly twice the cost of a primary-key lookup?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Rows live in the leaves of the primary-key (clustered) B+ tree. A secondary index's leaves store primary-key values, so a secondary lookup descends the secondary tree to find the PK, then descends the clustered tree to fetch the row.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/B+ Tree.md" class="internal-link">B+ Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do databases use B-trees instead of plain binary search trees?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A B-tree reads many keys per page, reducing random disk or SSD page reads because the tree is much shallower.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/B-tree.md" class="internal-link">B-tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does a B-tree stay balanced without rotations?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It only grows at the root: an overflowing node splits and pushes its median key up, and if the split cascades to the root, a new root adds one level. Since height changes only at the top, all leaves stay at the same depth by construction.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/B-tree.md" class="internal-link">B-tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What happens on delete when a node drops below its minimum fill?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">First try to borrow a key from an adjacent sibling through the parent; if both siblings are already minimal, merge the node with a sibling and the separating parent key. Merges can cascade up and shrink the tree by one level.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/B-tree.md" class="internal-link">B-tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is a B-tree the wrong choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">In pure memory (no page reads to save — simpler balanced trees or hash maps win on constants) and under write-dominated load, where LSM trees trade read predictability for far better write throughput.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/B-tree.md" class="internal-link">B-tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What breaks when a binary search tree receives already-sorted input?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Without balancing, it degenerates into a linked list and lookup drops from O(log n) expected height to O(n).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Binary Search Tree.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does a BST beat a hash map, given the hash map's O(1) lookup?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Whenever you need order: range queries, floor/ceiling, min/max, or sorted iteration — all O(log n) (or O(n) for full iteration) on a balanced BST, and impossible on a hash map without sorting first.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Binary Search Tree.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you delete a node with two children?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Replace its key with the in-order successor (minimum of the right subtree), then delete that successor — which has at most one child, so the delete reduces to the trivial leaf/one-child case.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Binary Search Tree.md" class="internal-link">Binary Search Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does <code>i &amp; -i</code> select in a Fenwick tree?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It selects the least significant set bit, which gives the size of the range represented by that array slot.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Fenwick Tree.md" class="internal-link">Fenwick Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a Fenwick tree do range sums but not range minimums?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Its native query is the prefix — <code>RangeSum(l, r)</code> is computed as <code>Prefix(r) − Prefix(l−1)</code>, which needs an inverse operation. Sum has subtraction; min has no inverse (knowing min(1..r) and min(1..l−1) tells you nothing about min(l..r)), so min/max need a segment tree.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Fenwick Tree.md" class="internal-link">Fenwick Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Prefix(13) reads exactly three slots. Which ones, and why three?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Slots 13, 12, and 8 — obtained by repeatedly clearing the lowest set bit of 13 (<code>1101₂</code> → <code>1100₂</code> → <code>1000₂</code>). Each slot covers the <code>i &amp; -i</code> elements ending at i, and the three ranges tile [1..13]; the slot count equals the number of set bits in the index.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Fenwick Tree.md" class="internal-link">Fenwick Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When do you pick a Fenwick tree over a segment tree?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the aggregate is invertible (sum, count, XOR) and updates are point updates: same O(log n), a quarter of the memory, a third of the code, better constants. Non-invertible aggregates or lazy range updates flip the choice to the segment tree.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Fenwick Tree.md" class="internal-link">Fenwick Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does the red-black invariant buy you?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It prevents the tree from becoming tall enough to turn ordered lookup into a linear scan, while using fewer rotations than stricter balancing schemes.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Red-Black Tree.md" class="internal-link">Red-Black Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a red-black tree's height at most 2·log₂(n+1)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Equal black counts on every path (rule 5) make the black skeleton balanced, and "no red parent with a red child" (rule 4) means reds can at most double a path by interleaving — so the longest path is at most twice the shortest.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Red-Black Tree.md" class="internal-link">Red-Black Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are new nodes inserted red, and when is the fixup expensive?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A red insert can only break the "no two reds" rule, fixable locally; a black insert breaks the black-height rule on a whole path. Fixup is cheap recoloring while the uncle is red (possibly bubbling up), and terminates with at most two rotations once a black uncle is hit.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Red-Black Tree.md" class="internal-link">Red-Black Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Which .NET collections are red-black trees, and when do you pick them over <code>Dictionary</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>SortedSet&lt;T&gt;</code> and <code>SortedDictionary&lt;TKey, TValue&gt;</code>. Pick them only when you need ordered iteration, or — with <code>SortedSet&lt;T&gt;</code> — <code>Min</code>/<code>Max</code> and <code>GetViewBetween</code> range queries; otherwise <code>Dictionary</code>'s O(1) average lookup wins.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Red-Black Tree.md" class="internal-link">Red-Black Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is a segment tree a better fit than prefix sums?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the underlying values change and you need both updates and range queries after those updates.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Segment Tree.md" class="internal-link">Segment Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a range query cost only O(log n) instead of touching every element in the range?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Any range <code>[l, r]</code> decomposes into at most O(log n) tree nodes whose intervals are fully contained in it. The query takes each such node's precomputed aggregate and stops descending there, never visiting the leaves underneath.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Segment Tree.md" class="internal-link">Segment Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What property must the aggregate function have, and what does that rule in and out?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Associativity — <code>merge(a, merge(b, c)) == merge(merge(a, b), c)</code> — so partial results combine in any grouping. Sum, min, max, and gcd qualify; mean does not directly (store <code>(sum, count)</code> pairs and derive it instead).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Segment Tree.md" class="internal-link">Segment Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What problem does lazy propagation solve?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Range updates. Without it, "add x to <code>[l, r]</code>" costs O(r − l) leaf updates; with a pending delta stored on covering nodes and pushed down on demand, range update and range query both stay O(log n).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Segment Tree.md" class="internal-link">Segment Tree</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Which built-in .NET collection is closest to a self-balancing tree?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>SortedSet&lt;T&gt;</code> (and <code>SortedDictionary&lt;TKey, TValue&gt;</code> for key-value scenarios).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Trees.md" class="internal-link">Trees</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you avoid recursive tree traversal?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">On unknown/deep depth, where iterative traversal with an explicit stack is safer.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Trees.md" class="internal-link">Trees</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a trie lookup O(k) regardless of the number of stored keys?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The walk follows one node per character of the query, so the work depends only on the key length k, not the set size n. Adding millions more keys does not lengthen the path for an existing query — unlike hashing's worst case or a tree's O(log n) depth.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Trie.md" class="internal-link">Trie</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is a <code>HashSet&lt;string&gt;</code> the better choice than a trie?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When you only need exact membership and don't care about prefixes. Both are effectively O(k) per operation (hashing must read the whole key too), but the hash set uses far less memory and is simpler. Choose the trie only when prefix or ordered traversal is a real requirement.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Trie.md" class="internal-link">Trie</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does a trie support autocomplete?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Walk to the node at the end of the typed prefix (O(k)), then DFS/BFS the subtree below it collecting every node with <code>IsEnd</code> — those are all completions of the prefix. Bounding the traversal (e.g. top-N) keeps it responsive on large dictionaries.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Trie.md" class="internal-link">Trie</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h4>Heap-like</h4><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a binomial queue make merge efficient?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The forest mirrors the binary representation of n — at most one tree per rank. Melding walks the ranks like binary addition: equal-rank trees link in O(1) (larger root becomes a child), producing a carry. With O(log n) ranks, the whole meld is O(log n), versus O(n) to merge two array-backed binary heaps.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Binomial Queues.md" class="internal-link">Binomial Queues</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does extractMin work when the heap is a forest, not one tree?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The minimum must be one of the roots (heap order holds within each tree), so scan the ≤ log n roots. Removing a rank-k root leaves its k children — ranks k−1 … 0, exactly a valid binomial forest — which are melded back into the queue. Total O(log n).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Binomial Queues.md" class="internal-link">Binomial Queues</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is insert amortized O(1) when the worst case is O(log n)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Insert is a binary increment. A long carry chain (linking at every rank) only happens after many cheap inserts left those ranks filled — the same potential argument as incrementing a binary counter, which is amortized O(1) per increment.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Binomial Queues.md" class="internal-link">Binomial Queues</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are Fibonacci heaps famous in graph algorithms?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Amortized O(1) decreaseKey. Dijkstra performs up to m decreaseKeys and n extractMins; with a binary heap that's O(m log n), with a Fibonacci heap O(m + n log n) — an asymptotic win on dense graphs. In practice the pointer-heavy nodes and cache misses usually make array-backed heaps faster anyway.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Fibonacci Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Fibonacci Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What problem do cascading cuts solve?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">They stop trees from getting too thin. DecreaseKey cuts nodes out of trees; unchecked, a node of high degree could lose almost all descendants, breaking the size-vs-degree relationship that keeps consolidation O(log n). The mark-then-cut rule guarantees a degree-k node keeps ≥ F(k+2) descendants, capping degrees at O(log n).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Fibonacci Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Fibonacci Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Where does the O(1) cost of insert "go"?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Into the potential function (number of roots + 2 × marked nodes). Insert adds a root, raising potential; extractMin's consolidation pass links those roots and spends the saved potential. Amortized accounting shifts the cost of linking from many cheap inserts to the occasional extractMin.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Fibonacci Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Fibonacci Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is <code>Dequeue</code> on a heap O(log n) instead of O(1)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Removing the root breaks heap order. The last node is moved to root and then pushed down through tree levels, which is bounded by tree height.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Heap.md" class="internal-link">Heap</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can heap iteration look unsorted even when the structure is valid?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A heap guarantees only parent child ordering, not full in order traversal across siblings and cousins.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Heap.md" class="internal-link">Heap</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the practical reason to use <code>PriorityQueue&lt;TElement, TPriority&gt;</code> in .NET?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It gives efficient dynamic priority scheduling without resorting full collections after each insert.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Heap.md" class="internal-link">Heap</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What invariant does a leftist heap maintain, and what does it buy?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>npl(left) ≥ npl(right)</code> at every node, where npl is the distance to the nearest missing child. It forces the right spine to length ≤ log(n+1), and since merge recurses only down right spines, merge is O(log n) worst case.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Leftist Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Leftist Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a leftist heap allowed to be wildly unbalanced?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because no operation walks the left side. Merge, insert, and extractMin all traverse right spines only; the left subtrees can form long chains without affecting any operation's cost. The invariant bounds the one path that matters, not the height.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Leftist Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Leftist Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do insert and extractMin reduce to merge?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Insert merges the heap with a single-node heap. ExtractMin removes the root and merges its left and right subtrees. One correct merge function gives you the whole API.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Leftist Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Leftist Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a skew heap simpler than a leftist heap?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It stores no null path lengths and checks no invariant. Merge recurses down the right spines and swaps children unconditionally at every node on the way back — self-adjustment replaces the stored-metadata invariant, at the cost of worst-case → amortized bounds.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Skew Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Skew Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How can O(log n) amortized hold when one merge can be O(n)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Potential-function argument: define a node as heavy when its right subtree has more nodes than its left. An expensive merge traverses many heavy nodes, but the unconditional swaps turn them light — the traversal pays down potential built up by earlier cheap operations, and leaves the heap cheap to merge again.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Skew Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Skew Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you take a leftist heap over a skew heap despite the extra bookkeeping?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When per-operation worst-case bounds matter (latency-sensitive paths), or when the heap is used persistently/immutably — re-merging a shared expensive shape from an old version repeatedly defeats amortized accounting, while leftist O(log n) is per-operation and survives sharing.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Computer Science/Data Structures/Trees/Heap-like/Skew Heaps.md" class="internal-link">Skew Heaps</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="data-persistence">Data Persistence</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What isolation level should you use for a read-modify-write transaction, and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use Serializable or Repeatable Read. Read Committed (the default in SQL Server and PostgreSQL) allows non-repeatable reads: a balance check and a debit in the same transaction can see different values if another transaction commits between them. For financial calculations or inventory updates, this causes incorrect results. Serializable prevents all anomalies but has the highest lock contention. Repeatable Read prevents non-repeatable reads with lower overhead. Optimistic concurrency (row version/timestamp) is a lighter alternative to Serializable for low-conflict workloads.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/ACID.md" class="internal-link">ACID</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does write-ahead logging (WAL) implement durability?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Before applying any change to data pages, the database writes the change to a sequential log file (WAL). The log write is synchronous — the transaction does not commit until the log entry is flushed to durable storage. On crash recovery, the database replays the log to restore all committed transactions and undo any uncommitted ones. WAL makes durability cheap: sequential log writes are fast; the actual data page updates can be deferred (write-behind). This is why databases can commit thousands of transactions per second despite disk I/O.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/ACID.md" class="internal-link">ACID</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you reduce cache stampede?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Add jitter to expirations so hot keys do not all expire simultaneously. Use request coalescing (singleflight pattern) so only one caller recomputes while others await the result. Consider stale-while-revalidate or background refresh to avoid blocking on recomputation entirely. <code>HybridCache</code> in .NET 9+ handles coalescing automatically.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/Caching.md" class="internal-link">Caching</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a bigger connection pool often worse, not better?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Each connection costs a server-side backend/worker plus memory, and once the database's CPU and disk are saturated, additional concurrent queries just contend and add latency — throughput can actually <em>drop</em>. The right size is a small multiple of the database's core count, sized against <code>max_connections</code> across the <em>whole fleet</em>, not maximized per app.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/Connection Pooling.md" class="internal-link">Connection Pooling</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What causes connection-pool exhaustion and how do you fix it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">All connections are checked out and new requests block until timing out. Usual causes: a pool too small for real concurrency, connections <strong>leaked</strong> by missing <code>Dispose()</code>/<code>using</code>, or connections held across slow I/O or long transactions. Fixes: scope every connection with <code>using</code>, keep the checkout window to just the query, right-size the pool, and add a server-side pooler if the fleet is large.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/Connection Pooling.md" class="internal-link">Connection Pooling</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is connection pooling hard in serverless environments?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Serverless platforms run many short-lived, isolated function instances that can't share an in-process pool, and each may open its own connections — so a spike spawns thousands of connections and exhausts the database. The standard remedy is an external pooler (RDS Proxy, PgBouncer) that all instances share, multiplexing them onto a small set of real connections.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/Connection Pooling.md" class="internal-link">Connection Pooling</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="data-persistence-nosql">NoSQL</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you choose between the four NoSQL families?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">By access pattern. <strong>Key-value</strong> when you only ever fetch by a single key (cache, session). <strong>Document</strong> when data forms self-contained aggregates with flexible schema (profiles, catalogs). <strong>Wide-column</strong> when you need extreme write throughput along a known partition key (time-series, logs). <strong>Graph</strong> when queries traverse relationships many hops deep (social, recommendations, fraud). The query shape, not the data size, drives the choice.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/NoSQL Database Types.md" class="internal-link">NoSQL Database Types</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is NoSQL data modeling "query-first" instead of normalized?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">There are no joins, so you can't assemble data from many tables at read time cheaply. Instead you store data pre-shaped for each read — often duplicating it across multiple "tables"/documents (one per query). You trade storage and write-time duplication for fast, single-lookup reads. This is the opposite of relational <a class="internal-link" href="Normalization Denormalization" aria-label="Normalization Denormalization" data-tooltip-position="top">normalization</a>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/NoSQL Database Types.md" class="internal-link">NoSQL Database Types</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does "polyglot persistence" mean and why is it common?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Using different databases for different jobs within one system — e.g. PostgreSQL as the system of record, Redis for caching/sessions, Elasticsearch for search, Neo4j for a recommendation graph. No single store is best at everything, so mature systems combine them, accepting the operational cost of running several.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/NoSQL Database Types.md" class="internal-link">NoSQL Database Types</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Which NoSQL family fits a user-profile API with very frequent reads by user id?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Key-value or document store, because the access pattern is dominated by point reads on a single id.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use key-value if it is almost entirely get/put by id with no rich querying.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use document if you read/update an aggregate (profile + preferences) and occasionally query a few indexed fields.</li>
<li dir="auto">Key-value gives the simplest, fastest id lookups but no secondary queries; the document store adds query flexibility at some indexing and storage cost.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/NoSQL.md" class="internal-link">NoSQL</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is NoSQL a bad idea?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When the core use case needs relational constraints and multi-entity ACID transactions, or queries are fundamentally join-heavy.</li>
<li dir="auto">Forcing those onto NoSQL pushes join logic and consistency into application code, which is error-prone.</li>
<li dir="auto">Often the better move is to keep SQL and add caching, read replicas, or a denormalized read model.</li>
<li dir="auto">NoSQL trades joins and strong consistency for scale and flexible schemas — if you need the former, that trade is a net loss.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/NoSQL.md" class="internal-link">NoSQL</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does NoSQL push you toward denormalization and data duplication?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Without joins, the cheapest read is one that fetches a whole aggregate in a single hit.</li>
<li dir="auto">So you model data per query, duplicating fields across documents/rows instead of normalizing them once.</li>
<li dir="auto">That makes reads fast and partition-friendly but means a single logical change may touch many copies.</li>
<li dir="auto">You accept write-side duplication and temporarily inconsistent copies in exchange for fast, scalable reads — the opposite of the normalized SQL bargain.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/NoSQL/NoSQL.md" class="internal-link">NoSQL</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="data-persistence-orms">ORMs</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does EF Core's change tracker work, and when should you disable it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">EF Core takes a snapshot of each loaded entity's property values. On <code>SaveChangesAsync()</code>, it compares current values to the snapshot and generates SQL for changed properties.</li>
<li dir="auto">Overhead: change tracking adds memory (snapshot storage) and CPU (comparison on save) per tracked entity.</li>
<li dir="auto">Disable with <code>.AsNoTracking()</code> for read-only queries (reports, API responses that don't modify data). This is the single most impactful EF Core performance optimization for read-heavy workloads.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: <code>.AsNoTracking()</code> entities cannot be modified and saved — you must re-attach them or use <code>ExecuteUpdateAsync()</code> for bulk updates.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/ORMs/Entity Framework.md" class="internal-link">Entity Framework</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the N+1 query problem and how do you detect it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">N+1 occurs when loading N entities and then accessing a navigation property on each, triggering N additional queries.</li>
<li dir="auto">Detection: enable EF Core query logging (<code>LogTo(Console.WriteLine)</code>) or use MiniProfiler/Application Insights to see query counts per request.</li>
<li dir="auto">Fix: use <code>Include()</code> for eager loading, or split into two queries and join in memory for large result sets.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: <code>Include()</code> generates a JOIN, which can produce a large result set if the included collection is large. For collections with &gt;100 items per parent, consider <code>AsSplitQuery()</code> to use separate queries instead of a JOIN.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/ORMs/Entity Framework.md" class="internal-link">Entity Framework</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="data-persistence-sql">SQL</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is an index and what types exist?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">An index is an auxiliary on-disk structure (usually a B+ tree) that lets the engine seek to rows without scanning the whole table.</li>
<li dir="auto">Common types: clustered, nonclustered, unique, composite, filtered, covering (nonclustered with INCLUDE), and columnstore.</li>
<li dir="auto">Clustered defines physical row order; nonclustered is a separate structure with a pointer back to the row.</li>
<li dir="auto">Columnstore stores data column-by-column for analytics; rowstore B+ tree is for OLTP.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Indexes.md" class="internal-link">Indexes</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should a column go in INCLUDE rather than the index key?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Key columns participate in B-tree navigation and are subject to the 1700-byte nonclustered key size limit (900-byte for clustered) and the 32-column key limit (SQL Server 2016+).</li>
<li dir="auto">INCLUDE columns live only at the leaf level. They bypass both limits and don't affect seek/sort behavior.</li>
<li dir="auto">Rule: SARGable columns (used in <code>WHERE</code>, <code>JOIN</code>, <code>GROUP BY</code>, <code>ORDER BY</code>) go in the key; SELECT-only columns go in INCLUDE.</li>
<li dir="auto">The goal is eliminating Key Lookup operators in execution plans. Each lookup is a round-trip to the clustered index per row.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Indexes.md" class="internal-link">Indexes</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can an index rebuild appear to fix a slow query, and how do you verify whether statistics were the real cause?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Rebuilding implicitly runs a FULLSCAN statistics update, giving the optimizer accurate cardinality estimates.</li>
<li dir="auto">Stale statistics causing a bad plan is a far more common culprit than fragmentation on SSD/cloud storage.</li>
<li dir="auto">Test first: run <code>UPDATE STATISTICS ... WITH FULLSCAN</code> without rebuilding. If that fixes the plan, fragmentation was never the issue.</li>
<li dir="auto">Rebuilding indexes purely for fragmentation on modern storage is often wasted maintenance work and causes unnecessary blocking.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Indexes.md" class="internal-link">Indexes</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is normalization and why do most systems stop at 3NF/BCNF?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Normalization eliminates redundancy by decomposing tables so each fact is stored once, preventing update anomalies. Most production OLTP systems stop at 3NF or BCNF because higher forms (4NF, 5NF) address rare anomalies (multivalued and join dependencies) at the cost of more tables and more joins. The decomposition overhead rarely pays off outside temporal or analytical databases.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Normalization Denormalization.md" class="internal-link">Normalization Denormalization</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you denormalize a table, and what risks does it introduce?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Denormalize when a read-heavy workload has expensive joins that indexes can't fix: common in reporting, analytics, or high-throughput APIs. Risks: update anomalies (copies get out of sync), data inconsistency if writes don't update all copies atomically, and increased write complexity. Always measure before denormalizing; premature denormalization adds maintenance cost for no proven benefit.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Normalization Denormalization.md" class="internal-link">Normalization Denormalization</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between 2NF and 3NF, and how would you recognize a violation of each?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">2NF eliminates partial dependencies: every non-key attribute must depend on the entire composite primary key, not just part of it. A violation looks like a column that depends on only one column of a multi-column PK. 3NF eliminates transitive dependencies: non-key attributes must depend directly on the PK, not through another non-key attribute. A violation looks like column A depending on the PK, column B depending on A (not on the PK directly). Fixing both involves decomposing the offending columns into separate tables.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Normalization Denormalization.md" class="internal-link">Normalization Denormalization</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the three replication lag anomalies, and how do you mitigate each?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Read-your-writes</strong>: user writes then reads from a stale replica and sees their write missing. Fix: route post-write reads to the leader for a short window, or use LSN/timestamp tracking to wait for the replica to catch up.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Monotonic reads</strong>: user sees newer data on one request, then older data on the next (different replicas). Fix: sticky sessions. Pin the user to the same replica.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Consistent prefix reads</strong>: causally related writes appear out of order (a reply before the original message). Fix: route causally related writes to the same partition so ordering is preserved.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Replication.md" class="internal-link">Replication</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose synchronous vs asynchronous replication?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Synchronous</strong>: when zero data loss is a hard requirement (financial transactions, audit logs). The leader waits for replica acknowledgment before confirming the write. Cost: every write pays the replica round-trip latency.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Asynchronous</strong>: when write latency matters more than guaranteed durability. Replicas may lag; any unconfirmed writes are lost if the leader crashes.</li>
<li dir="auto">Most production systems use async replication with one semi-synchronous replica for failover safety. SQL Server Always On supports both modes per replica. PostgreSQL <code>synchronous_standby_names</code> controls which standbys must confirm before commit.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Replication.md" class="internal-link">Replication</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does split-brain occur and how is it prevented?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A network partition isolates the primary from the rest of the cluster. A quorum of remaining nodes elects a new primary. When the partition heals, both nodes believe they are primary and have accepted divergent writes.</li>
<li dir="auto">Prevention: quorum-based election. A node must hold a majority of votes to become primary, so only one node can win. Fencing (STONITH) ensures the old primary is forcibly terminated before the new one starts accepting writes, eliminating the window where both are active.</li>
<li dir="auto">WSFC uses quorum witnesses; Patroni uses distributed locks in etcd/Consul. Without fencing, quorum alone is not sufficient. A slow primary that loses quorum might still be processing writes for a few seconds.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Replication.md" class="internal-link">Replication</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>WHERE</code> filters rows before grouping and cannot reference aggregate functions. <code>HAVING</code> filters groups after <code>GROUP BY</code> and can use aggregates like <code>COUNT(*)</code> or <code>SUM(amount)</code>.
