If a condition doesn't depend on an aggregate, always put it in <code>WHERE</code> because it reduces the rows that enter the grouping step.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/SQL.md" class="internal-link">SQL</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a stored procedure and how is it different from a function?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A stored procedure executes multi-step logic, can modify data, and returns result sets or output parameters. A function returns a value (scalar or table) and is designed to be called inline in a query. In SQL Server, legacy (non-inlined) scalar UDFs are evaluated row-by-row and can inhibit parallel query plans. SQL Server 2019+ may inline eligible scalar UDFs, avoiding this. Inline table-valued functions (iTVFs) are expanded like macros and don't have the parallelism problem.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/SQL.md" class="internal-link">SQL</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a Common Table Expression (CTE) and when should you use a temp table instead?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A CTE is a named temporary result set defined with <code>WITH</code>, scoped to a single statement. It improves readability and enables recursive queries for hierarchical data. A CTE is generally treated like an inline view; don't assume it's materialized or reused across multiple references. If you reference the same CTE twice in a query, the optimizer may execute the underlying query twice. For guaranteed reuse of an expensive result, use a temp table instead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/SQL.md" class="internal-link">SQL</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are SQL Server transaction isolation levels?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The five isolation levels are: <code>READ UNCOMMITTED</code> (dirty reads allowed), <code>READ COMMITTED</code> (default, reads only committed data), <code>REPEATABLE READ</code> (re-reading a row returns the same data), <code>SERIALIZABLE</code> (full isolation, highest blocking), and <code>SNAPSHOT</code> (transaction-level consistent snapshot via row versioning in tempdb). Separately, Read Committed Snapshot Isolation (RCSI) is a database-level option (<code>ALTER DATABASE ... SET READ_COMMITTED_SNAPSHOT ON</code>) that makes <code>READ COMMITTED</code> use statement-level row versioning instead of shared locks, eliminating most reader-writer blocking without changing the isolation level name. Switching to <code>READ UNCOMMITTED</code> or <code>NOLOCK</code> is a common anti-pattern: you can read rolled-back, partially-written, or duplicate data.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/SQL.md" class="internal-link">SQL</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you diagnose and optimize a slow query?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Start with the execution plan (<code>SET STATISTICS IO ON</code> or the graphical plan in SSMS). Look for table scans on large tables (missing index), key lookups (a non-clustered index was used but the engine must go back to the clustered index, or the heap via RID lookup, for extra columns; fix by including those columns in the index), and fat arrows indicating large row estimates. Check for stale statistics (<code>UPDATE STATISTICS</code>) when cardinality estimates are wildly off. For repeated slow queries, investigate parameter sniffing: a plan compiled for one parameter value may be terrible for another, fixable with <code>OPTION (RECOMPILE)</code> or <code>OPTIMIZE FOR</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/SQL.md" class="internal-link">SQL</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you shard a database, and what should you try first?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Shard only when write throughput or storage saturates a single node. Try these first:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Vertical scaling</strong>: operationally trivial, preserves full SQL semantics.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Read replicas</strong>: offload reads but don't help writes.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Caching</strong>: reduces DB load for hot reads.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Table partitioning</strong>: splits data within one server, preserving joins and transactions.</li>
</ul>
<p dir="auto">Sharding adds permanent operational complexity and limits cross-shard queries.<br />
[!QUESTION]- Why is shard key choice critical, and what makes a good shard key?<br />
The key determines data distribution and query routing. A bad key causes hotspot shards (uneven load) or scatter queries (fan-out to all shards on every read).</p>
<p dir="auto">A good shard key has high cardinality, even distribution (avoid sequential IDs with range sharding), query alignment (appears in most WHERE clauses), and stability (changing a key requires moving the row). Common choices: user ID, tenant ID, or a composite of region + entity ID.<br />
[!QUESTION]- How does consistent hashing reduce resharding cost compared to simple modulo hashing?<br />
With <code>hash(key) % N</code>, adding one shard changes N and remaps almost every key, requiring a near-total data migration.</p>
<p dir="auto">Consistent hashing maps keys and shards onto a circular ring. Adding a shard displaces only the keys between the new shard and its predecessor, roughly 1/N of all keys. Virtual nodes (multiple ring positions per physical shard) prevent any shard from owning a disproportionately large arc.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Data Persistence/SQL/Sharding.md" class="internal-link">Sharding</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="development-practices">Development Practices</h1><h2 id="development-practices-paradigms">Paradigms</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does event-driven architecture differ from event sourcing?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Event-driven architecture is a communication style: components publish events and others react asynchronously. Event sourcing is a persistence pattern: the state of an entity is derived by replaying its event history rather than storing current state. You can use event-driven communication without event sourcing (most systems do). Event sourcing requires event-driven communication but adds the constraint that events are the source of truth for state.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/Event-driven.md" class="internal-link">Event-driven</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you guarantee ordering of events in a distributed event-driven system?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Most message brokers guarantee ordering only within a partition or queue. Kafka guarantees ordering within a partition (use a consistent partition key, e.g., order ID). Azure Service Bus sessions guarantee ordering within a session. Cross-partition ordering is not guaranteed — design consumers to be idempotent and handle out-of-order delivery. If strict global ordering is required, use a single partition (which limits throughput) or a sequencer service.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/Event-driven.md" class="internal-link">Event-driven</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must event consumers be idempotent in an at-least-once delivery system?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Most message brokers guarantee at-least-once delivery: a message may be delivered more than once if the consumer crashes after processing but before acknowledging. Without idempotency, duplicate delivery causes double-charging, double-reserving, or duplicate records. Mitigation: track processed event IDs in a <code>ProcessedEvents</code> table and skip duplicates, or design operations to be naturally idempotent (SET stock = X instead of stock -= Y).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/Event-driven.md" class="internal-link">Event-driven</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes a function "pure" and why does purity matter for testing?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A pure function has no side effects and is referentially transparent: same inputs always produce the same output.</li>
<li dir="auto">No side effects means no I/O, no global state reads/writes, no exceptions from external dependencies.</li>
<li dir="auto">Testing cost: pure functions need zero mocks — just call with inputs and assert outputs.</li>
<li dir="auto">Parallelism: pure functions are safe to run concurrently without locks.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: real systems need side effects (DB writes, HTTP calls). The FP discipline is to push side effects to the edges and keep the core logic pure.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/Functional Programming.md" class="internal-link">Functional Programming</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does immutability prevent bugs in concurrent code?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Shared mutable state is the root cause of race conditions: two threads read-modify-write the same object.</li>
<li dir="auto">Immutable objects can be shared freely across threads — no lock needed because no mutation is possible.</li>
<li dir="auto">In C#: <code>record</code> types with <code>init</code>-only properties, <code>ImmutableDictionary&lt;K,V&gt;</code>, <code>string</code> (already immutable).</li>
<li dir="auto">Cost: every "mutation" allocates a new object. Acceptable for domain events and DTOs; expensive for high-frequency data structures.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: immutability shifts cost from runtime synchronization to GC pressure. Profile before applying everywhere.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/Functional Programming.md" class="internal-link">Functional Programming</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose LINQ over a manual loop in production code?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">LINQ: when the transformation is a pipeline of filter/map/reduce steps and readability matters more than micro-performance.</li>
<li dir="auto">Manual loop: when you need early exit with complex state, when profiling shows LINQ overhead is significant, or when you need to avoid multiple enumerations.</li>
<li dir="auto">Key risk with LINQ: deferred execution — materializing with <code>.ToList()</code> at the right point is non-obvious and a common source of double-query bugs.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: LINQ is more declarative and composable; loops are more explicit about control flow and allocation. In hot paths (&gt;10k iterations/sec), benchmark both.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/Functional Programming.md" class="internal-link">Functional Programming</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does <code>WebApplicationFactory</code> test that unit tests cannot?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">DI registration: if a service is missing from the container, the integration test fails at startup.</li>
<li dir="auto">Middleware ordering: authentication, authorization, exception handling, and routing all run in the real pipeline.</li>
<li dir="auto">Serialization contracts: JSON serialization settings (camelCase, nullable handling, custom converters) are applied.</li>
<li dir="auto">Controller binding: model validation, route constraints, and action filters run as they would in production.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: <code>WebApplicationFactory</code> tests are slower than unit tests (seconds vs milliseconds). Run them in a separate test project so they don't slow down the unit test feedback loop.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/Integration Testing.md" class="internal-link">Integration Testing</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use Testcontainers instead of EF In-Memory?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">When your queries use SQL features that EF In-Memory doesn't support: raw SQL, stored procedures, database constraints, <code>RETURNING</code> clauses, CTEs.</li>
<li dir="auto">When you need to test database migrations (EF In-Memory doesn't run migrations).</li>
<li dir="auto">When a bug was caused by SQL behavior that the in-memory provider masked.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: Testcontainers requires Docker in CI and adds 5-15 seconds of container startup per test class. The cost is worth it when in-memory tests give false confidence.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/Integration Testing.md" class="internal-link">Integration Testing</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you prefer composition over inheritance?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Prefer composition when the relationship is "has-a" rather than "is-a"</li>
<li dir="auto">Use composition when you need to combine behaviors from multiple sources (C# has no multiple class inheritance)</li>
<li dir="auto">Use composition when the base class is not designed for extension (<code>sealed</code> or has complex internal invariants)</li>
<li dir="auto">Inheritance creates tight coupling: a change to the base class can break all derived classes</li>
<li dir="auto">Composition lets you swap implementations at runtime and test components in isolation via interfaces</li>
<li dir="auto">Heuristic: if you override methods to suppress or fundamentally change base behavior, composition fits better</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: composition requires more explicit wiring (constructor injection, delegation) but buys flexibility and testability; inheritance is less boilerplate but creates coupling that compounds with depth<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/OOP.md" class="internal-link">OOP</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the Anemic Domain Model and why is it considered an anti-pattern?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Objects have only getters/setters with no behavior — all logic lives in service classes</li>
<li dir="auto">Violates encapsulation: services reach into objects to get data, compute, and set results back</li>
<li dir="auto">This is procedural programming with OOP syntax — invariants are enforced in scattered service code</li>
<li dir="auto">Objects can be put into invalid states because there is no guard at the mutation point</li>
<li dir="auto">Fix: move behavior that depends on an object's data into the object itself (Information Expert principle from GRASP)</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: rich domain models enforce invariants at the source but require more upfront design investment; anemic models are simpler to write initially but accumulate inconsistency bugs as the system grows<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/OOP.md" class="internal-link">OOP</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does polymorphism reduce conditional complexity?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Without polymorphism, varying behavior by type requires if/switch chains that grow with every new type</li>
<li dir="auto">Each new type forces editing existing code — a direct Open/Closed violation</li>
<li dir="auto">With polymorphism, each type implements a shared interface and handles its own behavior</li>
<li dir="auto">Adding a new type means adding a new class, not modifying existing code</li>
<li dir="auto">The caller iterates over <code>IEnumerable&lt;IReportGenerator&gt;</code> and calls <code>Generate()</code> — the runtime dispatches to the correct implementation</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: polymorphism adds indirection — debugging requires knowing which concrete type is active, and navigating call hierarchies takes extra IDE steps. Worth paying when you have 3+ variant types or expect new types over time<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/OOP.md" class="internal-link">OOP</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between an interface and an abstract class in C#?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Interface: defines a contract without instance state; can include default method implementations (C# 8+); a class can implement many interfaces</li>
<li dir="auto">Abstract class: defines a contract plus shared implementation and instance state; a class can inherit from only one abstract class</li>
<li dir="auto">Use interfaces when unrelated types share a capability (<code>IDisposable</code>, <code>IComparable</code>)</li>
<li dir="auto">Use abstract classes when related types form a genuine hierarchy with shared invariants</li>
<li dir="auto">In practice, most OOD designs use both: interface for external consumers, abstract class for the internal hierarchy (<code>Robot : IMovable</code>)</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: interfaces are more flexible (no coupling to shared state, multiple implementation) but force every implementer to write the full implementation; abstract classes reduce duplication but create a single coupling point that all derived classes depend on<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/OOP.md" class="internal-link">OOP</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does TDD improve design, not just test coverage?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Writing the test first forces you to define the public interface before the implementation exists.</li>
<li dir="auto">If the test is hard to write (too many constructor arguments, complex setup), that's a design signal: the class has too many responsibilities or too many dependencies.</li>
<li dir="auto">TDD naturally produces smaller classes with single responsibilities because each test targets one behavior.</li>
<li dir="auto">Test-after doesn't provide this signal — the implementation already exists and the test is shaped around it.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: TDD's design benefit requires discipline. Skipping the Red step (writing a test that already passes) eliminates the feedback loop.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/Test-Driven Development.md" class="internal-link">Test-Driven Development</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is TDD not worth the overhead?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Exploratory/spike code: you don't know the right design yet. Write the spike without tests, learn from it, then delete it and rebuild with TDD.</li>
<li dir="auto">UI rendering logic: visual behavior is hard to unit-test meaningfully; use snapshot tests or manual QA instead.</li>
<li dir="auto">Trivial CRUD with no branching: a single-line property setter doesn't need a test-first cycle.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: TDD overhead is ~20-30% more time upfront. The payback is faster debugging and safer refactoring over the life of the codebase. For short-lived code, the payback never arrives.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/Test-Driven Development.md" class="internal-link">Test-Driven Development</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you handle external dependencies (DB, HTTP) in TDD?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Inject dependencies as interfaces. In tests, provide a fake or in-memory implementation.</li>
<li dir="auto">For repositories: use an in-memory implementation (e.g., <code>Dictionary&lt;Guid, Order&gt;</code>) rather than mocking every method.</li>
<li dir="auto">For HTTP clients: use <code>HttpMessageHandler</code> fakes or <code>WireMock.Net</code> for realistic HTTP stubs.</li>
<li dir="auto">For time: inject <code>TimeProvider</code> (built into .NET 8+) so tests can control "now" without <code>DateTime.UtcNow</code> coupling.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: fakes require maintenance. If the real implementation changes behavior, the fake may diverge. Contract tests (testing the fake against the real implementation) catch this.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/Test-Driven Development.md" class="internal-link">Test-Driven Development</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between a stub and a mock?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A stub provides canned return values so the test can proceed — it answers questions ("what orders does customer X have?").</li>
<li dir="auto">A mock verifies interactions — it records calls and lets you assert that a specific method was called with specific arguments.</li>
<li dir="auto">Practical rule: stub data sources (repositories, config); mock side-effect sinks (email, SMS, audit log, event bus).</li>
<li dir="auto">Over-mocking (mocking everything including internal collaborators) produces tests that break on every refactor without catching real bugs.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: mocks couple tests to implementation details. Prefer fakes (working in-memory implementations) when the dependency has non-trivial behavior.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/Unit Testing.md" class="internal-link">Unit Testing</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you test code that depends on the current time?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Inject <code>TimeProvider</code> (built into .NET 8+) instead of calling <code>DateTime.UtcNow</code> directly.</li>
<li dir="auto">In tests, use <code>FakeTimeProvider</code> (from <code>Microsoft.Extensions.TimeProvider.Testing</code>) to control "now".</li>
<li dir="auto">This makes time-dependent logic (expiry checks, scheduling, TTL calculations) fully deterministic in tests.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: requires changing existing code that calls <code>DateTime.UtcNow</code> directly — a one-time refactor cost that pays off in every time-sensitive test.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/Unit Testing.md" class="internal-link">Unit Testing</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you NOT write unit tests?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Trivial property getters/setters with no logic — the test adds noise without catching real bugs.</li>
<li dir="auto">UI rendering logic — visual correctness is better verified with snapshot tests or manual QA.</li>
<li dir="auto">Infrastructure wiring (DI registration, config parsing) — test this with integration tests that boot the real container.</li>
<li dir="auto">Exploratory spike code — write the spike without tests, learn from it, then delete it before it becomes production code.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: every test has a maintenance cost. Tests that don't catch real bugs are pure overhead. Focus unit tests on logic with branching, edge cases, and business rules.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Paradigms/Unit Testing.md" class="internal-link">Unit Testing</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="development-practices-principles">Principles</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is DRY actually trying to prevent?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Duplicated knowledge and duplicated rules.<br />
If a change requires edits in multiple places, DRY is a signal.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Principles/DRY.md" class="internal-link">DRY</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is it OK to repeat code?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the repetition is small, the meaning is different, or the logic is expected to diverge.<br />
Local duplication is sometimes safer than a shared abstraction.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Principles/DRY.md" class="internal-link">DRY</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between IoC and Dependency Injection?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">IoC is the principle: the framework controls object creation and wiring instead of your code. Dependency Injection is the most common technique for implementing IoC: dependencies are passed in (injected) rather than created internally. You can implement IoC without DI (e.g., using a service locator or factory), but constructor injection via a DI container is the idiomatic .NET approach. IoC is the what; DI is the how.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Principles/IoC (Holywood Principle).md" class="internal-link">IoC (Holywood Principle)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between IoC and the Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">DIP (SOLID 'D') is a design rule: high-level modules should depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations. IoC is a runtime mechanism: the framework wires concrete implementations to those abstractions. DIP is the why (depend on interfaces); IoC/DI is the how (let the container provide the concrete class). You can follow DIP without a DI container by manually wiring dependencies in Main, but a container makes it practical at scale.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Principles/IoC (Holywood Principle).md" class="internal-link">IoC (Holywood Principle)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you distinguish 'simple' from 'simplistic' in a design review?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Simple: the design has the minimum number of moving parts needed to meet the current requirements, with clear failure modes and no hidden assumptions. Simplistic: the design ignores real requirements (edge cases, error handling, security) to appear simple. The test: can you explain every component's purpose? If a component exists 'just in case' or 'for future flexibility,' it is probably over-engineering. If a component is missing and the system fails in production, it was simplistic.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Principles/KISS.md" class="internal-link">KISS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is complexity justified despite KISS?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the complexity solves a proven, current problem: security controls (rate limiting, input validation, authentication) are mandatory for public APIs; retry logic and circuit breakers are mandatory for distributed systems; idempotency keys are mandatory for payment processing. The principle is: add complexity only to solve a proven problem, not a hypothetical one. Complexity that prevents production failures is not over-engineering.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Principles/KISS.md" class="internal-link">KISS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does KISS interact with YAGNI and DRY?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">They are complementary: YAGNI says don't build features you don't need yet; DRY says don't duplicate knowledge; KISS says keep the implementation simple. Tension arises when DRY requires an abstraction that adds complexity (KISS violation) for a single use case (YAGNI violation). Resolution: apply the Rule of Three — abstract when you have two concrete use cases, not one. One use case is speculation; two give you enough information to design a simple abstraction.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Principles/KISS.md" class="internal-link">KISS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Which SOLID principles does a typical Singleton violate, and why does it matter in a microservice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><strong>DIP</strong>: consuming code depends on a concrete global instance (<code>Singleton.Instance</code>) instead of an abstraction injected via DI. This makes it impossible to swap implementations per-tenant or per-environment.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>SRP</strong>: the singleton class mixes business logic with lifecycle management (lazy initialization, thread-safety) and global access control. Changes to any of these concerns affect the same class.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>OCP</strong>: replacing or extending behavior usually requires changing call sites or the singleton itself — you cannot plug in an alternative without modifying existing code.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Fix in .NET</strong>: expose an interface, register the implementation with <code>AddSingleton&lt;IService, ConcreteService&gt;()</code> in the DI container. The container manages lifetime; the class manages only its business logic.</li>
<li dir="auto">DI adds indirection and configuration overhead, so a static singleton is simpler for genuinely global, stateless utilities (<code>StringComparer.OrdinalIgnoreCase</code>). For anything stateful or with infrastructure dependencies, DI-managed singletons win outright.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Principles/SOLID.md" class="internal-link">SOLID</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How would you refactor a 2,000-line service class to satisfy SRP without breaking existing callers?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Step 1</strong>: Identify actors — group methods by which team or business process triggers changes to them. Common groupings: persistence, notification, validation, reporting.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Step 2</strong>: Extract method groups into focused classes (e.g., <code>OrderValidator</code>, <code>OrderNotifier</code>, <code>OrderRepository</code>). Each class gets one actor's methods.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Step 3</strong>: Introduce interfaces for each new class. The original <code>OrderService</code> becomes a thin facade that delegates to the extracted classes via interfaces.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Step 4</strong>: Existing callers continue using <code>OrderService</code> (facade). New callers depend on the focused interfaces directly.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Step 5</strong>: Gradually migrate existing callers away from the facade as you touch them for other reasons. Eventually remove the facade.</li>
<li dir="auto">The intermediate facade adds indirection without the full SRP payoff, but it buys an incremental migration over several sprints instead of a big-bang rewrite that freezes feature work.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Principles/SOLID.md" class="internal-link">SOLID</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is it acceptable to violate SOLID principles, and how do you decide?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Small scripts and prototypes</strong>: abstractions cost more than the code they protect. A 50-line console app does not need interfaces.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Performance-critical hot paths</strong>: virtual dispatch, interface resolution, and DI overhead are measurable in tight loops processing millions of items per second. Profile first — if the abstraction boundary appears in your flame graph, inline it.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Premature abstraction risk</strong>: when you have exactly one implementation and no foreseeable second one, extracting an interface adds navigation cost without substitution value. Wait for the second use case.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Decision heuristic</strong>: apply SOLID when you have evidence — when tests are hard to write, when unrelated teams edit the same file, when adding a feature requires modifying code that already works. Do not apply it to prevent hypothetical future problems.</li>
<li dir="auto">Every SOLID application trades simplicity for flexibility. The real question is never "should I apply SOLID" but "does the flexibility outweigh the complexity it adds right now?"<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Principles/SOLID.md" class="internal-link">SOLID</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Does YAGNI mean you should skip tests and refactoring?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">No. YAGNI applies to features and abstractions, not to engineering practices. Martin Fowler explicitly distinguishes: YAGNI says don't build features you don't need yet. It does not say skip tests, skip refactoring, or write messy code. Good engineering practices are always justified because they reduce the cost of future changes.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Principles/YAGNI.md" class="internal-link">YAGNI</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does YAGNI conflict with good design, and how do you resolve it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">YAGNI conflicts with design when adding an abstraction now would make future changes cheaper. The resolution: add the abstraction when you have two concrete use cases, not one. One use case is speculation; two use cases give you enough information to design the abstraction correctly. This is the Rule of Three from refactoring.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Development Practices/Principles/YAGNI.md" class="internal-link">YAGNI</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="devops">DevOps</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes a CI pipeline 'good' vs 'fast but unreliable'?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A good CI pipeline: (1) runs in &lt; 10 minutes (fast enough to not block the developer), (2) has zero flaky tests (every failure is a real failure), (3) masks secrets and never logs sensitive data, (4) promotes the same artifact through environments (no rebuilds), (5) has clear failure messages that point to the root cause. A fast but unreliable pipeline trains developers to ignore failures and re-run instead of fix — which defeats the purpose of CI.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/CI CD tools.md" class="internal-link">CI CD tools</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why use multi-stage builds for .NET applications?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The .NET SDK image (~800MB) includes compilers and build tools not needed at runtime.</li>
<li dir="auto">The ASP.NET runtime image (~200MB) is sufficient for running the published app.</li>
<li dir="auto">Multi-stage builds copy only the published output to the final image, reducing size by ~75%.</li>
<li dir="auto">Smaller images mean faster pulls, less attack surface, and lower registry storage costs.</li>
<li dir="auto">Cost: slightly more complex Dockerfile; worth it for any production workload.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Docker.md" class="internal-link">Docker</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you prevent secrets from leaking into Docker images?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Never use <code>ENV</code> or <code>ARG</code> for secrets in Dockerfiles — they are visible in <code>docker history</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Pass secrets at runtime via environment variables (<code>docker run -e</code>) or Docker secrets.</li>
<li dir="auto">For build-time secrets (e.g., NuGet feeds), use <code>RUN --mount=type=secret</code> (BuildKit).</li>
<li dir="auto">In Kubernetes, use Secrets mounted as environment variables or files.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: runtime injection adds operational complexity but is the only safe approach.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Docker.md" class="internal-link">Docker</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between declarative and imperative IaC?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Declarative</strong> IaC describes the <em>desired end state</em> (e.g. "an S3 bucket named X exists") and the tool computes and executes whatever create/update/delete actions converge reality to that state — it's idempotent, so re-applying is safe. <strong>Imperative</strong> IaC specifies the <em>exact sequence of commands</em> to run (a CLI script); it's flexible but not idempotent and gets brittle as it grows. Modern tools (Terraform, Bicep, CloudFormation) are declarative precisely because the computed diff (plan) and idempotency make changes predictable and reviewable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Infrastructure as Code.md" class="internal-link">Infrastructure as Code</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is the Terraform state file important, and how should it be managed?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The state file is Terraform's record of which real cloud resources correspond to your code; it's how <code>plan</code> computes a diff and how <code>apply</code> knows what already exists. If it's lost or corrupted, Terraform can't map config to reality and may try to recreate or orphan resources. Manage it in a <strong>remote, locked backend</strong> (S3 + DynamoDB, Terraform Cloud, Azure Storage) so the team shares one source of truth and concurrent applies can't corrupt it — and never commit it to Git, because it can contain secrets and full resource details.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Infrastructure as Code.md" class="internal-link">Infrastructure as Code</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is configuration drift and how does IaC address it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Drift is when the real infrastructure diverges from the declared definition — usually because someone changed a resource manually in the console. It's dangerous because the documented state no longer matches reality, so deployments behave unpredictably. IaC addresses it by making the code the single source of truth: <code>plan</code> surfaces the difference between declared and actual state, and <code>apply</code> re-converges the infrastructure back to the definition. The discipline that makes this work is forbidding manual changes — <em>all</em> changes go through the code.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Infrastructure as Code.md" class="internal-link">Infrastructure as Code</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between a Pod, Deployment, and Service?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Pod: the smallest unit; wraps one or more containers sharing a network namespace.</li>
<li dir="auto">Deployment: manages a set of identical pods; handles rolling updates and scaling.</li>
<li dir="auto">Service: stable network endpoint (DNS name + IP) that load-balances across pods.</li>
<li dir="auto">Pods are ephemeral (new IP on restart); Services provide stable addressing.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: Deployments add a layer of abstraction over pods, but that abstraction enables zero-downtime updates.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Kubernetes.md" class="internal-link">Kubernetes</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do Kubernetes Secrets need additional protection beyond base64 encoding?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Base64 is encoding, not encryption — anyone with etcd access can decode secrets trivially.</li>
<li dir="auto">By default, etcd stores secrets in plaintext (base64 is just a transport format).</li>
<li dir="auto">Mitigations: enable etcd encryption at rest, use Sealed Secrets (encrypted in git), or use Azure Key Vault with the Secrets Store CSI driver.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: external secret management adds operational complexity but is required for regulated workloads.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Kubernetes.md" class="internal-link">Kubernetes</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What causes CrashLoopBackOff and how do you debug it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">CrashLoopBackOff means the container starts, crashes, and Kubernetes keeps restarting it with exponential backoff.</li>
<li dir="auto">Common causes: app crashes on startup (missing config, DB connection failure), liveness probe failing before app is ready, OOMKilled (memory limit too low).</li>
<li dir="auto">Debug: <code>kubectl logs &lt;pod&gt; --previous</code> (logs from the crashed instance), <code>kubectl describe pod &lt;pod&gt;</code> (events and exit codes), <code>kubectl exec -it &lt;pod&gt; -- /bin/sh</code> (if the container starts briefly).</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: liveness probes are essential for self-healing but misconfigured probes cause the problem they are meant to solve.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Kubernetes.md" class="internal-link">Kubernetes</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you diagnose an intermittent p95/p99 latency bottleneck in a multi-service system?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Start from traces and filter to the slow requests — the p99, not the average — to see which span dominates. Correlate with per-service RED metrics to tell whether the latency is isolated or systemic, then walk the dependency spans (DB, cache, external API) to find where it originates. Pull structured logs by the same trace ID to check for edge-case payloads or retries on those requests. The point is using all three pillars together: traces locate it, metrics size it, logs explain it — guessing from one dashboard is how you waste an hour.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Observability.md" class="internal-link">Observability</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use RED vs USE?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">RED — Rate, Errors, Duration — is for customer-facing endpoints and request pipelines: it tells you what users are experiencing. USE — Utilization, Saturation, Errors — is for the resources underneath: CPU, thread pools, queue depth, DB connection pools. You want both, because they pair causally: RED is the symptom at the service boundary, USE is the pressure source beneath it. A rising p99 traced to a saturated connection pool is the canonical chain.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Observability.md" class="internal-link">Observability</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why instrument with OpenTelemetry from day one instead of adding observability later?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Instrumenting early bakes telemetry into your contracts and code paths before the system is complex enough to need it — and before an incident, when there's no time to add it. Retrofitting is worse than it sounds: no historical baselines to compare against, and invasive changes across many services at once, usually under pressure. Going vendor-neutral with OpenTelemetry also keeps your backend choice open, so you can switch APMs without re-instrumenting. The telemetry you never emitted is exactly the data you'll wish you had at 3am.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Observability.md" class="internal-link">Observability</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="devops-deployment-strategies">Deployment Strategies</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose canary over blue-green deployment?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Blue-green gives instant rollback by switching traffic back to the old environment, but requires running two full environments simultaneously.</li>
<li dir="auto">Canary gradually shifts traffic to the new version, catching issues in production with real users before full rollout.</li>
<li dir="auto">Choose canary when you need production validation with real traffic patterns that staging cannot replicate.</li>
<li dir="auto">Choose blue-green when you need atomic cutover and can afford double infrastructure cost during the switch.</li>
<li dir="auto">Canary requires traffic splitting infrastructure (service mesh, load balancer rules, or a progressive delivery controller like Argo Rollouts).</li>
<li dir="auto">Canary shrinks blast radius but stretches the deployment window and leans on monitoring automation to catch regressions; blue-green is simpler but doubles infrastructure cost during the switch.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Deployment Strategies/Deployment Strategies.md" class="internal-link">Deployment Strategies</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What failure mode makes rolling deployments unsafe for database schema changes?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Rolling deployments run old and new code side by side during the rollout window.</li>
<li dir="auto">A destructive schema change (dropping a column, renaming a table, changing a constraint) breaks the old instances still serving traffic.</li>
<li dir="auto">Safe approach: expand-and-contract migrations — add the new column first, deploy code that writes to both, then remove the old column in a later release.</li>
<li dir="auto">If schema changes are not backward-compatible, rolling deployment causes partial failures, data corruption, or 500 errors from old instances.</li>
<li dir="auto">Expand-and-contract spreads one change across several deploys and adds migration bookkeeping, but it is the only safe path to zero-downtime schema evolution under rolling deploys.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Deployment Strategies/Deployment Strategies.md" class="internal-link">Deployment Strategies</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you decide between A/B testing and canary when both are available?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Canary validates operational health — does the new version crash, timeout, or degrade throughput.</li>
<li dir="auto">A/B testing validates user behavior — does the new version improve conversion, engagement, or other business metrics.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use canary first to confirm the release is safe, then A/B test to measure business impact.</li>
<li dir="auto">A/B testing requires statistically significant traffic volumes and longer observation windows than canary health checks.</li>
<li dir="auto">Canary can auto-rollback on error rate spikes in minutes; A/B tests typically run days or weeks.</li>
<li dir="auto">Running both gives the most confidence but demands traffic management, metric pipelines, and longer release cycles — justified for high-impact user-facing changes, overkill for an internal service.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Deployment Strategies/Deployment Strategies.md" class="internal-link">Deployment Strategies</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="devops-version-control-systems">Version Control Systems</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does GitFlow create integration problems for teams practicing continuous deployment?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">GitFlow's long-lived <code>develop</code> branch accumulates divergence from <code>main</code> over days or weeks. Feature branches branch off <code>develop</code>, so they also diverge. When multiple features merge back, conflicts compound. The <code>release/*</code> stabilization phase adds a manual gate that prevents continuous deployment. For web services that deploy multiple times per day, GitFlow's overhead is pure cost with no benefit. Use trunk-based development or GitHub Flow instead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Version Control Systems/Branching Stratagies.md" class="internal-link">Branching Stratagies</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does trunk-based development require that makes it unsuitable for all teams?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Trunk-based development requires: (1) feature flags to hide incomplete features from users, (2) fast CI (&lt; 10 minutes) so broken commits are caught quickly, (3) team discipline to keep commits small and the trunk green. Without feature flags, incomplete features must be fully hidden behind code-level conditions or held back entirely. Without fast CI, a broken commit blocks the whole team. Teams without these practices should start with GitHub Flow (short-lived PRs) and evolve toward trunk-based as their CI matures.<br />
<em><a href="Home/DevOps/Version Control Systems/Branching Stratagies.md" class="internal-link">Branching Stratagies</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="networks">Networks</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the practical difference between a Layer 4 and a Layer 7 load balancer?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">An <strong>L4</strong> balancer routes by IP and port only — it's fast and protocol-agnostic but can't see inside the connection, so it distributes whole TCP connections (and pins all multiplexed HTTP/2/gRPC streams to one backend). An <strong>L7</strong> balancer parses the application protocol (HTTP), so it can route by URL/host/header, balance individual requests, terminate TLS, and retry — at higher per-request cost. The choice hinges on whether you need application-aware routing.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/OSI Model.md" class="internal-link">OSI Model</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does the OSI model map onto the actual TCP/IP stack?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">TCP/IP has four layers and folds OSI 5–7 into a single Application layer: Application (HTTP/gRPC/TLS/DNS) ↔ OSI 5–7, Transport (TCP/UDP) ↔ OSI 4, Internet (IP) ↔ OSI 3, Link (Ethernet/Wi-Fi) ↔ OSI 1–2. In conversation, "Layer 4" means TCP/UDP and "Layer 7" means HTTP-level protocols.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/OSI Model.md" class="internal-link">OSI Model</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">At which layer do IP addresses, ports, and MAC addresses each operate?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>MAC addresses</strong> at Layer 2 (Data Link) identify a NIC on a local link; <strong>IP addresses</strong> at Layer 3 (Network) identify a host across networks and enable routing; <strong>ports</strong> at Layer 4 (Transport) identify the specific process/socket on that host. A connection is uniquely identified by the L3+L4 tuple (src IP:port, dst IP:port).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/OSI Model.md" class="internal-link">OSI Model</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="networks-architecture-ops">Architecture &amp; Ops</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a CDN reduce latency, and what determines a cache hit?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Latency is dominated by distance (round-trip time grows with how far light/packets travel). A CDN serves content from an edge node geographically near the user instead of a distant origin, cutting RTT. Whether a request is a <strong>hit</strong> depends on the cache key (usually the URL) and freshness: if a fresh copy exists per the <code>Cache-Control</code>/<code>ETag</code> headers, it's served from the edge; otherwise the edge fetches from origin (a miss) and caches the result.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/CDN.md" class="internal-link">CDN</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you push a change live when content is cached on a CDN?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Either purge/invalidate the specific URL or cache tag (explicit but rate-limited and not instantaneous everywhere), or — better for static assets — use <strong>content-hashed, immutable URLs</strong> so a new version is a brand-new URL that nothing stale points to. For HTML that references those assets, keep a short TTL or <code>no-cache</code> so it picks up the new asset URLs promptly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/CDN.md" class="internal-link">CDN</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What content should you NOT cache on a CDN, and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Per-user or sensitive responses (anything containing a logged-in user's data, or behind auth) must not sit in a <em>shared</em> edge cache, or one user's data can be served to another. Mark them <code>Cache-Control: private</code> / <code>no-store</code>, or only cache with a per-user cache key. Static, public, identical-for-everyone content is the ideal CDN candidate.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/CDN.md" class="internal-link">CDN</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is consistency hard in P2P systems?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">P2P systems have no central authority to act as the source of truth. When peers hold different versions of data and there is no coordinator to resolve conflicts, the system must rely on eventual consistency — peers eventually converge to the same state through gossip or reconciliation protocols. This is acceptable for file distribution (BitTorrent) or content-addressed storage (IPFS) where data is immutable, but it makes P2P unsuitable for systems requiring strong consistency (financial transactions, inventory).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/Peer-2-Peer.md" class="internal-link">Peer-2-Peer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does WebRTC use P2P, and what is the role of STUN/TURN servers?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">WebRTC establishes direct peer connections between browsers for audio, video, and data. The challenge is NAT traversal: most browsers are behind NAT and don't have public IP addresses. STUN servers help peers discover their public IP/port. When direct connection fails (symmetric NAT), TURN servers relay traffic. The goal is to minimize relay usage — direct P2P connections reduce latency and server cost; TURN relay is the fallback.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/Peer-2-Peer.md" class="internal-link">Peer-2-Peer</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is split tunneling and when would you use it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Split tunneling routes only traffic destined for private resources through the VPN; internet traffic goes directly. Use it to reduce gateway bandwidth cost and latency for internet-bound traffic. Avoid it when corporate policy requires all traffic to pass through security inspection (content filtering, DLP).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/VPN.md" class="internal-link">VPN</a></em></p>
