</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does WireGuard have a smaller attack surface than IPsec?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">WireGuard is ~4,000 lines of code (auditable by a small team) vs IPsec's ~400,000 lines. Fewer lines means fewer potential vulnerabilities. WireGuard also uses a fixed, modern cryptographic suite (ChaCha20, Curve25519, BLAKE2) with no negotiation — removing cipher selection as an attack vector.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/VPN.md" class="internal-link">VPN</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What causes DNS leaks in a VPN setup, and how do you fix them?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">DNS queries bypass the encrypted tunnel — typically because the OS sends DNS requests to the default interface rather than the VPN interface. Fix: route DNS through the tunnel by setting DNS to an internal server and ensuring DNS requests use the VPN's routing table, or by running a local DNS resolver that forces all queries through the tunnel.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Architecture &amp; Ops/VPN.md" class="internal-link">VPN</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="networks-protocols">Protocols</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a DNS change "take time" to propagate?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because resolvers cache answers for the record's TTL. Old answers persist until they expire. A 24-hour TTL means up to 24 hours of propagation delay. Mitigation: lower TTL to 60–300s before planned changes, then restore it after.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/DNS.md" class="internal-link">DNS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between a recursive resolver and an authoritative server?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A recursive resolver (e.g., 8.8.8.8) does the work of walking the DNS hierarchy on behalf of the client. It caches results. An authoritative server holds the actual records for a zone and answers queries about names in that zone. The resolver queries authoritative servers; clients query resolvers.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/DNS.md" class="internal-link">DNS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does DNSSEC protect against cache poisoning?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">DNSSEC signs records with a private key. Resolvers verify signatures using the public key from the DNS hierarchy. A forged response without a valid signature is rejected. Cost: key rotation complexity, signing overhead, and larger DNS responses (signatures add bytes).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/DNS.md" class="internal-link">DNS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What problem does HTTP/2 multiplexing solve compared to HTTP/1.1?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">HTTP/1.1 processes requests sequentially per connection — a slow request blocks all subsequent ones. Browsers work around this with 6–8 parallel connections, which is wasteful. HTTP/2 multiplexes many streams on one connection, eliminating application-layer head-of-line blocking and reducing connection overhead.<br />
Cost: a single TCP connection means TCP-layer packet loss affects all streams simultaneously.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/HTTP 2.md" class="internal-link">HTTP 2</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does HTTP/2 still have head-of-line blocking?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">HTTP/2 eliminates it at the application layer but not at the TCP layer. TCP guarantees ordered delivery — a lost packet blocks all subsequent data until retransmitted, stalling all HTTP/2 streams on that connection. HTTP/3 (QUIC over UDP) solves this with per-stream loss recovery.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/HTTP 2.md" class="internal-link">HTTP 2</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose HTTP/1.1 over HTTP/2?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the network has high packet loss (mobile, satellite) and you cannot use HTTP/3 — multiple HTTP/1.1 connections isolate packet loss better than a single HTTP/2 connection. Also when connecting to legacy servers or proxies that don't support HTTP/2.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/HTTP 2.md" class="internal-link">HTTP 2</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is creating a new HttpClient per request dangerous in .NET, and what are the two correct alternatives?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><code>HttpClient.Dispose()</code> does not immediately release sockets — the OS holds them in <code>TIME_WAIT</code> for ~4 minutes.</li>
<li dir="auto">Under load, this exhausts the ephemeral port range → <code>SocketException</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Fix 1: <code>IHttpClientFactory</code> with default 2-minute handler lifetime — handles both socket pooling and DNS re-resolution.</li>
<li dir="auto">Fix 2: singleton <code>HttpClient</code> with <code>SocketsHttpHandler.PooledConnectionLifetime</code> set to force periodic connection recycling.</li>
<li dir="auto">Even the singleton approach has a DNS staleness trap without <code>PooledConnectionLifetime</code>.</li>
</ul>
<p dir="auto"><strong>Why this matters:</strong> the most common HTTP production failure in .NET; tests understanding of socket lifecycle and connection pool mechanics.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/HTTP.md" class="internal-link">HTTP</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between Cache-Control no-cache and no-store?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><code>no-cache</code> means the response CAN be stored, but the cache MUST revalidate with the origin server before every use via conditional request (<code>If-None-Match</code> or <code>If-Modified-Since</code>).</li>
<li dir="auto"><code>no-store</code> means the response MUST NOT be stored at all — not in browser cache, not in CDN, not on disk.</li>
<li dir="auto"><code>no-cache</code> is appropriate for content that changes but benefits from 304 responses (saves bandwidth when content has not changed). <code>no-store</code> is for sensitive data like banking pages or PII.</li>
<li dir="auto">The naming is misleading — <code>no-cache</code> does not mean "don't cache."</li>
</ul>
<p dir="auto"><strong>Why this matters:</strong> the most commonly misunderstood HTTP caching directive; wrong choice either leaks sensitive data or kills performance.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/HTTP.md" class="internal-link">HTTP</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Explain the difference between 401 and 403 status codes.</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">401 Unauthorized means the client is not authenticated — no credentials were provided, or they are invalid. The response MUST include a <code>WWW-Authenticate</code> header.</li>
<li dir="auto">403 Forbidden means the client IS authenticated but not authorized — the server knows who you are, but you lack permission for this resource.</li>
<li dir="auto">401 tells the client to retry with valid credentials. 403 tells the client that retrying with the same identity is pointless.</li>
<li dir="auto">Security consideration: some APIs return 404 instead of 403 to avoid revealing resource existence to unauthorized users.</li>
</ul>
<p dir="auto"><strong>Why this matters:</strong> authentication vs authorization is a fundamental security distinction; misusing these codes breaks client auth flows and retry logic.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/HTTP.md" class="internal-link">HTTP</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Explain <code>PUT</code> vs <code>PATCH</code> semantics and retry behavior.</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>PUT</code> replaces the whole resource representation, so sending it twice lands the resource in the same state — it's idempotent, and a client can safely auto-retry it when a network blip leaves the outcome unknown. <code>PATCH</code> applies a partial update, and whether it's idempotent depends on the patch format: <code>set status = "shipped"</code> is, but <code>increment quantity by 1</code> isn't. So servers should document the patch media type and conflict rules (<code>409</code>/<code>422</code>). The upshot: retry <code>PUT</code> freely, but make <code>PATCH</code> safe with an idempotency key unless the operation is provably repeatable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/REST.md" class="internal-link">REST</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do many teams stop at Richardson Level 2 instead of Level 3 HATEOAS?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Level 2 already buys most of what REST is good for — resource URIs, proper verbs, status codes, cacheability — and that's enough for the vast majority of APIs. Level 3 adds hypermedia: responses carry the links and actions valid for the current state. It's powerful for discoverability, but it needs clients that understand link relations plus the discipline and tooling to maintain them, and in a closed ecosystem clients already know the workflows, so the payoff shrinks. HATEOAS earns its cost when runtime discoverability and loose coupling are real goals; otherwise Level 2 is the pragmatic stopping point.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/REST.md" class="internal-link">REST</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is RPC's 'local call' abstraction considered leaky?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">RPC hides the network boundary, but the network introduces failures that local calls never have: calls can time out, be delivered twice (at-least-once delivery), or fail with the server having already executed the operation. A failed RPC does not mean the server didn't execute — the response may have been lost. This forces callers to implement idempotency keys, retries with backoff, and circuit breakers — concerns that don't exist for local calls. The abstraction is useful but must not be trusted blindly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/RPC.md" class="internal-link">RPC</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you choose gRPC over REST?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Choose gRPC for internal service-to-service communication where: (1) you need high throughput or low latency (binary Protobuf is 3-10x smaller than JSON), (2) you need bidirectional streaming, or (3) you want strongly typed contracts enforced at compile time. Choose REST for public APIs, browser clients (gRPC requires a proxy for browsers), or when human-readable payloads matter for debugging. The key constraint: gRPC requires HTTP/2 and a Protobuf toolchain; REST works with any HTTP client.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/RPC.md" class="internal-link">RPC</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does SMTP require SPF, DKIM, and DMARC for deliverability?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">SMTP has no built-in sender authentication — anyone can claim any From address. SPF restricts which IPs can send for a domain. DKIM adds a cryptographic signature that proves the message was not tampered with. DMARC tells receiving servers what to do when SPF/DKIM fail. Without all three, email from your domain will be marked as spam or rejected by major providers.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/SMTP.md" class="internal-link">SMTP</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does gRPC not work well with L4 load balancers, and how do you fix it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">gRPC multiplexes all calls over a single HTTP/2 TCP connection.</li>
<li dir="auto">L4 load balancers distribute at the TCP connection level — they cannot see individual HTTP/2 streams within that connection.</li>
<li dir="auto">All calls from one client land on the same backend, defeating load distribution.</li>
<li dir="auto">Fix: use an L7 proxy (Envoy, Linkerd, YARP) that terminates HTTP/2 and distributes individual streams across backends. Or use client-side load balancing with service discovery.</li>
</ul>
<p dir="auto"><strong>Why this matters:</strong> the most common production surprise when adopting gRPC; tests understanding of HTTP/2 multiplexing at the transport layer.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/gRPC.md" class="internal-link">gRPC</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What happens if you call a gRPC service without setting a deadline?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The call has no timeout and can hang indefinitely.</li>
<li dir="auto">Resources (threads, sockets, memory) on both client and server are consumed with no bound.</li>
<li dir="auto">In a microservice chain, one hanging call can exhaust connection pools upstream, causing cascading failures across services.</li>
<li dir="auto">gRPC intentionally has no default deadline because the right value depends on the operation.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use <code>EnableCallContextPropagation()</code> to automatically forward deadlines through a service chain.</li>
</ul>
<p dir="auto"><strong>Why this matters:</strong> deadlines are the single most important production gRPC configuration; missing them is the top cause of gRPC-related outages.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/gRPC.md" class="internal-link">gRPC</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is renaming a proto field safe but renumbering it is not?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>Expected answer:</strong></p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Protobuf binary encoding uses the field <strong>number</strong> as the wire identifier, not the name.</li>
<li dir="auto">Renaming a field changes only the generated code accessor — the wire format is unchanged, so old and new clients interoperate seamlessly.</li>
<li dir="auto">Renumbering changes the wire identity — old clients sending the old number will have their data silently interpreted as the new field by the updated server.</li>
<li dir="auto">When removing fields, use <code>reserved</code> to prevent the number and name from being reused in future schema changes.</li>
</ul>
<p dir="auto"><strong>Why this matters:</strong> proto versioning is the contract management layer of gRPC; getting it wrong causes silent data corruption that is extremely hard to debug.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/gRPC.md" class="internal-link">gRPC</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is a WebSocket different from a raw TCP socket?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A raw socket is the OS-level, unframed byte-stream endpoint — you implement your own message boundaries, handshakes, and (in a browser, you can't open one at all). A WebSocket is a standardized application protocol <em>over</em> TCP: it's established via an HTTP <code>Upgrade</code> handshake, has built-in message framing and ping/pong/close control frames, traverses HTTP proxies/firewalls on 80/443, and is exposed to browsers through the native <code>WebSocket</code> API.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/WebSockets.md" class="internal-link">WebSockets</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does a WebSocket connection start as an HTTP request?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">So it can reuse the web's existing infrastructure — ports 80/443, TLS, proxies, load balancers, and browser security model — and <em>upgrade</em> a normal HTTP connection in place (<code>101 Switching Protocols</code>) rather than requiring a new port or protocol that firewalls would block. After the upgrade, the same TCP connection carries WebSocket frames instead of HTTP.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/WebSockets.md" class="internal-link">WebSockets</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose Server-Sent Events over WebSockets?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When you only need <strong>server→client</strong> push (live feeds, notifications, progress updates) and not a client→server channel. SSE runs over plain HTTP, is simpler, and has automatic reconnection built into the browser <code>EventSource</code> API. Choose WebSockets when you need true bidirectional, low-latency messaging.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Protocols/WebSockets.md" class="internal-link">WebSockets</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="networks-transport-sockets">Transport &amp; Sockets</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do partial reads happen with TCP sockets?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">TCP is a byte stream protocol. The network may deliver data in smaller chunks than the sender wrote. A single <code>ReadAsync</code> call returns however many bytes are available at that moment, which may be less than the full message. Applications must loop until the expected number of bytes is received.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Transport &amp; Sockets/Sockets.md" class="internal-link">Sockets</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between <code>Socket</code>, <code>TcpClient</code>, and <code>TcpListener</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>Socket</code> is the low-level OS abstraction supporting TCP, UDP, and other protocols. <code>TcpClient</code> wraps a TCP socket and exposes a <code>NetworkStream</code> for stream-based I/O. <code>TcpListener</code> wraps a server-side TCP socket and provides <code>AcceptTcpClientAsync</code> for accepting connections. Use <code>TcpClient</code>/<code>TcpListener</code> for most TCP work; drop to <code>Socket</code> when you need protocol-level control.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Transport &amp; Sockets/Sockets.md" class="internal-link">Sockets</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose UDP over TCP for a production system?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When latency matters more than delivery guarantees and the application can tolerate or recover from packet loss. Examples: real-time game state updates (stale frames are discarded anyway), DNS queries (fast retry is cheaper than TCP handshake), telemetry/metrics (occasional loss is acceptable). The application must implement its own reliability if needed.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Transport &amp; Sockets/Sockets.md" class="internal-link">Sockets</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does TCP's three-way handshake exist, and when does its latency cost become a real problem?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The handshake synchronizes sequence numbers and confirms both sides are reachable before sending data.</li>
<li dir="auto">It adds one full round-trip of latency before the first byte of application data can be sent.</li>
<li dir="auto">For short-lived connections (single HTTP request, DNS-over-TCP), handshake latency dominates total request time.</li>
<li dir="auto">HTTP/2 multiplexing amortizes the handshake across many requests on one connection. QUIC (HTTP/3) eliminates it entirely by combining transport and TLS handshakes.</li>
<li dir="auto">The handshake guarantees reliable setup but costs a full round-trip; reach for connection pooling or QUIC when that overhead is unacceptable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Transport &amp; Sockets/TCP IP.md" class="internal-link">TCP IP</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do flow control and congestion control differ, and what happens when you confuse them?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Flow control protects the receiver: the receive window limits how much unacknowledged data the sender can push.</li>
<li dir="auto">Congestion control protects the network: the congestion window limits send rate based on detected packet loss.</li>
<li dir="auto">The effective send rate is the minimum of both windows.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tuning only one side causes problems: a large receive window with aggressive congestion control still causes network drops; a small receive window with conservative congestion control wastes available bandwidth.</li>
<li dir="auto">Flow control is endpoint-local and deterministic; congestion control is network-wide and heuristic — tune them together or one silently caps the other.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Transport &amp; Sockets/TCP IP.md" class="internal-link">TCP IP</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is UDP preferable to TCP, and what reliability mechanisms do applications add on top?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">UDP when latency matters more than reliability: real-time audio/video, gaming, DNS. A retransmitted old packet is worse than no packet. Applications add their own reliability: sequence numbers for ordering, FEC (Forward Error Correction) for loss recovery, selective retransmission for critical events. QUIC is the canonical example — it builds reliable ordered streams on UDP while avoiding TCP's head-of-line blocking.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Transport &amp; Sockets/UDP.md" class="internal-link">UDP</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does UDP have no congestion control, and what are the consequences?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">UDP sends at whatever rate the application dictates. Under congestion, UDP traffic doesn't back off — it can starve TCP connections sharing the same link. Applications using UDP for bulk transfer must implement their own congestion control (QUIC does this). For small datagrams (DNS, game state), the impact is minimal because the volume is low.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Transport &amp; Sockets/UDP.md" class="internal-link">UDP</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is QUIC and why is it built on UDP rather than TCP?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">QUIC (HTTP/3) implements reliable, ordered, multiplexed streams on top of UDP. It's built on UDP to avoid TCP's head-of-line blocking (a lost TCP segment blocks all streams; QUIC streams are independent), to enable faster connection establishment (0-RTT resumption), and to allow protocol evolution without OS kernel changes. TLS 1.3 is built into QUIC — there's no separate TLS handshake.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Networks/Transport &amp; Sockets/UDP.md" class="internal-link">UDP</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="programming">Programming</h1><h2 id="programming-net">NET</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you still target netstandard?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Target <code>netstandard2.0</code> when you must support .NET Framework or older ecosystem consumers that cannot load modern <code>netX</code> assemblies.</li>
<li dir="auto">Prefer adding it as part of multi-targeting (<code>net8.0;netstandard2.0</code>) instead of making it your only target for new libraries.</li>
<li dir="auto">If all known consumers are modern .NET, target modern <code>netX</code> directly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/NET Standart.md" class="internal-link">NET Standart</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the three layers of the .NET platform, and why does that distinction matter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Runtime (CLR), language (C#/F#), and framework libraries (ASP.NET Core, EF Core, extensions).<br />
It matters because most production issues cross layers: a performance problem might involve language-level allocations (C#), runtime GC behavior (CLR), and framework middleware configuration (ASP.NET Core). Understanding the boundaries helps you diagnose root causes instead of applying surface-level fixes.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/NET.md" class="internal-link">NET</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>ASP.NET Web API</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Where should authentication and authorization live in an ASP.NET Core API?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Put authentication and authorization in the pipeline so endpoints can assume an authenticated principal.<br />
Use middleware for auth and use endpoint metadata and policies to decide access per endpoint.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/ASP.NET Web API.md" class="internal-link">ASP.NET Web API</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can't you revoke a JWT before its expiry?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">JWTs are self-contained and the server stores no record of issued tokens. Revocation requires a server-side blacklist (typically Redis) or very short expiry (15–60 min) combined with long-lived refresh tokens stored server-side and revocable on logout.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Authentication.md" class="internal-link">Authentication</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose cookie authentication over JWT Bearer in an ASP.NET Core API?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">For browser-based web applications (Razor Pages/MVC) where the browser manages cookie transmission automatically, where session revocation (logout everywhere) is a hard requirement, and where CSRF risk is manageable via ASP.NET Core's built-in anti-forgery tokens.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Authentication.md" class="internal-link">Authentication</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does <code>ValidateIssuerSigningKey = true</code> actually enforce?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It forces the JWT middleware to verify the token's signature against your known signing key. Without it, a token signed by a different (attacker-controlled) key could be accepted — breaking the entire authentication model.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Authentication.md" class="internal-link">Authentication</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you return 403 Forbidden vs 404 Not Found for an unauthorized resource access?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Return 403 when the resource exists but the user lacks permission, and the resource's existence is not sensitive. Return 404 consistently when leaking the resource's existence is a security risk (e.g., private financial or health records).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Authorization.md" class="internal-link">Authorization</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between <code>context.Succeed()</code> and <code>context.Fail()</code> in an authorization handler?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>context.Succeed(requirement)</code> marks that requirement as satisfied. <code>context.Fail()</code> explicitly forces authorization failure regardless of other handlers' decisions — it cannot be overridden by a subsequent <code>Succeed</code>. Use <code>Fail</code> only when you have a definitive security reason to block access.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Authorization.md" class="internal-link">Authorization</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you implement OR logic across two authorization policies on a single endpoint?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Multiple <code>[Authorize]</code> attributes stack with AND semantics — all policies must pass. For OR logic, implement a single custom <code>IAuthorizationRequirement</code> that internally checks whether any of the conditions is met, then apply that single requirement via one policy.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Authorization.md" class="internal-link">Authorization</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why must app.UseCors() come before authentication middleware?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Preflight OPTIONS requests do not carry authentication credentials. If authentication middleware runs first, it rejects the preflight with 401 before CORS headers are added to the response. The browser then sees a failed preflight and blocks the actual request. Placing UseCors() before UseAuthentication() ensures preflight responses include the correct CORS headers and return 200, allowing the browser to proceed with the actual authenticated request.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/CORS.md" class="internal-link">CORS</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a captive dependency and why is it dangerous?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A captive dependency occurs when a long-lived service (Singleton) captures a shorter-lived service (Scoped or Transient) at construction time. The shorter-lived service then lives as long as the Singleton, violating its intended lifetime. For DbContext, this causes a single database context to be shared across all requests, leading to data corruption and race conditions.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Dependency Injection.md" class="internal-link">Dependency Injection</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you use a Scoped service inside a Singleton without a captive dependency?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Inject <code>IServiceScopeFactory</code> into the Singleton and call <code>scopeFactory.CreateScope()</code> at the point of use. Resolve the Scoped service from the new scope and dispose the scope when done. This ensures the Scoped service lives within a controlled scope, not captured inside the Singleton.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Dependency Injection.md" class="internal-link">Dependency Injection</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between <code>GetService&lt;T&gt;</code> and <code>GetRequiredService&lt;T&gt;</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>GetService&lt;T&gt;</code> returns null if the service is not registered; <code>GetRequiredService&lt;T&gt;</code> throws <code>InvalidOperationException</code>. Use <code>GetRequiredService&lt;T&gt;</code> in production code where a missing registration is a programming error that should fail loudly at startup rather than silently return null at call time.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Dependency Injection.md" class="internal-link">Dependency Injection</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Explain when to choose middleware over an MVC action filter.</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Middleware when concern is app-wide and independent of controller/action internals.</li>
<li dir="auto">Action filter when concern needs <code>ActionExecutingContext</code>, action arguments, or action result wrapping.</li>
<li dir="auto">Middleware runs earlier in pipeline and can affect all endpoints.</li>
<li dir="auto">Filters are more granular for controller/action scope.<br />
Why this matters: this is a common architecture tradeoff in API design interviews.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Filters.md" class="internal-link">Filters</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the execution order of ASP.NET Core filter types?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Authorization -&gt; Resource -&gt; Action -&gt; Exception -&gt; Result (with before/after phases where applicable).</li>
<li dir="auto">Scope affects order: global, then controller, then action.</li>
<li dir="auto"><code>IOrderedFilter</code> can override default order.<br />
Why this matters: ordering mistakes cause hidden bugs in validation, caching, and error handling.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Filters.md" class="internal-link">Filters</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you inject services into a filter safely?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Expected answer:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Register filter/service in DI container.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use <code>ServiceFilter</code>/<code>TypeFilter</code> or <code>options.Filters.AddService&lt;TFilter&gt;()</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Prefer constructor injection and async interfaces.</li>
<li dir="auto">Avoid <code>RequestServices.GetService</code> inside filter bodies unless absolutely necessary.<br />
Why this matters: DI misuse in filters causes brittle code and testing pain.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Filters.md" class="internal-link">Filters</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Action filter vs middleware: what is the difference?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Middleware is pipeline-level and can apply to all requests (before routing/MVC, around endpoint execution). Action filters are MVC-level and run only for MVC actions, with access to action context, model binding, and results; they are a better fit for cross-cutting concerns that are specific to controller actions.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Middlewares.md" class="internal-link">Middlewares</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How can you log execution time for all requests?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use a middleware that measures elapsed time around <code>next()</code> and logs it. An action filter works too, but only for MVC actions.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Middlewares.md" class="internal-link">Middlewares</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How can you centrally catch errors for all requests?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Add a global exception-handling middleware. In ASP.NET Core this is commonly done with <code>app.UseExceptionHandler(...)</code> (and <code>app.UseDeveloperExceptionPage()</code> in development). The handler can log the exception and return a consistent error response (for example, RFC 7807 Problem Details).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Middlewares.md" class="internal-link">Middlewares</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the ASP.NET request processing pipeline?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A request is received by the host (for example, Kestrel) and then flows through an ordered chain of middleware. Middleware can add features (routing, authN/authZ, CORS, compression, etc.), select an endpoint, and finally execute the endpoint (MVC action, Minimal API handler, etc.). On the way back out, the middleware chain unwinds, allowing post-processing of the response.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/ASP.NET Web API/Middlewares.md" class="internal-link">Middlewares</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>CSharp</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What makes C# a multi-paradigm language, and why does that matter for API design?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">C# supports object-oriented (classes, interfaces, inheritance), functional (LINQ, pattern matching, immutable records, expression-bodied members), and procedural styles.<br />
This matters because you can choose the paradigm that fits the problem: OOP for domain modeling, functional for data transformation pipelines, procedural for scripts and glue code.<br />
In API design, mixing paradigms intentionally (e.g., immutable records for DTOs, interfaces for extensibility, LINQ for queries) produces cleaner, more testable code than forcing everything into one style.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/CSharp.md" class="internal-link">CSharp</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does C#'s type system help prevent bugs at compile time?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Nullable reference types flag potential null dereferences before runtime.</li>
<li dir="auto">Generic constraints enforce type relationships without casting.</li>
<li dir="auto">Pattern matching with exhaustiveness checks catch missing cases in switch expressions.</li>
<li dir="auto">Strong typing prevents accidental type confusion that would be runtime errors in dynamic languages.<br />
The practical impact: many bugs that surface as runtime exceptions in Python or JavaScript are caught during compilation in C#.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/CSharp.md" class="internal-link">CSharp</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h4>Concurrency and Parallelism</h4><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is asynchrony different from multithreading?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Asynchrony is about not blocking while waiting (especially for I/O). An <code>async</code> method can release the current thread while awaiting, and continue later — potentially on the same thread.<br />
Multithreading is about executing work on multiple threads concurrently (for CPU-bound work). Async code can be single-threaded and still be asynchronous.<br />
The key cost difference: async I/O uses no thread while waiting; multithreading always occupies a thread.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Async Await.md" class="internal-link">Async Await</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between <code>await</code> and <code>Task.Result</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>await</code> waits asynchronously: it does not block the current thread, it unwraps exceptions as <code>Exception</code> (not <code>AggregateException</code>), and it respects <code>SynchronizationContext</code>.<br />
<code>Task.Result</code> waits synchronously: it blocks the current thread, wraps exceptions in <code>AggregateException</code>, and can deadlock under a <code>SynchronizationContext</code>.<br />
Cost: <code>.Result</code> in a context-aware environment is a deadlock waiting to happen; <code>await</code> is the safe default.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Async Await.md" class="internal-link">Async Await</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use <code>ConfigureAwait(false)</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">In library code that does not need to resume on a specific context. It avoids context-capture overhead and eliminates the deadlock risk when library code is called from a context-aware environment.<br />
Do not use it in application-layer code (controllers, view models) where you need to resume on the original context to update UI or access <code>HttpContext</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Async Await.md" class="internal-link">Async Await</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">If async does not always use extra threads, why does it improve scalability?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because waiting time is no longer paid by tying up worker threads. Released threads can process other requests while I/O is pending. A server with 100 threads can handle thousands of concurrent I/O-bound requests if each thread is released during the wait.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Async Await.md" class="internal-link">Async Await</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use <code>Task.Run</code> with async code?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">For CPU-bound work that you intentionally offload to a pool thread (e.g., image processing, heavy computation). Do not use it to wrap already-async I/O APIs — that wastes a thread for no benefit.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Async Await.md" class="internal-link">Async Await</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is it reasonable not to cancel immediately after the token is signaled?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When a tiny critical section must complete to keep state consistent — for example, finishing a single idempotent write or releasing resource ownership. The key is that the section is short and bounded. Never hold a lock or do I/O while ignoring a cancellation signal for an extended period.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/CancellationToken.md" class="internal-link">CancellationToken</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is cooperative cancellation safer than <code>Thread.Abort</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Cooperative cancellation stops at known safe points under your control. <code>Thread.Abort</code> (removed in .NET 5+) could interrupt arbitrary code paths — including <code>finally</code> blocks and lock releases — leaving resources or invariants in bad state. Cooperative cancellation keeps control flow explicit and cleanup reliable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/CancellationToken.md" class="internal-link">CancellationToken</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you propagate cancellation across a service boundary (e.g., HTTP call)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Pass the token to <code>HttpClient.GetAsync(url, cancellationToken)</code> or equivalent. The HTTP client registers a cancellation callback that aborts the underlying socket. For gRPC, pass the token to the call options. For message queues, check the token between message processing iterations.<br />
Cost: if the downstream service has already started processing, canceling the HTTP call does not cancel the server-side work — only the client-side wait.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/CancellationToken.md" class="internal-link">CancellationToken</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism in practice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Concurrency is about responsiveness and non-blocking progress, often with async I/O.<br />
Parallelism is about throughput for CPU-bound workloads by using multiple cores simultaneously.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Concurrency and Parallelism.md" class="internal-link">Concurrency and Parallelism</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do many production outages in .NET systems look like "performance" but are actually concurrency bugs?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because thread starvation, deadlocks, lock contention, and unbounded fan-out all manifest as latency spikes and timeouts before obvious crashes.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Concurrency and Parallelism.md" class="internal-link">Concurrency and Parallelism</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the first decision before choosing a primitive (<code>Task</code>, <code>lock</code>, <code>Parallel</code>, <code>Channel</code>)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Classify the workload as I/O-bound vs CPU-bound and define cancellation/error boundaries. Primitive choice follows that classification.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Concurrency and Parallelism.md" class="internal-link">Concurrency and Parallelism</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is unbounded <code>Task.WhenAll</code> often a bad first choice when calling around 300 external APIs in one request?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because it can overload downstream dependencies, saturate connection pools, and create timeout storms. Bounded fan-out keeps latency gains while preserving system stability.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Concurrency and Parallelism.md" class="internal-link">Concurrency and Parallelism</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">For one requirement ("update shared state safely"), when do you choose <code>lock</code> vs <code>SemaphoreSlim</code> vs <code>Channel&lt;T&gt;</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use <code>lock</code> for short synchronous sections, <code>SemaphoreSlim</code> for async flows that need awaiting, and <code>Channel&lt;T&gt;</code> when you also need queueing, ordering, or backpressure.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Concurrency and Parallelism.md" class="internal-link">Concurrency and Parallelism</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the four Coffman conditions and which is easiest to break in practice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait. Circular wait is easiest to break: enforce a global lock acquisition order across all code paths. This requires discipline but no runtime overhead.<br />
The cost of consistent ordering: you must document and enforce the order, which adds coordination overhead in large codebases.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Deadlocks.md" class="internal-link">Deadlocks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does calling <code>.Result</code> on a <code>Task</code> deadlock in a UI app but not in a console app?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">UI apps have a <code>SynchronizationContext</code> that marshals continuations back to the UI thread. <code>.Result</code> blocks that thread; the continuation needs it to resume — circular wait.<br />
Console apps and ASP.NET Core have no <code>SynchronizationContext</code>, so continuations resume on any pool thread and <code>.Result</code> merely blocks the calling thread without creating a <em>classic</em> cycle.<br />
<strong>But ASP.NET Core is not safe from sync-over-async</strong> — see the ThreadPool-starvation note below. The absence of a <code>SynchronizationContext</code> removes the single-thread cycle, not the risk of hanging the whole app.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Deadlocks.md" class="internal-link">Deadlocks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you diagnose a deadlock in a production .NET service?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Capture a process dump with <code>dotnet-dump collect</code> or <code>procdump</code>. Analyze with <code>dotnet-dump analyze</code> and <code>clrthreads</code>/<code>syncblk</code> commands to find threads blocked on monitors. For async deadlocks, look for threads blocked in <code>.Result</code> or <code>.Wait()</code> while holding a <code>SynchronizationContext</code>.<br />
Cost: dump capture briefly pauses the process; plan for a maintenance window or use a non-blocking snapshot tool.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Deadlocks.md" class="internal-link">Deadlocks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is a named <code>Mutex</code> the right tool in .NET?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When you need to coordinate access across multiple processes on the same machine (for example single-writer protection for shared file/database artifacts).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Mutex.md" class="internal-link">Mutex</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is <code>Mutex</code> often a poor default for web request hot paths?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It is OS-backed and blocking, so heavy contention can increase latency. In-process patterns (<code>lock</code>, <code>SemaphoreSlim</code>, <code>Channel&lt;T&gt;</code>) are usually more efficient.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Mutex.md" class="internal-link">Mutex</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does <code>AbandonedMutexException</code> signal?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A previous owner exited without releasing the mutex, which means exclusive ownership was recovered but shared state may be inconsistent and must be validated.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Mutex.md" class="internal-link">Mutex</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can adding more parallel workers reduce performance?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because of contention, synchronization overhead, cache misses, and context switching once you exceed useful core-level parallelism.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Parallelism.md" class="internal-link">Parallelism</a></em></p>
