</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you decide <code>MaxDegreeOfParallelism</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Start near <code>Environment.ProcessorCount</code>, then tune with workload benchmarks and production telemetry rather than assumptions.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Parallelism.md" class="internal-link">Parallelism</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you avoid PLINQ?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When correctness depends on strict ordering, side-effect sequencing, or when query-level debugging clarity matters more than terse syntax.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Parallelism.md" class="internal-link">Parallelism</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a parallel query be slower than sequential for small inputs?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Partitioning, scheduling, and result merge overhead can dominate when per-element CPU work is too small.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Parallelism.md" class="internal-link">Parallelism</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you choose <code>SemaphoreSlim</code> over <code>lock</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Choose <code>SemaphoreSlim</code> when critical sections include <code>await</code> and you need asynchronous waiting. <code>lock</code> cannot contain <code>await</code> safely.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Semaphore.md" class="internal-link">Semaphore</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is a semaphore useful for fan-out HTTP calls?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It limits in-flight requests, protecting downstream dependencies and your own resources from overload while preserving concurrency.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Semaphore.md" class="internal-link">Semaphore</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What bug pattern most often breaks semaphore-based code?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Missing or unbalanced <code>Release</code> calls. The safe pattern is acquire then <code>try/finally</code> release in the same method scope.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Semaphore.md" class="internal-link">Semaphore</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is <code>Task</code> not equivalent to a thread?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>Task</code> models completion and scheduling, while threads are execution resources. Many async tasks complete I/O without holding a thread during waiting. A single thread can drive thousands of concurrent I/O tasks by processing their continuations sequentially.<br />
Cost of confusing them: over-allocating threads (via <code>Task.Run</code> for I/O) wastes memory and increases context-switching overhead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Tasks.md" class="internal-link">Tasks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should <code>Task.Run</code> be used in ASP.NET Core?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Rarely for request I/O — async I/O APIs already don't block threads. Use <code>Task.Run</code> for CPU-bound work that must be isolated from the request thread (e.g., image processing, heavy computation), ideally with bounded concurrency via <code>SemaphoreSlim</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Tasks.md" class="internal-link">Tasks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is <code>Task.WhenAll</code> usually better than sequential <code>await</code> for independent calls?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Sequential <code>await</code> runs operations one after another — total latency is the sum. <code>Task.WhenAll</code> runs them concurrently — total latency is the slowest. For three 100ms calls: sequential = 300ms, concurrent = ~100ms.<br />
Cost: concurrent fan-out can overwhelm downstream services; bound concurrency when calling external APIs.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Tasks.md" class="internal-link">Tasks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use <code>ValueTask</code> instead of <code>Task</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Only when profiling shows allocation pressure from <code>Task</code> on a hot path where the result is frequently synchronously available (e.g., a cache hit). <code>ValueTask</code> has strict consumption rules and is harder to use correctly. Default to <code>Task</code>; switch to <code>ValueTask</code> only with measurement evidence.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/Tasks.md" class="internal-link">Tasks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is ThreadPool starvation and how does it usually start?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Starvation means queued work cannot get workers quickly enough. It starts when threads block synchronously (<code>.Result</code>, <code>.Wait()</code>, <code>Thread.Sleep</code>) instead of releasing back to the pool. The hill-climbing algorithm injects new threads slowly (~1 per 500ms), so starvation can persist for seconds under burst load.<br />
Cost of fixing: increasing <code>SetMinThreads</code> reduces ramp-up latency but increases baseline memory usage.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/ThreadPool.md" class="internal-link">ThreadPool</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can a fully async app still suffer ThreadPool issues?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Because only part of the call graph may be async. Any synchronous segment on a pool thread — a blocking filter, a sync-over-async call in a library, a <code>Thread.Sleep</code> — holds that thread and reduces pool capacity. Even one blocking call per request can starve the pool under high concurrency.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/ThreadPool.md" class="internal-link">ThreadPool</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is it appropriate to call <code>ThreadPool.SetMinThreads</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When you have measured sustained queue depth under burst load and confirmed the bottleneck is thread injection latency (not CPU saturation or I/O). Increase min threads to match your expected burst concurrency. Do not set it higher than needed — each thread costs ~1MB of stack and adds context-switching overhead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/ThreadPool.md" class="internal-link">ThreadPool</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between <code>Task.Run</code> and <code>ThreadPool.QueueUserWorkItem</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>Task.Run</code> returns a <code>Task</code> that supports <code>await</code>, cancellation, and exception propagation. <code>QueueUserWorkItem</code> is a lower-level fire-and-forget API with no built-in result or error handling. Prefer <code>Task.Run</code> in all modern code; <code>QueueUserWorkItem</code> is a legacy API.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Concurrency and Parallelism/ThreadPool.md" class="internal-link">ThreadPool</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h4>Fundamentals</h4><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between <code>throw;</code> and <code>throw ex;</code> inside a <code>catch</code> block?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>throw;</code> preserves the original stack trace, while <code>throw ex;</code> resets it to the current method. In practice, this means <code>throw;</code> keeps the real failure path for debugging and observability.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Exception Handling.md" class="internal-link">Exception Handling</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you wrap an exception instead of rethrowing it directly?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Wrap when you need to add domain context or translate infrastructure exceptions at a boundary (for example, repository to application service). Keep the original error in <code>InnerException</code> so root cause details remain available.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Exception Handling.md" class="internal-link">Exception Handling</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is throwing from <code>finally</code> considered dangerous?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A throw in <code>finally</code> can replace the original exception and hide root cause information. The safer pattern is cleanup-only logic in <code>finally</code>, with error handling done in <code>catch</code> or at higher boundaries.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Exception Handling.md" class="internal-link">Exception Handling</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When might <code>finally</code> not execute?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When the process terminates abruptly and normal stack unwinding does not happen (for example, crash/kill, <code>Environment.FailFast()</code>, or <code>StackOverflowException</code>).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Exception Handling.md" class="internal-link">Exception Handling</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What types can you use in <code>foreach</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Any type that implements <code>IEnumerable</code> / <code>IEnumerable&lt;T&gt;</code>, or any type that provides the enumerator pattern (<code>GetEnumerator()</code> + <code>Current</code> + <code>MoveNext()</code>).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Foreach.md" class="internal-link">Foreach</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is <code>foreach</code> implemented under the hood?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The compiler lowers it to enumerator code: call <code>GetEnumerator()</code>, loop <code>MoveNext()</code>, read <code>Current</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Foreach.md" class="internal-link">Foreach</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is <code>yield</code> and how does it work?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">It creates an iterator: each <code>yield return</code> produces a value and pauses the method; the method resumes on the next iteration request.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Foreach.md" class="internal-link">Foreach</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why and when should you use <code>yield return</code> instead of returning a materialized collection like <code>List&lt;T&gt;</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use <code>yield return</code> for deferred execution and streaming when consumers may stop early or the sequence is large, because it lowers peak memory usage. Materialize (<code>ToList()</code> / <code>ToArray()</code>) when you need a snapshot, random access or <code>Count</code>, or repeated enumeration without rerunning expensive or side-effectful generation logic.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Foreach.md" class="internal-link">Foreach</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does <code>IEnumerable&lt;string&gt;</code> assign to <code>IEnumerable&lt;object&gt;</code>, but <code>List&lt;string&gt;</code> does not assign to <code>List&lt;object&gt;</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>IEnumerable&lt;out T&gt;</code> is covariant, so it is safe to upcast because it only produces <code>T</code> values.<br />
<code>List&lt;T&gt;</code> is invariant because it both reads and writes <code>T</code>; if <code>List&lt;string&gt;</code> were assignable to <code>List&lt;object&gt;</code>, code could add a non-string object and break type safety.<br />
In practice, expose covariant interfaces (<code>IEnumerable&lt;T&gt;</code>, <code>IReadOnlyList&lt;T&gt;</code>) at API boundaries and keep mutable concrete collections internal.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Generics.md" class="internal-link">Generics</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you mark a generic interface type parameter as <code>out</code> or <code>in</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use <code>out</code> when the type parameter is output-only (returned values), and <code>in</code> when it is input-only (method arguments).<br />
If a parameter must be both consumed and produced, keep it invariant because variance would allow unsafe assignments.<br />
This design choice improves API flexibility without sacrificing compile-time safety.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Generics.md" class="internal-link">Generics</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">A generic method uses <code>default(T)</code> as a fallback value. Why can this be dangerous in production code?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>default(T)</code> can silently map to meaningful domain values (<code>0</code>, <code>DateTime.MinValue</code>, <code>null</code>), so failures look like valid data instead of explicit errors.<br />
Repeated fallback usage can spread bad state across caches, persistence, or downstream services before detection.<br />
Prefer explicit failure paths (<code>TryXxx</code>, exceptions, discriminated result types) and validate invariants at boundaries.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Generics.md" class="internal-link">Generics</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why might you need <code>ref</code> for reference types if reference types are already passed by reference?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Reference type <em>values</em> (the reference) are passed by value. <code>ref</code> is needed when you want the callee to replace the caller's reference (rebind it to a different object).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Methods.md" class="internal-link">Methods</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is an <code>in</code> parameter used for?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">To pass an argument by readonly reference: avoid copies for large structs and communicate that the method should not modify the argument.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Methods.md" class="internal-link">Methods</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are optional parameters in methods?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Parameters with default values that callers can omit; the default is substituted at compile time at the call site.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Methods.md" class="internal-link">Methods</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">In a hierarchy where <code>Animal a = new Dog();</code>, how can you make <code>a.Category()</code> return the derived value, and what does that imply for API design?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Mark the base member as <code>virtual</code> and the derived member as <code>override</code>. That enables polymorphic dispatch by runtime type. It also means the base API explicitly supports extensibility and behavioral substitution.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Methods.md" class="internal-link">Methods</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you prefer <code>new</code> over <code>override</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Prefer <code>new</code> only when polymorphism is not desired and you intentionally want different behavior based on the compile-time reference type (for compatibility or specialized APIs). In most extensible designs, <code>override</code> is the safer default.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Methods.md" class="internal-link">Methods</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">A base method is not marked <code>virtual</code>, but you need derived-specific behavior. What are your options?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Option 1: Change the base API to <code>virtual</code> (best if you own the base type and want polymorphism). Option 2: Hide with <code>new</code> (works, but no polymorphism and can confuse callers). Option 3: Redesign with composition/strategy if inheritance is not a good fit.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Methods.md" class="internal-link">Methods</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you prefer file-scoped namespaces over block-scoped namespaces?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Prefer file-scoped namespaces when a file contains one namespace and regular type declarations, because it reduces indentation and visual noise. Use block-scoped form when a file needs multiple namespace blocks or unusual nesting patterns.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Namespaces.md" class="internal-link">Namespaces</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is reflection often a bad default in performance-critical code?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Reflection shifts work from compile time to runtime: member discovery, argument handling, and dynamic dispatch all add overhead compared to direct calls.<br />
The cost is usually acceptable for startup/configuration paths, but on hot paths it compounds quickly.<br />
Practical rule: cache metadata and use compiled delegates when repeated invocation is required.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Reflection.md" class="internal-link">Reflection</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is an attribute and why is reflection central to attribute-driven frameworks?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">An attribute is metadata attached to code elements (types, methods, properties, parameters).<br />
Frameworks inspect these attributes at runtime (or generation time) to apply conventions like routing, validation, serialization, and test discovery.<br />
Without reflection (or generated equivalents), this metadata would remain passive and unused.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Reflection.md" class="internal-link">Reflection</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you choose reflection versus alternatives like interfaces, generics, or source generators?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Choose reflection when the shape of types/members is unknown until runtime (plugin ecosystems, late-bound tooling, extensibility points).<br />
Prefer interfaces/generics when contracts are known at compile time because they provide stronger safety and better performance.<br />
Prefer source generators in reflection-heavy infrastructure when you need predictable startup, high throughput, or trim/AOT compatibility.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Reflection.md" class="internal-link">Reflection</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h5>Types</h5><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between <code>abstract class</code> and <code>interface</code> with default interface methods (C# 8+)? When would you still choose an abstract class?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Both can define contracts with shared implementation. Key differences:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><strong>State</strong>: abstract classes can have instance fields and constructors; interfaces cannot hold instance state (only static fields).</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Inheritance</strong>: a class can implement many interfaces but inherit from only one class.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Access modifiers</strong>: abstract classes support <code>protected</code>/<code>internal</code> members; interface members are implicitly public (C# 8+ allows explicit modifiers but no <code>protected</code> instance state).</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Performance</strong>: virtual dispatch on abstract class methods is a single vtable lookup; default interface methods may involve additional dispatch overhead.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Use Case</strong>: The default implementation for interface methods have different goal compared to the abstract class implemented methods. While in class, semantically methods providing the basic shared functionality for the delivered classes, that don't have to be overriden. While default implementation exist for making extending the interfaces without breaking the inheritors.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Classes.md" class="internal-link">Classes</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Can a static class implement an interface? Why or why not?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">No. A static class compiles to an <code>abstract sealed</code> class at IL level — it cannot be instantiated, so there is no object to dispatch interface calls through. Interfaces require an instance for virtual dispatch. If you need a "static implementation" of a contract, the patterns are:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use static abstract/virtual interface members (C# 11+) with generics: <code>where T : IMyInterface</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use a singleton instance of a regular class that implements the interface.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use delegates/<code>Func&lt;T&gt;</code> instead of an interface.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Classes.md" class="internal-link">Classes</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">A <code>sealed override</code> stops further overriding, but can a derived class use <code>new</code> to hide the sealed method? What happens at runtime?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Yes, <code>new</code> compiles and hides the sealed method. But the behavior depends on the variable's compile-time type:</p>
<pre class="language-csharp"><code class="language-csharp is-loaded"><span class="token keyword">class</span> <span class="token class-name">Base</span> <span class="token punctuation">{</span> <span class="token keyword">public</span> <span class="token keyword">virtual</span> <span class="token return-type class-name"><span class="token keyword">void</span></span> <span class="token function">Do</span><span class="token punctuation">(</span><span class="token punctuation">)</span> <span class="token operator">=&gt;</span> Console<span class="token punctuation">.</span><span class="token function">WriteLine</span><span class="token punctuation">(</span><span class="token string">"Base"</span><span class="token punctuation">)</span><span class="token punctuation">;</span> <span class="token punctuation">}</span>
<span class="token keyword">class</span> <span class="token class-name">Middle</span> <span class="token punctuation">:</span> <span class="token type-list"><span class="token class-name">Base</span></span> <span class="token punctuation">{</span> <span class="token keyword">public</span> <span class="token keyword">sealed</span> <span class="token keyword">override</span> <span class="token return-type class-name"><span class="token keyword">void</span></span> <span class="token function">Do</span><span class="token punctuation">(</span><span class="token punctuation">)</span> <span class="token operator">=&gt;</span> Console<span class="token punctuation">.</span><span class="token function">WriteLine</span><span class="token punctuation">(</span><span class="token string">"Middle"</span><span class="token punctuation">)</span><span class="token punctuation">;</span> <span class="token punctuation">}</span>
<span class="token keyword">class</span> <span class="token class-name">Bottom</span> <span class="token punctuation">:</span> <span class="token type-list"><span class="token class-name">Middle</span></span> <span class="token punctuation">{</span> <span class="token keyword">public</span> <span class="token keyword">new</span> <span class="token return-type class-name"><span class="token keyword">void</span></span> <span class="token function">Do</span><span class="token punctuation">(</span><span class="token punctuation">)</span> <span class="token operator">=&gt;</span> Console<span class="token punctuation">.</span><span class="token function">WriteLine</span><span class="token punctuation">(</span><span class="token string">"Bottom"</span><span class="token punctuation">)</span><span class="token punctuation">;</span> <span class="token punctuation">}</span>

<span class="token class-name">Bottom</span> b <span class="token operator">=</span> <span class="token keyword">new</span> <span class="token constructor-invocation class-name">Bottom</span><span class="token punctuation">(</span><span class="token punctuation">)</span><span class="token punctuation">;</span>
b<span class="token punctuation">.</span><span class="token function">Do</span><span class="token punctuation">(</span><span class="token punctuation">)</span><span class="token punctuation">;</span>           <span class="token comment">// "Bottom" — resolved at compile time as Bottom.Do</span> <span class="token class-name">Middle</span> m <span class="token operator">=</span> b<span class="token punctuation">;</span>
m<span class="token punctuation">.</span><span class="token function">Do</span><span class="token punctuation">(</span><span class="token punctuation">)</span><span class="token punctuation">;</span>           <span class="token comment">// "Middle" — virtual dispatch resolves to Middle.Do (sealed)</span> <span class="token class-name">Base</span> x <span class="token operator">=</span> b<span class="token punctuation">;</span>
x<span class="token punctuation">.</span><span class="token function">Do</span><span class="token punctuation">(</span><span class="token punctuation">)</span><span class="token punctuation">;</span>           <span class="token comment">// "Middle" — same virtual dispatch</span> </code><button class="copy-code-button"><svg class="svg-icon lucide-copy" stroke-linejoin="round" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-width="2" stroke="currentColor" fill="none" viewBox="0 0 24 24" height="24" width="24" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect ry="2" rx="2" height="14" width="14" y="8" x="8"/><path d="M4 16c-1.1 0-2-.9-2-2V4c0-1.1.9-2 2-2h10c1.1 0 2 .9 2 2"/></svg></button></pre>

<p dir="auto">The <code>new</code> method is completely invisible to polymorphic code. This is almost always a design smell — if you need to change behavior, the method should not have been sealed, or you should use composition instead.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Classes.md" class="internal-link">Classes</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Can you have an abstract sealed class? What about in IL?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">In C# source code, you cannot write <code>abstract sealed class</code> — the compiler rejects it as contradictory. However, at the IL level, <strong>static classes</strong> compile to exactly <code>abstract sealed</code>. The CLR treats <code>abstract sealed</code> as "cannot be instantiated and cannot be inherited." So every <code>static class</code> in C# is literally an <code>abstract sealed</code> class in metadata. You can verify this with <code>ildasm</code> or reflection: <code>typeof(Math).IsAbstract &amp;&amp; typeof(Math).IsSealed</code> is <code>true</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Classes.md" class="internal-link">Classes</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can <code>partial</code> be dangerous with source generators? Give a concrete scenario.</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Partial classes merge at compile time, and source generators can add members to your type that you do not see in your source file. Dangers:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Name collisions</strong>: a generator adds a method <code>Validate()</code> and you also write <code>Validate()</code> — compile error with a confusing message pointing at generated code.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Implicit behavior changes</strong>: a generator adds <code>INotifyPropertyChanged</code> implementation and overrides <code>Equals</code>. Your tests pass locally but break in CI where a different generator version runs.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Debugging opacity</strong>: stepping through code jumps into generated files that may not be in source control.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Partial method removal</strong>: if a partial method (without accessibility modifier) has no implementing declaration, the compiler silently removes all calls to it. If you forget to generate the body, the call vanishes with no warning.<br />
Best practice: always inspect generated output (visible in IDE under Dependencies and Analyzers), and write tests that verify generator-dependent behavior explicitly.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Classes.md" class="internal-link">Classes</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Two classes have identical fields and values. You compare them with <code>==</code>. Why is it <code>false</code>, and what are the three ways to fix it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>==</code> compares references by default for classes — two separate <code>new</code> calls produce different heap objects with different references.<br />
Fixes:</p>
<ol>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Override <code>Equals</code>/<code>GetHashCode</code> and overload <code>==</code>/<code>!=</code></strong> — full manual control, but tedious and error-prone.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Implement <code>IEquatable&lt;T&gt;</code></strong> — avoids boxing in generic code and gives a clean <code>Equals(T)</code> method. Still need to overload <code>==</code>.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Use <code>record class</code> instead</strong> — the compiler generates value-based <code>Equals</code>, <code>GetHashCode</code>, <code>==</code>, and <code>!=</code> automatically. This is the recommended approach for data-carrier types.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Classes.md" class="internal-link">Classes</a></em></li>
</ol>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">A static constructor throws an exception. What happens on subsequent accesses to that type?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The runtime marks the type as permanently broken. Every subsequent attempt to access any member of the type throws a <code>TypeInitializationException</code> wrapping the original exception — even if the condition that caused the failure has been resolved. The type cannot be re-initialized for the lifetime of the AppDomain (or AssemblyLoadContext in .NET Core). This is why static constructors should be kept minimal and defensive because failures are unrecoverable.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Classes.md" class="internal-link">Classes</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does a delegate compile to in IL/runtime terms?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A delegate declaration becomes a sealed type derived from <code>System.MulticastDelegate</code> with <code>Invoke</code>, <code>BeginInvoke</code>, and <code>EndInvoke</code> metadata. Delegate instances carry a target object (or null for static methods), a method pointer, and optionally an invocation list. In modern .NET (6+), calling delegate <code>BeginInvoke</code>/<code>EndInvoke</code> is not supported and throws <code>PlatformNotSupportedException</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Delegates.md" class="internal-link">Delegates</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you isolate failures in a multicast delegate so one bad subscriber does not break the rest?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Iterate <code>GetInvocationList()</code>, cast each entry to the concrete delegate type, invoke in a per-handler <code>try/catch</code>, and optionally aggregate errors. Direct multicast invocation stops at first exception.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Delegates.md" class="internal-link">Delegates</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is using <code>Func&lt;Task&gt;</code> in multicast pipelines often wrong for async fan-out?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Direct multicast invocation returns only the last task, so earlier handlers can run unobserved. For async fan-out, iterate <code>GetInvocationList()</code> and await each task explicitly (sequentially or with <code>Task.WhenAll</code>), depending on ordering requirements.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Delegates.md" class="internal-link">Delegates</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How is an event different from a delegate field in terms of access control?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">An event exposes only <code>add</code>/<code>remove</code> from outside the declaring type. A delegate field can be invoked, replaced, or nulled by external callers. Events preserve publisher ownership of invocation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Events.md" class="internal-link">Events</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do event leaks happen, and how do you prevent them?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The publisher keeps strong references to subscriber handlers. If the publisher outlives subscribers, those subscribers cannot be garbage-collected. Prevent with explicit unsubscribe (<code>Dispose</code>), weak-event pattern, or scoped subscription helpers.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Events.md" class="internal-link">Events</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you handle exceptions in event subscribers without losing later handlers?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Copy the invocation list using <code>GetInvocationList()</code> and invoke handlers individually in <code>try/catch</code>. Direct event invocation stops at first exception.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Events.md" class="internal-link">Events</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">In <code>record Wrapper(List&lt;int&gt; Items)</code>, if <code>var b = a with { };</code> and an item is added to <code>b.Items</code>, does <code>a</code> observe the change, and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Yes — <code>a</code> sees the change. <code>with</code> performs a <em>shallow copy</em>: it copies references, not the underlying objects. Both <code>a.Items</code> and <code>b.Items</code> point to the same <code>List&lt;int&gt;</code> instance. Furthermore, <code>a == b</code> was <code>true</code> before the mutation (same reference in both), but the equality check still uses <code>List&lt;T&gt;.Equals</code> which is reference equality — so it remains <code>true</code> even after the content changes. To get proper deep value semantics, use immutable collections (<code>ImmutableList&lt;T&gt;</code>, <code>ImmutableArray&lt;T&gt;</code>) or override <code>Equals</code> to compare content.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Records.md" class="internal-link">Records</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose <code>record class</code> over <code>readonly record struct</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Choose <code>record class</code> when:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The data contains variable-length reference types (strings, collections) — the struct would not avoid heap allocation anyway.</li>
<li dir="auto">The type participates in an inheritance hierarchy (only record classes support inheritance).</li>
<li dir="auto">The data is large (more than ~16 bytes of value-type fields) — copy cost outweighs GC cost.</li>
<li dir="auto">You need <code>null</code> semantics (e.g. optional return values without <code>Nullable&lt;T&gt;</code>).</li>
</ul>
<p dir="auto">Choose <code>readonly record struct</code> when:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The data is small and all fields are value types — avoids heap allocation entirely.</li>
<li dir="auto">You are on a hot path where GC pressure matters (e.g. tight loops, high-throughput pipelines).</li>
<li dir="auto">No inheritance is needed.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Records.md" class="internal-link">Records</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">If <code>Equals</code> on a positional record is overridden to ignore one property, does <code>GetHashCode</code> still include that property, and what breaks?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Yes — the compiler-generated <code>GetHashCode</code> includes all positional properties regardless of your <code>Equals</code> override. This breaks the contract: two objects that are "equal" (by your custom <code>Equals</code>) may have different hash codes, causing them to land in different buckets in <code>Dictionary</code>, <code>HashSet</code>, or any hash-based collection. Lookups silently fail. <strong>Rule</strong>: whenever you override <code>Equals</code>, always override <code>GetHashCode</code> to match. The compiler emits a warning (CS8851) if you override one without the other, but it is only a warning — not an error.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Records.md" class="internal-link">Records</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Can a record struct be used as a <code>Dictionary</code> key safely? What do you need to watch out for?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Yes, <code>record struct</code> works well as a dictionary key because the compiler generates value-based <code>Equals</code> and <code>GetHashCode</code> by default. Watch out for:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Mutable record structs</strong> — if a key is mutated after insertion, its hash code changes and it becomes unreachable in the dictionary. Use <code>readonly record struct</code> for keys.</li>
<li dir="auto"><strong>Reference-type properties</strong> — if the record struct contains a reference-type property (e.g. <code>string[]</code>), the generated <code>GetHashCode</code> calls that property's <code>GetHashCode</code>, which for arrays is reference-based (not content-based). Two structurally identical keys with different array instances will hash differently. Override <code>GetHashCode</code> or use immutable value-semantic collections.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Records.md" class="internal-link">Records</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you choose <code>StringBuilder</code> over <code>string</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use <code>StringBuilder</code> for iterative construction (loops, batched appends, streaming transforms) where many intermediate strings would otherwise be allocated.</li>
<li dir="auto">Prefer interpolation/concatenation for one-off formatting with a small number of values because readability is usually better.</li>
<li dir="auto">In hot paths, benchmark both options and pre-size <code>StringBuilder</code> capacity to reduce buffer growth and copying.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Strings.md" class="internal-link">Strings</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can <code>ReferenceEquals(a, b)</code> be <code>false</code> even when <code>a == b</code> is <code>true</code> for strings?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto"><code>==</code> for strings compares content, while <code>ReferenceEquals</code> checks object identity.</li>
<li dir="auto">Two strings can contain identical text but be different objects (for example, literal vs runtime-composed value).</li>
<li dir="auto">Use <code>ReferenceEquals</code> only for diagnostics/allocation analysis, not for business equality logic.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Strings.md" class="internal-link">Strings</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How should string comparisons be written in production code?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Always call APIs that accept <code>StringComparison</code> (<code>string.Equals</code>, <code>StartsWith</code>, <code>EndsWith</code>, <code>IndexOf</code>) instead of culture-implicit overloads.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use <code>Ordinal</code>/<code>OrdinalIgnoreCase</code> for identifiers, protocol values, keys, and security-sensitive comparisons.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use culture-aware comparison only for user-facing natural-language text where locale behavior is expected.</li>
<li dir="auto">Keep the comparison policy explicit and consistent across reads/writes to avoid cross-locale bugs.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Strings.md" class="internal-link">Strings</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">For a high-throughput service processing 100k messages per second (Id, Timestamp, Status), would class or struct be the better model, and why?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A <code>readonly struct</code> (or <code>readonly record struct</code>) is the best fit:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Total payload is roughly 28 bytes before alignment (Guid 16 + DateTime 8 + enum ~4), so it is above the strict 16-byte heuristic and should be benchmarked in your workload.</li>
<li dir="auto">Value semantics avoid a separate object allocation per message when values stay inline and are not boxed/captured, reducing GC pressure at 100k/s.</li>
<li dir="auto"><code>readonly</code> prevents accidental mutation.</li>
<li dir="auto">If the message grows or needs richer behavior/reference sharing, a class can become the better tradeoff.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Structs.md" class="internal-link">Structs</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does mutating a struct returned from a property or indexer not compile (or silently do nothing)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Property/indexer access usually returns a value copy, not a reference to the original storage.</li>
<li dir="auto">Mutating that copy would be discarded immediately, so the compiler blocks common forms (for example CS1612 scenarios).</li>
<li dir="auto">Even when a pattern compiles, mutations can affect only the temporary copy, not the original struct instance.</li>
<li dir="auto">Mitigate with <code>readonly struct</code> for safer value semantics, or expose <code>ref</code>/<code>ref readonly</code> returns when true by-reference behavior is required.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Structs.md" class="internal-link">Structs</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does <code>ValueType.Equals</code> perform so poorly on structs with reference-type fields? What should you do about it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Default value-type equality is field-by-field and can be expensive in hot paths.</li>
<li dir="auto">Depending on type shape/runtime path, equality can include reflection-like overhead and extra indirection.</li>
<li dir="auto">Using such structs as keys in dictionaries/sets amplifies the cost because equality and hashing are called frequently.</li>
<li dir="auto">Implement <code>IEquatable&lt;T&gt;</code> and override <code>Equals</code>/<code>GetHashCode</code> to define stable semantics and avoid hidden performance costs.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Structs.md" class="internal-link">Structs</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can updating a value-type item inside <code>foreach</code> fail to persist, and what are safe fixes?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">The loop variable was a copy of a value type, so mutations were applied to the copy.</li>
<li dir="auto">The same issue appears when mutating structs returned by properties, because property access often returns a copy.</li>
<li dir="auto">Fix by making the struct immutable and replacing whole values, or by redesigning to avoid mutable structs in those paths.</li>
<li dir="auto">If mutation is required, use APIs with explicit <code>ref</code> semantics very carefully.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Types.md" class="internal-link">Types</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Where does boxing usually sneak in, and what is the practical mitigation in production code?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Boxing happens when a value type is converted to <code>object</code> or interface-typed APIs.</li>
<li dir="auto">Each boxing operation allocates and can increase GC pressure in hot paths.</li>
<li dir="auto">Prefer generic APIs (<code>List&lt;T&gt;</code>, <code>EqualityComparer&lt;T&gt;</code>, generic interfaces) so calls stay strongly typed.</li>
<li dir="auto">Verify with profiling before optimizing, then remove high-frequency boxing boundaries.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Types.md" class="internal-link">Types</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What criteria should drive choosing between <code>struct</code>, <code>class</code>, and <code>record class</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use <code>struct</code> for tiny immutable value objects when copy semantics are desired and boxing is controlled.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use <code>class</code> for identity-rich entities where reference identity and lifecycle matter.</li>
<li dir="auto">Use <code>record class</code> for data-centric models where value-based equality improves correctness.</li>
<li dir="auto">Validate the choice against mutation rules, size/copy costs, and equality requirements.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/CSharp/Fundamentals/Types/Types.md" class="internal-link">Types</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Other</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What problem did OWIN solve?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">It standardized the server-application boundary so middleware/app components were portable across hosts.</li>
<li dir="auto">It enabled a composable pipeline model independent of <code>System.Web</code> internals.</li>
<li dir="auto">It reduced host lock-in for teams maintaining ASP.NET-era applications.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Other/OWIN.md" class="internal-link">OWIN</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How should incremental migration vs full rewrite be chosen when modernizing an OWIN app under strict uptime requirements?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Prefer incremental migration when downtime risk and release pressure are high; isolate seams (auth, API endpoints, cross-cutting middleware) and move components in slices.</li>
<li dir="auto">Prefer rewrite when current architecture blocks critical goals (performance, security posture, operability) and business can fund a transition window.</li>
<li dir="auto">Decide with hard constraints: SLA tolerance, test coverage quality, team expertise, and ability to run parallel environments safely.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Other/OWIN.md" class="internal-link">OWIN</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use SignalR versus polling or server-sent events?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use SignalR when you need bidirectional real-time communication (chat, live dashboards, collaborative editing). SignalR abstracts the transport layer (WebSockets with SSE and long polling as fallbacks).<br />
Use server-sent events (SSE) when you only need server-to-client push and want a simpler protocol. Use polling when real-time is not actually needed and you want maximum simplicity and cacheability.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Other/Other.md" class="internal-link">Other</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why would a .NET team still need to understand OWIN today?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">OWIN matters for teams maintaining or migrating legacy ASP.NET Framework applications. Understanding the OWIN middleware pipeline model helps when migrating to ASP.NET Core's similar but distinct middleware pipeline, and when troubleshooting Katana-hosted services still running in production.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Other/Other.md" class="internal-link">Other</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is SignalR a good fit?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use SignalR when clients need server-pushed updates with low latency (chat, collaboration, live telemetry).</li>
<li dir="auto">It is most valuable when update frequency is high enough that polling wastes bandwidth or increases staleness.</li>
<li dir="auto">If updates are rare and latency tolerance is high, simpler HTTP polling can be cheaper to operate.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Other/SignalR.md" class="internal-link">SignalR</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the first scaling problem you will hit?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Cross-node message fan-out: messages sent on one server do not automatically reach clients connected to another node.</li>
<li dir="auto">Plan scale-out early with Azure SignalR Service or a supported backplane, then validate routing under load tests.</li>
<li dir="auto">Also validate sticky-session requirements for your chosen topology and transport strategy.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Other/SignalR.md" class="internal-link">SignalR</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are SignalR groups not enough for authorization?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Groups control message routing, not permission checks.</li>
<li dir="auto">Membership can change/rejoin over reconnect paths, so relying on groups alone risks privilege drift.</li>
<li dir="auto">Enforce security with authentication and policy/role-based authorization on hub methods.<br />
