<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Other/SignalR.md" class="internal-link">SignalR</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h3>Runtime</h3><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is managed vs unmanaged code? Why does unmanaged interop require careful lifetime management?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Managed code runs under the .NET runtime (CLR) and benefits from runtime services like type safety checks, exception handling, garbage collection, and JIT/AOT compilation.<br />
Unmanaged code runs directly as native machine code under the OS (for example, C/C++ binaries). It does not run under the CLR and typically requires explicit resource and lifetime management.<br />
P/Invoke calls into native libraries require marshaling data across the managed/unmanaged boundary, which adds overhead and risks memory corruption if signatures are wrong.<br />
Use <code>SafeHandle</code> (not raw <code>IntPtr</code>) to wrap unmanaged handles — it ensures deterministic release even if exceptions occur and prevents handle recycling attacks.<br />
Interop lets you reuse native libraries and OS APIs, but every boundary crossing costs marshaling overhead and opens the door to bugs (dangling pointers, double-free) the GC cannot prevent.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Common Language Runtime.md" class="internal-link">Common Language Runtime</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the CLR and IL? How does JIT compilation affect startup vs steady-state performance, and when is NativeAOT a better choice?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The CLR (Common Language Runtime) is the execution engine of .NET. It loads assemblies, verifies and executes IL, compiles IL to native code (JIT or AOT), manages memory (GC), handles exceptions, supports threading and interop, and provides other runtime services.<br />
IL (also called CIL or MSIL) is the CPU-independent intermediate instruction set produced by .NET language compilers and stored in assemblies together with metadata. The CLR turns IL into native code for the current platform.<br />
JIT compilation adds latency on first method call. Tiered compilation mitigates this: Tier 0 compiles quickly with minimal optimization, then hot methods are recompiled at Tier 1 with full optimization — giving fast startup and high steady-state throughput.<br />
NativeAOT eliminates JIT entirely by compiling to native code at publish time — fastest startup, smallest working set, but no runtime code generation (limits reflection, dynamic assembly loading, and some serialization patterns).<br />
JIT with tiered compilation is the right default for long-running server workloads; NativeAOT wins for CLI tools, serverless cold-start SLAs, and embedded scenarios where startup and binary size matter more than runtime flexibility.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Common Language Runtime.md" class="internal-link">Common Language Runtime</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose NativeAOT over JIT compilation?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">NativeAOT eliminates JIT startup cost and produces a self-contained native binary — ideal for CLI tools, serverless functions with cold-start SLAs, or embedded scenarios. The cost is longer publish time, larger binary, and loss of runtime reflection-heavy features (dynamic code generation, some serialization patterns). For long-running server workloads, JIT with tiered compilation typically wins on peak throughput.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Common Language Runtime.md" class="internal-link">Common Language Runtime</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does the GC use generations?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Most objects die young (short-lived allocations like request-scoped objects). Generational GC exploits this by collecting Gen 0 (newest, smallest) most frequently and cheaply. Long-lived objects are promoted to Gen 1 and Gen 2, which are collected less often. This reduces the cost of GC for the common case while still reclaiming long-lived garbage.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Common Language Runtime.md" class="internal-link">Common Language Runtime</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does .NET's generational GC work? Why does it use generations, and what are the main tuning tradeoffs?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The GC is the .NET runtime's automatic memory manager for managed objects. It periodically finds objects that are no longer reachable from GC roots, reclaims their memory, and (on the SOH) typically compacts surviving objects to reduce fragmentation.<br />
The GC is generational (Gen 0/1/2): most collections are small and fast, while full collections are less frequent. Generations exploit the generational hypothesis — most objects die young — so collecting Gen 0 frequently and cheaply avoids scanning the entire heap.<br />
Workstation GC (default) runs collections on the allocating thread; Server GC uses dedicated threads per logical processor for higher throughput but more memory overhead.<br />
Background GC (default for Gen 2) allows Gen 0/1 collections to proceed during a long Gen 2 collection, reducing pause times for latency-sensitive workloads.<br />
Server GC maximizes throughput for multi-core services but uses more memory per heap; Workstation GC minimizes footprint for client apps and small containers. Choose based on whether the workload cares more about pause duration or throughput.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Garbage Collector.md" class="internal-link">Garbage Collector</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are the Small Object Heap (SOH) and the Large Object Heap (LOH)?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The SOH stores most objects (typically smaller than ~85,000 bytes) and is compacted regularly.<br />
The LOH stores large allocations (typically 85,000 bytes and above, often large arrays). It is collected with Gen 2 and can become fragmented; compaction behavior differs from the SOH and is more expensive.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Garbage Collector.md" class="internal-link">Garbage Collector</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a memory leak? Is it possible in .NET? How?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A memory leak is memory that is no longer needed but cannot be reclaimed, so the process keeps growing over time.<br />
Yes, it is possible in .NET:</p>
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Managed leaks: objects stay reachable (for example via static caches, event subscriptions, long-lived collections), so GC cannot collect them.</li>
<li dir="auto">Unmanaged leaks: native memory/handles are allocated (directly or indirectly) and not released (for example, missing <code>Dispose()</code> / <code>using</code>).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Memory Leaks.md" class="internal-link">Memory Leaks</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the call stack? Can it overflow? What happens then?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The call stack is a per-thread LIFO memory region that stores stack frames for active method calls (return address, parameters, locals, etc.).<br />
It can overflow (for example, due to deep or infinite recursion or very large stack allocations). In .NET this typically results in <code>StackOverflowException</code>, and the process is terminated (it cannot be reliably handled).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Memory Leaks.md" class="internal-link">Memory Leaks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why do we need <code>using {}</code> if there is a GC?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>using</code> provides deterministic cleanup for resources that are not just managed memory (file handles, sockets, OS handles, unmanaged buffers). GC runs non-deterministically and does not guarantee timely release of such resources.<br />
The <code>using</code> statement compiles to <code>try/finally</code> so <code>Dispose()</code> is called even when exceptions occur.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Memory Leaks.md" class="internal-link">Memory Leaks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What are <code>IDisposable</code> and <code>Finalize</code>?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>IDisposable</code> is an interface for explicit, deterministic cleanup via <code>Dispose()</code>.<br />
<code>Finalize</code> (a finalizer, written as <code>~TypeName()</code> in C#) is called by the GC for objects that have a finalizer, but it is non-deterministic and adds overhead. Finalizers should only be used to release unmanaged resources, and <code>Dispose()</code> typically calls <code>GC.SuppressFinalize(this)</code>.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Memory Leaks.md" class="internal-link">Memory Leaks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the disposable (dispose) pattern?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A standard way to implement <code>IDisposable</code> so both explicit cleanup (<code>Dispose()</code>) and (optionally) finalization are supported.<br />
Typical shape: <code>Dispose()</code> calls <code>Dispose(true)</code> and then <code>GC.SuppressFinalize(this)</code>; a finalizer (if needed) calls <code>Dispose(false)</code>; <code>Dispose(bool disposing)</code> releases unmanaged resources and, when <code>disposing</code> is true, also disposes managed fields.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Memory Leaks.md" class="internal-link">Memory Leaks</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does the CLR do when your application starts, and why does startup behavior matter?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The CLR loads assemblies, verifies IL safety, JIT-compiles methods on first call (or uses tiered compilation to optimize hot paths later), sets up the GC, and initializes the thread pool.<br />
This matters because startup latency, JIT warmup effects, and thread pool sizing all affect real-world behavior — especially for serverless and containerized deployments with cold starts.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Runtime.md" class="internal-link">Runtime</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does garbage collection affect production latency, and what are the main tuning levers?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">GC pauses application threads (in workstation GC) or background-collects (in server GC) to reclaim memory. Gen0/Gen1 collections are fast; Gen2 collections are expensive and can cause visible latency spikes.<br />
Main tuning levers: choose Server vs Workstation GC mode, minimize large object heap allocations (objects over 85KB), reduce Gen2 promotion rates by controlling object lifetimes, and use <code>GC.TryStartNoGCRegion</code> for latency-critical paths.<br />
Always measure with GC event traces (dotnet-counters, PerfView) before tuning — premature GC optimization often makes things worse.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Runtime.md" class="internal-link">Runtime</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Can managed code have memory leaks, and what are the common causes?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Yes. Common causes: event handler subscriptions never unsubscribed, static collections that grow indefinitely, closures capturing references unexpectedly, and finalizer queue stalls blocking reclamation.<br />
These are not OS-level leaks but logical leaks — the GC cannot collect objects that are still reachable through a live reference chain, even if the application no longer needs them.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Programming/NET/Runtime/Runtime.md" class="internal-link">Runtime</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="sdlc">SDLC</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When does BDD overhead outweigh its benefits?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">BDD adds overhead: Gherkin scenarios must be written and maintained, step definitions must be kept in sync, and the collaboration process takes time. The overhead is not worth it for: pure technical components with no business language (algorithms, infrastructure), small teams where developers and stakeholders communicate directly without formal scenarios, and rapidly changing requirements where maintaining <code>.feature</code> files becomes a burden. BDD earns its cost when: business rules are complex and misunderstanding them is expensive, cross-functional alignment is genuinely needed, and scenarios serve as living documentation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/SDLC/BDD.md" class="internal-link">BDD</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How does BDD differ from TDD, and are they complementary?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">TDD focuses on unit behavior: does the code work correctly? Tests are written by developers in code. BDD focuses on system behavior: does the system meet requirements from the user's perspective? Scenarios are written in business language (Gherkin) collaboratively with stakeholders. They are complementary: use TDD for unit-level design and fast feedback loops; use BDD for acceptance criteria and cross-functional alignment. A well-tested system uses both — TDD for the inner loop, BDD for the outer loop.<br />
<em><a href="Home/SDLC/BDD.md" class="internal-link">BDD</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is 'specification by example' and how does it reduce requirement ambiguity?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Specification by example replaces abstract requirement statements ('the system should handle invalid input') with concrete examples ('given a blank email field, when I submit the form, then I see the error message X'). Concrete examples are unambiguous: they can be automated as tests and verified against the running system. Abstract requirements are interpreted differently by developers, testers, and business stakeholders. The Three Amigos practice (developer + tester + business) produces examples collaboratively, surfacing misunderstandings before implementation begins.<br />
<em><a href="Home/SDLC/BDD.md" class="internal-link">BDD</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are story points measured in relative complexity rather than hours?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Hours are absolute and vary by person, day, and context. A senior engineer estimates 2 hours; a junior estimates 8 hours for the same task. Story points measure relative complexity: a 5-point story is roughly twice as complex as a 2-point story, regardless of who does it. A team's velocity (points per sprint) emerges from historical data and automatically accounts for team-specific factors like skill level, interruptions, and tech debt. Treating story points as hours destroys this abstraction and makes velocity meaningless.<br />
<em><a href="Home/SDLC/Estimation Techniques.md" class="internal-link">Estimation Techniques</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the most common estimation mistake and how do you avoid it?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Treating story points as hours ('8 points = 8 hours'). This forces estimators to think in absolute time, reintroduces the person-dependency problem, and makes velocity comparisons between teams meaningless. The fix: calibrate story points against reference stories ('this is a 2-point story because it's similar to the login feature we built last sprint'), track velocity over 3-5 sprints to establish a baseline, and use that baseline for capacity planning — never convert points to hours.<br />
<em><a href="Home/SDLC/Estimation Techniques.md" class="internal-link">Estimation Techniques</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h1 id="security">Security</h1><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is blockchain justified over a traditional database?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Blockchain is justified when you need a shared ledger across mutually distrusting parties with no central authority and no single party can be trusted to maintain the record. If all parties trust a central authority, a traditional database with append-only audit logging is simpler, faster, and GDPR-compliant. The cost of blockchain — low throughput, immutability conflicts with erasure rights, consensus overhead — is only worth paying when decentralization is a hard requirement.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Block-chain.md" class="internal-link">Block-chain</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does PoW waste energy and what is the alternative?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Proof-of-Work requires miners to perform computationally expensive hash searches to earn the right to add a block. This energy expenditure is the security mechanism — attacking the chain requires outspending honest miners. Proof-of-Stake replaces energy expenditure with economic stake: validators lock up tokens as collateral and lose them if they act dishonestly. PoS achieves similar security guarantees at a fraction of the energy cost, which is why Ethereum migrated from PoW to PoS in 2022.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Block-chain.md" class="internal-link">Block-chain</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does signing use the private key to encrypt the hash, not the public key?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The private key is secret — only the signer can produce a valid signature. Anyone with the public key can verify it, but only the private key holder can create it. Reversing this would let anyone forge signatures.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Digital Signature.md" class="internal-link">Digital Signature</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between signing and encryption?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Signing proves authenticity and integrity — it does not hide the content. Encryption hides the content but does not prove who sent it. TLS uses both: the server certificate is signed (proves identity), and the session data is encrypted (provides confidentiality).<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Digital Signature.md" class="internal-link">Digital Signature</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is ECDSA preferred over RSA for new systems?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">ECDSA produces shorter signatures (256-bit key gives equivalent security to 3072-bit RSA) and is faster to compute. RSA is still widely used for compatibility, but ECDSA is the modern default for JWT signing (ES256) and TLS certificates.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Digital Signature.md" class="internal-link">Digital Signature</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use symmetric vs asymmetric encryption?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Use symmetric (AES-256-GCM) for bulk data encryption — it is 10-100x faster than asymmetric. Use asymmetric (RSA, ECDH) for key exchange and digital signatures, not for encrypting data directly. In practice, use hybrid encryption: asymmetric to exchange a symmetric key, then symmetric for the data. This is exactly what TLS does.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Encryption.md" class="internal-link">Encryption</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is envelope encryption and when is it used?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Envelope encryption uses two keys: a Data Encryption Key (DEK) encrypts the data; a Key Encryption Key (KEK) encrypts the DEK. The encrypted DEK is stored alongside the ciphertext. The KEK lives in a key management service (Azure Key Vault, AWS KMS) and never leaves it. This pattern is used in cloud storage (Azure Blob Storage, S3 server-side encryption) and allows key rotation without re-encrypting all data — you only re-encrypt the DEK.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Encryption.md" class="internal-link">Encryption</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why can't you use SHA-256 directly to store passwords?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">SHA-256 is engineered to be <em>fast</em>, which is exactly wrong for passwords: an attacker who steals the hash database can compute billions of guesses per second and crack weak/common passwords quickly, and without a salt they can use precomputed rainbow tables. Password hashing needs a <strong>slow, memory-hard, salted</strong> function (Argon2id, bcrypt, scrypt, PBKDF2) with a tunable work factor so each guess is expensive and every user's hash is unique.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Hashing.md" class="internal-link">Hashing</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the difference between a hash, an HMAC, and a digital signature?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A <strong>hash</strong> is keyless and only detects accidental change — an attacker can alter data and recompute it. An <strong>HMAC</strong> mixes in a shared secret key, so it proves integrity <em>and</em> authenticity between parties who both hold the key. A <strong>digital signature</strong> hashes then signs with a <em>private</em> key, so anyone with the public key can verify, adding <strong>non-repudiation</strong> (the signer can't deny it) that HMAC lacks because its key is shared. Escalating from hash → HMAC → signature trades simplicity for stronger guarantees.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Hashing.md" class="internal-link">Hashing</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What does a salt protect against, and why must it be unique per user?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A salt is a per-password random value stored with the hash. It defeats <strong>rainbow tables</strong> (precomputed digest→password lookups can't be built without knowing each salt) and ensures that two users who pick the same password produce <em>different</em> stored hashes, so a cracker can't crack many accounts at once or spot shared passwords. A single global salt would still let one rainbow table target the whole database — uniqueness per user is what forces the attacker to attack each hash individually.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Hashing.md" class="internal-link">Hashing</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is a JWT token and why is it not encrypted by default?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">JWT is a compact token format: <code>header.payload.signature</code> (base64url encoded).</li>
<li dir="auto">Signed (JWS) means the signature proves integrity and issuer, but the payload is readable by anyone with the token.</li>
<li dir="auto">Encryption (JWE) is a separate standard that wraps the JWT in an encrypted envelope.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: signed-only JWTs are simpler and faster to validate; JWE adds encryption overhead and key management complexity.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/JWT Bearer.md" class="internal-link">JWT Bearer</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why should JWTs have short expiry times?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">JWTs are stateless — the server cannot revoke them without a blacklist (which defeats the stateless benefit).</li>
<li dir="auto">A stolen JWT is valid until it expires. Short expiry (15-60 min) limits the damage window.</li>
<li dir="auto">Refresh tokens (stored server-side) allow issuing new JWTs without re-authentication.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: short expiry requires refresh token infrastructure; long expiry is simpler but riskier.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/JWT Bearer.md" class="internal-link">JWT Bearer</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">A secret was accidentally committed and pushed. What's the correct response?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Treat it as compromised and <strong>rotate (revoke + reissue) it immediately</strong> — that's the only real fix, because the value persists in Git history, clones, forks, and CI caches even after you delete it. Rewriting history (e.g. <code>git filter-repo</code>) is useful cleanup to stop further exposure, but it does not un-leak a secret that others may already have. Afterward, add push protection / secret scanning so it can't recur.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Secrets Management.md" class="internal-link">Secrets Management</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the "secret zero" (bootstrapping) problem and how is it solved?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">If all your secrets live in a vault, the app still needs <em>one</em> credential to authenticate to the vault — secret zero. Storing it just moves the problem. The modern solution is <strong>platform-provided identity</strong>: a managed identity (Azure) / IAM role (AWS) / workload identity (Kubernetes/GCP) that the infrastructure vouches for, letting the workload obtain a short-lived token with no stored secret. CI pipelines do the same via OIDC federation.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Secrets Management.md" class="internal-link">Secrets Management</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are environment variables a weak place to store secrets despite being common?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">They keep secrets out of the image (good, and the 12-factor standard), but the whole process and any child processes can read them, they're easy to leak accidentally (a crash dump, a <code>printenv</code> in a build log, an APM that captures env), they aren't audited or versioned, and rotating them means redeploying. A dedicated secret store adds access control, auditing, versioning, and rotation that env vars lack — so env vars are fine for dev but a managed store is better for production.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Secrets Management.md" class="internal-link">Secrets Management</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Which OWASP Top 10 item is most commonly found in production .NET apps?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">A01 (Broken Access Control) is the most prevalent — 94% of tested apps had at least one instance.</li>
<li dir="auto">Common .NET manifestation: missing <code>[Authorize]</code> attributes, IDOR (insecure direct object references) where IDs are not validated against the current user.</li>
<li dir="auto">A05 (Security Misconfiguration) is also common: developer exception pages in production, missing security headers.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: fixing access control requires understanding the authorization model, not just adding attributes — resource-based authorization is more work but more correct.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/OWASP.md" class="internal-link">OWASP</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">How do you prevent SQL injection in a .NET application?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<ul>
<li dir="auto">Use parameterized queries always — EF Core and Dapper with parameters are safe by default.</li>
<li dir="auto">Never concatenate user input into SQL strings, even for dynamic ORDER BY or table names.</li>
<li dir="auto">For dynamic SQL, use allowlists (validate column names against a known set) rather than sanitization.</li>
<li dir="auto">Enable SQL Server's Query Store to detect unusual query patterns.</li>
<li dir="auto">Tradeoff: ORMs prevent injection but can generate inefficient queries — use raw parameterized SQL for performance-critical paths.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/OWASP.md" class="internal-link">OWASP</a></em></li>
</ul>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why does parameterizing a query fully prevent SQL injection, where input filtering does not?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">A parameter is transmitted to the database <strong>separately from the SQL text</strong>, and the query is parsed/planned <em>before</em> the value is bound — so the value can never change the statement's structure, no matter what characters it contains. Filtering tries to anticipate every dangerous input and always misses encodings/edge cases. Parameterization removes the entire class of attack rather than playing whack-a-mole with payloads.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Web Vulnerabilities.md" class="internal-link">Web Vulnerabilities</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the difference between XSS and CSRF?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><strong>XSS</strong> runs the attacker's <em>code</em> in the victim's browser within your site's origin (so it can read the DOM, cookies, and act with full privilege) — caused by unescaped untrusted data in your output. <strong>CSRF</strong> runs no code in your origin; it tricks the victim's browser into <em>sending a request</em> to your site using its existing cookies, and the attacker can't even read the response — caused by browsers auto-attaching cookies. XSS is fixed by output encoding/CSP; CSRF by anti-forgery tokens and <code>SameSite</code>. Notably, an XSS hole can defeat CSRF tokens, so XSS is the more severe of the two.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Web Vulnerabilities.md" class="internal-link">Web Vulnerabilities</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why are header-token (JWT) APIs less exposed to CSRF than cookie-session apps?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">CSRF works because browsers automatically include cookies on cross-site requests without the attacker's page needing to read or set anything. A bearer token sent in the <code>Authorization</code> header is <em>not</em> auto-attached — JavaScript on the attacker's origin can't read your token (same-origin policy) and can't set the header on a forged cross-site request. So pure header-token APIs sidestep CSRF; the risk returns the moment you store the token in a cookie, which is why cookie storage (good against XSS) must be paired with <code>SameSite</code> + anti-forgery.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Web Vulnerabilities.md" class="internal-link">Web Vulnerabilities</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><h2 id="security-authentication">Authentication</h2><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is Basic Auth unsafe over HTTP?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Base64 is encoding, not encryption — it is trivially reversible. Over HTTP, the <code>Authorization</code> header is sent in plaintext and visible to any network observer. Over HTTPS, the header is encrypted by TLS, making Basic Auth safe to use.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Basic Auth.md" class="internal-link">Basic Auth</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When is Basic Auth acceptable in production?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">For machine-to-machine calls between trusted services on an internal network over HTTPS, Basic Auth is acceptable when simplicity matters and the credential is a service account (not a user password). For user-facing authentication, use OAuth 2.0 / JWT Bearer — Basic Auth requires sending credentials on every request, which increases exposure.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Basic Auth.md" class="internal-link">Basic Auth</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What's the difference between OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">OAuth 2.0 is an <strong>authorization</strong> framework — it issues <em>access tokens</em> that let an app call an API on the user's behalf, but it says nothing standard about <em>who</em> the user is. OpenID Connect is a thin <strong>authentication</strong> layer on top that adds an <em>ID token</em> (a JWT with verified identity claims: <code>sub</code>, <code>email</code>, <code>name</code>). Rule of thumb: "Sign in with…" needs OIDC; "let this app read my calendar" is OAuth. Using a raw OAuth access token as proof of login is a classic mistake.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Oauth OIDC (OpenId Connect).md" class="internal-link">Oauth OIDC (OpenId Connect)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is PKCE required even for confidential clients now?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">PKCE binds the authorization request to the token exchange via a one-time <code>code_verifier</code>/<code>code_challenge</code>, so an intercepted authorization code is useless without the verifier. It was designed for public clients (SPAs/mobile that can't keep a secret), but the OAuth 2.1 guidance now recommends it for <strong>all</strong> clients because it also defends confidential clients against code-injection/interception, at essentially no cost.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Oauth OIDC (OpenId Connect).md" class="internal-link">Oauth OIDC (OpenId Connect)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Which flow do you use for a CLI tool or a smart TV, and why not Authorization Code?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">The <strong>Device Authorization (Device Code) flow</strong>: the device shows a short code and a URL, the user authorizes on a phone/laptop, and the device polls the token endpoint until approved. Authorization Code assumes a browser redirect back to the app, which input-constrained or browserless devices can't do. (Implicit and ROPC are deprecated and shouldn't be used at all.)<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Oauth OIDC (OpenId Connect).md" class="internal-link">Oauth OIDC (OpenId Connect)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What is the difference between role-based and resource-based authorization?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Role-based authorization checks what type of user you are (e.g., Admin, Editor). Resource-based authorization checks your relationship to a specific resource instance (e.g., are you the owner of this document?). Use role-based for coarse-grained access control; use resource-based when the decision depends on data.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Resource-based Auth.md" class="internal-link">Resource-based Auth</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why inject <code>IAuthorizationService</code> instead of checking ownership in the controller directly?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto"><code>IAuthorizationService</code> centralizes authorization logic in handlers, making it testable and reusable across controllers. Direct ownership checks in controllers scatter authorization logic, making it easy to miss a check or apply it inconsistently. The handler pattern also supports multiple requirements composing into a single policy.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Resource-based Auth.md" class="internal-link">Resource-based Auth</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What problem does SSO solve and what does it not solve?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">SSO eliminates repeated interactive logins across applications — users authenticate once and access many services. It does not replace per-application authorization (what the user can do) or per-application session management (how long the session lasts). Each application still manages its own session and access control.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/SSO (Single Sign-On).md" class="internal-link">SSO (Single Sign-On)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">What must you validate when accepting an ID token from an IdP?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">Signature (using IdP's public key), issuer, audience (your client_id), expiry, and nonce. Also validate the redirect URI matches the registered value. Missing any of these opens the door to token forgery, replay attacks, or open redirect vulnerabilities.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/SSO (Single Sign-On).md" class="internal-link">SSO (Single Sign-On)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When would you choose SAML over OIDC?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">When integrating with legacy enterprise IdPs that only support SAML (older ADFS, some SaaS products). For new systems, OIDC is simpler, better supported on mobile/SPA, and has a richer ecosystem of libraries. The cost of SAML: XML parsing complexity, larger message sizes, and harder debugging.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/SSO (Single Sign-On).md" class="internal-link">SSO (Single Sign-On)</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">Why is FIDO2/WebAuthn more secure than TOTP?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">TOTP codes can be phished — an attacker can trick a user into entering their code on a fake site. FIDO2 keys are bound to the origin domain: the browser only uses the key for the exact domain it was registered on, making phishing impossible. The private key also never leaves the device.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Two-Factor Auth.md" class="internal-link">Two-Factor Auth</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span><span><blockquote class="callout is-collapsible is-collapsed node-insert-event" data-callout="question" data-callout-fold="" data-callout-metadata=""><div dir="auto" class="callout-title"><div class="callout-icon"><svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" height="16" width="16"/></div><div class="callout-title-inner">When should you use TOTP vs FIDO2?</div><div class="fold-callout-icon is-collapsed"></div></div><div style="display: none;" class="callout-content"><div class="callout-content-inner">
<p dir="auto">TOTP is simpler to implement and works on any device with an authenticator app — use it for most applications. FIDO2 is phishing-resistant and required for high-assurance scenarios (banking, admin access, NIST AAL3). The cost is hardware dependency and more complex enrollment flow.<br />
<em><a href="Home/Security/Authentication/Two-Factor Auth.md" class="internal-link">Two-Factor Auth</a></em></p>
</div></div></blockquote></span></div>
